topics.title: Load test on a 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEEL507 - AC MACHINES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Load test on a 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Load Test on a 3-Phase Squirrel-Cage Induction Motor:** A direct-loading type performance evaluation test in which a mechanical load is progressively applied to the shaft of a 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor using an electro-mechanical loading device (such as a spring-balance brake drum/pulley arrangement, rope-and-drum brake, or eddy-current/magnetic powder dynamometer), while the stator is energized at its rated voltage and rated frequency, in order to experimentally determine the **steady-state operating performance characteristics** of the machine. The test directly measures: - **No-load speed** (close to synchronous) - **Full-load speed** (slightly below synchronous) - **Load current** (stator current at various loads) - **Input electrical power** (2-wattmeter or 3-wattmeter method) - **Mechanical output torque and shaft power** - **Slip** (per revolution counter / stroboscope / tachometer) - **Power factor, efficiency, and speed regulation** under varying load conditions ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Auto-Rickshaw Hill Test":** > Imagine you own an auto-rickshaw and want to know how well it performs. A "load test" is exactly what happens when the mechanic makes you drive the rickshaw up a hill with **one passenger, then two, then three, then four** — measuring how speed drops, how fuel consumption rises, and how much pull the engine can sustain. > > In the motor lab, the "hill" is the **electromagnetic resisting torque** of the brake. The mechanic's instruments are the **voltmeter, ammeter, wattmeters, and tachometer**. The number of passengers is the **mechanical load on the shaft**. A squirrel-cage induction motor behaves like this because: - **At no-load**, the rotor spins almost at synchronous speed (only a tiny slip is needed to generate enough torque to overcome friction). - **As load increases**, the rotor slows down just enough so that the relative speed between the rotating magnetic field and the rotor conductors increases, inducing **larger rotor currents** → **stronger torque**. - The motor therefore has a **dropping speed-load characteristic** that is remarkably "shunt-like" (almost constant speed). ### 1.3 Why This Test is Important in Engineering > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Performance verification:** Confirms whether the manufactured motor meets its name-plate ratings of efficiency, power factor, slip, and starting current under realistic operating conditions. > - **Industry acceptance:** The only test where the motor is operated at **full rated mechanical load**; hence it is the **most reliable indicator** of motor capability. > - **Thermal validation:** Allows the motor to reach thermal equilibrium under load, confirming that insulation class temperature limits are respected. > - **Design feedback:** Helps designers validate the equivalent-circuit parameters against actual measured performance. > - **Standard reference:** Among all no-load, blocked-rotor, and load tests, the **load test gives the most realistic operating data** because the motor runs under its true working condition. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Working Principle — Why the Motor Develops Torque Under Load When the stator of a 3-phase induction motor is supplied with balanced 3-phase voltages, it produces a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** at synchronous speed: $$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P}$$ where: - $N_s$ = synchronous speed in **rpm** - $f$ = supply frequency in **Hz** (typically **50 Hz** in India) - $P$ = total number of **poles** This RMF cuts the short-circuited rotor bars (squirrel-cage), inducing an EMF (per Faraday's law) and hence currents. By Lenz's law, the rotor currents oppose the relative motion, so the rotor is dragged in the direction of the RMF but at a slightly lower speed. The relative speed is called the **slip speed**: $$N_{slip} = N_s - N_r$$ The **per-unit (or percentage) slip** is defined as: $$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{f_r}{f}$$ where $f_r = s \cdot f$ is the **rotor frequency**. ### 2.2 Why Speed Drops with Load — The "Why" Behind the Curve - At **no-load**, only friction and windage losses must be overcome → rotor needs very little torque → slip is tiny (typically 0.1 % to 0.5 %). - As **mechanical load increases**, the rotor must develop more torque. - Per the torque-slip relation, **higher torque requires higher slip** (in the operating region). - Therefore, $N_r$ decreases slightly as load torque increases. - This produces the characteristic **drooping speed-load curve** typical of an induction motor. ### 2.3 Three Logical Stages of the Load Test 1. **Stage 1 — No-load condition:** Brake fully released, motor runs light. Record $V_{NL}$, $I_{NL}$, $P_{NL}$, $N_{NL}$. 2. **Stage 2 — Stepwise loading:** Increase brake tension in steps. At each step, allow the motor to reach steady-state (thermal + mechanical equilibrium), then record all readings. 3. **Stage 3 — Overload / Stability check (optional but recommended):** Continue loading up to ~125 % rated load, observing that the motor remains stable on its operating region of the torque-slip curve (does not cross the breakdown/pulling torque point). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All formulas below are High-Yield — must be memorized for the University Practical Exam viva and the ESE written paper.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | rpm | $f$ = 50 Hz for KTU labs | | 2 | Slip (per-unit) | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | dimensionless | Expressed as fraction | | 3 | Percentage slip | $s\% = s \times 100$ | % | Often asked directly | | 4 | Output shaft torque | $T_{sh} = (S_1 - S_2) \cdot R \cdot g$ | N·m | Spring-balance method | | 5 | Net tangential force | $F = (S_1 - S_2)$ | kgf | Subtraction of two balances | | 6 | Output power (shaft) | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi N_r T_{sh}}{60}$ | W | Mechanical output | | 7 | Input power (2-wattmeter) | $P_{in} = W_1 + W_2$ | W | For balanced/unbalanced loads | | 8 | Input power (3-wattmeter) | $P_{in} = W_A + W_B + W_C$ | W | Used when neutral available | | 9 | Power factor (2-wattmeter) | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{W_1 + W_2}{\sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L}$ | — | Most common in lab | | 10 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | % | Always < 100 % | | 11 | Percentage speed regulation | $\text{Reg}\% = \dfrac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100$ | % | For induction motors typically low | | 12 | Rotor copper loss | $P_{rcu} = s \cdot P_{ag}$ | W | $P_{ag}$ = air-gap power | | 13 | Air-gap power | $P_{ag} = \dfrac{P_{rcu}}{s}$ | W | Useful for circle diagram | **Where:** - $S_1$, $S_2$ = spring balance readings (in **kgf**) - $R$ = effective brake-drum radius in **metres** - $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²** - $V_L$, $I_L$ = line voltage and line current - $W_1$, $W_2$ = wattmeter readings (2-wattmeter method) ### 2.5 Real-World Utility - **Industrial acceptance testing:** Manufacturers use load tests to certify motors to **IS 325 / IEC 60034** standards before shipment. - **Energy audit:** Field engineers perform load tests on installed motors to compute **operating efficiency** and recommend rewinding or replacement if efficiency has degraded. - **Predictive maintenance:** Comparing today's load-test curve with the original factory curve reveals bearing wear, rotor-bar cracks, or stator winding deterioration. - **Pump and fan applications:** Centrifugal pumps and fans have $T \propto N^2$ load torque — so understanding the motor's torque-speed curve is critical for matching. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Shaft Torque Equation (Spring-Balance Brake Method) **Setup:** A rope is wound around a brake drum of effective radius $R$ (metres). One end of the rope is attached to spring balance $S_1$; the other end is attached to a counter-balance or second spring balance $S_2$. As the drum rotates, friction between the rope and drum creates a tangential retarding force. **Step 1 — Identify the tangential braking force.** The net tangential force on the drum surface is the difference between the two spring-balance tensions: $$F_{net} = S_1 - S_2 \quad \text{(in kgf)}$$ **Step 2 — Convert the force from kgf to Newtons.** Since $1 \text{ kgf} = g$ Newtons, where $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$: $$F_{net}(\text{N}) = (S_1 - S_2) \cdot 9.81$$ **Step 3 — Apply the definition of torque.** Torque is force multiplied by the perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation (here, the brake-drum radius $R$): $$T_{sh} = F_{net} \cdot R$$ Substituting Step 2: $$T_{sh} = (S_1 - S_2) \cdot 9.81 \cdot R \quad \text{N·m}$$ This is the **braking (load) torque**. At steady state, this equals the **shaft torque developed by the motor**, i.e., the **electromagnetic torque minus rotational losses (friction + windage)** — which is the **shaft torque** $T_{sh}$ available to do useful work. > [!NOTE] > In practice, $S_1$ is the **tight side** reading and $S_2$ is the **slack side** reading. Some labs use a single balance with a dead weight — in that case, $F_{net} = W - S$, where $W$ is the dead weight in kgf. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Shaft Output Power **Step 1 — Mechanical power from torque and angular velocity.** $$P_{out} = T_{sh} \cdot \omega_r$$ where $\omega_r$ is the rotor angular velocity in rad/s. **Step 2 — Convert rotor speed from rpm to rad/s.** Since one revolution = $2\pi$ radians, and one minute = 60 seconds: $$\omega_r = \frac{2\pi N_r}{60}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute.** $$P_{out} = T_{sh} \cdot \frac{2\pi N_r}{60}$$ Substituting the torque expression from §3.1: $$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N_r}{60} \cdot (S_1 - S_2) \cdot 9.81 \cdot R \quad \text{Watts}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation of Slip and Percentage Slip **Step 1 — Slip speed.** By definition, slip speed is the difference between synchronous and rotor speeds: $$N_{slip} = N_s - N_r \quad \text{rpm}$$ **Step 2 — Per-unit slip.** Dividing by synchronous speed to obtain a dimensionless quantity: $$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$ **Step 3 — Percentage form.** Multiplying by 100: $$s\% = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} \times 100$$ ### 3.4 Derivation of Power Factor (Two-Wattmeter Method) For a **balanced 3-phase, 3-wire** system, total power is given by the **two-wattmeter method**: $$P_{in} = W_1 + W_2$$ The total apparent power is: $$S = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L$$ Power factor is defined as: $$\cos\phi = \frac{P_{in}}{S} = \frac{W_1 + W_2}{\sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L}$$ > [!TIP] > The two-wattmeter method works for both balanced and unbalanced loads as long as there is **no neutral wire**. KTU labs universally use this method because the induction motor is a 3-wire load. ### 3.5 Efficiency Computation Efficiency is the ratio of useful mechanical output power to electrical input power: $$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$$ Substituting the expanded forms: $$\eta = \frac{(S_1 - S_2) \cdot 9.81 \cdot R \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N_r}{60}}{W_1 + W_2} \times 100\%$$ ### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Lab Data) **Given:** - Motor rating: 3-phase, **400 V**, **50 Hz**, **4-pole**, **7.5 kW**, **Δ-connected** - Brake-drum effective radius $R = 0.1 \text{ m}$ - Spring-balance readings at a certain load step: $S_1 = 12 \text{ kgf}$, $S_2 = 2 \text{ kgf}$ - Tachometer reading: $N_r = 1430 \text{ rpm}$ - Voltmeter reading: $V_L = 400 \text{ V}$ - Ammeter reading: $I_L = 14 \text{ A}$ - Wattmeter readings: $W_1 = 4400 \text{ W}$, $W_2 = 2400 \text{ W}$ **Step 1 — Synchronous speed.** $$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$ **Step 2 — Slip.** $$s = \frac{1500 - 1430}{1500} = \frac{70}{1500} = 0.0467$$ $$s\% = 4.67\%$$ **Step 3 — Net force.** $$F = S_1 - S_2 = 12 - 2 = 10 \text{ kgf}$$ **Step 4 — Shaft torque.** $$T_{sh} = 10 \times 9.81 \times 0.1 = 9.81 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 5 — Output shaft power.** $$P_{out} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1430 \times 9.81}{60} = 1469.3 \text{ W} \approx 1.469 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 6 — Input electrical power.** $$P_{in} = W_1 + W_2 = 4400 + 2400 = 6800 \text{ W} = 6.8 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 7 — Power factor.** $$\cos\phi = \frac{6800}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 14} = \frac{6800}{9697.6} = 0.7013 \text{ (lagging)}$$ **Step 8 — Efficiency.** $$\eta = \frac{1469.3}{6800} \times 100\% = 21.61\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The low efficiency indicates the motor is operating **far below rated load** (only ~1.47 kW out of 7.5 kW rating). Induction motors are most efficient at 75–100 % of full load. As load increases toward rated value in subsequent steps, efficiency will rise to typically **85–90 %**. ### 3.7 Typical Performance Characteristics to Plot For the lab record and the university viva, plot: 1. **Output power vs. line current** — $P_{out}$ on X-axis, $I_L$ on Y-axis (a rising curve). 2. **Slip vs. output power** — $P_{out}$ on X-axis, $s$ on Y-axis (slightly rising). 3. **Power factor vs. output power** — $P_{out}$ on X-axis, $\cos\phi$ on Y-axis (rising sharply from no-load to full-load). 4. **Efficiency vs. output power** — $P_{out}$ on X-axis, $\eta$ on Y-axis (peaks near rated load). 5. **Torque vs. slip** — $s$ on X-axis, $T_{sh}$ on Y-axis (S-shaped; rising portion is the stable operating region). 6. **Speed vs. output power** — $P_{out}$ on X-axis, $N_r$ on Y-axis (slight droop). --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of the Load Test Setup The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete interconnection of all instruments and the energy flow from the 3-phase mains through the motor to the mechanical loading device. ```mermaid flowchart LR M3PH[3-Phase AC Mains
400 V 50 Hz]:::supply SW3[TPMC Switch
Triple Pole]:::sw FUSE[Cartridge Fuses
3 Nos]:::protect STARTER[DOL Starter
or Star Delta]:::sw VOLT[V Voltmeter
Line Voltage]:::meter AMP1[A1 Ammeter
R Phase]:::meter AMP2[A2 Ammeter
Y Phase]:::meter W1[W1 Wattmeter]:::meter W2[W2 Wattmeter]:::meter MOTOR[3-Phase Squirrel
Cage Induction Motor]:::machine TACH[Tachometer
Digital Optical]:::meter BRAKE[Brake Drum with
Rope and Springs S1 S2]:::machine LOAD[Mechanical Load
Applied via Friction]:::load M3PH --> SW3 --> FUSE --> STARTER --> VOLT STARTER --> AMP1 --> W1 --> MOTOR STARTER --> AMP2 --> W2 --> MOTOR MOTOR --> TACH MOTOR --> BRAKE --> LOAD classDef supply fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937 classDef sw fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937 classDef protect fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937 classDef meter fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#064e3b,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937 classDef machine fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#581c87,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937 classDef load fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#7c2d12,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937 ``` ### 4.2 Circuit Connection Schematic (Wattmeter Placement) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph StatorSide[STATOR SIDE R Y B] R[R Phase]:::line --> L1[L1]:::sw Y[Y Phase]:::line --> L2[L2]:::sw B[B Phase]:::line --> L3[L3]:::sw end subgraph Instruments[INSTRUMENT CHAIN] L1 --> F1[F1 Fuse]:::protect --> CC1[CC1 MC]:::sw --> AMP1[A1 Ammeter]:::meter L2 --> F2[F2 Fuse]:::protect --> CC2[CC2 MC]:::sw --> AMP2[A2 Ammeter]:::meter L3 --> F3[F3 Fuse]:::protect --> CC3[CC3 MC]:::sw --> AMP3[A3 Ammeter]:::meter AMP1 --> W1CC[W1 Current Coil]:::meter AMP1 --> W1VC[W1 Voltage Coil
Across R Y]:::meter AMP2 --> W2CC[W2 Current Coil]:::meter AMP2 --> W2VC[W2 Voltage Coil
Across Y B]:::meter end subgraph MotorLoad[MOTOR AND LOAD] W1CC --> MOTOR[3-Phase SCIM
Squirrel Cage IM]:::machine W2CC --> MOTOR MOTOR --> SHAFT[Coupled Shaft]:::machine SHAFT --> BRAKEDRUM[Brake Drum
Radius R metres]:::machine BRAKEDRUM --> S1[Spring Balance S1]:::meter BRAKEDRUM --> S2[Spring Balance S2]:::meter end VOLT[V Voltmeter
R to Y]:::meter classDef line fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#713f12,stroke-width:1px,color:#1f2937 classDef sw fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:1px,color:#1f2937 classDef protect fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:1px,color:#1f2937 classDef meter fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#064e3b,stroke-width:1px,color:#1f2937 classDef machine fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#581c87,stroke-width:1px,color:#1f2937 ``` ### 4.3 Sequential Procedure Flow (Control Logic) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Ensure all switches
in OFF position]:::step --> B[Verify zero spring
balance readings]:::step B --> C[Wear PPE and check
earthing continuity]:::step C --> D[Close TPMC switch:
apply 3-phase supply]:::step D --> E[Start motor via DOL
or Star Delta starter]:::step E --> F[Wait for rated speed
stabilisation no load]:::step F --> G[Record no load readings:
V I W1 W2 N_r]:::step G --> H[Tighten brake nuts:
apply load step 1]:::step H --> I[Wait for steady state
thermal mechanical]:::step I --> J[Record all instrument
readings at this load]:::step J --> K{More load steps
to apply}:::check K -->|Yes| H K -->|No| L[Loosen brake fully
return to no load]:::step L --> M[Stop motor using
starter push button]:::step M --> N[Open TPMC switch
isolate supply]:::step N --> O[Compute torque slip
power factor efficiency]:::step O --> P[Plot performance
characteristic curves]:::step P --> Q[End of experiment]:::step classDef step fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937 classDef check fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937 ``` ### 4.4 Mapping of Performance Curves (Conceptual Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Inputs[Measured Inputs] I1[I_L Line Current]:::in I2[W1 W2 Wattmeter]:::in I3[N_r Rotor Speed]:::in I4[S1 S2 Spring Balance]:::in end subgraph Computations[Derived Quantities] C1[Slip s]:::calc C2[Torque T_sh]:::calc C3[Output P_out]:::calc C4[Power Factor]:::calc C5[Efficiency eta]:::calc end subgraph Graphs[Performance Curves to Plot] G1[P_out vs I_L]:::graph G2[s vs P_out]:::graph G3[cos phi vs P_out]:::graph G4[eta vs P_out]:::graph G5[T_sh vs s]:::graph G6[N_r vs P_out]:::graph end I1 --> C3 I2 --> C4 I2 --> C5 I3 --> C1 I3 --> C3 I4 --> C2 C2 --> C3 C1 --> G2 C1 --> G5 C2 --> G5 C3 --> G1 C3 --> G2 C3 --> G3 C3 --> G4 C3 --> G6 C4 --> G3 C5 --> G4 classDef in fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:1px,color:#1f2937 classDef calc fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#713f12,stroke-width:1px,color:#1f2937 classDef graph fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#064e3b,stroke-width:1px,color:#1f2937 ``` ### 4.5 Important Practical Notes > [!IMPORTANT] > - The **rope must be tightly wound** around the brake drum, with no slip between rope and drum (or readings will be erroneous). > - The **effective radius** $R$ is measured to the **centre of the rope**, not the drum surface. > - Cool the brake drum with **continuous water flow** (in older labs) or use a **magnetic powder brake** with forced-air cooling (modern labs). > - Always take readings **after the motor has reached thermal equilibrium** (typically 5 minutes after each load step). > - Never apply load beyond **125 % of rated** — risk of damaging windings. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is meant by "slip" in a 3-phase induction motor? Why can an induction motor never run at synchronous speed under loaded conditions?* **Model Answer:** Slip $s$ is the relative difference between the synchronous speed $N_s$ of the rotating magnetic field and the actual rotor speed $N_r$, expressed as a fraction (or percentage) of $N_s$: $$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$ An induction motor cannot run at synchronous speed under load because torque production depends on **relative motion** between the rotating magnetic field and the rotor conductors. If $N_r = N_s$, then there is **no relative speed**, hence **no induced EMF in the rotor, no rotor current, and no torque**. The motor would therefore have no torque to overcome even friction, and any disturbance would cause it to slow down. Thus, under loaded operation, $N_r < N_s$ always, giving finite slip. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the "why") --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *List any three performance characteristics obtained from the load test on a 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor.* **Model Answer:** The three principal performance characteristics obtained are: 1. **Efficiency vs. output power** — rises from zero at no-load, peaks near rated load (typically 75–100 % of full load), and falls slightly beyond rated load. 2. **Power factor vs. output power** — very poor at no-load (≈ 0.1–0.2 lagging) because most input power is magnetising, rises steeply with load to about 0.8–0.9 at full load. 3. **Slip vs. output power** — almost linearly increasing with load; at full load typically 3–5 % for standard squirrel-cage motors. Other valid characteristics: speed vs. output, torque vs. slip, current vs. output, etc. (3 marks: 1 mark each) --- ### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks with internal choice. Sub-parts (a) and (b) typically carry 7 marks each. --- #### **QUESTION A — Choice 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Draw the circuit diagram for conducting the load test on a 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor using the **two-wattmeter method** and a **rope-and-drum brake**. Label all instruments clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** In a load test on a 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole, Δ-connected squirrel-cage induction motor, the following readings were obtained at a particular load: spring balances $S_1 = 18$ kgf and $S_2 = 3$ kgf; effective brake-drum radius $R = 0.075$ m; rotor speed $N_r = 1440$ rpm; line current $I_L = 12$ A; wattmeter readings $W_1 = 3600$ W and $W_2 = 2200$ W. Calculate: - (i) Synchronous speed and percentage slip - (ii) Shaft torque - (iii) Shaft output power - (iv) Power factor - (v) Efficiency at this load **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution to (a): The required circuit must show: 1. **3-phase supply** (R, Y, B) fed through a **TPMC isolator switch** and **fuses**. 2. **DOL starter** (or star-delta for larger motors) with overload relay. 3. **Voltmeter** connected between any two lines (say R–Y). 4. **Two ammeters** in lines R and Y (third line current inferred). 5. **Two wattmeters**: - $W_1$ current coil in line R; voltage coil between R and Y. - $W_2$ current coil in line Y; voltage coil between Y and B. 6. **Motor stator** terminals (U, V, W) connected to the load-side of the starter. 7. **Brake drum** mounted on the motor shaft, with rope wound around it; rope ends attached to two spring balances $S_1$ (tight side) and $S_2$ (slack side), resting on the ground. (7 marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for proper labelling, 1 for wattmeter connection correctness, 1 for brake arrangement, 1 for neatness) ##### Model Solution to (b): **(i) Synchronous speed and percentage slip** $$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$ $$s\% = \frac{1500 - 1440}{1500} \times 100 = \frac{60}{1500} \times 100 = 4\%$$ (2 marks) **(ii) Shaft torque** $$T_{sh} = (S_1 - S_2) \cdot 9.81 \cdot R = (18 - 3) \times 9.81 \times 0.075$$ $$T_{sh} = 15 \times 9.81 \times 0.075 = 11.036 \text{ N·m}$$ (1 mark) **(iii) Shaft output power** $$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N_r T_{sh}}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1440 \times 11.036}{60}$$ $$P_{out} = \frac{99807.4}{60} = 1663.46 \text{ W} \approx 1.663 \text{ kW}$$ (1 mark) **(iv) Power factor** $$P_{in} = W_1 + W_2 = 3600 + 2200 = 5800 \text{ W}$$ $$\cos\phi = \frac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} V_L I_L} = \frac{5800}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 12} = \frac{5800}{8313.8} = 0.6976 \text{ lagging}$$ (2 marks) **(v) Efficiency** $$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{1663.46}{5800} \times 100 = 28.68\%$$ (1 mark) --- #### **QUESTION B — Choice 2** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the construction of a **3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor**. List any **four advantages** of a squirrel-cage motor over a slip-ring (wound-rotor) induction motor. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz squirrel-cage induction motor running at a slip of 4 % draws a line current of 20 A at a power factor of 0.85 lagging from a 400 V supply. The friction and windage losses total 250 W. The stator copper losses are 600 W, and core losses are 400 W. Calculate: - (i) Rotor copper loss - (ii) Output (shaft) power - (iii) Efficiency **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution to (a): **Construction (brief):** A 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor consists of: - **Stator:** A laminated silicon-steel core with semi-closed slots housing a 3-phase distributed winding. The winding is connected to the 3-phase AC supply. - **Rotor:** A laminated cylindrical core mounted on the shaft, with **un-insulated copper or aluminium bars** placed in slots. All bars are short-circuited at both ends by **end rings**, forming a "cage" — hence the name "squirrel-cage". - **Bearings and end-shields:** To support the rotor and provide ventilation. - **Cooling fan:** Mounted on the shaft. (4 marks for construction) **Four Advantages:** 1. **Simple and rugged construction** — no brushes, slip rings, or rotor windings. 2. **Low maintenance** — fewer parts to wear out. 3. **High reliability and longer life** — no sparking, suitable for explosive environments. 4. **Lower cost** — cheaper to manufacture than wound-rotor motors. (3 marks: 1 each for first three, 1 collectively for the fourth) ##### Model Solution to (b): **Given:** $P = 6$ poles, $f = 50$ Hz, $s = 0.04$, $I_L = 20$ A, $\cos\phi = 0.85$, $V_L = 400$ V, $P_{fw} = 250$ W, $P_{sc} = 600$ W, $P_{core} = 400$ W. **(i) Rotor copper loss** Step 1 — Input power: $$P_{in} = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \cos\phi = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 20 \times 0.85 = 11777.6 \text{ W}$$ Step 2 — Air-gap power (input minus stator losses): $$P_{ag} = P_{in} - P_{sc} - P_{core} = 11777.6 - 600 - 400 = 10777.6 \text{ W}$$ Step 3 — Rotor copper loss: $$P_{rcu} = s \cdot P_{ag} = 0.04 \times 10777.6 = 431.1 \text{ W}$$ (3 marks) **(ii) Output (shaft) power** Mechanical power developed (gross) = $P_{ag} - P_{rcu} = P_{ag}(1-s) = 10777.6 \times 0.96 = 10346.5$ W Shaft output = Mechanical power developed − rotational losses: $$P_{out} = 10346.5 - P_{fw} = 10346.5 - 250 = 10096.5 \text{ W} \approx 10.10 \text{ kW}$$ (2 marks) **(iii) Efficiency** $$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{10096.5}{11777.6} \times 100 = 85.72\%$$ (2 marks) --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks — Read Carefully:** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert kgf to Newtons.** Many students directly multiply $(S_1 - S_2) \times R$ without multiplying by $g = 9.81$. This gives torque in **kgf·m**, not N·m. Result: all subsequent power and efficiency calculations are wrong by a factor of 9.81. **Maximum loss: 4–5 marks.** > > 2. **Using wrong synchronous-speed formula.** Some students write $N_s = 60f/P$ (which is the formula for synchronous frequency in rev/s, not rev/min). Always use $N_s = 120f/P$ for **rpm**. > > 3. **Sign errors in slip.** Slip must be computed as $(N_s - N_r)$, not the reverse. If $N_r > N_s$ (impossible in normal operation), the result indicates measurement error — note this in the record. > > 4. **Power factor sign.** Always write **"lagging"** after the numerical value. An induction motor is, by its nature, an inductive load. > > 5. **In the 2-wattmeter method, do not average $W_1$ and $W_2$.** Total power = $W_1 + W_2$, NOT their average. A common error. > > 6. **Not mentioning cooling of the brake drum** in the procedure. Examiners specifically look for this safety/operational point. > > 7. **In the record, do not forget to plot graphs with proper axis labels, units, and a clear title.** A neat graph with proper labels typically fetches 2–3 extra marks. > > 8. **No-load current is NOT zero.** It is typically 30–50 % of full-load current because the magnetising requirement is independent of load. Forgetting this is a viva-viva trap question. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final High-Density Summary — Memorise Before the Exam:** - **Aim:** To determine the performance characteristics of a 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor under varying mechanical load. - **Apparatus:** 3-phase SCIM, DOL/star-delta starter, ammeter(s), voltmeter, two wattmeters, tachometer, rope-brake drum with two spring balances, metre rule. - **Key formula — Synchronous speed:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ (in **rpm**) - **Key formula — Slip:** $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$; percentage slip = $s \times 100$ % - **Key formula — Shaft torque:** $T_{sh} = (S_1 - S_2) \cdot 9.81 \cdot R$ (in **N·m**) - **Key formula — Output power:** $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi N_r T_{sh}}{60}$ (in **W**) - **Key formula — Input power (2-wattmeter):** $P_{in} = W_1 + W_2$ - **Key formula — Power factor:** $\cos\phi = \dfrac{W_1 + W_2}{\sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L}$ (**lagging** for an induction motor) - **Key formula — Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ % - **Precautions:** - Take readings only after **steady state** is reached. - Keep **rope tight** around drum; no slip. - **Cool brake drum** adequately (water/air). - Do not overload beyond **125 %** of rated. - Verify **earthing** before starting. - Use **PPE** (insulated gloves, goggles). - **Graphs to plot:** - $P_{out}$ vs. $I_L$ (rising) - $P_{out}$ vs. $\cos\phi$ (rising sharply) - $P_{out}$ vs. $\eta$ (peaks near rated load) - $P_{out}$ vs. $s$ (slightly rising) - $P_{out}$ vs. $N_r$ (slightly falling) - **Inference:** - Speed regulation of an induction motor is **very small** (typically 2–5 %). - Power factor is **poor at no-load**, **best at full-load**. - Efficiency is **maximum near rated load**. - The motor has a **stiff speed characteristic** — suitable for constant-speed drives. - **Typical viva questions:** 1. Why is the no-load current not zero? 2. Why is the power factor low at no-load? 3. Why does the rotor slip increase with load? 4. What is the function of the centrifugal switch (in single-phase motors — useful comparison)? 5. Why is a squirrel-cage rotor called "short-circuited"? 6. What is the difference between air-gap power and shaft power? 7. Why is efficiency poor at very low loads? 8. Why is the rotor copper loss equal to $s \cdot P_{ag}$? ---

topics.title: Load test on a 3-phase slip-ring induction motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL507 - AC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Load test on a 3-phase slip-ring induction motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Load Test Definition (KTU Board Standard)**
> The **load test** on a 3-phase slip-ring induction motor is a *direct/indirect method* of testing in which the motor is mechanically loaded from **no-load to full-load** (and sometimes up to pull-out torque) using a calibrated loading device (rope-and-drum brake / eddy-current / DC generator), and its **performance characteristics** — speed, slip, torque, output power, input current, power factor, and efficiency — are computed and plotted against output power.

A **slip-ring (wound-rotor) induction motor** is a rotating AC machine in which the rotor winding is brought out through **slip rings and brushes** to an external **star-connected 3-phase rheostat**. By varying this external resistance, the rotor circuit resistance is altered, which in turn changes the starting current, starting torque, and the **slip at which maximum torque occurs**, while keeping the stator supply fixed.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Think of the slip-ring motor like a **bicycle with adjustable gears** (derailleur):

- The **stator** is the *fixed frame and the front chainring* — it always receives the same "pedalling effort" from the road (3-phase mains).
- The **rotor** is the *rear wheel and cassette*.
- The **slip rings and external rheostat** act like the **gear-shifter** — by moving to a "lower gear" (more rotor resistance), you can climb a steeper hill (start a heavy load) without the chain snapping (excessive inrush current).
- When the bike is unloaded (freewheeling), the rear wheel spins almost as fast as the pedalling cadence → low slip. As you load the bike, the wheel slows slightly → slip increases, just like a real induction motor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key NEP-2020 / KTU 2024 Highlight**
> In the lab, students are expected to (i) record readings, (ii) draw at least **5 characteristic curves**, and (iii) compute **efficiency, slip, and torque** for each loading step. The *interpretation* of curves (not just plotting) carries evaluation weight under **CO3: Design/Development of solutions** of the 2024 scheme.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- Synchronous speed reference: $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ in **rpm** (with $f = 50\text{ Hz}$ in India).
- Standard slip at full load for a slip-ring motor: **2 % – 5 %**.
- Torque constant for rope-brake: $T = (W_1 - W_2) \cdot g \cdot R$ in **N·m**.
- Mechanical output: $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi N_r T}{60}$ in **Watts**.
- 3-phase input: $P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \, V_L I_L \cos\phi$ in **Watts** (for balanced load).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why is the Load Test Performed?

The load test is the **only direct means** of obtaining the actual mechanical output of the motor. Unlike the no-load test or blocked-rotor test (which give separated losses), the load test gives:

- The **complete operating behaviour** under real loading.
- The **efficiency** as a function of load.
- The **speed regulation** and the **slip-load curve**.
- The **line current vs output** curve (used for protective relay settings in industry).
- The **torque-slip / torque-speed** characteristic — central to choosing a motor for a given drive (e.g., conveyor, hoist, crusher).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **slip-ring motor**, the load test is *typically conducted with the external rotor rheostat set to a value that gives good starting torque with acceptable starting current* (often the maximum tap is used at start, then cut out in steps as the motor accelerates).

### 2.2 Working Principle Behind Each Measured Quantity

1. **Stator input (Wattmeter reading $W$)** — total 3-phase active power consumed.
2. **Stator current $I_L$** — read from the line ammeter; rises with load.
3. **Speed $N_r$** — measured using a **tachometer** (non-contact preferred); falls slightly as load increases because slip rises.
4. **Slip $s$** — derived from speed: $s = (N_s - N_r) / N_s$.
5. **Torque $T_{sh}$** — measured *mechanically* using a rope-and-drum brake:

   - Two spring balances (dead weights $W_1$, $W_2$) pull a rope wrapped around a brake drum of radius $R$ fixed to the motor shaft.
   - Net tangential force = $(W_1 - W_2) \, g$ Newtons.
   - Net shaft torque = $(W_1 - W_2) g R$ N·m.
6. **Rotor current $I_2$** — read from the rotor-side ammeter (slip-ring motors only; cage motors cannot have this measurement).
7. **Power factor $\cos\phi$** — derived from input power, line voltage, and line current.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Print-Ready Table)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|------|---------|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | rpm | $f=50$ Hz, $P$ = number of poles |
| 2 | Fractional slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | per unit (p.u.) | Or $s \times 100$ for % |
| 3 | Rotor speed | $N_r = N_s(1 - s)$ | rpm | Measured directly |
| 4 | Shaft torque (rope brake) | $T_{sh} = (W_1 - W_2)\, g\, R$ | N·m | $g = 9.81$ m/s² |
| 5 | Mechanical output | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi N_r T_{sh}}{60}$ | W | Often expressed in kW |
| 6 | Input power (3-$\phi$) | $P_{in} = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | W | Or wattmeter reading |
| 7 | Power factor | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L}$ | — | Lagging for motor |
| 8 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | % | Should rise with load |
| 9 | Slip frequency | $f_2 = s f$ | Hz | Used in rotor-copper-loss calculations |
| 10 | Rotor Cu loss | $P_{rCu} = 3 I_2^2 R_2$ | W | For slip-ring motor with ext. $R_2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In the **steel, cement, and mining industries** of Kerala, slip-ring motors with load tests are routinely employed for crushers, ball mills, and hoists because their **high starting torque and low starting current** prevent mains dip during direct-on-line starting of large cages.

### 2.4 Underlying Theory — Why Torque Depends on Slip

The developed electromagnetic torque in an induction motor is:

$$T_e = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_{th}^2 \cdot (R_2 / s)}{\left(R_{th} + R_2 / s\right)^2 + \left(X_{th} + X_2\right)^2}$$

where $\omega_s = 2\pi N_s / 60$, $R_{th}$ and $X_{th}$ are the Thevenin equivalent resistance and reactance referred to the rotor, and $R_2$ is the rotor resistance **per phase** (including external rheostat for slip-ring motors).

- At $s = 0$ (synchronous speed) → $T_e = 0$ (motor produces no torque; would need to be driven).
- At $s = s_m$ (slip of max torque) → torque peaks.
- Slip of maximum torque: $s_m = \dfrac{R_2}{\sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_{th} + X_2)^2}}$.

> **Why this matters for slip-ring motors:** Because $s_m \propto R_2$, simply **adding external rotor resistance** shifts the max-torque point to a *higher slip* (closer to start), giving a **high starting torque** — exactly what heavy-load applications need.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Numerical & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Brake-Drum Torque Equation

Consider a brake drum of mean radius $R$ metres rigidly fixed to the motor shaft. A rope is wrapped around the drum, with one end attached to a **dead-weight side spring balance** reading $W_1$ (kg) and the other end to a **tightening side spring balance** reading $W_2$ (kg). The motor rotates such that the friction on the rope produces a tangential force.

The net tangential force on the drum surface is:

$$F_t = (W_1 - W_2) \, g \quad \text{(Newtons)}$$

The shaft torque developed (and equal to the electromagnetic torque minus rotational losses) is the product of this force and the drum radius:

$$\boxed{T_{sh} = (W_1 - W_2) \, g \, R \quad \text{(N·m)}}$$

**Why minus sign?** $W_1$ is on the side where the drum tries to *lift* the weight (effort side), $W_2$ is on the side where the operator pulls to *tighten* the brake (load side). The difference gives the *net useful* tangential force.

### 3.2 Derivation of Output Power

Mechanical power at the shaft equals torque times angular velocity:

$$P_{out} = T_{sh} \cdot \omega_r = T_{sh} \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N_r}{60} \quad \text{(Watts)}$$

If $T_{sh}$ is in N·m and $N_r$ in rpm, dividing by 60 converts revolutions per minute to revolutions per second, and $2\pi$ converts to radians.

$$\boxed{P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi N_r T_{sh}}{60} \quad \text{(W)}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Slip and Rotor Speed

By definition, slip is the relative difference between synchronous and actual speeds, expressed per unit:

$$s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; N_r = N_s (1 - s) \quad \text{and} \quad s = 1 - \dfrac{N_r}{N_s}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Format)

**Problem Statement:**
A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz, 400 V slip-ring induction motor is loaded by a rope-and-drum brake. The drum radius is $R = 0.1$ m. During a particular load step, the two spring balances read $W_1 = 9.5$ kg and $W_2 = 2.5$ kg. The line ammeter reads 18 A, the wattmeter (total) reads 8.5 kW, and the tachometer reads 940 rpm.

Compute: **(a)** Synchronous speed, slip, shaft torque and output power. **(b)** Power factor and efficiency. **(c)** Comment on whether the motor is overloaded.

#### 3.4.1 Part (a) — Speed, Slip, Torque, Output

**Step 1 — Synchronous speed:**

$$N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2 — Actual speed (given):** $N_r = 940$ rpm.

**Step 3 — Fractional slip:**

$$s = \dfrac{1000 - 940}{1000} = 0.06 \quad \text{i.e., } 6\%$$

**Step 4 — Shaft torque using rope-brake formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{sh} &= (W_1 - W_2) \cdot g \cdot R \\
&= (9.5 - 2.5) \times 9.81 \times 0.1 \\
&= 7.0 \times 9.81 \times 0.1 \\
&= 6.867 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Output power:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{out} &= \dfrac{2\pi N_r T_{sh}}{60} \\
&= \dfrac{2\pi \times 940 \times 6.867}{60} \\
&= \dfrac{40\,545.7}{60} \\
&\approx 675.76 \text{ W} \approx 0.676 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

#### 3.4.2 Part (b) — Power Factor and Efficiency

**Step 6 — Power factor (3-$\phi$ balanced):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos\phi &= \dfrac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L} \\
&= \dfrac{8500}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 18} \\
&= \dfrac{8500}{12\,470.7} \\
&\approx 0.6815 \text{ lagging}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \dfrac{675.76}{8500} \times 100 \approx 7.95\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> The efficiency obtained (≈ 8 %) appears **abnormally low** because the motor is operating at **light load** (only 0.676 kW of 5–7 kW rated output). This is *expected* and is a *teaching point* of the load test: induction motors have low efficiency at light loads and reach peak efficiency around 70–80 % of full load.

#### 3.4.3 Part (c) — Loading Comment

The motor is **grossly under-loaded**. The rotor current, power factor, torque and output are all far below full-load values. Recommend increasing the rope-brake tension to bring the load up to near the rated 5 HP / 3.7 kW.

### 3.5 Complete Numerical Reference Table (for the Above Example)

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Frequency | $f$ | 50 | Hz |
| Poles | $P$ | 6 | — |
| Line voltage | $V_L$ | 400 | V |
| Line current | $I_L$ | 18 | A |
| Input power | $P_{in}$ | 8500 | W |
| Synchronous speed | $N_s$ | 1000 | rpm |
| Rotor speed | $N_r$ | 940 | rpm |
| Slip | $s$ | 0.06 | p.u. |
| Net brake weight | $W_1 - W_2$ | 7.0 | kg |
| Drum radius | $R$ | 0.1 | m |
| Shaft torque | $T_{sh}$ | 6.867 | N·m |
| Output power | $P_{out}$ | 675.76 | W |
| Power factor | $\cos\phi$ | 0.6815 | lagging |
| Efficiency | $\eta$ | 7.95 | % |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Block-Level Architecture)

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Experimental Test Bench

> The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional interconnections** of the slip-ring induction motor load test setup, including the **stator-side 3-phase supply path, the rotor-side rheostat path, and the mechanical loading path** through the rope-and-drum brake.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[3 Phase 415 V 50 Hz AC Mains] --> B[TPMC Three Pole MCB and Variac]
    B --> C[Voltmeter VL Line to Line]
    B --> D[Ammeter IL Stator Line]
    B --> E[Wattmeter W1 and W2 Method]
    E --> F[Slip Ring IM Stator Terminals U V W]
    F --> G[Rotor Slip Rings a b c]
    G --> H[3 Phase Star Connected Rotor Rheostat]
    H --> I[Rotor Side Ammeter I2]
    F --> J[Mechanical Shaft Output]
    J --> K[Brake Drum Radius R Coupled to Shaft]
    K --> L[Spring Balance W1 Tight Side]
    K --> M[Spring Balance W2 Slack Side]
    J --> N[Tachometer Non Contact Speed Sensor]
    L --> O[Net Force W1 minus W2 into g into R equals Torque Tsh]
    M --> O
    O --> P[Mechanical Output Pout 2 pi N Tsh by 60]
    E --> Q[Electrical Input Pin]
    P --> R[Efficiency Eta Pout by Pin times 100]
    Q --> R
```

### 4.2 ASCII Schematic — Sequence of Operations Flow

```
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  START                                           |
+--------------------------------------------------+
            |
            v
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  STEP 1: Record nameplate (V, I, kW, rpm, P, f)  |
+--------------------------------------------------+
            |
            v
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  STEP 2: Verify all rotor-rheostat contacts open  |
+--------------------------------------------------+
            |
            v
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  STEP 3: Energize stator via TP-MCB              |
+--------------------------------------------------+
            |
            v
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  STEP 4: Cut out rotor resistance in steps until  |
|          motor accelerates to rated speed         |
+--------------------------------------------------+
            |
            v
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  STEP 5: Take NO-LOAD reading (V, I, W, N, W1,W2)|
+--------------------------------------------------+
            |
            v
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  STEP 6: Tighten rope brake gradually in 5-7 steps|
|          Record V, I, W, N, W1, W2, I2 at each   |
+--------------------------------------------------+
            |
            v
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  STEP 7: Release brake, switch off, compute and  |
|          plot 5 characteristic curves            |
+--------------------------------------------------+
            |
            v
+----------------------+
|         END          |
+----------------------+
```

### 4.3 Functional Roles of Each Instrument

| Block | Function | Why Required |
|-------|----------|--------------|
| MCB + Variac | Protection and voltage control | Safety, gradual build-up |
| Voltmeter $V_L$ | Line-to-line voltage | For $\sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$ |
| Ammeter $I_L$ | Stator line current | For input & PF |
| Wattmeter | 3-$\phi$ input power | For $\eta$ calculation |
| Tachometer | Rotor speed | Slip calculation |
| Spring balances $W_1, W_2$ | Rope-brake forces | Torque measurement |
| Rotor ammeter $I_2$ | Rotor current (slip-ring only) | Rotor copper loss & rheostat setting |
| Star rheostat | Variable rotor resistance | Adjust slip / starting torque |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1.** Why is a **slip-ring induction motor** preferred over a squirrel-cage motor for **high-inertia heavy starting loads** such as a ball-mill crusher? *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A slip-ring induction motor allows the **rotor circuit resistance to be increased externally** through slip rings and a star-connected rheostat at start. Increasing $R_2$ shifts the slip at which maximum torque occurs toward starting ($s \approx 1$), producing a **very high starting torque (up to 2–2.5 × full-load)** while **limiting the starting current**. A cage motor cannot have its rotor resistance varied; hence it draws a high inrush (6–7 × rated) and delivers only 1.5–2 × rated starting torque. For crushers and mills the mechanical load demands a soft but powerful start — exactly what a slip-ring motor with progressive rheostat cut-out delivers.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** What is the role of the **rope-and-drum brake** in the load test of an induction motor, and write the formula for shaft torque. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The rope-and-drum brake is a **mechanical loading device** that applies a controllable frictional torque to the motor shaft, simulating a real load. Two spring balances measure the tension in a rope wrapped around a drum of known radius fixed to the shaft.

$$T_{sh} = (W_1 - W_2) \cdot g \cdot R \quad \text{(N·m)}$$

This torque, combined with measured speed, gives the mechanical output $P_{out} = 2\pi N_r T_{sh}/60$ from which efficiency is computed.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Slot A]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat **block diagram**, explain the experimental procedure to conduct a load test on a **3-phase slip-ring induction motor** using a **rope-and-drum brake** loading arrangement. List the instruments used. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 4-pole, 50 Hz, 400 V, 3-phase slip-ring induction motor gave the following readings during load test:

| S.No. | $I_L$ (A) | $P_{in}$ (W) | $W_1$ (kg) | $W_2$ (kg) | $N_r$ (rpm) | $I_2$ (A) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 6.0 | 1200 | 1.5 | 0.5 | 1480 | 30 |
| 2 | 9.5 | 2900 | 4.0 | 1.0 | 1460 | 38 |
| 3 | 13.0 | 4500 | 6.0 | 1.5 | 1445 | 45 |
| 4 | 17.0 | 6100 | 8.0 | 2.0 | 1425 | 52 |
| 5 | 22.0 | 7900 | 9.5 | 2.5 | 1400 | 58 |

Brake-drum radius $R = 0.075$ m. Calculate for **load step 3**: (i) slip, (ii) shaft torque, (iii) output power, (iv) efficiency and (v) power factor. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution

**(a) Procedure & Block Diagram (7 marks):**
1. Note nameplate details: V, I, kW, rpm, P, f, connection, class of insulation.
2. Make connections as per the circuit diagram: 3-phase supply → MCB → variac (if used) → stator terminals; rotor terminals → star-connected external rheostat through slip rings and brushes.
3. Insert all rotor-rheostat resistance at start (all sections in circuit).
4. Switch on the supply; motor starts with high starting torque and low current.
5. As the motor accelerates, **progressively cut out** rotor resistance step by step until the rheostat is fully cut out and the rotor is short-circuited (for normal running).
6. Apply no-load; record all meter readings.
7. Gradually tighten the rope brake to load the motor. For each step record: $V_L$, $I_L$, wattmeter $W$, spring balances $W_1$ & $W_2$, tachometer $N_r$, and rotor ammeter $I_2$.
8. Take 5–6 sets of readings up to about 25 % overload.
9. Release brake gradually, switch off supply.
10. Compute torque, slip, output, efficiency, and PF; plot curves.

**Instruments used:** 3-phase variac, TP-MCB, voltmeter, ammeter, wattmeter (2-W method), tachometer, two spring balances, rope-and-drum brake, rotor ammeter, star-connected rheostat.

**(b) Numerical for Load Step 3 (7 marks):**

Given: $P = 4$ poles, $f = 50$ Hz, $V_L = 400$ V, $I_L = 13.0$ A, $P_{in} = 4500$ W, $W_1 = 6.0$ kg, $W_2 = 1.5$ kg, $N_r = 1445$ rpm, $R = 0.075$ m, $I_2 = 45$ A.

**(i) Slip:**

$$N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

$$s = \dfrac{1500 - 1445}{1500} = \dfrac{55}{1500} = 0.0367 = 3.67\%$$

**(ii) Shaft Torque:**

$$T_{sh} = (W_1 - W_2)\, g\, R = (6.0 - 1.5) \times 9.81 \times 0.075 = 4.5 \times 9.81 \times 0.075$$

$$T_{sh} = 3.311 \text{ N·m}$$

**(iii) Output Power:**

$$P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi N_r T_{sh}}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1445 \times 3.311}{60} = \dfrac{30\,053.6}{60} = 500.9 \text{ W}$$

**(iv) Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{500.9}{4500} \times 100 = 11.13\%$$

**(v) Power Factor:**

$$\cos\phi = \dfrac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L} = \dfrac{4500}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 13.0} = \dfrac{4500}{9007.4} = 0.4996 \text{ lagging}$$

---

#### **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Slot B]**

**(a)** Explain with a **neat sketch / block diagram** the constructional features of a **slip-ring (wound rotor) induction motor**. How does it differ from a squirrel-cage motor? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The following readings were taken during the **load test of a 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz slip-ring induction motor**:

| S.No. | $I_L$ (A) | $W$ (W) | $W_1$ (kg) | $W_2$ (kg) | $N_r$ (rpm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4.5 | 800 | 0.5 | 0.0 | 980 |
| 2 | 8.0 | 2000 | 2.5 | 0.5 | 970 |
| 3 | 12.5 | 3700 | 5.0 | 1.0 | 955 |
| 4 | 18.0 | 5500 | 7.0 | 1.5 | 940 |
| 5 | 24.0 | 7400 | 9.0 | 2.0 | 925 |

Drum radius $R = 0.08$ m. Plot the **torque vs slip** and **efficiency vs output** characteristics and determine the **load at which maximum efficiency** occurs. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution

**(a) Construction & Difference (7 marks):**

A slip-ring induction motor has:
- A **stator** identical to that of a squirrel-cage motor: laminated silicon-steel core with 3-phase distributed windings, housed in a cast-iron frame.
- A **rotor** with a *3-phase double-layer distributed winding* (similar to the stator) embedded in slots. The three rotor phases are connected internally in **star**, and the three free ends are brought out and soldered to **three slip rings** mounted on the shaft.
- **Brushes** ride on the slip rings and connect to an **external star-connected 3-phase rheostat** through terminals on a panel.

**Differences from Squirrel-Cage Motor:**

| Feature | Squirrel-Cage | Slip-Ring |
|---|---|---|
| Rotor winding | Conducting bars, shorted by end-rings | 3-phase insulated winding, brought out via slip rings |
| External resistance | Cannot be added | Can be varied through slip rings |
| Starting current | High (5–7 × FLC) | Low (1.5–2 × FLC) |
| Starting torque | Moderate (1.5–2 × FLT) | High (2–2.5 × FLT) |
| Cost & maintenance | Low | Higher (brushes, slip rings) |
| Speed control | Limited (PWM/VFD) | Possible via rotor resistance |
| Construction | Simple, rugged | Complex |

**(b) Plotting and Maximum Efficiency (7 marks):**

Step 1: Compute $N_s = 120 \times 50 / 6 = 1000$ rpm for all readings.

Step 2: For each reading, compute $s$, $T_{sh}$, $P_{out}$, $\eta$:

| S.No. | $s$ | $T_{sh}$ (N·m) | $P_{out}$ (W) | $\eta$ (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.020 | 0.3924 | 38.06 | 4.76 |
| 2 | 0.030 | 1.5696 | 159.43 | 7.97 |
| 3 | 0.045 | 3.1392 | 313.97 | 8.49 |
| 4 | 0.060 | 4.3164 | 424.83 | 7.72 |
| 5 | 0.075 | 5.4936 | 532.21 | 7.19 |

**Sample calculation for S.No. 3:**

$$s = (1000-955)/1000 = 0.045$$

$$T_{sh} = (5.0 - 1.0) \times 9.81 \times 0.08 = 3.1392 \text{ N·m}$$

$$P_{out} = 2\pi \times 955 \times 3.1392 / 60 = 313.97 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta = 313.97/3700 \times 100 = 8.49\%$$

Step 3: **Torque vs Slip** is a curve that rises monotonically (the operating point is on the *stable* region between $s = 0$ and $s = s_m$). Plot $s$ on X-axis, $T_{sh}$ on Y-axis.

Step 4: **Efficiency vs Output** is a curve that **rises, peaks, then falls**. From the table the maximum $\eta = 8.49\%$ occurs at **S.No. 3** with $P_{out} \approx 314$ W (about 4–4.5 % of rated motor capacity — light-load region).

> **Conclusion:** The motor exhibits peak efficiency at the **third load step** because beyond that the stator copper losses (rising with $I_L^2$) dominate over the rise in output. For best operation, the motor should be loaded to that operating point.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks (KTU Board Pattern 2024):**
> 1. **Unit errors in slip** — students often write $s = 0.04$ as **4 rpm** or **4 %**; the correct statement is "fractional slip = 0.04 p.u. = 4 %". Marks are lost for mixing units.
> 2. **Wrong sign in brake equation** — using $T = (W_1 + W_2) g R$ instead of $(W_1 - W_2) g R$. Always state clearly which balance is on the *tight* side and which on the *slack* side.
> 3. **Forgetting $\sqrt{3}$** in 3-phase input PF computation; some students use $P = V_L I_L \cos\phi$ (single-phase formula).
> 4. **Neglecting $g = 9.81$** — the formula is in **Newtons**, so the kg readings *must* be multiplied by $g$.
> 5. **Plotting torque vs speed instead of torque vs slip** — KTU specifically asks for **torque-slip** curve as one of the mandatory 5 graphs.
> 6. **Not stating "lagging"** for motor power factor — strictly write $\cos\phi = 0.68$ **lagging**.
> 7. **Rotor ammeter (slip-ring only)**: many students try to take $I_2$ reading for cage motors — examiners deduct marks for this conceptual error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Slip-ring motor**: rotor resistance **adjustable** via external rheostat → controls starting current, starting torque, and slip at max torque.
- **Load test purpose**: obtain speed, slip, torque, output, PF, and efficiency characteristics *under load*.
- **Loading device**: rope-and-drum brake (most common in KTU labs).
- **Mandatory graphs to plot** (5 curves, KTU 2024):
  1. Speed $N_r$ vs Output Power $P_{out}$
  2. Slip $s$ vs Output Power $P_{out}$
  3. Torque $T_{sh}$ vs Slip $s$
  4. Efficiency $\eta$ vs Output Power $P_{out}$
  5. Power Factor $\cos\phi$ vs Output Power $P_{out}$ (and often Line Current $I_L$ vs Output Power)
- **Key formulas** (memorize all):
  - $N_s = 120f/P$, $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$
  - $T_{sh} = (W_1 - W_2) g R$
  - $P_{out} = 2\pi N_r T_{sh}/60$
  - $P_{in} = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \cos\phi$
  - $\eta = P_{out}/P_{in} \times 100$
- **Use $g = 9.81$ m/s²** when converting kg → N.
- **PF is always lagging** for an induction motor.
- **Efficiency is low at light load**, peaks at 70–80 % of full load, falls beyond.
- **Rotor current can be measured** in slip-ring motors — used to compute rotor Cu loss.
- **External rheostat progressively cut out** during starting; fully removed in normal running.
- **Slip of maximum torque** $s_m = R_2 / \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_{th}+X_2)^2}$ is proportional to rotor resistance — the *fundamental* design insight for slip-ring drives.
- **Typical full-load slip**: 2–5 % for slip-ring motors (lower than cage, which is 3–6 %).
- **Safety first**: never start the motor with the brake fully tightened; never leave the rheostat fully in circuit during steady-state running.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: No-load and blocked-rotor tests on a 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL507 - AC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** No-load and blocked-rotor tests on a 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **No-Load Test** and the **Blocked-Rotor Test** are the two standardized, indirect experimental procedures prescribed by **IS 4029 / IEEE Standard 112** for determining the equivalent-circuit parameters of a three-phase squirrel-cage induction motor without loading the machine mechanically. Together, these two tests allow the complete prediction of the motor's steady-state performance — efficiency, power factor, slip, torque, and current — across its entire operating range.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCEEL507 – Module 2):** Conducting the no-load and blocked-rotor tests forms the experimental backbone of Module 2. The students are expected to plot the **circle diagram** of the induction motor from the test data and predict performance characteristics at any desired operating slip.

**No-Load Test (Running-Light Test):**
The motor is run at rated voltage and frequency with **no mechanical load** coupled to the shaft. The applied voltage is then varied from approximately **110 % to 50 %** of the rated value. The readings of input power, line voltage, line current, and stator winding temperature are recorded at each step.

**Blocked-Rotor Test (Locked-Rotor Test):**
The rotor is mechanically prevented from rotating (locked), and a **reduced balanced three-phase voltage** is applied to the stator. The voltage is gradually increased from zero until the motor draws **rated current**. Readings of input power, line voltage, line current, and stator winding temperature are recorded.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Health Check-up" Analogy 🩺**
> Imagine you want to assess a person’s fitness without making them run a marathon. You ask them to **stand still** (no-load test) to check their *resting metabolism* — how much energy they burn just to exist, and how their breathing system (magnetizing branch) functions. Then, you gently **push against a wall** (blocked-rotor test) to see how their muscles (rotor resistance) and joints (leakage reactance) respond under stress. From these two quiet measurements, doctors can estimate how the person will perform in a full sprint. The induction motor is similar: the no-load test reveals the **iron + friction + windage losses and magnetizing reactance**, while the blocked-rotor test reveals the **stator + rotor copper losses and leakage reactances**. Combining both, we can predict the *full marathon performance* — the complete running characteristic of the motor.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Rated Frequency:** 50 Hz (India), 60 Hz (USA/Japan) — **bold** = mandatory in reports.
- **Synchronous Speed:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ where $P$ is the number of poles.
- **Slip:** $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$.
- **At No-Load:** $s \approx 0.001$ to $0.005$ (negligible).
- **At Blocked-Rotor:** $s = 1$ (rotor is stationary).

## 1.4 Why These Two Tests? — KTU Perspective

> [!NOTE]
> A direct loaded test on an induction motor requires an expensive **mechanical loading arrangement** (DC generator with resistive load bank, or a pony brake). The no-load and blocked-rotor tests are preferred because:
> 1. They require **no shaft loading**.
> 2. They are **fast, economical, and repeatable**.
> 3. They yield the **complete equivalent circuit parameters**.
> 4. The **circle diagram** drawn from their results gives the motor's performance at *any slip* — a single graphical tool that KTU examiners love to test.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit

The complete per-phase equivalent circuit of a three-phase induction motor referred to the stator side consists of the following elements in series–parallel combination:

| Element | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Stator resistance | $R_1$ | Ohmic loss in stator winding |
| Stator leakage reactance | $X_1$ | Stator flux that does not cross the air gap |
| Magnetizing reactance | $X_m$ | Main flux linking both stator and rotor |
| Core-loss resistance | $R_c$ | Represents hysteresis + eddy current loss |
| Rotor resistance referred to stator | $R_2'$ | Rotor copper loss |
| Rotor leakage reactance referred to stator | $X_2'$ | Rotor flux that does not cross the air gap |
| Mechanical load | $\dfrac{R_2'}{s}(1-s)$ | Developed mechanical power component |

The circuit is configured as: $R_1$ and $X_1$ in **series** with the parallel combination of the **magnetizing branch** ($R_c \parallel X_m$) and the **rotor branch** ($R_2'/s + jX_2'$).

## 2.2 No-Load Test — Underlying Theory

### 2.2.1 Conditions
- Slip $s \approx 0$, hence $\dfrac{R_2'}{s} \rightarrow \infty$.
- The rotor branch becomes an **open circuit**.
- The motor draws only the **magnetizing current** $I_m$ to establish the air-gap flux.

### 2.2.2 What the Test Reveals
The equivalent circuit under no-load reduces to a series combination of $R_1$, $X_1$, with the magnetizing branch $(R_c \parallel X_m)$. The total no-load current $I_0$ is small (typically 25–35 % of full-load current for standard industrial motors).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power measurement at no-load gives the sum of:**
> 1. **Stator core (iron) losses** — $P_i$
> 2. **Friction and windage losses** — $P_{fw}$
> 3. **No-load stator copper loss** — $3 I_0^2 R_1$ (small but non-zero)
>
> Hence, $P_0 = P_i + P_{fw} + 3 I_0^2 R_1$

### 2.2.3 Why Vary the Voltage?
By varying the applied voltage and reading $P_0$ and $I_0$, we obtain a family of points. Extrapolating to **zero voltage** gives the **pure mechanical loss** (friction + windage), since at zero voltage there is no iron loss and no stator copper loss — only friction remains. This separation of $P_i$ and $P_{fw}$ is essential for accurate circle diagram construction.

## 2.3 Blocked-Rotor Test — Underlying Theory

### 2.3.1 Conditions
- Slip $s = 1$, hence $\dfrac{R_2'}{s} = R_2'$.
- The rotor is stationary, so **no mechanical power** is developed.
- The mechanical branch $\dfrac{R_2'}{s}(1-s) = 0$ — confirming that all input power (minus stator copper loss) goes into rotor copper loss.

### 2.3.2 What the Test Reveals
At blocked-rotor, the magnetizing branch $(R_c \parallel X_m)$ is **shunted by the very low rotor impedance** $R_2' + jX_2'$. Because $R_2' + jX_2' \ll R_c \parallel jX_m$, the magnetizing current is **negligible** and is often **ignored in KTU-level analysis**.

The equivalent circuit reduces to a simple series combination:
$$Z_{br} = (R_1 + R_2') + j(X_1 + X_2')$$

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU mistake is to assume $X_1 = X_2'$. This is **not** required by the standard. If not specified otherwise, the standard **design assumption** is $X_1 = X_2'$ (i.e., each equals $X_{br}/2$). This assumption is **explicitly** used in KTU board examinations.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes **every formula** required for solving KTU university questions on this experiment. The pipe symbol `|` is replaced by `\vert` to maintain table integrity.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Source Test |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | rpm | Rated nameplate |
| 2 | Full-load slip | $s_{fl} = \dfrac{N_s - N_{fl}}{N_s}$ | dimensionless | Nameplate |
| 3 | No-load power factor | $\cos \phi_0 = \dfrac{P_0}{\sqrt{3}\, V_L I_0}$ | dimensionless | No-load test |
| 4 | No-load phase voltage | $V_{ph} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ (star) or $V_L$ (delta) | V | No-load test |
| 5 | No-load phase current | $I_{ph} = I_L$ (star) or $\dfrac{I_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ (delta) | A | No-load test |
| 6 | No-load impedance magnitude | $Z_0 = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{I_{ph}}$ | $\Omega$ | No-load test |
| 7 | Stator resistance | $R_1 = \dfrac{\text{DC drop per phase}}{2 \cdot I_{dc}}$ (wound) | $\Omega$ | DC test / voltmeter-ammeter |
| 8 | Magnetizing + leakage branch (combined) | $X_0 = \sqrt{\vert Z_0 \vert^2 - (R_1 + R_{c0})^2}$ | $\Omega$ | No-load test |
| 9 | Blocked-rotor power factor | $\cos \phi_{br} = \dfrac{P_{br}}{\sqrt{3}\, V_{L,br} I_{br}}$ | dimensionless | Blocked-rotor test |
| 10 | Blocked-rotor impedance (per phase) | $Z_{br} = \dfrac{V_{ph,br}}{I_{ph,br}}$ | $\Omega$ | Blocked-rotor test |
| 11 | Rotor resistance referred to stator | $R_2' = Z_{br}\cos \phi_{br} - R_1$ | $\Omega$ | Blocked-rotor test |
| 12 | Total leakage reactance | $X_1 + X_2' = Z_{br}\sin \phi_{br}$ | $\Omega$ | Blocked-rotor test |
| 13 | Standard assumption (KTU default) | $X_1 = X_2' = \dfrac{X_1 + X_2'}{2}$ | $\Omega$ | Design assumption |
| 14 | Mechanical loss separation | Extrapolate $P_0$ vs $V^2$ to $V = 0$ → $P_{fw}$ | W | No-load curve |
| 15 | Iron loss at rated voltage | $P_i = P_0 \text{(rated)} - P_{fw} - 3 I_0^2 R_1$ | W | No-load test |
| 16 | Circle diagram line length | $\text{OA} = \dfrac{I_{fl}}{I_{br,ph}}$ (per unit) | cm | Circle diagram |
| 17 | Output line on circle | $\text{From O along } \cos\phi_{fl}\text{ line}$ | cm | Circle diagram |
| 18 | Slip from circle | $s = \dfrac{\text{running point to OA}}{\text{rotor line length}}$ | dimensionless | Circle diagram |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> The circle diagram obtained from these two tests is used by **motor design engineers** in industries like:
> - **Crompton Greaves, Siemens, ABB, Bharat Bijlee** — for performance certification of every induction motor manufactured.
> - **Energy audit teams** — to predict efficiency at any operating point and recommend replacement of inefficient motors.
> - **Power-system studies** — to compute reactive power demand, power factor, and starting current for **transformer and cable sizing**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Full KTU-Style Solution

> **Problem (Typical KTU 14-Mark Pattern):**
> A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz, 400 V induction motor gave the following test results:
>
> **No-load test (line values):** $V_0 = 400$ V, $I_0 = 8$ A, $P_0 = 600$ W
> **Blocked-rotor test (line values):** $V_{br} = 100$ V, $I_{br} = 25$ A, $P_{br} = 1500$ W
> **Stator DC resistance between two terminals:** $1.2\ \Omega$
> **Rotor full-load speed:** $1440$ rpm
>
> Determine:
> (a) The per-phase equivalent circuit parameters referred to the stator.
> (b) The slip, mechanical power output, torque, and efficiency at full load.
> (c) The starting torque and maximum torque.

Assume star-connected stator.

### 3.1.1 Part (a) — Equivalent Circuit Parameters

**Step 1: Identify the connection and convert line values to phase values.**

For a star-connected stator, phase voltage and phase current are obtained as:

$$V_{ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} = 230.94\ \text{V}$$

$$I_{ph} = I_L = 8\ \text{A (no-load)}, \quad I_{ph} = I_L = 25\ \text{A (blocked-rotor)}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the per-phase stator resistance $R_1$ from the DC test.**

The DC resistance is measured between two stator terminals. For a star-connected stator, the resistance measured between any two terminals is $2 R_1$ (since the path passes through two phase windings in series). Therefore:

$$R_1 = \frac{R_{dc}}{2} = \frac{1.2}{2} = 0.6\ \Omega \text{ per phase}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion to AC resistance:** KTU convention at B.Tech level is to **take $R_1$ as the DC value** and use it directly. However, in the absence of DC test data, $R_1$ is often estimated as $0.5 \times Z_{br} \cos \phi_{br}$ — a design fallback. The present problem gives explicit DC test data, so we use the DC value.

**Step 3: Compute the no-load parameters.**

The no-load power factor is calculated from the line measurements:

$$\cos \phi_0 = \frac{P_0}{\sqrt{3}\, V_L I_0} = \frac{600}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 8} = \frac{600}{5542.56} = 0.1083$$

$$\sin \phi_0 = \sqrt{1 - 0.1083^2} = \sqrt{1 - 0.01173} = \sqrt{0.9883} = 0.9941$$

The no-load impedance magnitude per phase is:

$$Z_0 = \frac{V_{ph}}{I_{ph}} = \frac{230.94}{8} = 28.87\ \Omega$$

The no-loop-loop input power per phase:

$$P_{0,ph} = \frac{P_0}{3} = \frac{600}{3} = 200\ \text{W per phase}$$

**Step 4: Compute the blocked-rotor parameters.**

The blocked-rotor power factor:

$$\cos \phi_{br} = \frac{P_{br}}{\sqrt{3}\, V_{L,br} I_{br}} = \frac{1500}{\sqrt{3} \times 100 \times 25} = \frac{1500}{4330.13} = 0.3464$$

$$\sin \phi_{br} = \sqrt{1 - 0.3464^2} = \sqrt{1 - 0.1200} = \sqrt{0.8800} = 0.9381$$

The blocked-rotor impedance magnitude per phase:

$$Z_{br} = \frac{V_{ph,br}}{I_{ph,br}} = \frac{100/\sqrt{3}}{25} = \frac{57.735}{25} = 2.309\ \Omega$$

**Step 5: Split $Z_{br}$ into $R$ and $X$ components.**

The total resistance as seen from the stator during blocked-rotor:

$$R_{br} = Z_{br} \cos \phi_{br} = 2.309 \times 0.3464 = 0.7999 \approx 0.80\ \Omega$$

The total leakage reactance:

$$X_{br} = Z_{br} \sin \phi_{br} = 2.309 \times 0.9381 = 2.166 \approx 2.166\ \Omega$$

**Step 6: Find $R_2'$ (rotor resistance referred to stator).**

$$R_2' = R_{br} - R_1 = 0.80 - 0.60 = 0.20\ \Omega$$

**Step 7: Find $X_1$ and $X_2'$ using the standard KTU assumption $X_1 = X_2'$.**

$$X_1 = X_2' = \frac{X_{br}}{2} = \frac{2.166}{2} = 1.083\ \Omega$$

**Step 8: Find the magnetizing branch parameters from no-load data.**

The no-loop-loop input power per phase equals stator copper loss plus the no-load core + mechanical loss delivered to the magnetizing + mechanical branch. To separate $R_c$ from $X_m$ we use the no-loop-loop power:

The stator copper loss at no-load:

$$P_{cu1,0} = 3 I_0^2 R_1 = 3 \times 8^2 \times 0.6 = 115.2\ \text{W}$$

The total core + mechanical + stray loss (assuming $P_{fw} = 0$ in this simplified problem):

$$P_{i} = P_0 - P_{cu1,0} = 600 - 115.2 = 484.8\ \text{W}$$

For the simplified KTU analysis, we ignore the mechanical loss and treat all of it as the iron loss. The magnetizing branch is approximately:

$$R_c = \frac{V_{ph}^2}{P_i / 3} = \frac{(230.94)^2}{484.8/3} = \frac{53333.3}{161.6} = 330.0\ \Omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> In many KTU problems, only $X_m$ is asked and $R_c$ is ignored (taken as infinite). The magnetizing reactance from the no-load data is found by:

$$X_m = \frac{V_{ph}}{I_m} \quad \text{where} \quad I_m = I_0 \sin \phi_0 - I_0' \sin \phi_0'$$

A simpler approach: treat the no-load impedance as approximately equal to the magnetizing branch, since the stator impedance drops are small at no-load:

$$X_0 \approx X_m + X_1 \Rightarrow X_m \approx \sqrt{Z_0^2 - R_1^2} - X_1 = \sqrt{28.87^2 - 0.6^2} - 1.083$$

$$X_m \approx \sqrt{833.48 - 0.36} - 1.083 = \sqrt{833.12} - 1.083 = 28.86 - 1.083 = 27.78\ \Omega$$

### 3.1.2 Part (b) — Performance at Full Load

**Step 9: Compute full-load slip.**

The synchronous speed:

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500\ \text{rpm}$$

The full-load slip:

$$s_{fl} = \frac{N_s - N_{fl}}{N_s} = \frac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \frac{60}{1500} = 0.04 \;\; (4\%)$$

**Step 10: Compute the rotor input and mechanical power developed.**

The rotor impedance at slip $s$:

$$Z_2 = \frac{R_2'}{s} + j X_2' = \frac{0.20}{0.04} + j\, 1.083 = 5.0 + j\, 1.083\ \Omega$$

$$\vert Z_2 \vert = \sqrt{5.0^2 + 1.083^2} = \sqrt{25 + 1.173} = \sqrt{26.173} = 5.116\ \Omega$$

The rotor current at full load (per phase, approximate — ignoring magnetizing branch):

$$I_2' = \frac{V_{ph}}{Z_1 + Z_2} = \frac{230.94}{(0.6 + j 1.083) + (5.0 + j 1.083)} = \frac{230.94}{5.6 + j 2.166}$$

$$= \frac{230.94}{\sqrt{5.6^2 + 2.166^2}} = \frac{230.94}{\sqrt{31.36 + 4.69}} = \frac{230.94}{\sqrt{36.05}} = \frac{230.94}{6.004} = 38.46\ \text{A}$$

**Step 11: Compute the air-gap power and developed mechanical power.**

The rotor input power (per phase):

$$P_{g,ph} = I_2'^2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s} = 38.46^2 \times 5.0 = 1479.2 \times 5.0 = 7396\ \text{W}$$

Total air-gap power (3 phases):

$$P_g = 3 \times 7396 = 22{,}188\ \text{W}$$

The developed mechanical power (gross):

$$P_m = (1 - s_{fl}) \cdot P_g = 0.96 \times 22{,}188 = 21{,}301\ \text{W}$$

The rotor copper loss:

$$P_{cu2} = s_{fl} \cdot P_g = 0.04 \times 22{,}188 = 887.5\ \text{W}$$

**Step 12: Compute the output power, net torque, and efficiency.**

The output power (assuming friction + windage = 200 W as a typical assumption, but here we are not given — assume it equals 0 for simplicity in this analytical step, or the problem provides it):

Assuming $P_{fw} = 150$ W (a reasonable assumption for a 22 kW motor), the net output power:

$$P_{out} = P_m - P_{fw} = 21{,}301 - 150 = 21{,}151\ \text{W} \approx 21.15\ \text{kW}$$

The net output torque:

$$T_{out} = \frac{P_{out} \times 60}{2 \pi N_{fl}} = \frac{21{,}151 \times 60}{2 \pi \times 1440} = \frac{1{,}269{,}060}{9047.79} = 140.27\ \text{N·m}$$

The input power:

$$P_{in} = P_g + \text{stator copper loss + core loss} = 22{,}188 + (3 \times 38.46^2 \times 0.6) + 484.8$$

$$P_{cu1} = 3 \times 1479.2 \times 0.6 = 2662.6\ \text{W}$$

$$P_{in} = 22{,}188 + 2662.6 + 484.8 = 25{,}335.4\ \text{W}$$

The efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{21{,}151}{25{,}335} \times 100 = 83.49\ \%$$

### 3.1.3 Part (c) — Starting Torque and Maximum Torque

**Step 13: Starting torque ($s = 1$).**

The rotor current at $s = 1$:

$$I_{2,start}' = \frac{V_{ph}}{(R_1 + R_2') + j(X_1 + X_2')} = \frac{230.94}{0.8 + j\, 2.166}$$

$$\vert Z_{start} \vert = \sqrt{0.8^2 + 2.166^2} = \sqrt{0.64 + 4.692} = \sqrt{5.332} = 2.309\ \Omega$$

$$I_{2,start}' = \frac{230.94}{2.309} = 100.0\ \text{A}$$

The starting torque per phase:

$$T_{start,ph} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{ph}^2 \cdot (R_2'/1)}{(R_1 + R_2'/1)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$$

The synchronous angular velocity:

$$\omega_s = \frac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08\ \text{rad/s}$$

The starting torque:

$$T_{start} = \frac{3}{157.08} \times \frac{230.94^2 \times 0.20}{0.8^2 + 2.166^2} = \frac{3}{157.08} \times \frac{10{,}666 \times 0.20}{5.332}$$

$$T_{start} = 0.01910 \times \frac{2133.2}{5.332} = 0.01910 \times 400.0 = 7.64\ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 14: Slip at maximum torque.**

The slip at which maximum torque occurs is independent of load and is given by:

$$s_{mT} = \frac{R_2'}{\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}$$

$$s_{mT} = \frac{0.20}{\sqrt{0.6^2 + 2.166^2}} = \frac{0.20}{\sqrt{0.36 + 4.692}} = \frac{0.20}{\sqrt{5.052}} = \frac{0.20}{2.248} = 0.0890$$

**Step 15: Maximum torque value.**

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{2 \omega_s} \times \frac{V_{ph}^2}{R_1 + \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}$$

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{2 \times 157.08} \times \frac{230.94^2}{0.6 + \sqrt{5.052}} = 0.00955 \times \frac{53{,}333}{0.6 + 2.248}$$

$$T_{max} = 0.00955 \times \frac{53{,}333}{2.848} = 0.00955 \times 18{,}728 = 178.85\ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 16: Analytical Matrix — Summary of Results.**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stator resistance | $R_1$ | 0.6 | $\Omega$ |
| Rotor resistance referred | $R_2'$ | 0.20 | $\Omega$ |
| Stator leakage reactance | $X_1$ | 1.083 | $\Omega$ |
| Rotor leakage reactance referred | $X_2'$ | 1.083 | $\Omega$ |
| Magnetizing reactance | $X_m$ | 27.78 | $\Omega$ |
| Core-loss resistance | $R_c$ | 330.0 | $\Omega$ |
| Full-load slip | $s_{fl}$ | 0.04 | — |
| Full-load rotor current | $I_2'$ | 38.46 | A |
| Air-gap power | $P_g$ | 22.19 | kW |
| Developed mechanical power | $P_m$ | 21.30 | kW |
| Net output power | $P_{out}$ | 21.15 | kW |
| Output torque | $T_{out}$ | 140.27 | N·m |
| Efficiency | $\eta$ | 83.49 | % |
| Starting torque | $T_{start}$ | 7.64 | N·m |
| Maximum torque | $T_{max}$ | 178.85 | N·m |
| Slip at max torque | $s_{mT}$ | 0.0890 | — |

---
