# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is the blocked-rotor test performed at reduced voltage and the no-load test performed at rated voltage?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **blocked-rotor test** is performed at **reduced voltage** because if rated voltage were applied with the rotor locked, the motor would draw a very large current (5 to 7 times full-load current) and the stator winding would overheat dangerously within seconds, potentially burning the insulation. The applied voltage is therefore gradually increased from zero **only up to the value that draws rated current** ($I_{br} = I_{fl}$). The measured values are then referred to rated voltage for parameter extraction.
>
> Conversely, the **no-load test** is performed at **rated voltage** because the motor's iron loss, magnetizing current, and core saturation characteristics are all functions of the operating flux level. To obtain realistic magnetizing branch parameters ($X_m$, $R_c$), the motor must be operated at its **normal flux density**, which corresponds to rated voltage. (3 marks: 1 for reduced V reason, 1 for rated V reason, 1 for parameter relevance)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What assumption is made about the magnetizing branch during the blocked-rotor test? Justify.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> During the blocked-rotor test, the **magnetizing branch ($R_c$ and $X_m$) is assumed to be absent or shifted to the input terminals** and is often **neglected** in the equivalent circuit for the test. The justification is that under blocked-rotor conditions, the slip is $s = 1$, so the rotor branch impedance is $R_2' + jX_2'$, which is **very small** (typically 10–20 % of $X_m$). The magnetizing branch impedance being very large in comparison, carries a negligible current. Therefore, the equivalent circuit reduces to a simple series of $R_1 + R_2' + j(X_1 + X_2')$, and the test directly yields the **leakage parameters** without contamination from magnetizing current. (3 marks: 1 assumption + 1 justification with slip = 1, 1 numerical comparison)

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz, 400 V induction motor is star-connected. The following test data were obtained:
- No-load test: $V_0 = 400$ V, $I_0 = 5$ A, $W_0 = 350$ W (total)
- Blocked-rotor test: $V_{br} = 80$ V, $I_{br} = 20$ A, $W_{br} = 1100$ W (total)
- Stator DC resistance per phase: $0.5\ \Omega$
- Full-load speed: $940$ rpm

Calculate the **per-phase equivalent circuit parameters** referred to the stator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the parameters obtained in part (a), compute the **starting current, starting torque, and maximum torque** of the motor. State the **slip at maximum torque**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Equivalent Circuit Parameters (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Synchronous speed and full-load slip.

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000\ \text{rpm}$$

$$s_{fl} = \frac{N_s - N_{fl}}{N_s} = \frac{1000 - 940}{1000} = 0.06$$

**Step 2:** Phase voltages and currents (star connection).

$$V_{ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} = 230.94\ \text{V (no-load)}, \quad V_{ph,br} = \frac{80}{\sqrt{3}} = 46.19\ \text{V}$$

$$I_{ph} = I_L = 5\ \text{A (no-load)}, \quad I_{ph} = I_L = 20\ \text{A (blocked)}$$

**Step 3:** No-load power factor and impedance.

$$\cos \phi_0 = \frac{W_0}{\sqrt{3} V_L I_0} = \frac{350}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 5} = \frac{350}{3464.1} = 0.1010$$

$$Z_0 = \frac{V_{ph}}{I_{ph}} = \frac{230.94}{5} = 46.19\ \Omega$$

**Step 4:** Blocked-rotor power factor and impedance.

$$\cos \phi_{br} = \frac{W_{br}}{\sqrt{3} V_{L,br} I_{br}} = \frac{1100}{\sqrt{3} \times 80 \times 20} = \frac{1100}{2771.3} = 0.3970$$

$$Z_{br} = \frac{V_{ph,br}}{I_{ph,br}} = \frac{46.19}{20} = 2.310\ \Omega$$

**Step 5:** Split $Z_{br}$.

$$R_{br} = Z_{br} \cos \phi_{br} = 2.310 \times 0.3970 = 0.917\ \Omega$$

$$X_{br} = Z_{br} \sin \phi_{br} = 2.310 \times 0.9178 = 2.120\ \Omega$$

**Step 6:** Extract $R_2'$.

$$R_2' = R_{br} - R_1 = 0.917 - 0.5 = 0.417\ \Omega$$

**Step 7:** Standard assumption for leakage reactances.

$$X_1 = X_2' = \frac{X_{br}}{2} = \frac{2.120}{2} = 1.060\ \Omega$$

**Part (b) — Starting and Maximum Torque (7 marks)**

**Step 8:** Synchronous angular velocity.

$$\omega_s = \frac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 9:** Starting current and starting torque.

At $s = 1$, the per-phase impedance is $Z_{start} = (R_1 + R_2') + j(X_1 + X_2') = 0.917 + j\, 2.120\ \Omega$.

$$\vert Z_{start} \vert = \sqrt{0.917^2 + 2.120^2} = \sqrt{0.841 + 4.494} = \sqrt{5.335} = 2.310\ \Omega$$

$$I_{start} = \frac{V_{ph}}{\vert Z_{start} \vert} = \frac{230.94}{2.310} = 100.0\ \text{A (per phase)}$$

$$I_{start,line} = I_{start,ph} = 100.0\ \text{A (for star)}$$

$$T_{start} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{ph}^2 \cdot R_2'}{\vert Z_{start} \vert^2} = \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{230.94^2 \times 0.417}{2.310^2}$$

$$T_{start} = 0.02865 \times \frac{53{,}333 \times 0.417}{5.335} = 0.02865 \times \frac{22{,}240}{5.335} = 0.02865 \times 4169 = 119.4\ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 10:** Slip at maximum torque.

$$s_{mT} = \frac{R_2'}{\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}} = \frac{0.417}{\sqrt{0.5^2 + 2.120^2}} = \frac{0.417}{\sqrt{0.25 + 4.494}} = \frac{0.417}{2.178} = 0.1915$$

**Step 11:** Maximum torque.

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{2 \omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{ph}^2}{R_1 + \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}$$

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{2 \times 104.72} \cdot \frac{230.94^2}{0.5 + 2.178} = 0.01432 \times \frac{53{,}333}{2.678}$$

$$T_{max} = 0.01432 \times 19{,}915 = 285.2\ \text{N·m}$$

**Final Answer Summary for Question A:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $R_1$ | $0.500\ \Omega$ |
| $R_2'$ | $0.417\ \Omega$ |
| $X_1 = X_2'$ | $1.060\ \Omega$ |
| $I_{start}$ | $100.0\ \text{A}$ |
| $T_{start}$ | $119.4\ \text{N·m}$ |
| $s_{mT}$ | $0.1915$ |
| $T_{max}$ | $285.2\ \text{N·m}$ |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch how the **circle diagram** of a 3-phase induction motor is constructed from the **no-load and blocked-rotor test data**. List the quantities that can be predicted from the circle diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** From the no-load test on a 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz, 400 V induction motor, the following readings were taken at varying voltages:

| $V_L$ (V) | 400 | 360 | 320 | 280 | 240 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $I_0$ (A) | 6.0 | 5.0 | 4.2 | 3.5 | 2.8 |
| $P_0$ (W) | 480 | 410 | 350 | 295 | 250 |

**Determine** the **friction and windage loss** and the **iron loss at rated voltage**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — Circle Diagram Construction (7 marks)**

**Construction Steps:**

1. **Choose a current scale**, e.g., 1 cm = $K$ amperes.
2. **Draw the no-load vector OV₀** at angle $\phi_0$ (no-load power factor angle) with the horizontal axis. Length = $I_0/K$ cm.
3. **Draw the blocked-rotor vector OI_br** at angle $\phi_{br}$ (blocked-rotor power factor angle). Length = $I_{br}/K$ cm.
4. **Mark point K** by drawing a horizontal line from the tip of OI_br intersecting the vertical from V₀. K separates the no-load current $I_0$ into the **core-loss component $I_w$** (horizontal) and the **magnetizing component $I_\mu$** (vertical).
5. **Draw a semicircle** with OV₀ and OI_br as the diameter (passing through K).
6. **Draw the performance / output line** through K tangent to the circle (parallel to the torque line). Mark the **slip scale** by dividing the line from the point corresponding to $s = 1$ (on the bottom of the circle) to $s = 0$ (at V₀ end).
7. **Mark point A** on the circle at the rated full-load current $I_{fl}$. The perpendicular from A to the output line gives the **output power**. Similarly, the perpendicular to the torque line gives the **developed torque**.

**Quantities predicted from the circle diagram:**

1. Slip at any load point
2. Output power (shaft)
3. Torque (shaft torque)
4. Developed torque
5. Power factor at any load
6. Input current and input power
7. Efficiency at any load
8. Maximum power output and slip at which it occurs
9. Starting torque (at the point where $s = 1$)

(7 marks: 3 for construction steps with sketch, 2 for quantities listed, 2 for KTU-level neatness and labeling)

**Part (b) — Loss Separation (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Calculate $\cos \phi_0$ and $V_L^2$ for each point.

| $V_L$ (V) | $V_L^2$ (V²) | $\cos \phi_0 = \frac{P_0}{\sqrt{3} V_L I_0}$ | $I_0^2$ (A²) | $P_{cu1,0} = 3 I_0^2 R_1$ (assume $R_1$ given as $0.5\ \Omega$ from previous problem context, else estimate) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 400 | 160000 | 0.1155 | 36.00 | $54.0$ W |
| 360 | 129600 | 0.1310 | 25.00 | $37.5$ W |
| 320 | 102400 | 0.1500 | 17.64 | $26.5$ W |
| 280 | 78400 | 0.1737 | 12.25 | $18.4$ W |
| 240 | 57600 | 0.2147 | 7.84 | $11.8$ W |

**Step 2:** Compute the iron + mechanical loss $P_{i+fw} = P_0 - P_{cu1,0}$.

| $V_L$ (V) | $P_{i+fw}$ (W) |
|---|---|
| 400 | $426.0$ |
| 360 | $372.5$ |
| 320 | $323.5$ |
| 280 | $276.6$ |
| 240 | $238.2$ |

**Step 3:** Since the iron loss is proportional to $V^2$ (or $f$ times $B^2$), the **iron loss $P_i$ is linear in $V^2$**, while the **friction and windage loss $P_{fw}$ is constant** (independent of V).

Therefore: $P_{i+fw} = a V^2 + P_{fw}$

This is a linear equation in $V^2$. Fitting a straight line by any two extreme points:

Using $(V^2 = 57600, P_{i+fw} = 238.2)$ and $(V^2 = 160000, P_{i+fw} = 426.0)$:

$$\text{slope} = a = \frac{426.0 - 238.2}{160000 - 57600} = \frac{187.8}{102400} = 1.834 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{W/V}^2$$

$$\text{Intercept } P_{fw} = 238.2 - 1.834 \times 10^{-3} \times 57600 = 238.2 - 105.7 = 132.5\ \text{W}$$

**Verification with another point** (using $V^2 = 129600$): $P_{fw} = 372.5 - 1.834 \times 10^{-3} \times 129600 = 372.5 - 237.7 = 134.8\ \text{W}$ ✓ (close to 132.5)

**Step 4:** Iron loss at rated voltage ($V_L = 400$ V):

$$P_i = a \times V^2 = 1.834 \times 10^{-3} \times 160000 = 293.4\ \text{W}$$

$$P_{fw} \approx 133\ \text{W}$$

**Final Answer for Question B (b):**

$$P_{fw} = 133\ \text{W}, \quad P_i \text{ at 400 V} = 293.4\ \text{W}$$

(7 marks: 2 for setting up the linear equation, 2 for slope + intercept calculation, 2 for final loss values, 1 for correct units and reasoning)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $R_{dc}$ to $R_1$:** The DC resistance measured between two terminals is $2R_1$ for a star-connected motor. Students who write $R_1 = 1.2\ \Omega$ directly (without dividing by 2) lose **1 full mark** in Part A.
> 2. **Mixing up $\phi_0$ and $\phi_{br}$:** The no-load power factor is **low** (0.1–0.2) due to magnetizing current dominance, while blocked-rotor power factor is **medium** (0.3–0.5). Reversing these values is a fatal error worth **2 marks**.
> 3. **Ignoring the factor of 3:** No-load power $P_0$ is total three-phase. When computing $R_c$ per phase, divide by 3. Forgetting this loses **1 mark**.
> 4. **For circle diagram:** Many students fail to **draw K clearly** and forget the **output line** parallel to the torque line. Examiners specifically check this — up to **2 marks** deduction.
> 5. **Loss separation pitfall:** Do NOT directly equate the lowest $P_0$ value to $P_{fw}$. The correct method is **linear extrapolation** of $P_0$ vs $V^2$ plot to $V = 0$. This is a **frequently tested 2-mark concept**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — No-Load and Blocked-Rotor Tests on 3-Phase Induction Motor**

- **Two tests, one goal:** Both tests together yield the **complete equivalent circuit parameters** of the induction motor.
- **No-load test conditions:** Rated voltage and frequency, **no mechanical load**, **slip ≈ 0**.
- **No-load test yields:** Magnetizing branch parameters ($X_m$, $R_c$) and constant losses ($P_i + P_{fw}$).
- **Blocked-rotor test conditions:** **Rotor locked**, **reduced voltage**, current adjusted to **rated full-load value**.
- **Blocked-rotor test yields:** Leakage parameters $R_2'$, $X_1 + X_2'$ (with standard assumption $X_1 = X_2'$).
- **Star connection phase values:** $V_{ph} = V_L/\sqrt{3}$ and $I_{ph} = I_L$.
- **Delta connection phase values:** $V_{ph} = V_L$ and $I_{ph} = I_L/\sqrt{3}$.
- **DC resistance conversion:** $R_1 = R_{dc}/2$ for star, $R_1 = 1.5 R_{dc}$ for delta.
- **Power factor formulas:** $\cos \phi = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3} V_L I_L}$ — used in **both** tests.
- **$Z_{br}$ decomposition:** $R_{br} = Z_{br} \cos \phi_{br}$, $X_{br} = Z_{br} \sin \phi_{br}$.
- **Magnetizing branch neglected** during blocked-rotor (justify with $s = 1$, low rotor impedance).
- **Slip formulas:** $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$; $s_{mT} = R_2'/\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$.
- **Maximum torque** is **independent of rotor resistance** — it depends only on $V$, $R_1$, and total leakage reactance.
- **Slip at max torque** is **directly proportional to** $R_2'$.
- **Circle diagram** is the **graphical equivalent** of the analytical equivalent circuit; can predict performance at any slip.
- **Loss separation** is done by extrapolating the linear portion of $P_0$ vs $V^2$ curve to $V = 0$.
- **Standard KTU assumption:** $X_1 = X_2'$ unless otherwise explicitly stated.
- **Synchronous speed formula:** $N_s = 120 f / P$ — memorize this for the 3-mark rapid-fire questions.
- **Practical tip:** For a cage rotor, $R_2'$ **cannot** be measured by DC test — only by blocked-rotor test.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **3-phase pole-changing induction motor** is a squirrel-cage induction machine whose stator is wound with special connections (Dahlander / dual-winding) that permit operation at **two discrete synchronous speeds** by altering the effective number of stator poles (P). For KTU lab evaluation, the machine is experimentally characterised at each pole setting through the **No-Load Test (NLT)** and the **Blocked-Rotor Test (BRT)** — the two classical indirect tests used to construct the per-phase equivalent circuit and hence predict the complete steady-state performance of the induction motor without loading it on a dynamometer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition — Pole-Changing Motor**: An induction motor in which the stator winding is reconnected (through a switch / contactor logic) to alter the number of magnetic poles P, giving synchronous speeds n_s = 120f / P at the same supply frequency f = **50 Hz**.

### 1.1.1 The Two Canonical Tests

| Test | Rotor Condition | Applied Voltage | Purpose |
|------|-----------------|------------------|---------|
| **No-Load Test** | Free to rotate at near-synchronous speed | Rated line voltage V_L | Determines magnetising branch parameters R_c, X_m and rotational losses (core + friction + windage) |
| **Blocked-Rotor Test** | Mechanically locked (s = 1) | Reduced voltage giving rated current | Determines equivalent rotor resistance R_2′ and total leakage reactance X_01 = X_1 + X_2′ |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are evaluating a **ceiling fan** before installing it in a hall:

- **No-load test** ≈ Spinning the fan freely with full electricity and watching how much power it draws just to keep the blades cutting through air (air drag) and to magnetise its iron core. You measure that "idling" power — this tells you about the *internal magnetic appetite* of the motor.

- **Blocked-rotor test** ≈ Pinching the blade with your hand so it cannot move, then feeding a small voltage until the motor draws its full rated current. The current "sees" only the copper winding resistance and the leakage flux paths — exactly the way a stalled motor behaves at start-up. This gives you the *internal copper friction* of the motor.

- **Pole-changing** is like giving the fan **two gear ratios**: a slow mode (more magnetic poles → weaker magnetic field per revolution but stronger torque) and a fast mode (fewer poles → faster rotation). For each gear, the motor's "internal appetite" is different, so both tests must be **repeated for every pole setting**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two tests are needed together?** A single test cannot reveal all five equivalent-circuit parameters (R_1, X_1, R_2′, X_2′, R_c, X_m). The no-load test exposes the *shunt* (magnetising) branch, while the blocked-rotor test exposes the *series* (leakage) branch. Together they fully characterise the machine — this is a **classic KTU viva question**.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit of a 3-Phase Induction Motor

The standard **approximate equivalent circuit referred to the stator** is the analytical backbone of this experiment:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stator} &: \quad R_1 \;+\; jX_1 \\[2pt]
\text{Magnetising branch (shunt)} &: \quad R_c \;\parallel\; jX_m \\[2pt]
\text{Rotor (referred)} &: \quad \dfrac{R_2'}{s} \;+\; jX_2'
\end{aligned}
$$

This yields **six unknowns**: R_1, X_1, R_2′, X_2′, R_c, X_m. The two tests together resolve all six.

## 2.2 Why the Tests Work — Physical Reasoning

### 2.2.1 No-Load Test (s ≈ 0)
At no load, mechanical torque demand is almost zero, so slip **s ≈ 0.001 to 0.005**. The rotor branch term R_2′/s becomes very large (≈ R_2′ × 200 to 1000). Almost no rotor current flows. Therefore:

- The stator draws only the **magnetising current I_0**.
- I_0 splits into two orthogonal components:
  - **I_w = I_0 cos φ_0** → in phase with V → dissipates **core (iron) loss** in R_c.
  - **I_μ = I_0 sin φ_0** → quadrature to V → establishes the **magnetising flux** in X_m.
- The total no-load input power P_0 = **core loss + friction & windage loss** (rotor copper loss is negligible because I_2′ is tiny).

### 2.2.2 Blocked-Rotor Test (s = 1)
Rotor is mechanically locked. Slip **s = 1**, so R_2′/s = R_2′. The magnetising branch X_m is **much larger** than the leakage branch, so almost all current bypasses X_m (it flows through the short-circuited rotor). A low voltage (≈ 10–25 % of rated) is applied to circulate rated current.

- Input power P_sc = **stator copper loss + rotor copper loss** (core loss negligible because flux is very small at reduced voltage).
- Therefore P_sc gives the **total equivalent resistance R_01 = R_1 + R_2′**.

### 2.2.3 The Special Case of Pole-Changing Motors

When poles are changed from **2p (high speed)** to **4p (low speed)** by reconnecting the same winding in a Dahlander arrangement:

- **Synchronous flux** per pole is unchanged but the per-phase EMF constant changes.
- **Leakage reactances X_1, X_2′** are inversely proportional to the square of the number of poles (because magnetising inductance scales with pole area and frequency is constant):
  $$
  X \;\propto\; \dfrac{1}{P^{2}}
  $$
- **Rotor resistance** R_2′ is essentially unchanged (it is purely geometric and resistivity-based), but the effective copper losses per cycle differ because I_2 changes.
- Therefore the **X_01 / R_01 ratio** is different at each pole setting — both tests MUST be repeated at each configuration. This is the *central experimental challenge* of Module Experiment 2.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below assume a **star-connected stator** unless stated otherwise. For delta, replace V_L with V_ph = V_L.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Test Source |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | No-load power factor | $\cos\phi_{0} = \dfrac{P_{0}}{\sqrt{3}\,V_{L}I_{L0}}$ | dimensionless | NLT |
| 2 | Core-loss component | $I_{w} = I_{L0}\cos\phi_{0}$ | A | NLT |
| 3 | Magnetising component | $I_{\mu} = I_{L0}\sin\phi_{0}$ | A | NLT |
| 4 | Phase voltage (star) | $V_{ph} = \dfrac{V_{L}}{\sqrt{3}}$ | V | NLT |
| 5 | Core-loss resistance | $R_{c} = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{I_{w}}$ | Ω | NLT |
| 6 | Magnetising reactance | $X_{m} = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{I_{\mu}}$ | Ω | NLT |
| 7 | Total rotational loss | $P_{rot} = P_{0} - 3 I_{w}^{2} R_{c}$ | W | NLT |
| 8 | Blocked-rotor power factor | $\cos\phi_{sc} = \dfrac{P_{sc}}{\sqrt{3}\,V_{sc}I_{sc}}$ | dimensionless | BRT |
| 9 | Equivalent resistance | $R_{01} = \dfrac{P_{sc}}{3 I_{sc}^{2}}$ | Ω | BRT |
| 10 | Equivalent impedance (per phase) | $Z_{01} = \dfrac{V_{sc}/\sqrt{3}}{I_{sc}}$ | Ω | BRT |
| 11 | Equivalent leakage reactance | $X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^{2} - R_{01}^{2}}$ | Ω | BRT |
| 12 | Approximate split (assumed) | $X_{1} \approx X_{2}' \approx \dfrac{X_{01}}{2}$ | Ω | BRT |
| 13 | Rotor resistance referred | $R_{2}' = R_{01} - R_{1}$ | Ω | BRT + nameplate |
| 14 | Synchronous speed (high pole) | $n_{s1} = \dfrac{120 f}{P_{1}}$ | rpm | Theory |
| 15 | Synchronous speed (low pole) | $n_{s2} = \dfrac{120 f}{P_{2}}$ | rpm | Theory |
| 16 | Pole ratio (Dahlander) | $P_{2} = 2 P_{1}$ | — | Dahlander |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note**: Formula 12 (the 50-50 split of leakage reactance) is an *assumption* (since the two tests alone cannot separate X_1 and X_2′). KTU expects students to *explicitly state* this assumption during the viva. In real engineering practice, this split is done by design calculation or by LCR measurement on individual phases.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The no-load and blocked-rotor tests are **universal acceptance tests** in every induction-motor manufacturing plant (Siemens, ABB, Crompton Greaves, Kirloskar). They are used:

- During **factory acceptance testing (FAT)** of every new motor.
- For **energy-efficiency classification** (IE2 / IE3 / IE4 ratings) — the no-load current and losses directly determine efficiency.
- To validate **FEA-designed** equivalent-circuit parameters against physical prototypes.
- In **pole-changing motors** used in elevators, machine tools, conveyor drives (where two discrete speeds are required without the cost of a VFD).
- To compute the **complete circle diagram** and hence predict starting current, starting torque, pull-out torque, full-load slip, and full-load current without actually loading the motor.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — No-Load Test Analysis

### Step 1: Read the three instruments
At rated voltage V_L, record:
- Line voltage V_L (voltmeter)
- Line current I_L0 (ammeter)
- Total input power P_0 (wattmeter × 2 for two-wattmeter method)

### Step 2: Compute no-load power factor
For a balanced 3-phase machine, total power:

$$
P_{0} = \sqrt{3}\,V_{L}I_{L0}\cos\phi_{0}
$$

Solving for the no-load power factor:

$$
\cos\phi_{0} = \frac{P_{0}}{\sqrt{3}\,V_{L}I_{L0}}
$$

### Step 3: Resolve I_L0 into orthogonal components
Treat I_L0 as a phasor. Its component in phase with V_ph is the **core-loss current I_w**, and its quadrature component is the **magnetising current I_μ**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{w} &= I_{L0}\cos\phi_{0} \\[4pt]
I_{\mu} &= I_{L0}\sin\phi_{0} \;=\; I_{L0}\sqrt{1 - \cos^{2}\phi_{0}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the shunt-branch parameters
For a star-connected stator, phase voltage V_ph = V_L / √3. The magnetising branch (R_c ∥ jX_m) is fed by V_ph. So, by Ohm's law applied to each branch:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{c} &= \frac{V_{ph}}{I_{w}} \;=\; \frac{V_{L}}{\sqrt{3}\,I_{L0}\cos\phi_{0}} \\[4pt]
X_{m} &= \frac{V_{ph}}{I_{\mu}} \;=\; \frac{V_{L}}{\sqrt{3}\,I_{L0}\sin\phi_{0}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Compute the rotational (mechanical) losses
The total no-load input P_0 covers both the core loss and the friction-plus-windage loss. The core loss (per phase) dissipated in R_c is I_w²·R_c. For three phases:

$$
P_{core} = 3 I_{w}^{2} R_{c}
$$

Therefore the rotational loss (friction + windage) is:

$$
P_{rot} = P_{0} - 3 I_{w}^{2} R_{c}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This P_rot is *assumed constant* with slip and is carried forward to the circle diagram / efficiency calculation as a constant rotational loss.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Blocked-Rotor Test Analysis

### Step 1: Apply reduced voltage, read instruments
With the rotor mechanically locked (s = 1) and a reduced applied voltage V_sc adjusted to circulate **rated** stator current I_sc, record V_sc, I_sc and P_sc (two-wattmeter sum).

### Step 2: Compute blocked-rotor power factor

$$
\cos\phi_{sc} = \frac{P_{sc}}{\sqrt{3}\,V_{sc}I_{sc}}
$$

### Step 3: Compute per-phase equivalent impedance
For star connection:

$$
Z_{01} = \frac{V_{sc}/\sqrt{3}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{V_{sc}}{\sqrt{3}\,I_{sc}}
$$

### Step 4: Separate into resistance and reactance
The equivalent series impedance of the induction motor at s = 1 (with magnetising branch neglected) is R_01 + jX_01.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{01} &= Z_{01}\cos\phi_{sc} \;=\; \frac{P_{sc}}{3 I_{sc}^{2}} \\[4pt]
X_{01} &= Z_{01}\sin\phi_{sc} \;=\; \sqrt{Z_{01}^{2} - R_{01}^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Disaggregate R_01 and X_01
Two sub-steps are needed:

**Sub-step (a) — Resistance split:**
R_01 = R_1 + R_2′. The DC stator resistance R_1 can be measured separately (or taken from the nameplate / a DC drop test). Therefore:

$$
R_{2}' = R_{01} - R_{1}
$$

**Sub-step (b) — Reactance split (assumption-based):**
Because the two tests alone cannot separate X_1 and X_2′, the **standard KTU-grade assumption** is the equal-split:

$$
X_{1} \approx X_{2}' \approx \frac{X_{01}}{2}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (KTU)**: Many students forget to convert V_sc to V_ph (i.e., divide V_sc by √3 for star connection). This single error produces a Z_01 that is √3 times too large, and every downstream reactance is wrong. **Always state the connection assumption explicitly.**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Pole-Setting Comparison

The defining feature of this experiment is that **every calculation above must be repeated for each pole configuration**. For a Dahlander motor with pole count P_1 → P_2 = 2P_1, the analytical matrix is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Speed}_1 &: \; n_{s1} = \frac{120 \cdot 50}{P_{1}} \\[4pt]
\text{Speed}_2 &: \; n_{s2} = \frac{120 \cdot 50}{P_{2}} = \frac{n_{s1}}{2} \\[4pt]
X_{01}^{(2)} &\approx \frac{X_{01}^{(1)}}{4} \quad \text{(since } X \propto 1/P^{2}\text{)} \\[4pt]
R_{01}^{(2)} &\approx R_{01}^{(1)} \quad \text{(geometry-invariant)} \\[4pt]
\cos\phi_{sc}^{(2)} &> \cos\phi_{sc}^{(1)} \quad \text{(higher pole → larger X, smaller R/X)}
\end{aligned}
$$

A complete KTU lab record should tabulate:

| Quantity | High-Speed Pole (P_1) | Low-Speed Pole (P_2 = 2P_1) |
|----------|----------------------|-----------------------------|
| V_L (NLT) | rated | rated |
| I_L0 | — | — |
| P_0 | — | — |
| cos φ_0 | — | — |
| R_c | — | — |
| X_m | — | — |
| V_sc (BRT) | — | — |
| I_sc | — | — |
| P_sc | — | — |
| cos φ_sc | — | — |
| R_01 | — | — |
| X_01 | — | — |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Why is the blocked-rotor test performed at reduced voltage, not at rated voltage?**

**Model Answer:**
At rated voltage with the rotor locked, the motor would draw a current **5 to 7 times** the rated full-load current (because the magnetising branch is bypassed and only the small leakage impedance limits current). This would (a) cause severe I²R overheating and burn the winding, and (b) saturate the leakage flux paths, invalidating the linear assumption of equivalent-circuit analysis. Therefore the voltage is reduced to ~10–25 % of rated so that the *rated* current circulates. The linear equivalent circuit is then valid and the parameters are correctly determined. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Why must the no-load test and blocked-rotor test both be performed on a pole-changing induction motor at each pole configuration?**

**Model Answer:**
When the number of poles P is changed, the synchronous speed n_s = 120f/P changes, and the per-phase leakage reactances X_1 and X_2′ scale as 1/P² (because magnetising inductance depends on the pole area and flux distribution). Rotor resistance R_2′ also changes slightly due to different current paths. Therefore the equivalent-circuit parameters R_c, X_m, R_01, X_01 take **different numerical values** at each pole setting. The complete torque-speed characteristic at each speed can only be predicted if both tests are repeated at each pole setting. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question B — Option (A) [14 Marks] [KTU Model Question Paper Pattern]

**(a)** With a neat per-phase equivalent circuit of a 3-phase induction motor, derive the expressions for **R_c, X_m** from the no-load test data. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** From the blocked-rotor test data, derive the expressions for **R_01 and X_01**, and explain clearly how the individual parameters **R_1, R_2′, X_1, X_2′** are obtained. State any assumptions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

1. *(1 mark)* Draw the per-phase approximate equivalent circuit showing R_1, X_1, magnetising branch (R_c ∥ jX_m), and rotor branch R_2′/s + jX_2′.

2. *(2 marks)* At no-load, s ≈ 0, so R_2′/s → ∞ and the rotor branch is open. The magnetising branch is connected directly across V_ph.

3. *(1 mark)* From instrument readings (V_L, I_L0, P_0), compute the no-load power factor:
   $\cos\phi_{0} = \dfrac{P_{0}}{\sqrt{3}\,V_{L}I_{L0}}$

4. *(1 mark)* Resolve I_L0 into two orthogonal components:
   $I_{w} = I_{L0}\cos\phi_{0}$ (in-phase with V_ph) and $I_{\mu} = I_{L0}\sin\phi_{0}$ (quadrature).

5. *(2 marks)* Apply Ohm's law across the magnetising branch (V_ph = V_L/√3 for star):
   $R_{c} = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{I_{w}} = \dfrac{V_{L}}{\sqrt{3}\,I_{L0}\cos\phi_{0}}$ and $X_{m} = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{I_{\mu}} = \dfrac{V_{L}}{\sqrt{3}\,I_{L0}\sin\phi_{0}}$.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

1. *(1 mark)* At blocked-rotor (s = 1), the magnetising branch current is negligible because X_m >> X_01. The equivalent circuit reduces to a simple series R-L: R_01 + jX_01.

2. *(1 mark)* Compute blocked-rotor power factor:
   $\cos\phi_{sc} = \dfrac{P_{sc}}{\sqrt{3}\,V_{sc}I_{sc}}$

3. *(1 mark)* Per-phase impedance:
   $Z_{01} = \dfrac{V_{sc}}{\sqrt{3}\,I_{sc}}$

4. *(1 mark)* Decompose:
   $R_{01} = Z_{01}\cos\phi_{sc} = \dfrac{P_{sc}}{3 I_{sc}^{2}}$ and $X_{01} = Z_{01}\sin\phi_{sc} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^{2} - R_{01}^{2}}$

5. *(2 marks)* Disaggregation:
   - Measure R_1 separately (DC drop test) → R_2′ = R_01 − R_1.
   - **Assumption (state explicitly)**: X_1 ≈ X_2′ ≈ X_01 / 2.

6. *(1 mark)* Summarise the six parameters and comment that without the assumption, the two tests alone cannot separate X_1 and X_2′.

---

### Question B — Option (B) [14 Marks] [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the **construction and principle of a Dahlander pole-changing motor**. Show how a single stator winding can produce 2P poles or P poles through external reconnections. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 3-phase, 4/2-pole, 50 Hz Dahlander induction motor, the following data are obtained:

| Test | Pole Setting | V_L (V) | I_L (A) | P (W) |
|------|--------------|---------|---------|-------|
| No-load | 4-pole (low speed) | 415 | 6.2 | 580 |
| No-load | 2-pole (high speed) | 415 | 8.4 | 920 |
| Blocked-rotor | 4-pole | 95 | 18.0 | 1180 |
| Blocked-rotor | 2-pole | 110 | 18.0 | 1620 |

Assume star connection and DC stator resistance per phase R_1 = 0.55 Ω. Compute the equivalent-circuit parameters at **both** pole settings. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

1. *(2 marks)* A Dahlander motor uses a single stator winding whose coils are tapped at the midpoint and rearranged via a changeover switch. Two connections are possible:
   - **Δ (Delta) connection of coil halves in series** → produces **2P** poles (low speed).
   - **YY (Double Star) connection of coil halves in parallel** → produces **P** poles (high speed, double speed).
   Synchronous speeds are n_s = 60f/P (Hz convention) or 120f/P for full-pole pairs.

2. *(2 marks)* The two speeds are in a **1 : 2 ratio** (e.g., 1500/750 rpm at 50 Hz for 4/2-pole, or 3000/1500 rpm for 2/1-pole). The "consequent pole" principle allows pole count doubling by reversing current direction in alternate coil groups.

3. *(1 mark)* Typical applications: machine-tool spindle drives, fans, pumps where two discrete speeds suffice and a VFD is not needed.

4. *(2 marks)* Limitations and KTU viva facts: torque at the two speeds differs (constant-power or constant-torque characteristic depending on connection); harmonic content in YY connection can be higher; cannot provide a continuous speed range.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Convert line to phase voltage.** Star → V_ph = V_L / √3 = 415/√3 = **239.6 V**.

**Step 2: No-load test — 4-pole (low speed).**
$$
\cos\phi_{0} = \frac{P_{0}}{\sqrt{3}\,V_{L}I_{L0}} = \frac{580}{\sqrt{3}\cdot 415 \cdot 6.2} = \frac{580}{4456} = 0.1302
$$

$$
\sin\phi_{0} = \sqrt{1 - 0.1302^{2}} = 0.9915
$$

$$
I_{w} = 6.2 \times 0.1302 = 0.807 \text{ A} \quad ; \quad I_{\mu} = 6.2 \times 0.9915 = 6.147 \text{ A}
$$

$$
R_{c} = \frac{239.6}{0.807} = 296.9\;\Omega \quad ; \quad X_{m} = \frac{239.6}{6.147} = 39.0\;\Omega
$$

**Step 3: No-load test — 2-pole (high speed).**
$$
\cos\phi_{0}' = \frac{920}{\sqrt{3}\cdot 415 \cdot 8.4} = \frac{920}{6037} = 0.1524
$$

$$
\sin\phi_{0}' = 0.9883
$$

$$
I_{w}' = 8.4 \times 0.1524 = 1.280 \text{ A} \quad ; \quad I_{\mu}' = 8.4 \times 0.9883 = 8.302 \text{ A}
$$

$$
R_{c}' = \frac{239.6}{1.280} = 187.2\;\Omega \quad ; \quad X_{m}' = \frac{239.6}{8.302} = 28.86\;\Omega
$$

**Step 4: Blocked-rotor test — 4-pole.**
$$
Z_{01} = \frac{95}{\sqrt{3}\cdot 18} = 3.046\;\Omega
$$

$$
\cos\phi_{sc} = \frac{1180}{\sqrt{3}\cdot 95 \cdot 18} = \frac{1180}{2962} = 0.3984
$$

$$
R_{01} = \frac{1180}{3\cdot 18^{2}} = \frac{1180}{972} = 1.214\;\Omega
$$

$$
X_{01} = \sqrt{3.046^{2} - 1.214^{2}} = \sqrt{9.279 - 1.474} = \sqrt{7.805} = 2.794\;\Omega
$$

**Step 5: Blocked-rotor test — 2-pole.**
$$
Z_{01}' = \frac{110}{\sqrt{3}\cdot 18} = 3.527\;\Omega
$$

$$
\cos\phi_{sc}' = \frac{1620}{\sqrt{3}\cdot 110 \cdot 18} = \frac{1620}{3429} = 0.4725
$$

$$
R_{01}' = \frac{1620}{3\cdot 18^{2}} = \frac{1620}{972} = 1.667\;\Omega
$$

$$
X_{01}' = \sqrt{3.527^{2} - 1.667^{2}} = \sqrt{12.44 - 2.779} = \sqrt{9.66} = 3.108\;\Omega
$$

**Step 6: Disaggregate (with R_1 = 0.55 Ω assumed equal at both poles):**

| Parameter | 4-pole (low speed) | 2-pole (high speed) |
|-----------|--------------------|---------------------|
| R_1 | 0.55 Ω | 0.55 Ω |
| R_2′ | 1.214 − 0.55 = **0.664 Ω** | 1.667 − 0.55 = **1.117 Ω** |
| X_01 | **2.794 Ω** | **3.108 Ω** |
| X_1 = X_2′ | 1.397 Ω | 1.554 Ω |
| R_c | 296.9 Ω | 187.2 Ω |
| X_m | 39.0 Ω | 28.86 Ω |

*(1 mark for unit consistency and final tabulation)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Most common mark-losing mistakes in this experiment:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert V_L → V_ph** (V_L/√3 for star) when computing R_c, X_m, Z_01. This single mistake loses 2-3 marks across the solution.
> 2. **Not stating the assumption** X_1 = X_2′ = X_01/2. The KTU answer key allocates 1 full mark just for stating it.
> 3. **Mixing up I_w and I_μ** — I_w (in-phase with V) gives R_c; I_μ (quadrature to V) gives X_m. Reversing them gives a 1/X type error and wrong physical interpretation.
> 4. **Not repeating the tests for both pole settings** — this is the *defining requirement* of a pole-changing motor experiment. A report that gives parameters for only one speed is incomplete and attracts a 3-mark deduction.
> 5. **Forgetting the two-wattmeter power formula P = W_1 + W_2** (not just one wattmeter reading). The no-load and blocked-rotor power factors are typically low (< 0.5), so one wattmeter may read negative — students often forget to subtract instead of add.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **no-load test** runs the motor at rated voltage with no mechanical load — its purpose is to find **R_c, X_m** and rotational losses.
- ✅ The **blocked-rotor test** locks the rotor and applies reduced voltage to circulate rated current — its purpose is to find **R_01** and **X_01**.
- ✅ The **six equivalent-circuit parameters** (R_1, X_1, R_2′, X_2′, R_c, X_m) are fully determined only when both tests are combined, *plus* a DC drop test for R_1 *plus* the equal-split assumption for X_1/X_2′.
- ✅ In a **pole-changing motor**, leakage reactance **X_01 ∝ 1/P²** — higher pole count gives lower leakage reactance.
- ✅ **Dahlander connection** gives speeds in ratio 1:2 (typically Δ/YY switching). **Dual-winding** gives any arbitrary speed ratio.
- ✅ Always convert line to phase quantities for star: **V_ph = V_L / √3**.
- ✅ **Power formula**: $P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ for balanced 3-phase.
- ✅ **Two-wattmeter method**: $P_{total} = W_1 + W_2$ (with sign convention).
- ✅ **Per-phase calculations** give per-phase parameters — multiply copper losses by 3 to get total.
- ✅ The complete test (NLT + BRT) at both pole settings is the *minimum KTU requirement* for this experiment to be considered complete.
- ✅ Circle diagram construction is an *optional extension* — KTU often awards bonus marks if it is drawn correctly and used to predict full-load slip, torque, and efficiency.
- ✅ The **viva-question goldmine** for this experiment: "Can you separate X_1 and X_2′ from these two tests alone?" — answer: **No**, additional assumption or measurement is required.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load test on 3-phase pole-changing induction motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEEL507 - AC MACHINES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Load test on 3-phase pole-changing induction motor # ⚙️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Pole-Changing Induction Motor (Multi-Speed Induction Motor):** > A **squirrel-cage three-phase induction motor** whose stator winding is so arranged that the number of magnetic poles can be changed to two (or more) discrete values by simple external switching, thereby producing two (or more) discrete synchronous speeds. The rotor, being a **cage rotor**, automatically adapts to the changing stator field without any rotor rewiring. For a standard 3-phase, 50 Hz supply, the synchronous speed is governed by the universal relationship: $$N_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{P}$$ Therefore, by switching pole count $P$, the synchronous — and hence running — speed of the motor can be stepped. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Pole-changing motors are widely used in **lifts, hoists, fans, pumps, washing machines, and machine tool drives** where discrete (not stepless) speed variation is acceptable and economically preferable to variable-frequency drives. ## 1.2 The Two Major Techniques of Pole Changing | Technique | Description | Pole Ratio | Winding Used | |---|---|---|---| | **Dahlander Connection** (Consequent Pole) | A single stator winding is reconnected to give **half** the original poles | **1 : 2** (e.g., 4/2, 6/4, 8/4) | One tapped winding | | **Two Independent Stator Windings** | Two completely separate windings are wound in the same slots | Any ratio (e.g., 4/6, 6/8) | Two full windings | ## 1.3 The Dahlander Pole-Changer — Configurations & Characteristics By reconnecting the same Dahlander winding, three practical torque/power characteristics can be obtained: | Connection | Speed (Low : High) | Characteristic | Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Δ – YY** | 1 : 2 | **Constant Torque** | Conveyors, compressors, mixers | | **Y – YY** | 1 : 2 | **Variable (Fan) Torque** | Fans, blowers, centrifugal pumps | | **YY – Δ** | 1 : 2 | **Constant Power** | Machine tool spindles, winders | > [!TIP] > **Memory Trick for KTU Viva:** > - **Y–YY** → Less copper in low speed → Low torque → **Fan / Variable torque** > - **Δ–YY** → Same copper, all coils carry current in both modes → **Constant torque** > - **YY–Δ** → Double copper active in low speed → **Constant power** ## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Gearbox of AC Motors" Imagine a **bicycle with only two gears**: - **Low gear (high poles, low speed):** Easy to start uphill, lots of pulling force, but slow. - **High gear (low poles, high speed):** Fast on the flat, but cannot climb easily. The pole-changing motor behaves **exactly the same way**: - **More poles → Lower speed → Higher starting torque** - **Fewer poles → Higher speed → Lower torque** A squirrel-cage rotor is like a freewheeling back wheel that **automatically** rotates at whatever synchronous speed the stator field dictates. The lab test simply measures how well the motor delivers mechanical power at **each of these two speeds** when a mechanical brake is applied. ## 1.5 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics Used - **Supply frequency:** $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$ (Indian standard) - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$ - **Brake drum effective radius:** $R$ in metres (always measured, never assumed) - **Motor rating:** typically **1.5 kW – 5 kW** in the KTU lab - **Rated line voltage:** $415\,\text{V}$, 3-phase, 50 Hz # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Pole Changing Works A 3-phase winding produces a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** whose speed depends only on the number of poles actually created in the air gap. By altering the **direction of current in selected coil-groups** (the *consequent-pole* technique), the total pole count is effectively halved. **Dahlander's trick — the "Consequent Pole" principle:** > [!IMPORTANT] > If current in every alternate pole-pair is **reversed** in direction, those poles no longer alternate N–S–N–S but become N–N–S–S (or S–S–N–N). Adjacent same-polarity poles **merge** to form a single, wider pole. Result: **Poles are halved, speed is doubled.** For a winding originally wound for $2p$ poles (say 4 poles): - Reconnect so alternate pole-pair currents reverse → effective poles become $p$ (i.e., 2). - Synchronous speed jumps from $1500$ RPM to $3000$ RPM. ## 2.2 Speed-Pole Table (for 50 Hz, mandatory for KTU problems) | Number of Poles (P) | Synchronous Speed $N_s$ (RPM) | Typical Full-Load Speed (RPM) | |---|---|---| | 2 | **3000** | 2900 | | 4 | **1500** | 1440 | | 6 | **1000** | 960 | | 8 | **750** | 720 | | 10 | 600 | 575 | | 12 | 500 | 480 | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (HIGH-YIELD — memorize this table) > [!WARNING] > In KTU answer scripts, **wrong units** cause silent mark deductions. Always write units in **every line** of the calculation. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120\,f}{P}$ | RPM | $f$ in Hz, $P$ in poles | | 2 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | per-unit or % | $N_r$ = rotor speed | | 3 | Slip RPM | $N_{slip} = N_s - N_r$ | RPM | | | 4 | Slip frequency | $f_r = s \cdot f$ | Hz | | | 5 | Brake torque | $T_{brake} = (S_1 - S_2) \cdot R \cdot g$ | N·m | $S_1$, $S_2$ in kg, $R$ in m | | 6 | Shaft (output) power | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi N_r T_{brake}}{60}$ | Watts | | | 7 | Input power | $P_{in} = W_1 + W_2$ | Watts | 2-wattmeter method | | 8 | Power factor | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L\,I_L}$ | — | 3-ϕ balanced | | 9 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | % | | | 10 | Output HP | $HP = \dfrac{P_{out}}{746}$ | HP | | | 11 | Speed ratio (Dahlander) | $\dfrac{N_{s,\text{low}}}{N_{s,\text{high}}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{high}}}{P_{\text{low}}} = \dfrac{1}{2}$ | — | Pole ratio 2:1 | | 12 | Torque constant-torque mode | $T_{\text{low}} \approx T_{\text{high}}$ | N·m | For Δ-YY | | 13 | Power constant-power mode | $P_{\text{low}} \approx P_{\text{high}}$ | W | For YY-Δ | | 14 | Torque ratio variable-torque mode | $T_{\text{low}} \approx 0.5\,T_{\text{high}}$ | N·m | For Y-YY (fan law) | > [!NOTE] > In **LaTeX inside markdown tables**, never use the bare vertical bar `\vert` directly between two table columns — use `$\vert$` to avoid breaking the table. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Pole-Changing Motors - **Energy saving:** Fan and pump drives using pole-changing can save 30–50 % energy compared to throttling/damper control. - **Robustness:** No electronics, no VFD → ideal for **explosive, dusty, hot environments** (mines, cement plants). - **Simplicity:** A **Dahlander switch** is far cheaper than a VFD for 2-speed applications. - **Reliability:** Fewer components → minimal maintenance → preferred in remote installations. ## 2.5 Load Test — What It Reveals The load test on the pole-changing motor is essentially **two load tests performed in succession** — one at each pole configuration. From the readings we draw: - **Speed vs Output Power** curve (must drop with load) - **Slip vs Output Power** (linear-ish) - **Torque vs Output Power** (linear, since $T = P/\omega$) - **Efficiency vs Output Power** (peaks near rated load) - **Power factor vs Output Power** (improves with load) - **Current vs Output Power** > [!IMPORTANT] > A pole-changing motor running on the **higher pole count (lower speed)** typically shows **higher starting torque and better low-end efficiency**; on the **lower pole count (higher speed)**, it delivers **higher power** but lower starting torque. # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Brake-Drum Torque Equation Consider a brake drum of effective radius $R$ (metres) pressed against the motor pulley by two spring balances. Let: - $S_1$ = reading of the **tight side** spring balance (kgf) - $S_2$ = reading of the **slack side** spring balance (kgf) - The net tangential force on the drum = $(S_1 - S_2)$ kgf **Step 1 — Convert the net force to Newtons** (1 kgf = $g$ Newtons, where $g = 9.81$ m/s²): $$F_{net} = (S_1 - S_2) \times 9.81 \quad \text{Newtons}$$ **Step 2 — Torque is force × perpendicular distance** (force is tangential, so distance = radius $R$): $$T_{brake} = F_{net} \times R = (S_1 - S_2) \times 9.81 \times R \quad \text{N·m}$$ **This is the standard KTU formula** to be used in all calculations. ## 3.2 Derivation of Output (Shaft) Power The motor shaft rotates at $N_r$ RPM, i.e., $\dfrac{N_r}{60}$ revolutions per second. One revolution sweeps an angle of $2\pi$ radians. So angular velocity: $$\omega = 2\pi \cdot \frac{N_r}{60} = \frac{\pi N_r}{30} \quad \text{rad/s}$$ Mechanical power is the **rate of doing work** = Torque × Angular velocity: $$P_{out} = T_{brake} \times \omega = T_{brake} \times \frac{2\pi N_r}{60} \quad \text{Watts}$$ Substituting torque from §3.1: $$P_{out} = (S_1 - S_2) \times 9.81 \times R \times \frac{2\pi N_r}{60}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation of Efficiency & Power Factor **Input power** from the 2-wattmeter method (used in 3-ϕ labs): $$P_{in} = W_1 + W_2 \quad \text{Watts}$$ **Power factor** for a balanced 3-ϕ load: $$P_{in} = \sqrt{3}\,V_L\,I_L\,\cos\phi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos\phi = \frac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L\,I_L}$$ **Efficiency:** $$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\,\%$$ ## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical — Full KTU-Style Solution > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the model problem you must be able to solve under exam conditions.** **Problem Statement (KTU University Exam - June 2023 style):** A 3-phase, 4/2-pole, Δ-YY connected squirrel-cage induction motor is subjected to a load test at both pole settings. At the **4-pole (low-speed)** setting, the following readings were taken at one particular load: - Line voltage $V_L = 415$ V, Line current $I_L = 4.8$ A - Wattmeter readings: $W_1 = 1480$ W, $W_2 = 460$ W - Tachometer reading: $N_r = 1430$ RPM - Spring-balance readings: $S_1 = 6.4$ kg, $S_2 = 0.8$ kg - Effective brake-drum radius: $R = 7.5$ cm Calculate: **(a)** Synchronous speed, slip %, and slip RPM, **(b)** Brake torque, output power, output HP, **(c)** Power factor, **(d)** Efficiency. --- ### (a) Synchronous Speed, Slip % and Slip RPM **Step 1:** For 4 poles and 50 Hz: $$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ RPM}$$ **Step 2:** Slip RPM: $$N_{slip} = N_s - N_r = 1500 - 1430 = 70 \text{ RPM}$$ **Step 3:** Slip in per-unit: $$s = \frac{N_{slip}}{N_s} = \frac{70}{1500} = 0.04667$$ **Step 4:** Slip in %: $$s\,\% = 4.667\,\%$$ > **Result:** $\;N_s = 1500$ RPM, $\;N_{slip} = 70$ RPM, $\;s = 4.67\,\%$ --- ### (b) Brake Torque, Output Power and Output HP **Step 1 — Net spring balance reading:** $$\Delta S = S_1 - S_2 = 6.4 - 0.8 = 5.6 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 2 — Convert to Newtons:** $$F_{net} = 5.6 \times 9.81 = 54.936 \text{ N}$$ **Step 3 — Brake torque** (convert $R$ to metres: $7.5$ cm $= 0.075$ m): $$T_{brake} = 54.936 \times 0.075 = 4.1202 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 4 — Output power in Watts:** $$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N_r T_{brake}}{60} = \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 1430 \times 4.1202}{60}$$ $$P_{out} = \frac{37015.4}{60} = 616.92 \text{ W}$$ **Step 5 — Output HP:** $$HP = \frac{616.92}{746} = 0.827 \text{ HP}$$ > **Result:** $\;T_{brake} = 4.12$ N·m, $\;P_{out} = 616.92$ W, $\;P_{out} = 0.827$ HP --- ### (c) Power Factor **Step 1 — Total input power** (2-wattmeter method): $$P_{in} = W_1 + W_2 = 1480 + 460 = 1940 \text{ W}$$ **Step 2 — Apply 3-ϕ formula:** $$\cos\phi = \frac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L\,I_L} = \frac{1940}{1.732 \times 415 \times 4.8}$$ $$\cos\phi = \frac{1940}{3450.0} = 0.5623 \text{ (lagging)}$$ > **Result:** $\;\cos\phi = 0.562$ lagging --- ### (d) Efficiency $$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{616.92}{1940} \times 100 = 31.80\,\%$$ > **Result:** $\;\eta = 31.80\,\%$ > [!NOTE] > A low efficiency at this light load (~33 %) is **expected** for a small induction motor at part-load. The efficiency will peak near the rated load (about 1.5 kW in this example). ## 3.5 Graphical Analysis — What the Examiner Expects You must draw **six curves** with Output Power ($P_{out}$) on the X-axis: 1. **Speed vs $P_{out}$** — drops slightly with load (drooping characteristic). 2. **Slip vs $P_{out}$** — increases almost linearly. 3. **Torque vs $P_{out}$** — linear (slope = $1/\omega$). 4. **Current vs $P_{out}$** — rises with load. 5. **Efficiency vs $P_{out}$** — rises to a peak, then falls. 6. **Power factor vs $P_{out}$** — improves sharply with load. > **Repeat all six curves for BOTH pole settings** (high-speed and low-speed) on the same graph, with **different-coloured/legend-marked lines** so the KTU examiner can see the contrast. # 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram of the Pole-Changing Motor Concept ```mermaid flowchart LR A[3-Phase 50 Hz
AC Mains Supply] --> B[Dahlander
Pole-Changing Switch] B --> C[Stator Winding
Pole Reconfigurable] C --> D[Rotating Magnetic Field
Variable Pole Count P] D --> E[Squirrel Cage Rotor
Induced Currents] E --> F[Mechanical Shaft
Output Rotation] F --> G[Brake Drum /
Prony Brake Load] G --> H[Spring Balances
S1 and S2] F --> I[Tachometer
Speed Measurement] style A fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333 style B fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333 style C fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333 style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333 style F fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333 style G fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333 style H fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#333 style I fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.2 Complete Load Test Setup — Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SUPPLY[3-Phase 415 V, 50 Hz AC Mains] S1[L1 Phase] S2[L2 Phase] S3[L3 Phase] end subgraph METERING[Metering Panel] V[Voltmeter V_L] AM[Ammeter I_L] W1[Wattmeter W1] W2[Wattmeter W2] end subgraph CONTROL[Dahlander Switch and Contactor Bank] SW[Pole Selector
LOW or HIGH Speed] end subgraph MACHINE[Test Motor] M[Pole-Changing
Induction Motor
Delta Star-Star Winding] end subgraph LOAD[Mechanical Loading Assembly] BD[Brake Drum
Pulley R radius] SB1[Spring Balance S1 Tight] SB2[Spring Balance S2 Slack] end subgraph INSTR[Speed and Torque Measurement] TACH[Digital Tachometer
N_r RPM] CALC[Derived Quantities
T P_out PF eta] end S1 --> V S2 --> V S3 --> V S1 --> AM S2 --> W1 S3 --> W2 S1 --> SW S2 --> SW S3 --> SW SW --> M M --> BD BD --> SB1 BD --> SB2 M -. Shaft .-> TACH SB1 --> CALC SB2 --> CALC TACH --> CALC W1 --> CALC W2 --> CALC V --> CALC AM --> CALC style SUPPLY fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333 style METERING fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333 style CONTROL fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333 style MACHINE fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333 style LOAD fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333 style INSTR fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.3 Dahlander Winding Reconnection Logic ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Original 4-Pole Winding
P=4, Ns=1500 RPM] -->|Reverse Alternate
Pole Pair Current| B[Consequent Pole
P=2, Ns=3000 RPM] A -->|Delta Connection| A1[High Speed Star-Star
Constant Torque Mode] A -->|Star Connection| A2[Low Speed Star
Variable Torque Mode] B -->|YY Connection| B1[High Speed Star-Star
High Power Output] style A fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 style B fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333 style A1 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333 style A2 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333 style B1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.4 Tabular Equivalent of the Schematic (for quick recall) | Element | Function in Lab | |---|---| | **3-ϕ Autotransformer / MCB** | Adjusts/isolates the supply | | **Dahlander switch** | Selects LOW-speed (high P) or HIGH-speed (low P) winding | | **Voltmeter (V)** | Measures $V_L$ | | **Ammeter (A)** | Measures $I_L$ | | **2-Wattmeter panel** | Reads $W_1$, $W_2$ → gives $P_{in}$ | | **Prony brake / brake drum** | Applies measurable mechanical load | | **Two spring balances** | $S_1$ (tight) and $S_2$ (slack) | | **Tachometer (digital/optical)** | Reads $N_r$ in RPM | | **Connecting cables** | Star / Delta links for the winding tap | > [!TIP] > **KTU Viva Question:** *"Why is the rotor a cage and not a slip-ring type in a pole-changing motor?"* > **Answer:** A slip-ring rotor would require a **different rotor pole count** for each stator configuration — practically impossible. A cage rotor **induces whatever number of poles match the stator field** and so works at every speed. # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) > **A1.** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** What is a pole-changing induction motor? How is pole changing achieved in a 3-phase induction motor? **Model Answer:** A pole-changing induction motor (also called a **multi-speed induction motor**) is a 3-phase induction motor in which the **number of stator poles can be changed** to obtain two or more discrete running speeds, while the supply frequency is kept constant. Pole changing is achieved by **two methods**: 1. **Dahlander connection (consequent-pole technique):** A single stator winding is reconnected so that the current in alternate pole-pairs is reversed. This effectively **halves the number of poles** (pole ratio **1 : 2**). 2. **Two independent stator windings:** Two separate windings with different pole counts are placed in the same slots and selectively energised. The rotor is always a **squirrel-cage type**, which automatically conforms to the changing stator field. The speed changes are stepped (e.g., 1500/750 RPM or 3000/1500 RPM), not continuous. > **A2.** **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** State any **three** advantages of a pole-changing induction motor over a conventional single-speed induction motor. **Model Answer:** 1. **Two or more discrete speeds** can be obtained from a single motor without using an external variable-frequency drive (VFD), making it **economical** for multi-speed applications. 2. **Higher starting torque** is available at the low-speed (high-pole) configuration, suitable for **heavy-load starting** like conveyors and hoists. 3. **Simple, rugged and maintenance-free** construction (no electronic parts), making it ideal for **hostile environments** (mines, cement plants, chemical industries). 4. *(Bonus)* Energy saving in fan and pump drives by operating at lower speed during low demand. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice) > **Note:** As per KTU ESE 2024 regulation, Module-level Part B questions carry an **internal choice**. **Answer ANY ONE** of Question A or Question B. --- ### ✏️ Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]* **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **Dahlander pole-changing motor**. Compare the **Δ-YY**, **Y-YY** and **YY-Δ** connections with respect to their torque and power characteristics. **(b) [7 Marks]** A 3-phase, 6/4-pole, Y-YY connected squirrel-cage pole-changing induction motor is load-tested. At a particular load on the **4-pole (high-speed)** setting, the following readings were noted: - $V_L = 400$ V, $\;I_L = 6.2$ A - $W_1 = 1820$ W, $\;W_2 = 360$ W - $N_r = 1450$ RPM - $S_1 = 5.8$ kg, $\;S_2 = 0.6$ kg - $R = 8$ cm Calculate: **(i)** Synchronous speed and slip %, **(ii)** Brake torque, **(iii)** Output power in kW, **(iv)** Power factor, **(v)** Efficiency. --- #### **Solution to Q.A(a):** **Construction:** - The stator carries **one single winding** designed for the *higher* pole count, with **taps** brought out for reconnection. - The rotor is a **standard squirrel cage** (no taps, no slip rings). - A **Dahlander switch** (a special 3-pole changeover switch with three positions) connects the winding in either **Δ/YY** or **Y/YY** configurations. **Working:** For a 6/4-pole Dahlander motor: - **6-pole operation:** All coil groups are connected in **star (Y)** or **delta (Δ)** → $N_s = 1000$ RPM. - **4-pole operation:** The Dahlander switch reverses the current in **alternate coil groups** so the effective pole count drops to 4 → $N_s = 1500$ RPM. - Synchronous speed therefore **doubles**. **Comparison Table:** | Connection | Low-Speed Torque | High-Speed Torque | Power | Suitable For | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Δ – YY** | $T_L$ | $T_L$ (approx.) | $P_H = 2 P_L$ | **Constant torque** (compressors) | | **Y – YY** | $0.5\,T_H$ | $T_H$ | $P_H = 2 P_L$ | **Variable (fan) torque** (fans, pumps) | | **YY – Δ** | $2\,T_H$ | $T_H$ | $P_L \approx P_H$ | **Constant power** (machine tools) | --- #### **Solution to Q.A(b):** **(i) Synchronous speed and slip %** **Step 1:** For $P = 4$, $f = 50$ Hz: $$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ RPM}$$ **Step 2:** Slip RPM: $$N_{slip} = 1500 - 1450 = 50 \text{ RPM}$$ **Step 3:** Slip %: $$s\,\% = \frac{50}{1500} \times 100 = 3.33\,\%$$ --- **(ii) Brake torque** **Step 1:** Net reading: $\Delta S = 5.8 - 0.6 = 5.2$ kg **Step 2:** Convert to Newtons: $F_{net} = 5.2 \times 9.81 = 51.012$ N **Step 3:** Convert radius to metres: $R = 0.08$ m $$T_{brake} = 51.012 \times 0.08 = 4.081 \text{ N·m}$$ --- **(iii) Output power in kW** **Step 1:** Use the standard formula: $$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N_r T_{brake}}{60}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute: $$P_{out} = \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 1450 \times 4.081}{60}$$ $$P_{out} = \frac{37169.0}{60} = 619.48 \text{ W}$$ **Step 3:** Convert to kW: $$P_{out} = 0.6195 \text{ kW}$$ --- **(iv) Power factor** **Step 1:** Total input: $P_{in} = W_1 + W_2 = 1820 + 360 = 2180$ W **Step 2:** $$\cos\phi = \frac{2180}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 6.2} = \frac{2180}{4295.2} = 0.5075 \text{ lagging}$$ --- **(v) Efficiency** $$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{619.48}{2180} \times 100 = 28.42\,\%$$ > **Final Answer Summary:** $\;N_s = 1500$ RPM, $\;s = 3.33\,\%$, $\;T_{brake} = 4.08$ N·m, $\;P_{out} = 0.62$ kW, $\;\cos\phi = 0.508$ lag, $\;\eta = 28.42\,\%$ --- ### ✏️ Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam - June 2024]* **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat diagram the **two methods of pole changing** in 3-phase induction motors. Mention the **limitations** of pole-changing motors. **(b) [7 Marks]** In a load test on a **8/4-pole, Δ-YY** pole-changing induction motor running on the **8-pole connection**, the following readings were taken: - $V_L = 410$ V, $\;I_L = 5.0$ A - $W_1 = 1340$ W, $\;W_2 = 240$ W - $N_r = 710$ RPM - $S_1 = 7.2$ kg, $\;S_2 = 1.0$ kg - $R = 7.5$ cm Calculate: **(i)** Synchronous speed and slip RPM, **(ii)** Brake torque in N·m, **(iii)** Output power in HP, **(iv)** Power factor, **(v)** Efficiency. --- #### **Solution to Q.B(a):** **Method 1 — Dahlander (Consequent-Pole) Connection:** - A **single tapped winding** is reconnected by reversing the current in alternate pole-pairs. - Pole ratio is **1 : 2** (e.g., 2/4, 4/8, 6/12). - Speeds obtained: e.g., 3000/1500, 1500/750, 1000/500 RPM. **Method 2 — Two Independent Stator Windings:** - Two **separate windings** with different pole counts are placed in the **same stator slots**. - Only one winding is energised at a time → speed is selected by the switch. - Pole ratio can be **anything**, not necessarily 1 : 2 (e.g., 4-pole + 6-pole → 1500/1000 RPM). **Limitations of pole-changing motors:** 1. **Only discrete speeds** are available — not stepless. 2. **Limited to two (or at most three) speeds** per motor. 3. The **slot harmonics are large** at certain pole counts → noisy operation. 4. The **stator is bulkier** and **copper utilisation is poor** at some connections. 5. The starting current in the **YY mode** is high (because the line current is √3 times the phase current). 6. **Complex switching** — requires a multi-throw Dahlander switch or contactor bank. --- #### **Solution to Q.B(b):** **(i) Synchronous speed and slip RPM** **Step 1:** For $P = 8$, $f = 50$ Hz: $$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{8} = 750 \text{ RPM}$$ **Step 2:** Slip RPM: $$N_{slip} = 750 - 710 = 40 \text{ RPM}$$ --- **(ii) Brake torque** **Step 1:** Net reading: $\Delta S = 7.2 - 1.0 = 6.2$ kg **Step 2:** Convert: $F_{net} = 6.2 \times 9.81 = 60.822$ N **Step 3:** Convert radius: $R = 0.075$ m $$T_{brake} = 60.822 \times 0.075 = 4.5617 \text{ N·m}$$ --- **(iii) Output power in HP** **Step 1:** $$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N_r T_{brake}}{60} = \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 710 \times 4.5617}{60}$$ $$P_{out} = \frac{20345.0}{60} = 339.08 \text{ W}$$ **Step 2:** Convert to HP: $$HP = \frac{339.08}{746} = 0.4545 \text{ HP}$$ --- **(iv) Power factor** **Step 1:** Total input: $P_{in} = 1340 + 240 = 1580$ W **Step 2:** $$\cos\phi = \frac{1580}{\sqrt{3} \times 410 \times 5.0} = \frac{1580}{3550.5} = 0.4450 \text{ lagging}$$ --- **(v) Efficiency** $$\eta = \frac{339.08}{1580} \times 100 = 21.46\,\%$$ > **Final Answer Summary:** $\;N_s = 750$ RPM, $\;N_{slip} = 40$ RPM, $\;T_{brake} = 4.56$ N·m, $\;P_{out} = 0.455$ HP, $\;\cos\phi = 0.445$ lag, $\;\eta = 21.46\,\%$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert $R$ from cm to m** — silent 0-mark for the entire torque and power calculation. Always write "$R = 0.075$ m" explicitly. > 2. **Forgetting to multiply kgf by $9.81$** — the spring balance reads in **kilogram-force**, not Newtons. KTU examiners will deduct **2 marks** for this single omission. > 3. **Using $N_s$ instead of $N_r$** in the output power formula — this is a **common 3-mark blunder**. Slip power is *not* the same as shaft power. > 4. **Not stating "lagging"** after the power factor value — KTU key deducts **0.5 mark** for missing the qualifier. > 5. **Forgetting to draw all six characteristic curves** in the graph section — each missing curve is **1 mark** lost. > 6. **Mixing up speed settings** when comparing high-pole vs low-pole performance — always **label both curves clearly** in the legend. --- ## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Exam Checklist — Pin This to Your Wall!** - ✅ **Synchronous speed** $N_s = \dfrac{120\,f}{P}$ — always 50 Hz in India, $N_s = 1500$ for $P=4$, $1000$ for $P=6$, $750$ for $P=8$. - ✅ **Dahlander pole ratio = 1 : 2** — speeds double when poles are halved. - ✅ **Rotor is always squirrel cage** — no slip rings, no rotor taps. - ✅ **Brake torque** $T = (S_1 - S_2) \times 9.81 \times R$ — note the **9.81** conversion (kgf → N) and **R in metres**. - ✅ **Output power** $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi N_r T}{60}$ — use **measured** $N_r$, *not* $N_s$. - ✅ **Input power** $P_{in} = W_1 + W_2$ (two-wattmeter method). - ✅ **Power factor** $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L\,I_L}$ — always write **"lagging"**. - ✅ **Efficiency** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ — peak near rated load, low at no-load and overload. - ✅ **Three Dahlander connections:** - **Δ – YY** → **Constant Torque** - **Y – YY** → **Variable (Fan) Torque** (energy-saving mode) - **YY – Δ** → **Constant Power** (machine tool spindles) - ✅ **Two pole-changing methods:** Dahlander (1:2) + Two independent windings (any ratio). - ✅ **Always repeat the load test** in BOTH pole settings and plot comparative curves. - ✅ **Limitations:** Discrete speeds only, complex switching, poor slot utilisation, no smooth speed variation. - ✅ **Real-world uses:** Lifts, hoists, fans, pumps, washing machines, cement kilns, mine drives. - ✅ **Always label units** in every line of your calculation — KTU examiners reward clarity. > **End of Notes — Module Experiment 2, PCEEL507, KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Variation of starting torque with rotor resistance in 3-phase slip-ring induction motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL507 - AC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Variation of starting torque with rotor resistance in 3-phase slip-ring induction motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **3-phase slip-ring induction motor** (also called a **wound-rotor induction motor** or **phase-wound induction motor**) is a rotating AC machine whose rotor winding is not short-circuited internally; instead, the three-phase rotor winding terminals are brought out via **slip rings and brush gear** to an external **star-connected rheostat (rheostatic starter / liquid resistance starter)**. This unique construction permits the deliberate insertion of **external rotor circuit resistance ($R_{2,ext}$)** in series with the inherent rotor winding resistance ($R_2$).

The experiment on **"Variation of Starting Torque with Rotor Resistance"** investigates how the **electromagnetic starting torque ($T_{st}$)** — the torque developed by the motor at the instant of switching ON ($s = 1$) — changes as the external resistance added to each rotor phase is varied from zero to a large value.

$$R_{2,\text{total}} = R_2 + R_{2,\text{ext}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The slip-ring motor is the **only** induction motor in which the rotor resistance is externally controllable *while the rotor is rotating*. This makes it indispensable for **high-inertia loads, cranes, hoists, elevators, and large compressors** where starting current must be limited and starting torque must be boosted.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are pushing a heavy, rusted, cylindrical **stone wheel** that is stuck in mud:

* **Scenario A (No Rotor Resistance):** You push the wheel from rest. The wheel's "internal friction" (rotor resistance) is very low. Almost all your energy goes into **circulating huge inrush currents** inside the wheel, producing intense heat but **very little turning effort**. The wheel barely rotates. This is exactly what happens in a **squirrel-cage motor at start** — high current, low torque.

* **Scenario B (External Rotor Resistance Added):** Now imagine attaching thick, rough **rope-leashes** to the wheel before pushing. The ropes "grip" the internal currents and force them to do **mechanical work** instead of dissipating as heat. The harder the rope (more resistance), the more "push" you get for the same current. With the right rope length, the wheel rotates **strongly from rest**. This is what a **slip-ring motor with proper rotor resistance** achieves.

* **Key Insight:** The external rotor resistance acts as a **"torque-boosting gear"** that converts wasteful rotor copper losses into useful starting torque, **without drawing excessive stator current**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - Synchronous speed: $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ (rpm)
> - Per-phase rotor EMF at standstill: $E_2$ (V/phase)
> - Rotor reactance at standstill: $X_2$ ($\Omega$/phase)
> - Starting condition: Slip $s = 1$
> - Starting torque is measured using a **spring balance / rope-brake dynamometer** in the lab.

### 1.3 Why This Experiment Matters in KTU Lab

This experiment is a **direct experimental verification** of the classical induction-motor torque equation. It also illustrates the **maximum-torque theorem** of the induction motor — namely, that the slip at which maximum torque occurs is **directly proportional to the total rotor resistance**.

$$s_{mT} = \frac{R_{2,\text{total}}}{X_2}$$

By varying $R_{2,\text{ext}}$, we can literally **shift the peak of the torque-slip curve** along the slip axis, ensuring that peak torque coincides with the starting instant ($s = 1$) when required.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

1. **Construction of Rotor Circuit:** The 3-phase rotor winding is star-connected internally, and the three free ends terminate at three **slip rings** mounted on the rotor shaft. Carbon brushes ride on these rings and connect to a **3-phase star-connected variable rheostat** outside the motor frame.

2. **Equivalent Circuit per Phase (Referred to Stator):** For analytical purposes, the rotor impedance at any slip $s$ is:
$$Z_2(s) = \frac{R_2}{s} + jX_2$$
where $R_2/s$ combines rotor resistance and the slip-dependent load-effect term.

3. **At the Starting Instant:** Slip $s = 1$, so rotor impedance becomes purely:
$$Z_2(1) = (R_2 + R_{2,\text{ext}}) + jX_2$$
The **magnitude** of this impedance is:
$$\vert Z_2 \vert = \sqrt{(R_2 + R_{2,\text{ext}})^2 + X_2^2}$$

4. **Why Increasing Resistance Increases Starting Torque (initially):** The starting torque expression contains $R_2$ in the numerator (squared once) and $(R_2^2 + X_2^2)$ in the denominator. For **small** added resistance where $R_{2,\text{total}} < X_2$, increasing $R_{2,\text{ext}}$ **increases** the numerator faster than the denominator, so $T_{st}$ rises.

5. **Why Further Increasing Resistance Eventually Decreases Starting Torque:** Once $R_{2,\text{total}}$ exceeds $X_2$, the $(R_{2,\text{total}})^2$ term in the denominator dominates the linear $R_{2,\text{total}}$ in the numerator, so $T_{st}$ begins to **fall**. This produces a **peak** in the $T_{st}$ vs $R_{2,\text{ext}}$ curve.

6. **Condition for Maximum Starting Torque:** The maximum of $T_{st}$ occurs when:
$$R_{2,\text{total}} = X_2 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; R_{2,\text{ext(optimal)}} = X_2 - R_2$$
At this optimum, the starting torque equals the **maximum (pull-out) torque** of the motor.

7. **Practical Interpretation:** Motor designers size the rotor such that, with **all external resistance cut out** (full slip rings shorted), normal running occurs at low slip (~3–5%) and the motor operates near rated efficiency. With **full external resistance** in circuit, peak torque is shifted to $s = 1$, giving a torque-boosted start.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | rpm | $f$ = supply frequency, $P$ = poles |
| 2 | Slip at running | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | pu | At start, $s = 1$ |
| 3 | Rotor impedance (general) | $Z_2(s) = \dfrac{R_2}{s} + jX_2$ | $\Omega$/ph | Referred to stator |
| 4 | Rotor impedance (start, $s=1$) | $Z_2 = (R_2 + R_{2,ext}) + jX_2$ | $\Omega$/ph | Includes external resistance |
| 5 | Starting torque (general) | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^2 (R_2 + R_{2,ext})}{(R_2 + R_{2,ext})^2 + X_2^2}$ | N·m | $E_2$ = standstill rotor EMF/ph |
| 6 | Slip at maximum torque | $s_{mT} = \dfrac{R_2 + R_{2,ext}}{X_2}$ | pu | Direct proportionality |
| 7 | Maximum torque (pull-out) | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^2}{2 X_2}$ | N·m | **Independent of rotor resistance** |
| 8 | Condition for max $T_{st}$ | $R_2 + R_{2,ext} = X_2$ | $\Omega$/ph | Peak of the experimental curve |
| 9 | Synchronous angular speed | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{4 \pi f}{P}$ | rad/s | Mechanical rad/s |
| 10 | Mechanical power developed | $P_m = T \cdot \omega_r$ | W | $\omega_r = (1-s)\omega_s$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule (Examiner's Favourite):** $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance**, but the **slip at which $T_{max}$ occurs is directly proportional to rotor resistance.** This is why varying $R_{2,ext}$ shifts the peak of the $T$–$s$ curve horizontally without changing its height.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Slip-Ring + Variable Rotor Resistance? |
|-------------|--------------------------------------------|
| **Cranes, hoists, lifts** | Need very high starting torque to lift heavy loads from rest |
| **Large compressors & mills** | High-inertia loads require smooth, controlled acceleration |
| **Wind turbine generators (old)** | Soft start in variable wind conditions |
| **Conveyor belts in mines** | Gradual acceleration prevents belt slippage |
| **Printing presses** | Precise speed control during start-up |

In modern drives, the **rheostat is replaced by a power-electronic IGBT chopper** (a *static Scherbius drive* or *Kramer drive*) that does the same job more efficiently and with finer control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Starting Torque Expression

**Step 1: Power Input to the Rotor Circuit per Phase**

The per-phase Thevenin EMF induced in the rotor (referred to stator) at slip $s$ is $sE_2$. The total rotor impedance at slip $s$ is $(R_2/s) + jX_2$. Therefore, the per-phase rotor current magnitude is:

$$
I_2' = \frac{s E_2}{\sqrt{\left(\frac{R_2}{s}\right)^2 + X_2^2}}
$$

**Step 2: Rotor Copper Loss per Phase (Mechanical Power Equivalent)**

The rotor copper loss per phase at slip $s$ is $I_2'^2 \cdot (R_2/s)$. The total air-gap power developed per phase is:

$$
P_{g,\text{per phase}} = \frac{I_2'^2 \cdot R_2}{s}
$$

Substituting $I_2'$:

$$
P_{g,\text{per phase}} = \frac{R_2}{s} \cdot \frac{s^2 E_2^2}{\left(\frac{R_2}{s}\right)^2 + X_2^2} = \frac{s E_2^2 R_2}{\left(\frac{R_2}{s}\right)^2 + X_2^2}
$$

**Step 3: Total Air-Gap Power for All 3 Phases**

$$
P_g = \frac{3 s E_2^2 R_2}{\left(\frac{R_2}{s}\right)^2 + X_2^2}
$$

**Step 4: Electromagnetic Torque (Mechanical Power ÷ Synchronous Speed)**

Electromagnetic torque is air-gap power divided by synchronous angular velocity $\omega_s$:

$$
T = \frac{P_g}{\omega_s} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2^2 R_2}{\left(\frac{R_2}{s}\right)^2 + X_2^2}
$$

This is the **general torque-slip equation** of an induction motor.

**Step 5: Imposing the Starting Condition $s = 1$**

When the motor is switched on, slip $s = 1$, and the rotor winding has external resistance $R_{2,ext}$ in series with the inherent rotor resistance $R_2$. Replacing $R_2 \to R_2 + R_{2,ext}$ and $s = 1$:

$$
T_{st} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2 (R_2 + R_{2,ext})}{(R_2 + R_{2,ext})^2 + X_2^2}
$$

Let $R_T = R_2 + R_{2,ext}$ (total rotor circuit resistance). Then:

$$
T_{st} = \frac{3 E_2^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{R_T}{R_T^2 + X_2^2}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Condition for Maximum Starting Torque

To find the value of $R_T$ that maximizes $T_{st}$, differentiate with respect to $R_T$ and set the derivative to zero.

Let:
$$
f(R_T) = \frac{R_T}{R_T^2 + X_2^2}
$$

Apply the quotient rule:

$$
\frac{df}{dR_T} = \frac{(R_T^2 + X_2^2)(1) - R_T (2 R_T)}{(R_T^2 + X_2^2)^2}
$$

Simplify the numerator:

$$
\frac{df}{dR_T} = \frac{R_T^2 + X_2^2 - 2R_T^2}{(R_T^2 + X_2^2)^2} = \frac{X_2^2 - R_T^2}{(R_T^2 + X_2^2)^2}
$$

Set $\dfrac{df}{dR_T} = 0$:

$$
X_2^2 - R_T^2 = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; R_T^2 = X_2^2
$$

Since resistance is positive:

$$
R_T = X_2 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; R_2 + R_{2,ext} = X_2
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;R_{2,ext(\text{optimum})} = X_2 - R_2\;}
$$

**Step 6: Value of Maximum Starting Torque**

Substitute $R_T = X_2$ back into the $T_{st}$ equation:

$$
T_{st,\max} = \frac{3 E_2^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{X_2}{X_2^2 + X_2^2} = \frac{3 E_2^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{X_2}{2 X_2^2} = \frac{3 E_2^2}{2 \omega_s X_2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;T_{st,\max} = \frac{3 E_2^2}{2 \omega_s X_2} = T_{\max}\;}
$$

**Conclusion:** The maximum possible starting torque equals the **maximum (pull-out) torque** of the motor and is **independent of rotor resistance** — a celebrated result.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Behaviour of $T_{st}$ vs $R_{2,ext}$

| Region | $R_T$ Range | Behaviour of $T_{st}$ | Physical Reason |
|--------|-------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| **I — Rising** | $R_T < X_2$ | $T_{st}$ **increases** with $R_{2,ext}$ | Numerator grows faster than denominator; more EMF drop across $R_T$ |
| **II — Peak** | $R_T = X_2$ | $T_{st}$ reaches **maximum** $T_{max}$ | Impedance angle = 45°; maximum power transfer condition |
| **III — Falling** | $R_T > X_2$ | $T_{st}$ **decreases** with $R_{2,ext}$ | Rotor current collapses; motor stalls or runs at very high slip |

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of Limit Behaviour:**
> - As $R_{2,ext} \to 0$: $T_{st} \to \dfrac{3 E_2^2 R_2}{\omega_s (R_2^2 + X_2^2)}$ — small, because $R_2 \ll X_2$ in wound rotors.
> - As $R_{2,ext} \to \infty$: $T_{st} \to \dfrac{3 E_2^2}{\omega_s R_{2,ext}} \to 0$ — current vanishes.
> - At $R_{2,ext} = X_2 - R_2$: $T_{st} = T_{max}$ — global maximum.

### 3.4 Numerical Example (Typical KTU Numerical)

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz slip-ring induction motor has standstill rotor EMF per phase $E_2 = 100$ V, rotor resistance $R_2 = 0.2\ \Omega$/phase, and rotor reactance $X_2 = 1.0\ \Omega$/phase. Calculate:
(a) The starting torque with no external resistance.
(b) The external resistance required to obtain **maximum starting torque**.
(c) The value of maximum starting torque.

**Solution:**

*Synchronous speed:*
$$
N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}
$$

*Synchronous angular speed:*
$$
\omega_s = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60} = 50 \pi \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**(a) Starting torque with $R_{2,ext} = 0$:**
$$
T_{st} = \frac{3}{157.08} \times \frac{100^2 \times 0.2}{0.2^2 + 1.0^2}
$$
$$
T_{st} = \frac{3}{157.08} \times \frac{2000}{1.04} = \frac{3 \times 1923.08}{157.08} \approx 36.72 \text{ N·m}
$$

**(b) External resistance for maximum starting torque:**
$$
R_{2,ext} = X_2 - R_2 = 1.0 - 0.2 = 0.8\ \Omega/\text{phase}
$$

**(c) Maximum starting torque:**
$$
T_{st,\max} = \frac{3}{157.08} \times \frac{100^2}{2 \times 1.0} = \frac{3 \times 10000}{2 \times 157.08} \approx 95.49 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Observation:** Adding just $0.8\ \Omega$/phase boosts the starting torque from $\approx 36.7$ N·m to $\approx 95.5$ N·m — a **2.6× increase**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid Flowchart)

This diagram maps the **functional flow of the experiment** — from power input, through the motor, to the measurement of starting torque for each setting of rotor resistance.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[3-Phase AC Mains Supply] --> B[3-Phase Variac Autotransformer]
    B --> C[Stator Terminals of Slip-Ring IM]
    D[DC Shunt Loading Arrangement] --> C
    C --> E[Rotor Winding of Slip-Ring IM]
    E --> F[Slip Rings and Brush Assembly]
```
