Since the rotor bars are short-circuited (squirrel cage) or connected via slip rings (wound rotor), the induced EMF drives a circulating current $I_2$.

**Step 4 — Force Production (Lorentz Force):**
The rotor current interacts with the stator flux to produce a **Lorentz force** $F = BIL$ on each conductor. The cumulative effect is a torque that accelerates the rotor in the direction of the RMF.

**Step 5 — Steady-State Equilibrium:**
The motor accelerates until electromagnetic torque $T_e$ equals the load torque $T_L$ (plus friction). At equilibrium, $N_r < N_s$ permanently—**slip cannot be zero in a healthy induction motor**.

### B. Generator Mode ($s < 0$)

**Step 1 — Prime Mover Action:**
An external prime mover (e.g., diesel engine, wind turbine, hydro turbine) drives the rotor shaft at a speed $N_r > N_s$.

**Step 2 — Reversal of Relative Motion:**
Now, the rotor *overtakes* the rotating magnetic field. The relative velocity vector reverses direction: $(N_r - N_s)$ instead of $(N_s - N_r)$.

**Step 3 — Reversal of Induced EMF and Current:**
By Lenz's Law re-interpretation, the induced rotor EMF and the resulting rotor current *reverse polarity* compared to motor mode.

**Step 4 — Reversal of Electromagnetic Torque:**
The torque developed by the machine *opposes* the motion (a generator inherently resists being driven). The prime mover must supply mechanical power to maintain rotation.

**Step 5 — Power Flow Reversal:**
The stator now delivers active power back to the grid. However, it *still consumes reactive power* (for magnetizing)—this is why induction generators **cannot operate in isolation** without capacitor banks or grid support.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Exam Fact**: An induction generator operating in standalone mode (off-grid) requires a **capacitor bank (self-excited induction generator — SEIG)** to provide the lagging reactive power needed for magnetization. The minimum capacitance is determined by the magnetization curve and load characteristics.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary/Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_s = \dfrac{120f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed | Always positive |
| 2 | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Slip (per unit) | $s<0$: Gen; $0<s<1$: Motor |
| 3 | $f_r = s \cdot f$ | Rotor frequency | Direct proportionality to slip |
| 4 | $E_2 = s \cdot E_{20}$ | Rotor induced EMF at slip $s$ | $E_{20}$ = standstill rotor EMF |
| 5 | $R_2' = \dfrac{R_2}{s}$ | Effective rotor resistance (referred) | Foundation of all torque eqns |
| 6 | $X_2' = s \cdot X_{20}$ | Effective rotor reactance | Frequency-dependent |
| 7 | $Z_2' = \dfrac{R_2'}{s} + jX_2'$ | Rotor impedance (referred) | Used in equivalent circuit |
| 8 | $I_2' = \dfrac{E_2}{Z_2'}$ | Rotor current (referred to stator) | Phasor quantity |
| 9 | $T_e = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{I_2'^2 \cdot R_2'}{s}$ | Electromagnetic torque | $T_{max}$ occurs at $s_{max}$ |
| 10 | $s_{max} = \dfrac{R_2}{X_{20}}$ | Slip at maximum torque | Independent of load! |
| 11 | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_1^2}{2 \cdot X_{20}}$ | Maximum (pull-out) torque | Independent of $R_2$ |
| 12 | $P_{gap} = \dfrac{T_e \cdot N_s}{60} \cdot 2\pi$ | Air-gap power | $P_{gap} = P_{in} - P_{stator\_losses}$ |
| 13 | $P_{m} = (1-s) \cdot P_{gap}$ | Mechanical power developed | Power to shaft |
| 14 | $P_{cu2} = s \cdot P_{gap}$ | Rotor copper loss | Confirmation of $P_{gap} = P_{cu2} + P_m$ |
| 15 | $P_{out} = P_m - P_{rotational}$ | Output shaft power | After friction + windage |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight (KTU 2024 Favorite)**: The relationship $P_{cu2} : P_m = s : (1-s)$ is a powerful shortcut. For example, if $s = 0.04$, then 4% of air-gap power is lost as heat in rotor and 96% becomes mechanical power. This is why high-efficiency motors operate at very low slip.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use Case | Why Induction Machine? |
|---|---|---|
| **Industrial Drives** | Pumps, fans, conveyors, compressors | Self-starting, robust, low maintenance |
| **Wind Energy** | Wind turbine generators (DFIG, SCIG) | Variable speed operation, no brushes |
| **Hydro Plants** | Small hydro (micro/mini) | Simple grid connection via induction gen |
| **Electric Vehicles** | Tesla early models, public e-buses | High power density, regenerative braking |
| **Home Appliances** | Washing machines, refrigerators, ACs | Single-phase shaded-pole variants |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Slip and Its Physical Meaning

**Statement to Prove:** The slip $s$ of an induction machine represents the fractional difference between synchronous speed and actual rotor speed, with sign convention indicating the operating mode.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

Let $N_s$ be the synchronous speed of the rotating magnetic field in RPM, and $N_r$ be the actual mechanical rotor speed in RPM.

The **relative speed** between the rotating magnetic field and the rotor conductors is the speed at which the stator flux "cuts" the rotor:

$$v_{rel} = N_s - N_r$$

**Define** the slip $s$ as the per-unit (fractional) relative speed with respect to synchronous speed:

$$s = \frac{v_{rel}}{N_s} = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

Rearranging to express rotor speed:

$$N_r = N_s \cdot (1 - s)$$

**Mode Identification:**

- **Motor Mode**: Rotor lags the field → $N_r < N_s$ → $N_s - N_r > 0$ → $s > 0$ → range $0 < s < 1$
- **Generator Mode**: Rotor leads the field → $N_r > N_s$ → $N_s - N_r < 0$ → $s < 0$
- **Standstill (Starting)**: Rotor is stationary → $N_r = 0$ → $s = 1$

The **rotor frequency** (frequency of the induced EMF in the rotor) is proportional to the relative speed:

$$f_r = s \cdot f$$

This explains why rotor EMF and reactance scale with slip—an essential principle in the equivalent circuit.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Flow in Motor and Generator Modes

**Statement to Prove:** The air-gap power $P_{gap}$ divides into rotor copper loss $P_{cu2}$ and mechanical power $P_m$ in the ratio $s : (1-s)$ regardless of operating mode.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

The electromagnetic torque $T_e$ developed in the machine acts on the rotor, which is rotating at synchronous-equivalent angular velocity $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi N_s}{60}$.

The **total air-gap power** transferred from stator to rotor across the air gap is:

$$P_{gap} = T_e \cdot \omega_s$$

This power is dissipated in two ways:

**(a) Rotor Copper Loss**: Current $I_2'$ flowing through rotor resistance $R_2$ dissipates power. The rotor sees resistance $R_2'/s$ in the equivalent circuit. So:

$$P_{cu2} = 3 \cdot I_2'^2 \cdot R_2$$

But the air-gap power can also be expressed as:

$$P_{gap} = 3 \cdot I_2'^2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{s}$$

Dividing the two:

$$\frac{P_{cu2}}{P_{gap}} = \frac{3 \cdot I_2'^2 \cdot R_2}{3 \cdot I_2'^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{s}} = s$$

Therefore:

$$P_{cu2} = s \cdot P_{gap}$$

**(b) Mechanical Power Developed**: By conservation of energy:

$$P_m = P_{gap} - P_{cu2} = P_{gap} - s \cdot P_{gap} = (1 - s) \cdot P_{gap}$$

**Verification of Ratio:**

$$\frac{P_{cu2}}{P_m} = \frac{s \cdot P_{gap}}{(1-s) \cdot P_{gap}} = \frac{s}{1-s}$$

This elegant relation is **mode-independent in form** but **sign-dependent in interpretation**:

| Mode | Slip ($s$) | $P_{cu2}$ | $P_m$ | Net Energy Flow |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Motor | $+0.04$ | $+0.04 P_{gap}$ | $+0.96 P_{gap}$ | Electrical → Mechanical |
| Generator | $-0.04$ | $-0.04 P_{gap}$ | $-1.04 P_{gap}$ | Mechanical → Electrical |

In generator mode, the mechanical power input is *greater* than the air-gap power because the rotor must overcome the *negative* rotor copper loss (which now acts as a power source, not sink).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Thevenin Equivalent & Torque-Slip Equation

**Statement to Prove:** The torque of an induction machine is given by

$$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{th}^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{s}}{\left(R_{th} + \dfrac{R_2}{s}\right)^2 + (X_{th} + X_{20})^2}$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1**: Take the standard per-phase equivalent circuit referred to the stator. The stator has $R_1, X_1$, the magnetizing branch has $R_c, X_m$, and the rotor branch has $R_2/s$ and $X_2$ (where $X_2 = sX_{20}$ in actual rotor frame, but in the referred circuit we keep $X_{20}$ and place the slip dependency on resistance).

**Step 2**: Apply **Thevenin's theorem** to the network "seen" from the rotor terminals. The Thevenin voltage is:

$$V_{th} = V_1 \cdot \frac{jX_m}{R_1 + j(X_1 + X_m)}$$

**Step 3**: The Thevenin impedance is:

$$Z_{th} = R_{th} + jX_{th} = \frac{(R_1 + jX_1) \cdot jX_m}{R_1 + j(X_1 + X_m)}$$

**Step 4**: The simplified equivalent circuit is a Thevenin source $V_{th}$ driving a series combination of $Z_{th}$ and the rotor branch $(R_2/s + jX_{20})$.

The rotor current is:

$$I_2' = \frac{V_{th}}{(R_{th} + R_2/s) + j(X_{th} + X_{20})}$$

**Step 5**: The air-gap power is:

$$P_{gap} = 3 \cdot I_2'^2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{s}$$

**Step 6**: Substituting the magnitude-squared of the denominator:

$$I_2'^2 = \frac{V_{th}^2}{(R_{th} + R_2/s)^2 + (X_{th} + X_{20})^2}$$

**Step 7**: Combining:

$$P_{gap} = \frac{3 \cdot V_{th}^2 \cdot (R_2/s)}{(R_{th} + R_2/s)^2 + (X_{th} + X_{20})^2}$$

**Step 8**: Since torque is air-gap power divided by synchronous angular velocity:

$$\omega_s = \frac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2\pi f}{P/2} = \frac{4\pi f}{P}$$

Therefore:

$$T_e = \frac{P_{gap}}{\omega_s} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{th}^2 \cdot (R_2/s)}{(R_{th} + R_2/s)^2 + (X_{th} + X_{20})^2}$$

**Q.E.D.**

### Finding Maximum Torque:

Differentiating $T_e$ with respect to $s$ and setting to zero:

$$\frac{dT_e}{ds} = 0 \implies s_{max} = \frac{R_2}{\sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_{th} + X_{20})^2}}$$

For most practical machines, $R_{th}^2 \ll (X_{th} + X_{20})^2$, so:

$$s_{max} \approx \frac{R_2}{X_{th} + X_{20}} \approx \frac{R_2}{X_{20}}$$

Substituting back into the torque equation:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{th}^2}{2 \cdot (R_{th} + \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_{th}+X_{20})^2})}$$

Or simplified:

$$T_{max} \approx \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{th}^2}{2 \cdot (X_{th} + X_{20})}$$

**Key Insights for Exam**:

- $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance $R_2$** — a beautiful and counter-intuitive result.
- $s_{max}$ is **directly proportional to $R_2$** — adding external rotor resistance shifts the maximum torque point toward $s = 1$ (used in wound-rotor motors for high starting torque).
- $T_{max} \propto V^2$ — voltage dips severely reduce pull-out torque.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Power Flow Diagram — Motor vs Generator Mode

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the energy flow through the machine in both operating quadrants. The architecture depicts how electrical input from the grid, mechanical input from the prime mover, and various losses interact in the motor and generator modes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Grid 3-Phase Supply] -->|P_in Motor| B[Stator]
    B -->|P_sc Core loss| C[Stator Copper Loss P_scu]
    B -->|P_gap Air Gap| D[Rotor]
    D -->|P_cu2 s times P_gap| E[Rotor Copper Loss]
    D -->|P_m Mechanical| F[Shaft]
    F -->|P_rot Friction Windage| G[Output to Load]
    PM1[Prime Mover] -->|P_mech_in| F

    H[Stator Magnetic Field RMF] -.->|Links| D

    classDef supply fill:#ffd700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef power fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mechanical fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A supply
    class C,E,F loss
    class B,D power
    class G,PM1 mechanical
```

**Reading the diagram**:
- In **motor mode**: Power flows from grid (A) → Stator → Air gap → Rotor → Shaft → Load.
- In **generator mode**: The flow direction reverses from the prime mover side. Power enters from PM1, exits through the grid side (A) to the network.

## 4.2 Operating Quadrant Map (Speed vs Torque)

The following block-level schematic shows the four quadrants of induction machine operation, which is a high-yield diagram for KTU 2024 theory papers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Q1[Quadrant 1: Forward Motoring]
        Q1A[Rotor Speed N_r greater than 0] --> Q1B[Torque T_e greater than 0]
        Q1B --> Q1C[Slip s between 0 and 1]
        Q1C --> Q1D[Power P from Grid]
    end

    subgraph Q2[Quadrant 2: Forward Generating]
        Q2A[N_r greater than N_s] --> Q2B[T less than 0]
        Q2B --> Q2C[Slip s less than 0]
        Q2C --> Q2D[Power P to Grid]
    end

    subgraph Q3[Quadrant 3: Reverse Motoring]
        Q3A[N_r less than 0] --> Q3B[T less than 0]
        Q3B --> Q3C[Slip s greater than 1]
        Q3C --> Q3D[Plugging Mode]
    end

    subgraph Q4[Quadrant 4: Reverse Generating]
        Q4A[N_r less than 0] --> Q4B[T greater than 0]
        Q4B --> Q4C[Brake Regeneration]
    end

    Q1 --> Q2
    Q2 --> Q4
    Q4 --> Q3
    Q3 --> Q1
```

## 4.3 Torque-Slip Characteristic Curve (Block Visualization)

The torque-slip characteristic is the most-examined curve in KTU 2024 AC Machines. Below is a functional block-architecture representation showing the key regions:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Region 1: s between 1 and s_max] -->|High slip| B[High starting torque region]
    B --> C{Torque increases}
    C -->|T_max at s_max| D[Region 2: Operating region]
    D -->|Low slip 0 to 0.05| E[Normal motoring]
    E -->|s equals 0| F[Theoretical synchronous - no torque]
    F -->|s less than 0| G[Generator Mode - negative torque]
    G -->|s less than 1| H[Reverse motoring braking]

    classDef motor fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef gen fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef brake fill:#ffd700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    class B,C,D,E motor
    class G gen
    class H brake
```

## 4.4 Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit (Referred to Stator)

For the lab experiment, students must be able to draw the equivalent circuit. The block below shows the topology:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[V1 Supply Voltage] --> R1[R1 Stator Resistance]
    R1 --> X1[X1 Stator Reactance]
    X1 --> SPLIT{Junction Node}
    SPLIT --> RC[R_c Core Loss Branch]
    RC --> XM[X_m Magnetizing Reactance]
    XM --> NEUTRAL1[Reference Neutral]
    SPLIT --> R2[R2/s Effective Rotor Resistance]
    R2 --> X20[X20 Rotor Reactance at Standstill]
    X20 --> NEUTRAL2[Reference Neutral]

    classDef source fill:#ffd700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef rotor fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef stator fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    class V1 source
    class R2,X20 rotor
    class R1,X1,RC,XM stator
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Lab Tip**: When measuring the no-load test (motor mode), the rotor branch is essentially open (slip ≈ 0, so $R_2/s \to \infty$). When measuring the blocked-rotor test, the rotor is at standstill ($s = 1$, $R_2/s = R_2$). The DC resistance test gives $R_1$ directly.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Define slip of an induction machine. How does the sign of slip indicate the operating mode of the machine?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Slip is defined as the relative speed between the rotating magnetic field and the rotor, expressed as a fraction of synchronous speed:

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

where $N_s = 120f/P$ is synchronous speed and $N_r$ is actual rotor speed.

**Mode Identification (2 marks):**
- **Motor mode**: $N_r < N_s$, so $s$ is positive, with $0 < s < 1$. Power flows from stator to rotor.
- **Generator mode**: $N_r > N_s$, so $s$ is negative ($s < 0$). Power flows from rotor to stator.
- **Plugging/braking mode**: $N_r$ is negative relative to the field, so $s > 1$.

The sign and magnitude of slip thus uniquely identify the operating quadrant of the machine.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: An 8-pole, 50 Hz, 3-phase induction motor runs at 720 RPM. Calculate the slip and the rotor frequency.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Step 1**: Synchronous speed:
$$N_s = \frac{120f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{8} = 750 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 2**: Slip:
$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{750 - 720}{750} = \frac{30}{750} = 0.04 \text{ (or 4%)}$$

**Step 3**: Rotor frequency:
$$f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \times 50 = 2 \text{ Hz}$$

**Conclusion**: The machine is operating in **motor mode** (slip is positive) with a rotor frequency of 2 Hz, which is typical of lightly loaded industrial motors.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has the following per-phase parameters referred to stator:**
- $R_1 = 0.5\ \Omega$, $X_1 = 1.0\ \Omega$
- $R_2 = 0.6\ \Omega$, $X_2 = 1.0\ \Omega$
- $R_c = 350\ \Omega$, $X_m = 35\ \Omega$

**The machine operates at a slip of 0.04.**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Draw the per-phase equivalent circuit and calculate:
1. The Thevenin voltage and impedance as seen from the rotor.
2. The rotor current referred to stator.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: Using the results of (a), determine:
1. The stator input current and power factor.
2. The electromagnetic torque developed and the output power assuming rotational losses are 200 W.

---

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Step 1 — Equivalent Circuit**: The standard per-phase equivalent circuit is drawn with $R_1, X_1$ in series, then the magnetizing branch ($R_c \parallel jX_m$), then the rotor branch ($R_2/s + jX_2$).

**Step 2 — Calculate $V_{th}$**:

The Thevenin voltage is the voltage across the magnetizing branch, formed by $V_1$ acting on the voltage divider between $(R_1 + jX_1)$ and $jX_m$:

$$V_{th} = V_1 \cdot \frac{jX_m}{R_1 + j(X_1 + X_m)} = V_1 \cdot \frac{j35}{0.5 + j(1 + 35)} = V_1 \cdot \frac{j35}{0.5 + j36}$$

Computing the magnitude:

$$\left\vert \frac{j35}{0.5 + j36} \right\vert = \frac{35}{\sqrt{0.5^2 + 36^2}} = \frac{35}{36.0035} = 0.9721$$

So $V_{th} \approx 0.9721 \cdot V_1$ per phase.

**Step 3 — Calculate $Z_{th}$**:

$$Z_{th} = \frac{(R_1 + jX_1) \cdot jX_m}{R_1 + j(X_1 + X_m)} = \frac{(0.5 + j1)(j35)}{0.5 + j36}$$

Numerator: $(0.5)(j35) + (j1)(j35) = j17.5 - 35 = -35 + j17.5$

$$Z_{th} = \frac{-35 + j17.5}{0.5 + j36}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by complex conjugate of denominator:

$$Z_{th} = \frac{(-35 + j17.5)(0.5 - j36)}{0.5^2 + 36^2} = \frac{-17.5 + j1260 + j8.75 - j^2 630}{1296.25}$$

$$= \frac{-17.5 + 630 + j1268.75}{1296.25} = \frac{612.5 + j1268.75}{1296.25}$$

$$Z_{th} \approx 0.4725 + j0.9788\ \Omega$$

So $R_{th} \approx 0.4725\ \Omega$ and $X_{th} \approx 0.9788\ \Omega$.

**Step 4 — Rotor Impedance at $s = 0.04$**:

$$Z_2' = \frac{R_2}{s} + jX_2 = \frac{0.6}{0.04} + j1.0 = 15 + j1.0\ \Omega$$

**Step 5 — Total Impedance from Thevenin Side**:

$$Z_{total} = Z_{th} + Z_2' = (0.4725 + 15) + j(0.9788 + 1.0) = 15.4725 + j1.9788\ \Omega$$

$$\vert Z_{total} \vert = \sqrt{15.4725^2 + 1.9788^2} = \sqrt{239.40 + 3.92} = \sqrt{243.32} \approx 15.60\ \Omega$$

$$\angle Z_{total} = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1.9788}{15.4725}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.1279) = 7.29°$$

**Step 6 — Rotor Current (assuming $V_1 = 415$ V line, $V_{ph} = 415/\sqrt{3} = 239.6$ V)**:

$$V_{th} = 0.9721 \times 239.6 = 232.95 \text{ V per phase}$$

$$I_2' = \frac{V_{th}}{Z_{total}} = \frac{232.95 \angle 0°}{15.60 \angle 7.29°} = 14.93 \angle -7.29° \text{ A}$$

**Rotor current magnitude = 14.93 A per phase**.

---

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Step 1 — Calculate Magnetizing Current**:

The voltage across the magnetizing branch is $V_{th} = 232.95$ V.

$$I_m = \frac{V_{th}}{jX_m} = \frac{232.95}{j35} = 6.656 \angle -90° \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Core Loss Current**:

$$I_c = \frac{V_{th}}{R_c} = \frac{232.95}{350} = 0.6656 \text{ A (in phase with } V_{th}\text{)}$$

**Step 3 — Stator Current** (phasor sum of $I_2'$ and $I_m + I_c$):

$$I_{total\_mag} = I_c + I_2'(\cos\theta_2 - j\sin\theta_2)$$

Converting to rectangular:
- $I_2' = 14.93 \cos(-7.29°) - j \cdot 14.93 \sin(-7.29°) = 14.80 + j(-1.894)$
- $I_c = 0.6656 + j0$
- $I_m = 0 - j6.656$

Sum:
$$I_1 = (14.80 + 0.6656) + j(-1.894 - 6.656) = 15.466 - j8.550 \text{ A}$$

$$\vert I_1 \vert = \sqrt{15.466^2 + 8.550^2} = \sqrt{239.20 + 73.10} = \sqrt{312.30} = 17.67 \text{ A}$$

$$\angle I_1 = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-8.550}{15.466}\right) = -28.92°$$

**Step 4 — Power Factor**:

$$\cos\phi = \cos(28.92°) = 0.875 \text{ (lagging)}$$

**Step 5 — Electromagnetic Torque**:

Air-gap power:
$$P_{gap} = 3 \cdot I_2'^2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{s} = 3 \times (14.93)^2 \times \frac{0.6}{0.04} = 3 \times 222.9 \times 15 = 10031 \text{ W}$$

Synchronous angular velocity:
$$\omega_s = \frac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

Electromagnetic torque:
$$T_e = \frac{P_{gap}}{\omega_s} = \frac{10031}{157.08} = 63.86 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 6 — Output Power**:

Mechanical power developed:
$$P_m = (1-s) \cdot P_{gap} = 0.96 \times 10031 = 9629.8 \text{ W}$$

Output power (subtracting rotational losses):
$$P_{out} = P_m - P_{rot} = 9629.8 - 200 = 9429.8 \text{ W} \approx 9.43 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Answers**:
- Stator current: $I_1 = 17.67$ A at 0.875 PF lagging
- Electromagnetic torque: $T_e = 63.86$ N·m
- Output power: $P_{out} = 9.43$ kW

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Alternative]

**A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz wound-rotor induction machine is mechanically driven by a prime mover at 1050 RPM. The per-phase standstill rotor parameters are $R_2 = 0.4\ \Omega$ and $X_2 = 1.2\ \Omega$.**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]:
1. Identify the operating mode of the machine and calculate the slip.
2. Calculate the rotor copper loss and mechanical power developed if the air-gap power is 15 kW.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]:
1. Explain why the induction generator cannot start without external excitation.
2. Compare the magnetizing requirements of an induction motor and induction generator operating on the same grid.

---

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Step 1 — Synchronous Speed**:

$$N_s = \frac{120f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 2 — Operating Mode Identification**:

The rotor is driven at $N_r = 1050$ RPM, which is **greater than** $N_s = 1000$ RPM. This means:

$$N_r > N_s \implies s < 0$$

Therefore, the machine operates in **Generator Mode** (specifically, an induction generator feeding power to the grid).

**Step 3 — Slip Calculation**:

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{1000 - 1050}{1000} = -0.05$$

**Step 4 — Rotor Copper Loss**:

$$P_{cu2} = s \cdot P_{gap} = -0.05 \times 15000 = -750 \text{ W}$$

The negative sign indicates that the rotor copper loss is acting as a *source* (the rotor is essentially acting like a "negative resistor" — extracting energy from the prime mover).

**Step 5 — Mechanical Power Developed**:

$$P_m = (1 - s) \cdot P_{gap} = (1 - (-0.05)) \times 15000 = 1.05 \times 15000 = 15750 \text{ W}$$

**Verification**: $P_{cu2} + P_m = -750 + 15750 = 15000 = P_{gap}$ ✓

**Interpretation**: Of the 15.75 kW of mechanical power input (from prime mover), 15 kW is converted to electrical power at the air gap, and 0.75 kW represents the *negative* copper loss, which means the prime mover is actually recovering some of its rotational energy.

---

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Step 1 — Why Induction Generator Cannot Self-Start**:

An induction machine has no permanent magnets and no field winding on the rotor. Magnetization of the air gap requires a **magnetizing current** that must be supplied externally. When the machine is at standstill or being spun up:

- There is no residual magnetism in a freshly manufactured rotor (in most cases).
- Without flux in the air gap, no EMF is induced in the stator windings.
- No stator current can flow without an external connection.
- The machine cannot "bootstrap" itself into magnetization.

**Solution**: 
- For **grid-connected operation**: The grid acts as an infinite source of magnetizing VARs (volt-ampere reactive). The stator draws exciting current from the grid, magnetizes the air gap, and once the rotor reaches above-synchronous speed, the machine starts generating active power.
- For **standalone operation**: A **capacitor bank** must be connected across the stator terminals. The capacitors supply the leading reactive current needed for self-excitation. This is the principle of a **Self-Excited Induction Generator (SEIG)**, widely used in wind energy systems.

**Step 2 — Comparison of Magnetizing Requirements**:

| Aspect | Induction Motor | Induction Generator |
|---|---|---|
| **Magnetizing VAR source** | Grid (or supply) | Grid (if connected) or Capacitors (off-grid) |
| **Active Power** | Consumed from grid | Delivered to grid |
| **Reactive Power** | Consumed from grid | Consumed from grid (or capacitors) |
| **Power Factor** | Lagging (typically 0.7-0.9) | Lagging (typically 0.8-0.95) |
| **Net Power Flow** | Grid → Machine | Machine → Grid |
| **Self-excitation** | Not needed (grid provides) | Need capacitors if standalone |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight**: Both induction motor AND induction generator *consume* reactive power. The generator is **not** purely delivering power — it always draws magnetizing current. This is a key reason synchronous generators are used in conventional power plants; they can *control* their reactive power output (over- or under-excited), while induction generators cannot.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign of slip confusion**: Students often write $s = -0.05$ and forget to explicitly state the machine is in **generator mode**. KTU examiners specifically look for the *interpretation* of the sign.
>
> 2. **Confusing $P_{gap}$ with $P_{in}$**: Air-gap power is **not** input power. You must subtract stator copper loss and core loss to get $P_{gap}$ from $P_{in}$. A common mistake is using $P_{in} \cdot s$ instead of $P_{gap} \cdot s$ for rotor copper loss.
>
> 3. **Rotor reactance at slip**: Beginners write $X_2 = sX_{20}$ for the equivalent circuit. In the **referred** equivalent circuit, $X_2$ remains as the standstill value $X_{20}$, and slip enters only through $R_2/s$. The frequency dependency is absorbed into the resistance ratio.
>
> 4. **Forgetting $\sqrt{3}$ in 3-phase calculations**: The torque equation uses per-phase quantities with a factor of 3 (for 3 phases). The synchronous speed $\omega_s$ is in rad/s = $2\pi N_s / 60$. Missing these conversions loses 2-3 marks.
>
> 5. **Drawing equivalent circuit incorrectly**: KTU 2024 expects the *magnetizing branch* in **shunt (parallel)** with the rotor branch, not in series. This is the standard "exact" equivalent circuit.
>
> 6. **Saying "induction generator produces reactive power"**: This is **wrong**. Induction generators *consume* reactive power. Only synchronous generators with over-excitation can *produce* reactive power.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Slip Formula**: $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ — sign indicates mode.
- **Synchronous Speed**: $N_s = 120f/P$ — depends only on supply frequency and poles.
- **Rotor Frequency**: $f_r = s \cdot f$ — at standstill, $f_r = f$.
- **Rotor EMF/Reactance**: Both scale linearly with slip.
- **Three operating modes**: $0 < s < 1$ (motor), $s < 0$ (generator), $s > 1$ (braking).
- **Power split**: $P_{cu2} = sP_{gap}$ and $P_m = (1-s)P_{gap}$ — independent of mode in form.
- **Torque-Slip Curve**: $T_{max}$ independent of $R_2$; $s_{max}$ directly proportional to $R_2$.
- **Induction Generator Caveat**: Consumes reactive power — needs grid or capacitor bank.
- **Equivalent circuit rotor branch**: Uses $R_2/s$ to capture slip dependence.
- **Lab test identification**: No-load test → $s \approx 0$; Blocked-rotor test → $s = 1$.
- **Slip at full load**: Typically 2-5% for industrial motors.
- **4-pole, 50 Hz motor synchronous speed**: 1500 RPM.
- **Mechanical-to-electrical conversion in generator**: Power flow reverses; rotor still needs magnetization.
- **Maximum torque expression**: $T_{max} \propto V^2 / X$ — voltage sensitivity is critical.
- **Squirrel-cage vs wound rotor**: Cage has fixed $R_2$; wound has adjustable $R_2$ for slip control.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Speed control of 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor using V/f technique
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL507 - AC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Speed control of 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor using V/f technique

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**V/f Control (also called VVVF – Variable Voltage Variable Frequency control)** is a scalar speed-control technique for a 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor (SCIM) in which the **stator voltage-to-supply frequency ratio (V/f)** is held approximately constant so that the **air-gap flux (Φ)** remains nominally constant over the operating speed range. By varying *f* (and proportionally *V*), the synchronous speed $N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$ is changed, and hence the rotor speed $N$ is controlled from well below the rated speed up to base speed while preserving the torque-producing capability of the machine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCEEL507 – Module Exp. 2):**
> The experiment aims to *plot the torque–speed characteristics* of a 3-phase SCIM at different V/f settings using a PWM/IGBT-based inverter drive, and to verify the *constant-torque region* of operation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are riding a bicycle on a flat road, and you want to climb a gentle slope:

- If you **shift to a lower gear (lower speed)**, the pedals become easier to push, but you must **push harder (more "voltage effort")** to keep the same muscle power.
- If you **shift to a higher gear (higher speed)**, the pedals feel lighter, so you naturally **reduce the push (less effort)**.

The induction motor behaves similarly:

- **Frequency `f`** → like the **gear ratio** (sets the synchronous speed $N_s$).
- **Voltage `V`** → like the **leg effort** (sets the push, i.e., the air-gap flux).
- If `f` is reduced (low gear) without raising `V` (low effort), the motor becomes weak — it cannot produce rated torque.

Hence, we **reduce `f` and `V` together in the same proportion** to keep the "muscle power" (torque) of the motor intact. This is the heart of **V/f control**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Supply frequency (base) | $f_{base}$ | **50 Hz** (India) |
| Rated line voltage | $V_L$ | **415 V (rms)** |
| Number of poles | $P$ | **4 (typical lab motor)** |
| Rated synchronous speed | $N_s$ | **1500 rpm** |
| Rated slip | $s$ | **0.03 – 0.05** |
| Rated rotor speed | $N_r$ | **≈ 1440 rpm** |
| V/f ratio (constant region) | $V/f$ | **≈ 8.3 V/Hz** (415/50) |

> [!NOTE]
> A V/f drive is sometimes called an **"open-loop, scalar VVVF drive"** because no encoder/feedback of rotor speed is needed; speed is set by *f* and *V* references generated by the controller.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why is V/f Held Constant? – The Core "Why"

The per-phase equivalent circuit of an induction motor referred to the stator gives the **air-gap EMF**:

$$E_g \;\approx\; 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, k_w \, \Phi$$

Rearranging for flux:

$$\Phi \;=\; \frac{E_g}{4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, k_w} \;\propto\; \frac{V}{f}$$

The **electromagnetic torque** developed by the motor is:

$$T_e \;=\; \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_g^{\,2} \cdot (R_2^{\prime}/s)}{(R_2^{\prime}/s)^2 + (X_2^{\prime})^2}$$

Three operating regions emerge depending on what is held constant:

| Region | Frequency Range | V/f Behaviour | Flux Φ | Torque Capability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Low-speed / Boost** | $0 < f < f_{low}$ | $V/f$ ratio increased | Slightly boosted | Compensates $IR$ drop |
| **Constant-torque** | $f_{low} \le f \le f_{base}$ | $V/f$ = constant | Constant Φ | Rated torque available |
| **Field-weakening** | $f > f_{base}$ | V clamped to rated | Φ decreases ($1/f$) | Torque falls ∝ $1/f$ |

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — every KTU numerical in this module is built from this set.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_s \;=\; \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed | rpm |
| 2 | $s \;=\; \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Slip (per-unit) | — |
| 3 | $N_r \;=\; N_s \,(1 - s)$ | Rotor speed | rpm |
| 4 | $\Phi \;\propto\; \dfrac{V}{f}$ | Air-gap flux | Wb |
| 5 | $E_g \;\approx\; 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, k_w \, \Phi$ | Air-gap EMF / phase | V |
| 6 | $T_e \;\approx\; \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_g^{\,2} \cdot (R_2^{\prime}/s)}{(R_2^{\prime}/s)^2 + (X_2^{\prime})^2}$ | Electromagnetic torque | N·m |
| 7 | $\omega_s \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi f}{P/2} \;=\; \dfrac{4\pi f}{P}$ | Synchronous angular speed | rad/s |
| 8 | $\dfrac{V}{f} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{rated}}{f_{rated}}$ | Constant V/f law (linear region) | V/Hz |
| 9 | $P_{gap} \;=\; T_e \cdot \omega_s$ | Air-gap power | W |
| 10 | $P_{m} \;=\; (1 - s) \, P_{gap}$ | Mechanical power developed | W |

> **Important rule for using `|` in formulas:** In LaTeX renderings, use `$\vert$` or `$\mid$` instead of the keyboard pipe `|` to avoid markdown-table breakage. Example: $\vert V \vert$ for magnitude.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Industrial fans, pumps, blowers, HVAC compressors** – energy savings of 20–50 % by reducing speed.
- **Conveyor belts, mixers, extruders** – soft start + adjustable throughput.
- **Elevators & cranes** – smooth low-speed high-torque operation with boost.
- **Electric vehicle traction (auxiliary)** – though modern EVs use vector control, V/f is the basic building block.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU lab, the V/f drive is typically a **3-phase IGBT inverter with sinusoidal PWM (SPWM) or space-vector PWM (SVPWM)** fronted by a diode-bridge rectifier and DC-link capacitor.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Air-Gap Flux ∝ V/f

**Step 1 (KVL on stator side).** Neglecting the small stator resistance drop at rated frequency, the per-phase applied voltage balances the stator leakage reactance drop and the air-gap EMF:

$$V_{ph} \;\approx\; E_g \;=\; 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, k_w \, \Phi$$

**Step 2 (Solve for flux).** Treating $N_{ph}$, $k_w$ as constant machine parameters:

$$\Phi \;=\; \frac{V_{ph}}{4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, k_w}$$

**Step 3 (Proportionality statement).** Hence:

$$\Phi \;\propto\; \frac{V}{f}$$

> **Engineering meaning:** To keep Φ (and therefore the torque capability) constant, the ratio $V/f$ must be maintained constant.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Synchronous Speed Control by Frequency

**Step 1.** Synchronous angular speed:

$$\omega_s \;=\; \frac{2\pi N_s}{60} \;=\; \frac{2\pi f}{(P/2)} \;=\; \frac{4\pi f}{P}$$

**Step 2.** Rotor mechanical speed:

$$N_r \;=\; \frac{120 \, f}{P} \, (1 - s)$$

**Step 3.** Change in rotor speed for a small change in frequency (slip held at $s_0$ for light load):

$$\Delta N_r \;=\; \frac{120 \, (1 - s_0)}{P} \, \Delta f$$

This is **linear** in the constant-torque region — a key experimental observation.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Why Voltage "Boost" is Needed at Low Frequency

**Step 1.** At low frequency, $X_{1} = 2\pi f L_{1}$ becomes very small, so the **stator resistance drop $I_s R_1$** can no longer be neglected:

$$V_{ph} \;=\; I_s R_1 \;+\; j I_s X_1 \;+\; E_g$$

**Step 2.** Solve for $E_g$:

$$E_g \;=\; V_{ph} \;-\; I_s R_1 \;-\; j I_s X_1$$

**Step 3.** To keep $E_g \propto f$ at low $f$, the controller adds a **fixed boost voltage $V_{boost}$** so that:

$$V_{ph}^{cmd} \;=\; \left(\frac{V_{rated}}{f_{rated}}\right) f \;+\; V_{boost}$$

Typical boost: **5–10 % of rated voltage** for a 50 Hz machine, applied below ~5 Hz.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Torque–Speed Curve at a Given V/f Setting

**Step 1.** Replace $E_g$ by $(V/f) \cdot f \cdot k_E$, then substitute in the torque expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_e(f,\,s) &\;=\; \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{\left( \dfrac{V}{f} f \right)^{2} \cdot \dfrac{R_2^{\prime}}{s}}{\left( \dfrac{R_2^{\prime}}{s} \right)^{2} + \left( 2\pi f L_2^{\prime} \right)^{2}} \\[6pt]
&\;=\; \frac{3 \, P}{4\pi f} \cdot \frac{(k_V)^{2} f^{2} \cdot \dfrac{R_2^{\prime}}{s}}{\left( \dfrac{R_2^{\prime}}{s} \right)^{2} + \left( 2\pi f L_2^{\prime} \right)^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.** Identify the **maximum (pull-out) torque**:

$$T_{max} \;=\; \frac{3 \, P \, (V/f)^{2}}{4\pi \cdot 2 \, L_2^{\prime}} \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi} \;=\; \frac{3}{4\pi^{2} L_2^{\prime}/R_2^{\prime}} \cdot \left(\frac{V}{f}\right)^{2}$$

The key conclusion: **$T_{max} \propto (V/f)^{2}$**. Therefore, holding $V/f$ constant preserves pull-out torque across the linear region.

**Step 3.** Slip at maximum torque:

$$s_{max} \;=\; \frac{R_2^{\prime}}{2\pi f_s \, L_2^{\prime}} \;\propto\; \frac{R_2^{\prime}}{f_s}$$

As $f$ is reduced, $s_{max}$ increases — useful for starting heavy loads.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical (KTU-style)

**Given:** 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz, 415 V SCIM. V/f drive sets $V/f = 8.3$ V/Hz. Motor runs at slip $s = 0.04$ when $f = 30$ Hz.

**Find:** (a) Synchronous speed, (b) Rotor speed, (c) Per-phase voltage applied.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_s &\;=\; \frac{120 \times 30}{4} \;=\; 900 \; \text{rpm} \\[4pt]
N_r &\;=\; N_s (1 - s) \;=\; 900 \times 0.96 \;=\; 864 \; \text{rpm} \\[4pt]
V_{ph} &\;=\; \left(\frac{V/f}{}\right) \cdot f \;=\; 8.3 \times 30 \;=\; 249 \; \text{V/phase} \\
&\;\approx\; 0.6 \times 415 \; \text{V (line-to-line)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** A common numerical asks — *"If the V/f ratio is kept constant at 8.3 V/Hz and frequency is reduced from 50 Hz to 25 Hz, what happens to torque capability?"* Answer: **It remains the same** in the constant-torque region (ignoring $IR$ drop).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete V/f Drive System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  ACMAINS[3 PHASE AC MAINS 50 HZ 415 V] --> RECT[3 PHASE DIODE RECTIFIER]
  RECT --> DCLINK[DC LINK CAPACITOR SMOOTHING]
  DCLINK --> INVERTER[3 PHASE IGBT INVERTER PWM]
  CTRL[V F CONTROLLER MICROCONTROLLER DSP] --> GATEDRV[GATE DRIVE CIRCUIT]
  GATEDRV --> INVERTER
  INVERTER --> MOTOR[3 PHASE SQUIRREL CAGE INDUCTION MOTOR]
  MOTOR --> LOAD[MECHANICAL LOAD DYNAMOMETER]
  CTRL --> VFREF[V F REFERENCE TABLE]
  POT[SPEED POTENTIOMETER 0 TO 10 V] --> CTRL
  CTRL --> DISPLAY[LCD DISPLAY FREQUENCY HZ SPEED RPM]
```

**Explanation of blocks:**
- **AC MAINS → RECTIFIER → DC LINK**: Convert fixed 50 Hz AC to a stiff DC bus (~565 V DC for 415 V line).
- **INVERTER**: Six IGBT switches generate variable-V, variable-f PWM waveforms.
- **V F CONTROLLER**: Holds V/f ratio constant (8.3 V/Hz linear) + low-frequency boost.
- **LOAD**: A **eddy-current / DC dynamometer** acts as controllable mechanical load to measure torque at each speed point.

---

### 4.2 V/f Characteristic Profile (3 Regions)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  subgraph REG1[LOW FREQUENCY REGION 0 TO 5 HZ]
    R1[V BOOST ADDED TO COMPENSATE STATOR IR DROP]
  end
  subgraph REG2[CONSTANT TORQUE REGION 5 TO 50 HZ]
    R2[LINEAR V F SLOPE EQUAL TO 8.3 V PER HZ]
  end
  subgraph REG3[FIELD WEAKENING REGION ABOVE 50 HZ]
    R3[V CLAMPED TO RATED FLUX FALLS AS 1 OVER F]
  end
  REG1 --> REG2
  REG2 --> REG3
```

---

### 4.3 Experimental Flowchart — Lab Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[START POWER ON RECTIFIER] --> B[CHARGE DC LINK CHECK 565 V DC]
  B --> C[SET V F DRIVE TO MIN FREQUENCY 5 HZ]
  C --> D[SET V F RATIO TO 8.3 V PER HZ]
  D --> E[APPLY DYNAMOMETER LOAD STEP BY STEP]
  E --> F[MEASURE SPEED N R TORQUE T USING LOAD CELL]
  F --> G[RECORD V LINE F SLIP SPEED TORQUE POWER]
  G --> H[INCREMENT FREQUENCY 5 HZ REPEAT]
  H --> I[PLOT TORQUE VS SPEED FOR EACH F]
  I --> J[VERIFY CONSTANT TORQUE REGION]
  J --> K[SWITCH OFF LOAD THEN DRIVE POWER OFF]
```

---

### 4.4 Expected Torque–Speed Family of Curves

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  subgraph S1[F EQUALS 50 HZ BASE SPEED 1500 RPM]
    S1A[STARTING TORQUE 1.5 PU]
    S1B[PULL OUT TORQUE 2.5 PU]
    S1C[LINEAR SLOPE]
  end
  subgraph S2[F EQUALS 30 HZ 900 RPM]
    S2A[STARTING TORQUE 1.5 PU]
    S2B[PULL OUT TORQUE 2.5 PU]
    S2C[LINEAR SLOPE]
  end
  subgraph S3[F EQUALS 15 HZ 450 RPM]
    S3A[STARTING TORQUE 1.5 PU]
    S3B[PULL OUT TORQUE 2.5 PU]
    S3C[LINEAR SLOPE]
  end
  S1 --> S2
  S2 --> S3
```

> **Interpretation:** When V/f is held constant, the *shape* of the torque–speed curve (peak torque and starting torque magnitudes) is **preserved**; only the *frequency of occurrence* (i.e., the speed at which the peak occurs) shifts to the left as $f$ is reduced. This is the experimental signature of correct V/f control.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define V/f control of a 3-phase induction motor. Why is it necessary to vary voltage along with frequency?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
V/f control is a scalar speed-control method in which the **ratio of stator voltage to supply frequency is held constant** so that the air-gap flux remains constant. (1 mark)
The synchronous speed $N_s = 120 f / P$ depends only on $f$; hence varying $f$ controls rotor speed. (1 mark)
Voltage must be varied proportionally to keep flux $\Phi \propto V/f$ constant; otherwise, at low $f$ the flux would saturate the core (if $V$ unchanged) or drop too low (if $V$ reduced disproportionately), destroying the torque-producing capability. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is the purpose of voltage "boost" in a V/f drive at low frequencies?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
At low frequencies, the stator leakage reactance $X_1 = 2\pi f L_1$ becomes very small, and the **stator copper drop $I_s R_1$** is no longer negligible. (1 mark)
Hence the air-gap EMF $E_g$ becomes much less than the applied voltage, reducing flux and torque. (1 mark)
A small additional "boost" voltage (typically 5–10 % of rated) is injected below ~5 Hz so that the effective V/f ratio is slightly higher, restoring rated flux and torque. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2023]*

**(a)** Derive the relationship between air-gap flux, voltage, and frequency for a 3-phase induction motor. Show that the V/f ratio must be kept constant to maintain constant flux. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz, 415 V squirrel-cage induction motor is controlled by a V/f drive with V/f = 8 V/Hz. The drive is set to 25 Hz and the motor runs at a slip of 0.05. Calculate: (i) synchronous speed, (ii) rotor speed, (iii) per-phase voltage, (iv) slip in rpm. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

The per-phase EMF induced in the stator (which equals the air-gap EMF $E_g$ neglecting small drops) is:

$$E_g \;=\; 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, k_w \, \Phi \quad \text{...(i)}$$

(1 mark — for writing the EMF equation correctly)

The applied per-phase stator voltage, ignoring stator impedance, is $V_{ph} \approx E_g$. Rearranging (i):

$$\Phi \;=\; \frac{V_{ph}}{4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, k_w} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

(1 mark)

Since $N_{ph}$ and $k_w$ are constants of the machine, we get:

$$\Phi \;\propto\; \frac{V}{f}$$

(1 mark — for proportionality statement)

To keep $\Phi$ constant, the ratio $V/f$ must remain constant at the rated value:

$$\frac{V}{f} \;=\; \frac{V_{rated}}{f_{rated}} \;=\; \frac{415/\sqrt{3}}{50} \;\approx\; 4.79 \;\text{V/Hz per phase}$$

(2 marks — for stating the condition explicitly with the numerical ratio)

**Conclusion:** Constant V/f ⇒ constant air-gap flux ⇒ constant torque capability across the speed range. (2 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Given:** $P = 4$, $V_L = 415$ V, $f_{drive} = 25$ Hz, $V/f = 8$ V/Hz, $s = 0.05$.

**(i) Synchronous speed:**

$$
N_s \;=\; \frac{120 \times 25}{4} \;=\; 750 \; \text{rpm}
$$

(2 marks)

**(ii) Rotor speed:**

$$
N_r \;=\; N_s (1 - s) \;=\; 750 \times (1 - 0.05) \;=\; 750 \times 0.95 \;=\; 712.5 \; \text{rpm}
$$

(2 marks)

**(iii) Per-phase voltage:**

The 8 V/Hz ratio is conventionally referenced to the line-to-line value for simplicity in drives; if per-phase is required, divide by $\sqrt{3}$:

$$
V_{ph} \;=\; 8 \times 25 \;=\; 200 \; \text{V (line-to-line, if drive uses $V_L/f$)}
$$

$$
V_{ph,\;true} \;=\; \frac{200}{\sqrt{3}} \;\approx\; 115.5 \; \text{V/phase}
$$

(2 marks)

**(iv) Slip in rpm:**

$$
N_{slip} \;=\; N_s \, s \;=\; 750 \times 0.05 \;=\; 37.5 \; \text{rpm}
$$

(1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a V/f-controlled PWM inverter drive for a 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the torque–speed characteristics of a SCIM at three different supply frequencies (say 50 Hz, 30 Hz, 15 Hz) under constant V/f operation and explain the shape of the curves. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Block diagram (refer Section 4.1 above).** (1 mark)

**Working — step by step:**

1. **Rectifier stage:** The 3-phase 50 Hz, 415 V mains is converted to DC by an uncontrolled 6-pulse diode bridge, producing approximately 565 V DC across the DC-link capacitor. (1 mark)
2. **DC-link filter:** A large electrolytic capacitor smooths the rectified DC to a near-constant value. (½ mark)
3. **Inverter stage:** Six IGBTs arranged in three legs switch at a high frequency (typically 2–20 kHz) using **sinusoidal PWM (SPWM) or SVPWM**. The low-frequency fundamental component of the PWM waveform has a magnitude proportional to the **modulation index $m_a$** and a frequency equal to the **desired output frequency $f^*$**. (1 mark)
4. **Controller:** A microcontroller/DSP generates the reference sine waves whose amplitude is set by $V^* = (V/f) \cdot f^*$ and whose frequency is $f^*$. The speed-setting potentiometer feeds $f^*$ to the controller. (1 mark)
5. **Gate drivers:** Optically isolated gate-drive circuits amplify the low-power PWM logic signals to ±15 V levels required by the IGBT gates. (½ mark)
6. **Output filter (optional):** A small LC filter removes high-frequency PWM ripple, giving nearly sinusoidal voltage to the motor. (½ mark)
7. **Voltage boost:** Below ~5 Hz, the controller adds a fixed offset voltage to compensate stator $IR$ drop. (½ mark)
8. **Display & protection:** The drive shows frequency, speed, and current; protections include over-current, over-voltage, and ground-fault trips. (1 mark — for safety/protection aspects)

**Total: 7 marks distributed across functional blocks.**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Graph (refer Section 4.4 above for the qualitative shape).** (1 mark)

**Explanation:**

- For each frequency, with V/f held constant, the **maximum (pull-out) torque** is preserved because $T_{max} \propto (V/f)^2$, and $V/f$ is the same for all three curves. (2 marks)
- The **starting torque** at $s = 1$ is also nearly the same across the family because the rotor resistance referred to stator dominates at standstill and is unaffected by frequency (ignoring skin effect). (1 mark)
- The **slip at maximum torque** $s_{max} = R_2^{\prime} / (2\pi f L_2^{\prime})$ is inversely proportional to $f$. Therefore, as $f$ is reduced (50 → 30 → 15 Hz), the speed at which pull-out occurs shifts **left** (towards lower rpm). (1 mark)
- The **stable operating region** (small positive slope, $0 < s < s_{max}$) preserves its shape; only the *position* along the speed axis moves. (1 mark)
- This is the **experimental verification** that constant V/f gives a **constant-torque drive** up to base speed, and that the motor's torque envelope is essentially "shifted" along the speed axis. (1 mark)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing $V_{ph}$ and $V_L$:** Drives often specify V/f using the *line-to-line* rating. Students write $V_{ph} = 415$ V when they should write $V_{ph} = 415/\sqrt{3}$. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Forgetting voltage boost at low frequency:** When asked "why is the V/f curve NOT a straight line all the way to the origin?", examiners expect an answer about stator $IR$ drop and the need for voltage boost below ~5 Hz. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 3. **Stating $T_{max} \propto V^2$ instead of $T_{max} \propto (V/f)^2$:** The frequency dependence is the crux; missing it loses the conceptual core. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 4. **Plotting torque-speed curves with crossing points:** Under V/f control, the **pull-out torque stays constant**; students sometimes wrongly show it increasing at low frequency. The maximum torque is the same; only its location on the speed axis shifts.
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical answers:** Always write rpm, N·m, V/Hz — not just the number.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **V/f = constant** ⇒ air-gap flux Φ constant ⇒ torque capability preserved.
- [x] **Synchronous speed** $N_s = 120 f / P$ is set by frequency alone.
- [x] **Rotor speed** $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$.
- [x] **Pull-out torque** $T_{max} \propto (V/f)^2$ — independent of $f$ when V/f is constant.
- [x] **Slip at max torque** $s_{max} \propto 1/f$ — moves left as $f$ drops.
- [x] **Voltage boost** is required below ~5 Hz to compensate stator $IR$ drop.
- [x] **Above base frequency** ($f > 50$ Hz), voltage is clamped to rated; flux weakens as $1/f$ → constant-power region.
- [x] **Hardware chain:** Mains → Diode rectifier → DC link → IGBT inverter (SPWM/SVPWM) → SCIM.
- [x] **Lab verification:** Plot T–N curves at multiple $f$ values; confirm constant $T_{max}$ envelope.
- [x] **Advantages:** Simple, sensorless, low cost, good for fans/pumps.
- [x] **Disadvantages:** Open-loop, no precise speed/torque control, no field orientation.
- [x] **Speed regulation is poor** at low frequencies unless slip-compensation (a small f-boost proportional to load) is added.
- [x] **KTU keywords to memorise:** *Scalar control, VVVF, constant V/f, IGBT inverter, SPWM, boost voltage, field-weakening region, pull-out torque.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voltage regulation of 3-phase synchronous generator by EMF and MMF method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL507 - AC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Voltage regulation of 3-phase synchronous generator by EMF and MMF method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Voltage Regulation

**Voltage Regulation (VR)** of a synchronous generator is defined as the percentage rise in terminal voltage when the load is removed from the generator, operating at constant speed and constant field excitation, divided by the rated terminal voltage.

$$VR \,\% \,=\, \dfrac{E_0 \,-\, V}{V} \,\times\, 100 \,\%$$

where $E_0$ is the no-load induced EMF per phase and $V$ is the full-load rated terminal voltage per phase. A **positive** regulation indicates voltage rises when load is thrown off; a **negative** regulation (common in leading-PF loads) indicates voltage actually drops when load is removed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 syllabus, VR is a **performance index** — it tells the operator how stable the bus voltage remains as the load varies. Industrial generators are usually designed for VR < **5 %** at unity PF and rated load.

### 1.2 Why Two Indirect Methods?

In an actual lab, performing a sudden load rejection on a running 1500 kVA machine is **unsafe** (arc flash, mechanical stress, severe voltage transient). Hence KTU-approved lab practice uses indirect computation based on **Open-Circuit (OC)** and **Short-Circuit (SC)** test data.

Two classical indirect methods dominate:

1. **EMF Method (M.G. Say / Synchronous Impedance Method)** — replaces armature reaction with a fictitious reactance.
2. **MMF Method (Ampere-Turn / Rothert's Method)** — replaces armature reaction with an equivalent field MMF.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden Hose" Model

Imagine a water pump (generator) supplying a sprinkler (load):

- **EMF method** treats the sprinkler nozzle blockage as an *equivalent extra pipe-length* (reactance) added to the suction side. You compute the new pressure at the pump.
- **MMF method** treats the blockage as a *reduction in pump thrust* (equivalent ampere-turns lost). You compute how hard the pump must push to deliver the same flow.

Both predict the same output, but the EMF method over-estimates pressure drops (**pessimistic**), while the MMF method under-estimates them (**optimistic**). The true regulation lies in between — and KTU lab viva questions frequently test this understanding.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Standard Procedure** calls for performing both methods on the same machine and tabulating results against the actual value obtained from the **direct load test** (sudden load rejection or progressive loading).

### 1.4 Standard Definitions

| Term | Symbol | Units | Definition |
|------|--------|-------|------------|
| Synchronous Impedance | $Z_s$ | Ω/phase | $\sqrt{R_a^2 + X_s^2}$ |
| Synchronous Reactance | $X_s$ | Ω/phase | Reactance corresponding to combined leakage & AR effects |
| Armature Resistance | $R_a$ | Ω/phase | DC resistance of one phase winding |
| Open-Circuit EMF | $E_0$ | V/phase | Induced EMF at given field current, no load |
| Short-Circuit Current | $I_{sc}$ | A | Armature current with terminals shorted at given field current |
| Potier Reactance | $X_p$ | Ω/phase | Pure leakage reactance (from zero-PF test) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 EMF Method — Theoretical Foundation

The EMF method is based on two fundamental tests:

**(a) Open-Circuit Test (OCC):**
The generator is driven at rated synchronous speed with the armature terminals open. Field current $I_f$ is varied in steps, and the corresponding line-to-line open-circuit EMF $E_{0,\text{line}}$ is recorded. The OCC is the magnetization curve, **non-linear** due to iron saturation.

**(b) Short-Circuit Test (SCC):**
The armature terminals are short-circuited through ammeters. Field current is varied in steps and the corresponding armature (short-circuit) current $I_{sc}$ is recorded. The SCC is essentially a straight line (air-gap line behaviour) because the armature reaction is strongly demagnetizing and keeps the iron unsaturated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Assumption:** The EMF method assumes that the armature reaction MMF is equivalent to a voltage drop across a fictitious reactance $X_s$ (called synchronous reactance). The actual machine is thereby replaced by a *simple series circuit* of $R_a$ and $X_s$.

**Derivation of Synchronous Impedance:**
At a chosen field current $I_{f1}$, from OCC we read the open-circuit EMF $E_0$. From SCC at the same field current $I_{f1}$, we read the short-circuit current $I_{sc}$. The synchronous impedance is:

$$Z_s \,=\, \dfrac{E_{0,\text{phase}}}{I_{sc,\text{phase}}}$$

Since $Z_s = \sqrt{R_a^2 + X_s^2}$, and $R_a$ is measured by a DC test or voltmeter-ammeter method,

$$X_s \,=\, \sqrt{Z_s^2 \,-\, R_a^2}$$

**Phasor Equation (per phase, lagging PF):**

$$E_0 \,=\, \vec{V} \,+\, \vec{I_a}\,(R_a + jX_s)$$

**Magnitude form:**

$$E_0 \,=\, \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 \,+\, (V\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}$$

For **leading** PF, the sign of the reactive drop term is negative:

$$E_0 \,=\, \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 \,+\, (V\sin\phi - I_a X_s)^2}$$

### 2.2 MMF Method — Theoretical Foundation

The MMF method replaces the armature reaction by an *equivalent field current* rather than a reactance drop.

**(a) From OCC at rated phase voltage $V$**, read the field current required to generate $V$ on open circuit. Denote this as $O_b$.

**(b) From SCC at full-load armature current**, read the corresponding field current. Denote this as $O_a$. (This is the field current needed to circulate full-load current on short circuit — it is essentially the field MMF that just cancels the armature reaction MMF.)

**Vector Construction:**

- Take $\vec{V}$ as the reference phasor along the horizontal.
- Draw $\vec{O_b}$ along the reference axis at the value found from OCC at voltage $V$ (perpendicular to $\vec{V}$ if measuring in MMF space).
- Draw $\vec{O_a}$ along the direction of armature current $\vec{I_a}$ (which lags or leads $\vec{V}$ by angle $\phi$).
- Total field current at load: $\vec{O_c} = \vec{O_b} + \vec{O_a}$ (vector addition).
- From OCC, read the EMF $E_0$ corresponding to field current $O_c$.

The MMF method uses **actual saturation** of the iron from the OCC; this is why it is more accurate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MMF method is "optimistic":** It uses the OCC (saturated curve) for the resultant excitation, so the iron's saturation reduces the predicted $E_0$ and hence the regulation. The EMF method, by contrast, uses a *constant* $X_s$ derived from unsaturated air-gap conditions, over-estimating the voltage rise.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | EMF Method (Phasor form) | MMF Method (Vector form) |
|----------|--------------------------|---------------------------|
| Synchronous Impedance | $Z_s = E_0 \text{ (OCC)} / I_{sc} \text{ (SCC)}$ at same $I_f$ | Not used directly |
| Synchronous Reactance | $X_s = \sqrt{Z_s^2 - R_a^2}$ | Not used |
| Total field current | Not applicable | $\vec{O_c} = \vec{O_b} + \vec{O_a}$ |
| $E_0$ calculation (lagging PF) | $\sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}$ | Read from OCC at $O_c$ |
| $E_0$ calculation (leading PF) | $\sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi - I_a X_s)^2}$ | Read from OCC at $O_c$ |
| Voltage Regulation | $VR \,\% = (E_0 - V)/V \times 100$ | Same formula |
| $O_b$ field current | — | OCC value at rated phase voltage $V$ |
| $O_a$ field current | — | SCC value at full-load $I_a$ |
| Accuracy trend | Pessimistic (over-estimates VR) | Optimistic (under-estimates VR) |
| Industry use | Rough initial design estimate | More accurate lab estimate |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **EMF method** is used in **design offices** during the preliminary sizing of alternators where SC/OC tests cannot be performed yet.
- **MMF method** is preferred in **commissioning tests** of large alternators in thermal/hydro power stations.
- **KTU lab experiments** mandate comparison of both against the *direct load test* regulation to compute the **percentage error** of each method.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the EMF Method Equation

**Starting principle (per phase, lagging PF):**

$$\vec{E_0} \,=\, \vec{V} \,+\, \vec{I_a}\,(R_a + jX_s)$$

**Step 1 — Resolve $\vec{V}$ into rectangular components** along the real ($V\cos\phi$) and imaginary ($V\sin\phi$) axes, taking $\vec{V}$ along the positive real axis:

$$\vec{V} \,=\, V\cos\phi \,+\, jV\sin\phi$$

**Step 2 — Resolve $\vec{I_a}$ into components**, with $\vec{I_a}$ lagging $\vec{V}$ by angle $\phi$:

$$\vec{I_a} \,=\, I_a\cos\phi \,-\, jI_a\sin\phi$$

**Step 3 — Multiply $\vec{I_a}$ by $(R_a + jX_s)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{I_a}(R_a + jX_s) &= (I_a\cos\phi - jI_a\sin\phi)(R_a + jX_s) \\
&= I_aR_a\cos\phi + jI_aX_s\cos\phi - jI_aR_a\sin\phi + I_aX_s\sin\phi \\
&= (I_aR_a\cos\phi + I_aX_s\sin\phi) \,+\, j(I_aX_s\cos\phi - I_aR_a\sin\phi)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Add $\vec{V}$ and the drop:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{E_0} &= [V\cos\phi + I_aR_a\cos\phi + I_aX_s\sin\phi] \,+\, j[V\sin\phi + I_aX_s\cos\phi - I_aR_a\sin\phi] \\
&= (V\cos\phi + I_aR_a)\cos\phi \text{ ... rearranging}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Magnitude (since $R_a \ll X_s$, the $I_aR_a\sin\phi$ term is usually negligible, yielding the standard textbook form):**

$$E_0 \,=\, \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_aR_a)^2 \,+\, (V\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU lab manual uses the simplified version above. For rigorous derivation, retain the cross-term $I_aX_s\sin\phi$ in the real part — it is small but not zero.

### 3.2 Worked Example — EMF Method (Lag and Lead)

**Given Machine Data (KTU Standard Problem):**
- 3-phase, star-connected synchronous generator
- Rating: **500 kVA, 3.3 kV, 50 Hz**
- Armature resistance $R_a = 0.3 \,\Omega$/phase
- Synchronous reactance $X_s = 5.0 \,\Omega$/phase
- Load: rated full-load at (i) **0.8 PF lagging** and (ii) **0.8 PF leading**

**Step 1 — Compute rated phase voltage:**

$$V_{\text{phase}} \,=\, \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} \,=\, \dfrac{3300}{\sqrt{3}} \,=\, 1905.0 \,\text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute rated phase (and line) current:**

$$I_a \,=\, \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L} \,=\, \dfrac{500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 3300} \,=\, 87.47 \,\text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\cos\phi$ and $\sin\phi$ at 0.8 PF:**

$$\cos\phi \,=\, 0.8, \qquad \sin\phi \,=\, 0.6$$

**Case (i) — Lagging PF (machine supplying lagging current to inductive load):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V\cos\phi &= 1905.0 \times 0.8 = 1524.0 \,\text{V} \\
V\sin\phi &= 1905.0 \times 0.6 = 1143.0 \,\text{V} \\
I_aR_a &= 87.47 \times 0.3 = 26.24 \,\text{V} \\
I_aX_s &= 87.47 \times 5.0 = 437.35 \,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_0 &= \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_aR_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi + I_aX_s)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{(1524.0 + 26.24)^2 + (1143.0 + 437.35)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{(1550.24)^2 + (1580.35)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{2{,}403{,}244 + 2{,}497{,}506} \\
&= \sqrt{4{,}900{,}750} \\
&= 2213.8 \,\text{V/phase}
\end{aligned}
$$

Line value:

$$E_{0,\text{line}} \,=\, \sqrt{3} \times 2213.8 \,=\, 3834.5 \,\text{V}$$

**Regulation (lagging):**

$$VR_{\text{lag}} \,\% \,=\, \dfrac{3834.5 - 3300}{3300} \times 100 \,=\, \boxed{16.20 \,\%}$$

**Case (ii) — Leading PF (machine supplying capacitive load or synchronous motor load):**

The reactive drop term changes sign:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_0 &= \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_aR_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi - I_aX_s)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{(1550.24)^2 + (1143.0 - 437.35)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{(1550.24)^2 + (705.65)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{2{,}403{,}244 + 497{,}942} \\
&= \sqrt{2{,}901{,}186} \\
&= 1703.3 \,\text{V/phase}
\end{aligned}
$$

Line value:

$$E_{0,\text{line}} \,=\, \sqrt{3} \times 1703.3 \,=\, 2950.3 \,\text{V}$$

**Regulation (leading):**

$$VR_{\text{lead}} \,\% \,=\, \dfrac{2950.3 - 3300}{3300} \times 100 \,=\, \boxed{-10.60 \,\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **negative** regulation at leading PF is a real physical phenomenon — the armature reaction is *magnetizing* and reinforces the main field, so terminal voltage is *higher* at full load than at no-load.

### 3.3 Worked Example — MMF Method

**Given OCC and SCC data for the same 500 kVA / 3.3 kV machine:**

| Field Current $I_f$ (A) | 10 | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 | 60 | 70 |
|--------------------------|----|----|----|----|----|----|----|
| OCC $E_{0,\text{line}}$ (V) | 1100 | 2000 | 2700 | 3300 | 3650 | 3920 | 4120 |
| SCC $I_{sc,\text{line}}$ (A) | 25 | 50 | 75 | 100 | 125 | 150 | 175 |

**Step 1 — Read $O_b$ from OCC at rated phase voltage $V = 1905$ V (i.e., line voltage 3300 V):**

$$O_b \,\approx\, 40 \,\text{A} \quad (\text{since } 3300 \,\text{V line} \rightarrow 40 \,\text{A})$$

**Step 2 — Read $O_a$ from SCC at rated armature current $I_a = 87.47$ A:**

Interpolating between 75 A and 100 A:

$$O_a \,\approx\, 20 \,+\, (87.47 - 50) \times \dfrac{30 - 20}{75 - 50} \times \dfrac{1}{1} \,=\, 30.0 \,\text{A (approximately)}$$

(SCC is linear: $I_{sc} \approx 2.5 I_f$, so $O_a \approx 87.47/2.5 = 35.0$ A. Let us use $O_a = 35$ A.)

**Step 3 — Vector construction at 0.8 PF lagging:**

- Place $\vec{V}$ along reference axis (length represents $O_b = 40$ A in MMF units).
- $\vec{I_a}$ lags $\vec{V}$ by $\phi = 36.87°$.
- $\vec{O_a}$ (in phase with $\vec{I_a}$) is drawn at angle $-36.87°$ from reference.

**Step 4 — Compute $O_c$ by vector addition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_{c,\text{real}} &= O_b + O_a \cos\phi = 40 + 35 \times 0.8 = 40 + 28 = 68 \,\text{A} \\
O_{c,\text{imag}} &= O_a \sin\phi = 35 \times 0.6 = 21 \,\text{A} \\
O_c &= \sqrt{68^2 + 21^2} = \sqrt{4624 + 441} = \sqrt{5065} = 71.17 \,\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Read $E_0$ from OCC at $O_c = 71.17$ A:**

Interpolating between 70 A ($E_0 = 4120$ V line) — extrapolation gives:

$$E_{0,\text{line}} \approx 4120 + \dfrac{(71.17 - 70)}{(80 - 70)} \times (4250 - 4120) \approx 4120 + 15 \approx 4135 \,\text{V}$$

**Regulation (MMF method, lagging 0.8 PF):**

$$VR_{MMF} \,\% \,=\, \dfrac{4135 - 3300}{3300} \times 100 \,\approx\, \boxed{25.3 \,\%}$$

Hmm — this is **higher** than the EMF method's 16.2 %. That contradicts our earlier statement that MMF is optimistic. Let us re-examine:

> [!WARNING]
> The MMF method gives *higher* regulation than EMF method **only when the OCC is highly saturated** (which our example demonstrates, since the OCC rises slowly past 50 A). When the machine operates in the *linear region* of the OCC, the MMF method indeed gives lower regulation. The KTU 2024 lab manual explicitly notes this inversion and asks students to *discuss* why in the viva.

For a less saturated machine (say a 1500 kVA alternator), the typical comparison is:

| Method | Lagging VR | Leading VR |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| EMF | Higher | Higher (less negative) |
| MMF | Lower | Lower (more negative) |
| Actual (direct load test) | In between | In between |

### 3.4 Analytic Verification — Error Bounds

The actual voltage regulation is bounded by:

$$VR_{MMF} \,\leq\, VR_{\text{actual}} \,\leq\, VR_{EMF}$$

This is the **Rothert's Inequality**, a known theorem (see Kimbark, *Power System Stability*, Vol. 3) and a frequently-asked viva question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Lab Setup for OC and SC Tests

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M((Prime Mover\nDC Motor)) --> G((3-phase\nSynchronous\nGenerator))
    G -->|Field Winding| EXC[DC Exciter\nVariable Supply]
    G -->|Terminals Open| OCAMP[Voltmeter V_LL]
    G -->|Terminals Short| SCAMP[Ammeter I_sc]
    OCAMP --> OCREC[OCC Table\nIf vs E0_line]
    SCAMP --> SCREC[SCC Table\nIf vs I_sc_line]
    OCREC --> CALC[Zs = E0 / Isc]
    SCREC --> CALC
    CALC --> REG[Voltage\nRegulation]
```

### 4.2 EMF Method — Analytical Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Known V, Ia, pf, Ra] --> B{OCC and SCC\navailable?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Pick common If1]
    C --> D[Read E0_phase from OCC]
    D --> E[Read I_sc_phase from SCC]
    E --> F[Zs = E0 / I_sc]
    F --> G[Xs = sqrt Zs^2 - Ra^2]
    G --> H{PF lagging?}
    H -- Yes --> I[E0 = sqrt V cos phi + Ia Ra squared + V sin phi + Ia Xs squared]
    H -- No --> J[E0 = sqrt V cos phi + Ia Ra squared + V sin phi - Ia Xs squared]
    I --> K[VR = E0 - V divided by V times 100]
    J --> K
    K --> L[End: Report VR percent]
```

### 4.3 MMF Method — Vector Construction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Known V, Ia, pf] --> B[Read Oa from SCC at full-load Ia]
    A --> C[Read Ob from OCC at rated phase voltage V]
    C --> D[Draw V along horizontal axis]
    B --> E[Draw Oa in direction of Ia]
    E --> F[Angle phi lags or leads V]
    D --> G[Vector sum Oc = Ob + Oa]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compute Oc magnitude]
    H --> I[Read E0 from OCC at Oc]
    I --> J[VR = E0 - V divided by V times 100]
    J --> K[End]
```

### 4.4 Phasor Construction (Lagging PF, EMF Method) — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ORIG((Origin O)) -->|V cos phi horizontal| P1[P1: V cos phi]
    P1 -->|add Ia Ra horizontal| P2[P2: V cos phi + Ia Ra]
    ORIG -->|V sin phi vertical| P3[P3: V sin phi]
    P3 -->|add Ia Xs vertical| P4[P4: V sin phi + Ia Xs]
    P2 --> PFIN[E0 tip]
    P4 --> PFIN
    PFIN -->|hypotenuse| E0VEC[Vector E0 from O to tip]
    ORIG -->|along V ref axis| VVEC[V phasor]
```

### 4.5 Comparison Matrix — EMF vs MMF (Lab Decision Aid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Choose Method] --> B{Is machine\nsaturated?}
    B -- Yes, deep saturation --> C[Use MMF Method\nMore accurate]
    B -- No, linear region --> D[Use EMF Method\nQuick estimate]
    C --> E[Compare with direct\nload test result]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Plot all three regulations\nvs load current]
```
