<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define voltage regulation of a synchronous generator. Why is it negative for leading power factor loads?*

**Model Answer:**
Voltage regulation is defined as the change in terminal voltage from full-load to no-load, expressed as a percentage of rated voltage, with constant speed and constant field excitation:

$$VR \,\% \,=\, \dfrac{E_0 - V}{V} \times 100$$

For leading PF loads, the armature reaction is *magnetizing* (it supports the main field). At full load, the terminal voltage is *higher* than at no-load because the magnetizing armature reaction compensates the internal drops. Therefore $V > E_0$ when the field is set for rated no-load voltage, giving a negative regulation when computed using the conventional formula.

**(3 Marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for leading-PF explanation)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State two differences between the EMF method and the MMF method of determining voltage regulation.*

**Model Answer:**

| S.No | EMF Method | MMF Method |
|------|------------|------------|
| 1 | Replaces armature reaction by an equivalent reactance $X_s$ | Replaces armature reaction by an equivalent field MMF (current $O_a$) |
| 2 | Uses unsaturated value of $Z_s$ (linear assumption) | Uses actual saturated OCC at resultant excitation |
| 3 | Gives higher (pessimistic) regulation | Gives lower (optimistic) regulation for unsaturated machines |
| 4 | Easier to compute (closed-form equation) | Requires graphical/vector construction |

**(3 Marks: any 3 distinct points with comparison)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**

**Question A (14 Marks):**
A 3-phase, star-connected synchronous generator has the following data:
- Rating: **1500 kVA, 6600 V, 50 Hz**
- Armature resistance $R_a = 0.4 \,\Omega$/phase
- From OC and SC tests at field current $I_f = 40$ A:
  - OC line voltage = **9000 V**
  - SC armature current = **rated value**
- The generator supplies full-load at **0.8 PF lagging**.

Calculate:
(a) Synchronous impedance and synchronous reactance per phase (7 Marks)
(b) Voltage regulation by the EMF method (7 Marks)

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**Part (a):**

Phase voltage from OC: $E_0 = 9000/\sqrt{3} = 5196.15$ V/phase
Rated phase current: $I_a = 1500 \times 10^3/(\sqrt{3} \times 6600) = 131.22$ A

$$Z_s = \dfrac{E_0}{I_a} = \dfrac{5196.15}{131.22} = 39.60 \,\Omega/\text{phase}$$

$$X_s = \sqrt{Z_s^2 - R_a^2} = \sqrt{39.60^2 - 0.4^2} = \sqrt{1568.16 - 0.16} = 39.60 \,\Omega/\text{phase}$$

**Part (b):**

For 0.8 PF lagging: $\cos\phi = 0.8$, $\sin\phi = 0.6$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_0 &= \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2} \\
V &= 6600/\sqrt{3} = 3810.5 \,\text{V} \\
V\cos\phi &= 3048.4, \quad V\sin\phi = 2286.3 \\
I_a R_a &= 52.49, \quad I_a X_s = 5196.3 \\
E_0 &= \sqrt{(3048.4 + 52.49)^2 + (2286.3 + 5196.3)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{(3100.9)^2 + (7482.6)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{9{,}615{,}581 + 55{,}989{,}295} \\
&= \sqrt{65{,}604{,}876} \\
&= 8099.7 \,\text{V/phase}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$E_{0,\text{line}} = \sqrt{3} \times 8099.7 = 14{,}029 \,\text{V}$$

$$VR \,\% = \dfrac{14029 - 6600}{6600} \times 100 = \boxed{112.6 \,\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly forget to convert line voltage to **phase voltage** before plugging into the EMF equation (or vice-versa). The OC test typically records *line-to-line* voltage, but the phasor equation is per *phase*. **Loss: 2 marks**. Also, ensure your calculator is in *degree* mode for $\sin\phi$ and $\cos\phi$. **Loss: 1 mark**.

---

**Question B (14 Marks):**
A 3-phase, star-connected synchronous generator rated **500 kVA, 3.3 kV, 50 Hz** has the following OCC and SCC data:

| $I_f$ (A) | 10 | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 | 60 |
|-----------|----|----|----|----|----|----|
| $E_{0,\text{line}}$ (V) | 1100 | 2000 | 2700 | 3300 | 3650 | 3920 |
| $I_{sc,\text{line}}$ (A) | 25 | 50 | 75 | 100 | 125 | 150 |

The armature resistance is $R_a = 0.3 \,\Omega$/phase. Determine the voltage regulation at full-load, 0.8 PF lagging, using the **MMF method**.

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**Part (a): Identify $O_a$ and $O_b$ (7 Marks)**

Rated phase voltage: $V = 3300/\sqrt{3} = 1905.0$ V
Rated line current: $I_a = 500 \times 10^3/(\sqrt{3} \times 3300) = 87.47$ A

From OCC at line voltage = 3300 V (rated): $O_b = 40$ A
From SCC at $I_{sc} = 87.47$ A (interpolation between 75 A and 100 A):

$$O_a = 20 + (87.47 - 50) \times \dfrac{30 - 20}{75 - 50} \approx 20 + 14.99 = 34.99 \approx 35.0 \,\text{A}$$

(SCC is linear: $I_{sc} = 2.5 I_f$, so $O_a = 87.47/2.5 = 35.0$ A.)

**Part (b): Vector addition and read $E_0$ from OCC (7 Marks)**

For 0.8 PF lagging: $\cos\phi = 0.8$, $\sin\phi = 0.6$

$O_a$ is in phase with $I_a$ (lags $V$ by $\phi$).
$O_b$ is perpendicular to $V$ (in MMF space, where $V$ is along the d-axis if we use $E$ instead).

Reference: $O_b$ along the reference axis (in MMF/field space). $O_a$ at angle $-36.87°$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_{c,\text{real}} &= O_b + O_a \cos\phi = 40 + 35 \times 0.8 = 68.0 \\
O_{c,\text{imag}} &= O_a \sin\phi = 35 \times 0.6 = 21.0 \\
O_c &= \sqrt{68.0^2 + 21.0^2} = \sqrt{4624 + 441} = \sqrt{5065} = 71.17 \,\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

From OCC at $O_c = 71.17$ A, extrapolating between 60 A ($E_{0,\text{line}} = 3920$ V) and the saturation trend, we estimate:

$$E_{0,\text{line}} \approx 4120 \,\text{V} \quad (\text{approx, by extrapolation})$$

$$VR \,\% = \dfrac{4120 - 3300}{3300} \times 100 = \boxed{24.85 \,\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — MMF Method:** (1) Students frequently confuse whether $O_a$ is *in phase with* or *perpendicular to* the armature current. **Rule:** $O_a$ corresponds to the SC field current and aligns with the field MMF that opposes the demagnetizing armature reaction on short circuit — it is **in phase with $I_a$**. **Loss: 3 marks.** (2) Extrapolation of OCC beyond measured field current requires careful plotting; students often use *linear* extrapolation and ignore the saturation knee. **Loss: 2 marks.** (3) The MMF method requires **vector** addition, not scalar; students often do $O_c = O_a + O_b$ (scalar). **Loss: 2 marks.**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Recall Checklist for KTU Exam Hall:**
>
> - **Voltage Regulation formula:** $VR \,\% = (E_0 - V)/V \times 100$ — **per phase** values must be used internally; line values only for final reporting.
> - **EMF Method (4-step recipe):** (i) Pick common $I_f$; (ii) Read $E_0$ from OCC and $I_{sc}$ from SCC; (iii) $Z_s = E_0/I_{sc}$, $X_s = \sqrt{Z_s^2 - R_a^2}$; (iv) Use phasor equation with **$+IX_s$ for lagging** and **$-IX_s$ for leading** PF.
> - **MMF Method (4-step recipe):** (i) Read $O_b$ from OCC at rated voltage $V$; (ii) Read $O_a$ from SCC at rated current $I_a$; (iii) Vector-add: $\vec{O_c} = \vec{O_b} + \vec{O_a}$ (with $O_a$ along direction of $I_a$); (iv) Read $E_0$ from OCC at $O_c$.
> - **EMF method → Pessimistic (higher VR); MMF method → Optimistic (lower VR)** in the unsaturated region. Actual VR lies between.
> - **Leading PF gives negative regulation** — memorize this exception; many students lose marks by reporting a positive value.
> - **Always convert** line voltage to phase voltage before plugging into per-phase phasor equation: $V_{\text{phase}} = V_L/\sqrt{3}$ (star) or $V_{\text{phase}} = V_L$ (delta).
> - **Potier triangle** is a refinement of MMF method (corrects leakage reactance drop separately). Not required for basic KTU module but mentioned in viva.
> - **Lab viva question:** *Why is $Z_s$ called "synchronous" impedance?* — Because it is the impedance offered by the machine only at synchronous speed, when the rotor field is stationary relative to the armature MMF.
> - **Lab viva question:** *Why does the SCC remain linear even when OCC saturates?* — Because the demagnetizing armature reaction on short circuit is so strong that the iron operates in the unsaturated region.
> - **Rothert's Inequality:** $VR_{MMF} \leq VR_{\text{actual}} \leq VR_{EMF}$ — this is the theoretical bracket for actual regulation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Skill Outcomes Mapping:** This experiment tests **CO2** (Apply EMF and MMF methods for synchronous generator performance) and **CO4** (Analyse test data and validate against standards). The typical 14-mark question tests both computation skill and conceptual clarity of method limitations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voltage regulation of 3-phase synchronous generator by direct loading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL507 - AC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Voltage regulation of 3-phase synchronous generator by direct loading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Voltage Regulation (VR)** of a synchronous generator is defined as the percentage rise in terminal voltage when the load is removed from the machine, keeping the speed and field excitation constant, with respect to the rated terminal voltage.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$\text{VR}\% = \frac{E_0 - V}{V} \times 100$$

Where:
- $E_0$ = No-load induced EMF per phase (also called Open-Circuit EMF)
- $V$ = Full-load terminal voltage per phase
- The difference $(E_0 - V)$ is the voltage drop across the synchronous impedance $Z_s = R_a + jX_s$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **low voltage regulation** is always desirable because it indicates that the terminal voltage remains nearly constant regardless of the load condition. For an ideal generator, VR = 0%.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Intuition 💧

Imagine a **water pump station** supplying water to your house through long pipes:

- **Voltage (V)** → Water pressure at your tap
- **Current (I)** → Water flow rate (your demand)
- **Internal Impedance (Z_s)** → Friction/resistance in the pipes
- **Excitation (Field Current)** → Pump motor speed setting

When you open the tap fully (full load), the pressure at your tap **drops** because of pipe friction. Similarly, when a generator supplies full load current, its terminal voltage **drops** because of the voltage drop across the synchronous impedance. The moment you remove all loads (close every tap), the pressure returns to its maximum — this is the **No-Load Voltage ($E_0$)**.

The **voltage regulation** simply tells you *how much pressure drops when you suddenly open all the taps together* — the smaller the drop, the better the system!

## 1.3 Why "Direct Loading"?

The **Direct Loading Method** is the most straightforward and *experimentally accurate* way to determine voltage regulation. Unlike indirect methods (EMF, MMF, ZPF, or ASA methods), here we:

1. **Actually load** the synchronous generator with real 3-phase loads (resistive lamp banks, inductive loads, or motors).
2. **Directly measure** $V$ (full-load voltage) and $I_L$ (load current).
3. **Remove the load** (keeping speed & excitation constant) and measure $E_0$ (open-circuit voltage).
4. Substitute into the regulation formula.

> [!TIP]
> KTU lab examinations heavily favour the **Direct Loading Method** because it provides real experimental data without relying on approximations or synchronous reactance values, which require additional short-circuit tests.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standards Highlighted

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical KTU Lab Value |
|-----------|----------------|----------------------|
| Synchronous Speed | $N_s$ | **1500 rpm** (4-pole, 50 Hz) |
| Rated Line Voltage | $V_L$ | **415 V** (3-phase) |
| Rated Frequency | $f$ | **50 Hz** |
| Power Factor | $\cos\phi$ | **0.8 lagging** (typical) |
| Phase Voltage (Star) | $V_{ph}$ | $V_L / \sqrt{3}$ = **239.6 V** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Voltage Regulation

When a 3-phase synchronous generator delivers a balanced load current $I_a$ at a power factor angle $\phi$, the **internal generated EMF per phase** ($E_0$) and the **terminal voltage per phase** ($V$) are related by the synchronous impedance equation:

$$E_0 = V + I_a \cdot Z_s = V + I_a(R_a + jX_s)$$

### 2.1.1 Phasor Interpretation for Different Load Types

For a **lagging power factor** load (most common):
- $E_0$ leads $V$ by a significant angle
- $I_a R_a$ drop is in phase with current
- $I_a X_s$ drop leads current by 90°

For a **leading power factor** load:
- Voltage regulation can become *negative* (terminal voltage rises on loading)

For a **unity power factor** load:
- Purely resistive drop, moderate regulation

## 2.2 EMF Equation of a Synchronous Generator

The RMS value of EMF induced per phase is:

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph} \, K_w$$

Where:
- $f$ = supply frequency in Hz
- $\phi$ = flux per pole in Webers
- $T_{ph}$ = number of turns per phase
- $K_w$ = winding factor (typically 0.95–0.96 for distributed windings)

## 2.3 Synchronous Speed Relationship

$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P}$$

Where $P$ = total number of poles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> During the **direct loading test**, $N_s$ MUST be held constant by the prime mover (DC motor or 3-phase induction motor with VFD). Any speed variation invalidates the experiment.

## 2.4 Power and Loading Relationships

For a balanced 3-phase load:

$$P_{out} = \sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L \, \cos\phi \quad \text{(watts)}$$

$$Q = \sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L \, \sin\phi \quad \text{(VAR)}$$

$$S = \sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L \quad \text{(VA)}$$

## 2.5 🎯 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` (not `|`)** for absolute values to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Description | Unit / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $\text{VR}\% = \dfrac{E_0 - V}{V} \times 100$ | Voltage Regulation (definition) | Percentage ($\%$) |
| 2 | $E_0 = V + I_a(R_a + jX_s)$ | Synchronous impedance equation | Volts per phase |
| 3 | $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph} \, K_w$ | Induced EMF per phase | Volts (RMS) |
| 4 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed | rpm |
| 5 | $V_{ph} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ | Star-connected phase voltage | Volts |
| 6 | $P = \sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L \, \cos\phi$ | Total 3-phase active power | Watts |
| 7 | $Z_s = \sqrt{R_a^2 + X_s^2}$ | Synchronous impedance magnitude | Ohms |
| 8 | $\text{Slip} = 0$ | Synchronous operation guarantee | Dimensionless |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Voltage regulation is critical in:

- **Power Grid Stability** — Utilities specify VR $\leq \pm 5\%$ at consumer terminals
- **Standalone Generators** — Hospitals, data centres, and remote sites require tight VR
- **AVR Design (Automatic Voltage Regulators)** — Modern generators use feedback loops to keep VR minimal
- **Synchronization of Generators** — Before paralleling, VR behaviour must be known for load sharing
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Voltage Regulation Formula

Starting from the synchronous impedance equation:

$$E_0 = V + I_a(R_a + jX_s)$$

Separating real and imaginary components by taking the dot product with $V$ (terminal voltage as reference phasor):

Let $V = V \angle 0°$ (reference), $I_a = I_a \angle -\phi$ (lagging load).

The voltage drop across the synchronous impedance:

$$I_a Z_s = I_a R_a \angle -\phi + j I_a X_s \angle -\phi$$

The magnitude of $E_0$ can be computed using:

$$E_0^2 = (V + I_a R_a \cos\phi + I_a X_s \sin\phi)^2 + (I_a X_s \cos\phi - I_a R_a \sin\phi)^2$$

For an approximate (and KTU-accepted) simplified form, when $R_a \ll X_s$:

$$E_0 \approx \sqrt{V^2 + (I_a X_s)^2 + 2V \cdot I_a X_s \sin\phi}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Convention:** Since we use *direct measurement*, the regulation is computed using the **directly measured** values of $E_0$ and $V$ — no phasor algebra is needed for the result. The above derivation only helps in **understanding WHY** the regulation has its specific value.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Experimental Procedure (Analytical Walkthrough)

### **Step 1: Pre-Experiment Setup**
- Identify nameplate ratings: $V_L$, $I_L$, $f$, $P$, $\cos\phi$, $N_s$, $I_f$ (field current).
- Verify prime mover coupling (typically a DC shunt motor with armature voltage control).

### **Step 2: Run-Up to Rated Speed**
- Start the prime mover.
- Adjust speed to **rated synchronous speed** using a tachometer or digital RPM meter.
- Confirm $N_s = 120 f / P$ matches the rated value.

### **Step 3: Initial Excitation (No-Load)**
- Gradually increase the **DC field current** $I_f$ of the synchronous generator using the field rheostat.
- Until the **open-circuit terminal voltage** equals the **rated voltage** $V_L = 415$ V (line-to-line).
- Record: $V_{OC,\text{line}}$, then convert: $V_{OC,\text{phase}} = V_{OC,\text{line}} / \sqrt{3}$.

### **Step 4: Apply Load Progressively**
- Switch ON the 3-phase load bank (resistive lamps or RL load).
- Increase load in **discrete steps** (e.g., 25%, 50%, 75%, 100% of rated current).
- At each step, record:
  - $V_L$ (line voltage)
  - $I_L$ (line current)
  - $P$ (3-phase power via wattmeters)
  - $\cos\phi$ (computed)
  - $N_s$ (verify constant)

### **Step 5: Capture Full-Load Condition**
- At **100% rated load**, record the terminal voltage $V_{FL}$.
- Calculate: $V_{FL,\text{phase}} = V_{FL,\text{line}} / \sqrt{3}$

### **Step 6: Remove Load (Critical Step!)**
- ⚠️ **DO NOT change** the field excitation $I_f$ or the prime mover speed.
- Slowly reduce the load back to zero.
- Measure the **no-load voltage** $V_{NL}$.

### **Step 7: Compute Regulation**

$$\text{VR}\% = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$$

### **Step 8: Plot the External Characteristic**
- Plot $V_L$ (Y-axis) vs. $I_L$ (X-axis) — this is the **External Characteristic Curve**.
- Note the downward slope confirms positive regulation.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Sample KTU Lab Calculation)

**Given:**
- 3-phase, 4-pole, star-connected synchronous generator
- Rated: 5 kVA, 415 V, 7 A, 50 Hz, 1500 rpm
- Power factor = 0.8 lagging

**Measured Data at full load:**
- $V_{FL,\text{line}} = 415$ V → $V_{FL,\text{phase}} = 415 / \sqrt{3} = 239.6$ V
- $I_L = 7$ A (line current) → $I_a = 7$ A (star, $I_{ph} = I_L$)
- $V_{NL,\text{line}} = 445$ V → $V_{NL,\text{phase}} = 445 / \sqrt{3} = 256.9$ V

**Solution:**

Compute regulation per phase:

$$\text{VR}\% = \frac{E_0 - V}{V} \times 100 = \frac{256.9 - 239.6}{239.6} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{17.3}{239.6} \times 100 = 7.22\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Validity Check:** A regulation of 5–10% is typical for medium-sized synchronous generators at 0.8 lagging power factor. If your result exceeds 15%, suspect measurement errors or unstable prime-mover speed.

## 3.4 Sources of Error in Direct Loading

| Source | Effect | Mitigation |
|--------|--------|------------|
| Speed drift during load removal | Induced $E_0$ becomes erroneous | Use closed-loop speed control |
| Field current fluctuation | Changes the flux → wrong $E_0$ | Lock the field rheostat after initial setting |
| Unbalanced 3-phase load | Causes negative-sequence currents | Use balanced lamp bank or 3-phase RL load |
| Hot resistance vs. cold resistance | $R_a$ changes with temperature | Measure $R_a$ by DC test (cold) |
| Wattmeter error at low PF | Cosine of small angle ≈ 0 → high error | Use precision 0.2-class meters |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Experimental Setup — Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Prime Mover: DC Shunt Motor] -->|Mechanical Shaft Coupling| B[3-Phase Synchronous Generator]
    C[3-Phase AC Supply 415V] -->|Armature Voltage Control| A
    D[DC Field Supply with Rheostat] -->|Field Winding Excitation| B
    B -->|3-Phase Output Terminals U V W| E[3-Phase Load Bank]
    F[Voltmeter V1 across U-V] --> B
    G[Ammeter A1 in U-line] --> B
    H[Frequency Meter / Tachometer] --> B
    I[Wattmeter W1, W2 Two-Method] --> B
    J[Auto-Transformer Variac] --> A
    K[3-Phase Variac for Field Supply] --> D
    L[Switch S1 Load Switch] --> E
    M[Switch S2 Field Switch] --> D

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Experimental Procedure Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Start Prime Mover] --> S2[Step 2: Adjust Speed to Ns 1500 rpm]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Set Field Current to get Rated No-Load Voltage 415V]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Record No-Load V_OC]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Apply Load in Steps 25 50 75 100 percent]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: At Full Load Record V_FL and I_L]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Without Changing If or N remove Load]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Record No-Load Voltage V_NL]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Compute VR percent using Formula]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Plot V versus I External Characteristic]

    style S1 fill:#E0F7FA
    style S5 fill:#FFF9C4
    style S7 fill:#FFCCBC
    style S10 fill:#C8E6C9
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of Voltage Regulation Phenomenon

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E0[Internal EMF E0 induced in Armature] -->|Minus Drop| V[Terminal Voltage V]
    IR[I_a times R_a Resistive Drop] --> V
    IX[I_a times X_s Reactive Drop] --> V
    V --> R[Result: VR percent equals E0 minus V divided by V times 100]
    R --> M1[Load Increase I_a rises]
    M1 --> M2[Drop across Z_s increases]
    M2 --> M3[Terminal Voltage V falls]
    M3 --> R

    style E0 fill:#FFEB3B
    style V fill:#4FC3F7
    style R fill:#FF8A65
```

## 4.4 Phasor Diagram Description (For Reference)

Although a true phasor diagram cannot be drawn in Mermaid, the following relationships must be understood for the KTU viva:

- **Reference phasor:** Terminal voltage $V$ along the horizontal (positive X-axis).
- **Current phasor $I_a$:** Lags $V$ by angle $\phi$ for lagging power factor.
- **Resistive drop $I_a R_a$:** Parallel to $I_a$ (in phase with current).
- **Reactive drop $I_a X_s$:** Perpendicular to $I_a$, leading by 90°.
- **Internal EMF $E_0$:** Sum vector — leads $V$ for lagging loads.

> [!TIP]
> **Viva Tip:** When examiners ask "Why does voltage regulation increase with lagging power factor?", answer: *"Because the reactive drop $I_a X_s \sin\phi$ adds to the terminal voltage constructively, requiring a larger $E_0$ to maintain the same $V$, hence larger regulation."*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📚 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define voltage regulation of a synchronous generator. Why is low voltage regulation desirable?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Voltage regulation of a synchronous generator is defined as the percentage change in terminal voltage from no-load to full-load condition, keeping the speed and field excitation constant.

$$\text{VR}\% = \frac{E_0 - V}{V} \times 100$$

A **low voltage regulation is desirable** because it ensures that the terminal voltage supplied to consumers remains nearly constant irrespective of load fluctuations, leading to stable operation of connected equipment and improved power quality. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for justification)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What is meant by "direct loading" of a synchronous generator? List two advantages over the EMF method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Direct loading refers to the experimental method of determining voltage regulation by **physically connecting a real 3-phase load** (lamp bank / inductive load) to the generator and directly measuring the full-load voltage $V$ and the subsequent no-load voltage $E_0$.

**Two advantages over the EMF (indirect) method:**
1. **Higher accuracy** — no assumptions required about synchronous reactance, which must be indirectly estimated in the EMF method.
2. **Realistic operating conditions** — actual machine behaviour under load (saturation, heating, armature reaction) is captured naturally. **(1 mark definition + 2 marks advantages)**

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Most students lose marks by (1) forgetting to maintain **constant field excitation** between load and no-load readings, (2) computing regulation on **line** values instead of **phase** values, and (3) not stating the **speed condition** explicitly. Always state assumptions first!

---

### **Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q.A.** A 3-phase, 4-pole, star-connected synchronous generator rated at 10 kVA, 415 V, 50 Hz has its open-circuit voltage measured as 450 V (line) at no-load. When the generator is loaded to its rated current of 13.9 A at 0.8 power factor lagging, the terminal voltage drops to 415 V (line).

**(a)** Calculate the **voltage regulation** of the generator using the direct loading principle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the same generator is now loaded at **unity power factor** and the terminal voltage is observed to be 425 V at rated current, calculate the new regulation. Comment on why the regulation value changed. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A.(a) — 7 marks**

**Given Data:**
- 3-phase, star-connected, 4-pole
- Rated: 10 kVA, 415 V, 50 Hz, $I_L = 13.9$ A
- PF = 0.8 lagging
- $V_{OC,\text{line}} = 450$ V → $E_0 = 450 / \sqrt{3} = 259.81$ V per phase
- $V_{FL,\text{line}} = 415$ V → $V = 415 / \sqrt{3} = 239.60$ V per phase

**Step 1:** Identify per-phase values. (1 mark)

**Step 2:** Apply regulation formula:

$$\text{VR}\% = \frac{E_0 - V}{V} \times 100 = \frac{259.81 - 239.60}{239.60} \times 100$$

$$\text{VR}\% = \frac{20.21}{239.60} \times 100 = 8.43\%$$

**Step 3:** State the assumption (constant field excitation, constant synchronous speed). (1 mark)

**Answer:** **Voltage Regulation = 8.43%** (5 marks for computation + 2 marks for correct approach)

---

#### **Solution to Q.A.(b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** Same generator, UPF loading, $V_{FL,\text{line}} = 425$ V

**Step 1:** Compute phase voltage: $V = 425 / \sqrt{3} = 245.38$ V

**Step 2:** The no-load voltage at the same field excitation as before ($E_0 = 259.81$ V) is **unchanged** because excitation is constant.

$$\text{VR}\% = \frac{259.81 - 245.38}{245.38} \times 100 = \frac{14.43}{245.38} \times 100 = 5.88\%$$

**Step 3:** Explanation (2 marks):
- Voltage regulation is **lower at UPF** (5.88%) compared to 0.8 lagging (8.43%).
- **Reason:** At lagging PF, the demagnetizing armature reaction is stronger, requiring a larger flux to maintain rated voltage — but in direct loading, since $E_0$ is fixed by field, the terminal voltage drops more. At UPF, only the cross-magnetizing effect exists, which has less impact on the resultant flux.

**Answer:** **VR (UPF) = 5.88%**, lower than lagging case due to reduced armature reaction.

---

### **Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Q.B.** A laboratory synchronous generator is rated 5 kVA, 220 V (line), 3-phase, 50 Hz, star-connected. During a direct loading test at unity power factor, the following readings were observed at full load:

- Full-load line voltage = 220 V
- Full-load line current = 13.1 A
- No-load voltage (after removing load, same excitation) = 232 V
- Synchronous speed = 1500 rpm

**(a)** Determine the **number of poles** and compute the **voltage regulation** per phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Estimate the **synchronous impedance** per phase of the machine and comment on its practical significance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.B.(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Find number of poles using $N_s = 120f / P$: (2 marks)

$$P = \frac{120 f}{N_s} = \frac{120 \times 50}{1500} = 4 \text{ poles}$$

**Step 2:** Convert line voltages to phase voltages (star-connected): (1 mark)

$$V_{FL,\text{phase}} = \frac{220}{\sqrt{3}} = 127.02 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{NL,\text{phase}} = \frac{232}{\sqrt{3}} = 133.95 \text{ V} = E_0$$

**Step 3:** Apply regulation formula: (4 marks)

$$\text{VR}\% = \frac{133.95 - 127.02}{127.02} \times 100 = \frac{6.93}{127.02} \times 100 = 5.46\%$$

**Answer:** **P = 4 poles, VR = 5.46%**

---

#### **Solution to Q.B.(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Compute voltage drop across $Z_s$ per phase: (1 mark)

$$\Delta V = E_0 - V = 133.95 - 127.02 = 6.93 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $Z_s$ magnitude. For a star connection, phase current = line current = 13.1 A: (3 marks)

$$Z_s = \frac{E_0}{I_a} = \frac{133.95}{13.1} = 10.23 \, \Omega$$

(Equivalent approximation since the drop $\Delta V$ is small compared to $V$)

$$Z_s \approx \frac{\Delta V}{I_a} = \frac{6.93}{13.1} = 0.529 \, \Omega \text{ (for the resistive-plus-reactive drop)}$$

> For more rigorous calculation: $Z_s = \sqrt{R_a^2 + X_s^2}$ where $R_a$ comes from DC resistance test.

**Step 3:** Practical Significance (3 marks):
- A **low $Z_s$** means low voltage regulation, better load-handling capability, and higher short-circuit current capability.
- **High $Z_s$** would mean significant voltage drop on loading → poor performance.
- $Z_s$ is critical for **short-circuit current calculations** during fault analysis.
- Modern alternators are designed with optimized $X_s / R_a$ ratios (typically $X_s / R_a \approx 10$ to 50) for transient stability.

**Answer:** **$Z_s \approx 10.23 \, \Omega$**, signifying the internal opposition to current flow.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Flashpoints:**

- ✅ **Definition:** VR% = $(E_0 - V) / V \times 100$ — remember the denominator is **full-load voltage**, not no-load!
- ✅ **Direct Loading is most accurate** — no need for synchronous reactance data.
- ✅ **Keep $I_f$ and $N_s$ constant** between load and no-load readings — otherwise the test fails.
- ✅ **Star-connected phase voltage** = $V_L / \sqrt{3}$; phase current = line current.
- ✅ **Synchronous speed** $N_s = 120 f / P$ — must verify with tachometer before starting.
- ✅ **Voltage regulation increases** as load PF becomes more lagging (more demagnetizing armature reaction).
- ✅ **Voltage regulation decreases** (can become negative!) for leading power factor loads.
- ✅ **External Characteristic Curve** — plot $V_L$ on Y-axis, $I_L$ on X-axis; slope is always negative.
- ✅ **For 50 Hz, 4-pole machine:** $N_s = 1500$ rpm — memorize this.
- ✅ **KTU Viva Favorite:** *"What happens if the field rheostat slips during load removal?"* → Answer: $E_0$ changes incorrectly, regulation value becomes invalid.
- ✅ **Plot at least 4–5 load points** (25%, 50%, 75%, 100%, and a slight overload if permitted) for a smooth curve.
- ✅ **Use precision meters** (0.5 class or better) — KTU evaluators specifically check instrumentation quality.
- ✅ **Mention the assumption** of balanced loading in your answer script to fetch full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voltage regulation of 3-phase synchronous generator by Potier method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEEL507 - AC MACHINES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Voltage regulation of 3-phase synchronous generator by Potier method # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Voltage Regulation (VR)** of a synchronous generator is defined as the rise in terminal voltage when full load is removed, expressed as a percentage of the rated terminal voltage, with field excitation and speed held constant. > > $$VR(\%) = \frac{E_0 - V}{V} \times 100$$ The **Potier Method (ZPF Method)** is an experimentally-based, accurate procedure for determining the voltage regulation of a salient-pole synchronous generator. It determines the **Potier Reactance (Xₚ)** and the **magnetizing effect of armature reaction (excitation component F_AR)** by exploiting the **Zero Power Factor (ZPF) characteristic** of the machine at rated armature current. The method works on the fundamental relationship: $$E' = V + I_a(R_a + jX_p)$$ where **E'** is the internal generated EMF behind the Potier leakage reactance, determined from a *shifted* open-circuit characteristic (OCC). ## 1.2 The Underlying Intuition — A Real-World Analogy > [!TIP] > **Analogy: The Noisy Restaurant** > > Imagine you are trying to hear your friend speak in a noisy restaurant. > - The **terminal voltage (V)** is your friend's soft voice. > - The **load current (Iₐ)** is the restaurant noise — the louder the crowd, the more you strain to hear. > - The **armature reaction** is the *type* of noise (bassoons in a music shop vs. clinking spoons in a cafe). You need *more effort* (extra excitation) to cut through one type of noise versus another. > - The **Potier reactance (Xₚ)** is the *specific pitch distortion* introduced by your hearing — it adds its own voltage drop. > > To know how loud your friend *could* speak in silence (the no-load EMF, E₀), you must: (1) figure out the *extra effort* needed to cut through the noise (armature reaction excitation), and (2) correct for your *specific hearing distortion* (the Xₚ voltage drop). The Potier method does exactly this for the synchronous generator using three curves: the **silent room (OCC)**, the **noise level (SCC)**, and the **specific noisy room test (ZPF)**. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Rated line-to-line voltage (V_L)** — typically **415 V**, **3.3 kV**, or **6.6 kV** for KTU lab machines > - **Rated armature current (Iₐ)** — typically **10 A**, **20 A**, or **50 A** depending on machine rating > - **Rated field current (I_f)** — usually **1 A to 5 A** for lab alternators > - **Power factor (cos φ)** — load power factor (assumed lagging for regulation) > - **Frequency (f)** — **50 Hz** (Indian standard) > - **Speed (Nₛ)** — **1500 rpm** for 4-pole, 50 Hz machines > - **Efficiency class** — typically tested at **0.8 lagging** power factor ## 1.4 Why Potier Method? — Syllabus Relevance | Method | Accuracy | Data Required | KTU Preference | |---|---|---|---| | EMF Method | Poor (assumes no armature reaction) | Xₛ (synchronous reactance) | ❌ | | MMF Method | Moderate | Xₐl (armature reaction reactance) | ⚠️ | | ASA Method | Better | Xₛ (unsaturated) + Xₛ (saturated) | ⚠️ | | **Potier (ZPF) Method** | **Best for salient pole** | **OCC + SCC + ZPF tests** | ✅ **KTU Preferred** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Required Tests The Potier method requires **three experimental curves**, all plotted on a common field-current axis (X-axis) with the same machine at synchronous speed: > [!NOTE] > 1. **Open-Circuit Characteristic (OCC)** — Y-axis: E₀ (phase voltage or line voltage), driven with rated speed, field varied from 0 to ~1.25 V_rated. > 2. **Short-Circuit Characteristic (SCC)** — Y-axis: I_sc (armature current), terminals shorted through ammeters, field increased from 0 up to ~1.2 I_rated. > 3. **Zero Power Factor Characteristic (ZPF)** — Y-axis: V (terminal voltage), machine loaded on a purely inductive load (e.g., reactor bank) at rated current. Field varied and terminal voltage is noted while keeping Iₐ = I_rated. ## 2.2 Construction Logic — Step-by-Step Philosophy ### Step 1: Find the Synchronous Reactance Point Determine the short-circuit field current **I_sc_rated** that produces **rated armature current** during the short-circuit test. On the linear (initial) part of the OCC, find the voltage **V_OC** corresponding to this same **I_sc_rated**. $$X_s(\text{unsat}) = \frac{V_{OC}(\text{linear})}{I_{sc,\text{rated}}}$$ > [!TIP] > This **Xs** is the *unsaturated* synchronous reactance, useful only as a reference line, not for regulation calculation. ### Step 2: Locate Point P on the ZPF Curve The ZPF curve is plotted at **rated armature current** and zero power factor. The point **P** on the ZPF curve is defined as the point where the terminal voltage equals **rated voltage (V_rated)**. ### Step 3: Draw the Potier Triangle The Potier triangle is constructed using two classical properties: > **Property 1**: The ZPF curve is **parallel to the air-gap line** (the linear extension of the initial OCC tangent), displaced by a horizontal amount **ΔI_f** representing the field current needed to overcome armature reaction at rated current. > **Property 2**: The horizontal distance from the origin to the ZPF curve at **any** rated-current point gives the **total field current** required to (a) overcome armature reaction **plus** (b) produce the internal EMF E'. #### Construction Procedure: 1. From point **P** (on ZPF at V_rated), draw a **horizontal line PQ** of length equal to **V_rated** (terminal voltage) on the X-axis scale. 2. From **P**, draw a line **PR parallel to the air-gap line** (linear OCC tangent), extended backwards. 3. The intersection **R** of these two lines defines the **shifted origin O'**. 4. **PR** is parallel to the air-gap line → it traces a *shifted* OCC. 5. **OQ** (along the X-axis) = field current to overcome armature reaction = **ΔI_f** 6. **PQ** (vertical) = voltage drop due to Potier reactance = **I_rated × X_p** ### Step 4: Determine Potier Reactance $$X_p = \frac{I_{rated} \cdot X_p^{\text{drop}}}{I_{rated}} = \frac{\text{Vertical side of Potier triangle}}{I_{rated}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > In practice, **X_p** represents the **armature leakage reactance** (Xₐl), which is much more constant (less affected by saturation) than Xₛ. ### Step 5: Compute Armature Reaction Excitation The horizontal side **OO' = ΔI_f** is the field current required to overcome armature reaction at rated current. ### Step 6: Shift the OCC to Get the Internal EMF Curve Using the Potier triangle as a template, slide the **entire OCC** horizontally to the right by **ΔI_f** and vertically upward by **I_rated × X_p** (per phase). This new curve passes through origin **O'** and represents the **internal generated EMF (E')** vs total field current. ### Step 7: Find E₀ for the Required Load For a given load at p.f. cos φ: - Calculate the **back-emf behind X_p**: $E' = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi \pm I_a X_p)^2}$ - `+` for lagging, `−` for leading - On the shifted OCC, locate the field current **I_f'** corresponding to **E'**. - The total field current is: $I_{f,total} = I_f' + \Delta I_f$ - On the **original OCC**, find **E₀** corresponding to $I_{f,total}$. - Finally: $VR = \frac{E_0 - V}{V} \times 100\%$ ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Unsaturated Synchronous Reactance | $X_s = V_{OC,\text{linear}} \diagup I_{sc,\text{rated}}$ | Ω/phase | From OCC linear part + SCC | | 2 | Potier Reactance | $X_p = I_{rated} \cdot \text{drop} \diagup I_{rated} = PQ \text{ voltage} \diagup I_{rated}$ | Ω/phase | From Potier triangle | | 3 | Armature Reaction MMF (in field A) | $\Delta I_f = OO'$ (horizontal shift) | A | From Potier triangle | | 4 | EMF behind Potier reactance | $E' = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi \pm I_a X_p)^2}$ | V/phase | + lagging, − leading | | 5 | Shifted OCC origin | $O' = O + \Delta I_f$ (horizontal) and $+I_a X_p$ (vertical) | – | Template shift | | 6 | Total field current | $I_{f,total} = I_{f,OCC}(E') + \Delta I_f$ | A | Sum of two components | | 7 | No-load EMF | $E_0$ read from **original OCC** at $I_{f,total}$ | V/phase | Final lookup | | 8 | Voltage Regulation | $VR(\%) = (E_0 - V) \cdot 100 \diagup V$ | % | Per-unit × 100 | > [!CAUTION] > For tables: every `|` in math has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve the KTU markdown table syntax. Always use **per-phase** values unless specified otherwise. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Salient-pole hydro generators** (KTU textbook machine): Xₐl and X_AR are physically distinct, making the EMF/MMF methods inaccurate. Potier is the industry standard. - **Large turbo-alternators**: Used in pre-commissioning tests in power plants for **regulatory compliance** and **ISO/IEC 60034 validation**. - **Synchronous motor design**: The same X_p value is used to determine the **motor's stability limit** and **V-curves** (KTU Module 5 reference). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Internal EMF Equation E' Consider one phase of the synchronous generator delivering load current **Iₐ** at lagging p.f. **cos φ** to terminal voltage **V**. The phasor diagram includes: - Stator resistance drop: **IₐRₐ** (in phase with Iₐ) - Potier reactance drop: **IₐX_p** (leading Iₐ by 90°) - Internal EMF behind X_p: **E'** (sum of V, IₐRₐ, IₐX_p) Resolving along the real and imaginary axes: $$ \begin{aligned} E'_{\text{real}} &= V\cos\phi + I_a R_a \cos\phi - I_a X_p \sin\phi \\ E'_{\text{imag}} &= V\sin\phi + I_a R_a \sin\phi + I_a X_p \cos\phi \end{aligned} $$ > Since **Rₐ ≪ X_p** for synchronous machines (typically Rₐ is 1–2% of X_p), we can neglect Rₐ terms for the imaginary component. The standard simplified form is: $$ E' = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi \pm I_a X_p)^2} $$ The **±** sign convention: - **+ I_a X_p** for **lagging** p.f. (inductive load) — reactance drop adds to the quadrature component - **− I_a X_p** for **leading** p.f. (capacitive load) — reactance drop subtracts ## 3.2 Derivation of the ZPF Curve Parallelism The ZPF characteristic at rated current is the locus of terminal voltage **V** vs field current **I_f** when the load is purely inductive (φ = 90°). The phasor equation becomes: $$E' = V + j I_a X_p \quad (\text{since } \cos 90° = 0)$$ Magnitude: $$E' = \sqrt{V^2 + (I_a X_p)^2}$$ > But at ZPF, the field current has two components: > 1. **I_f1** → produces E' on the air-gap line (linear region, unsaturated) > 2. **ΔI_f** → overcomes armature reaction > > Thus: $I_{f,\text{ZPF}} = I_{f1} + \Delta I_f$ The ZPF curve, therefore, is the **air-gap line displaced by (ΔI_f, I_aX_p)** — confirming it is **parallel to the air-gap line** and shifted by exactly the Potier triangle. ## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (Full Step-by-Step) **Given** (typical KTU lab data for a 3-phase, 5 kVA, 415 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole salient-pole alternator): - Rated phase voltage $V_{ph} = \frac{415}{\sqrt{3}} = 239.6 \, V$ - Rated current per phase $I_a = \frac{5000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415} = 6.96 \, A$ - Power factor (assumed) $\cos\phi = 0.8$ lagging → $\sin\phi = 0.6$ - Stator resistance per phase $R_a = 1.2 \, \Omega$ ### Test Data (read from plots): - **OCC** (V_ph vs I_f): Linear up to (1.5 A, 180 V), knee at (2.5 A, 240 V), saturated above. - **SCC** (I_sc vs I_f): Linear, gives I_sc = 6.96 A at I_f = 1.30 A. - **ZPF** (V vs I_f at I_a = 6.96 A): At V = 239.6 V, I_f = 4.20 A. At V = 0, I_f = 0.60 A. ### Step A: Compute Unsaturated Synchronous Reactance On linear OCC, at I_f = 1.30 A, V_OC = 180 × (1.30/1.50) = 156 V (linear interpolation). $$X_s = \frac{156}{6.96} = 22.41 \, \Omega/\text{phase}$$ ### Step B: Construct Potier Triangle Plot P on ZPF curve at V = 239.6 V → I_f = 4.20 A. - **From P**, draw horizontal line PQ of length = 239.6 V (on Y-axis scale). - **From P**, draw line PR parallel to the air-gap line. - The air-gap line slope: $156/1.50 = 104$ V/A. - For a horizontal PQ of 239.6 V, the parallel line PR rises at 104 V/A → vertical drop = 239.6 × (1/104) × 1.50 ... let's use the exact construction. By graphical measurement (or by calculation assuming air-gap slope): > Vertical side $PQ = I_a X_p^{\text{component}}$ > Horizontal side $OO' = \Delta I_f$ Assume from construction: - $PQ = 70$ V → $X_p = 70/6.96 = 10.06 \, \Omega$ - $OO' = 1.50 \, A$ → $\Delta I_f = 1.50 \, A$ ### Step C: Calculate E' (EMF behind X_p) $$ \begin{aligned} E' &= \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi + I_a X_p)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{(239.6 \times 0.8 + 6.96 \times 1.2)^2 + (239.6 \times 0.6 + 6.96 \times 10.06)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{(191.68 + 8.35)^2 + (143.76 + 70.02)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{(200.03)^2 + (213.78)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{40012 + 45702} \\ &= \sqrt{85714} \\ &= 292.78 \, V/\text{phase} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step D: Find I_f' on the Shifted OCC The shifted OCC starts at **O' = (1.50 A, 70 V)** and follows the original OCC shape. We need the field current I_f' that produces E' = 292.78 V on this shifted curve. On the shifted curve, E' = 292.78 V corresponds to a point E' above the shifted origin O' by: $$E' - 70 = 222.78 \, V$$ This is the **E' minus drop** which on the *original* OCC corresponds to (assume from OCC: 222.78 V maps to I_f1 = 2.40 A on the original curve). Thus: $I_f' = 2.40 + 1.50 = 3.90 \, A$ — *this is the total field current on the shifted OCC producing E'.* ### Step E: Total Field Current and E₀ $$I_{f,total} = I_f' + \Delta I_f = 3.90 + 1.50 = 5.40 \, A$$ From the **original OCC**, at I_f = 5.40 A → E₀ ≈ 320 V/phase (saturated region lookup). ### Step F: Voltage Regulation $$VR(\%) = \frac{320 - 239.6}{239.6} \times 100 = 33.56\%$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix | Step | Input | Operation | Output | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | OCC, SCC | Linear OCC @ I_sc_rated | X_s (unsaturated) | | 2 | OCC, ZPF | Draw Potier triangle | X_p, ΔI_f | | 3 | V, Iₐ, cos φ, Rₐ, X_p | Phasor sum | E' | | 4 | E', Shifted OCC | Reverse-lookup | I_f' | | 5 | I_f' + ΔI_f | Arithmetic | I_f,total | | 6 | I_f,total, OCC | Forward-lookup | E₀ | | 7 | E₀, V | Percentage formula | VR (%) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Phasor Diagram — Potier Method (Lagging PF) > [!NOTE] > The Mermaid block below renders a structured **phasor relationship diagram** showing the vector addition of **V**, **I_aR_a**, **I_aX_p**, and **E'**, along with the relationship between **E₀**, **E'**, **ΔI_f**, and the Potier triangle dimensions. ```mermaid graph TD A["V (Terminal Voltage / Reference)"] -->|Add I_aR_a in-phase with I_a| B["V + I_aR_a"] B -->|Add I_aX_p perpendicular lagging| C["E' (Internal EMF behind X_p)"] C -->|Add E_AR contribution along field axis| D["E_0 (No-load EMF)"] A -.->|Horizontal projection| A1["V cos phi"] A -.->|Vertical projection| A2["V sin phi"] B -.->|Horizontal projection| B1["V cos phi + I_a R_a"] C -.->|Vertical projection| C1["V sin phi + I_a X_p"] D -->|Lookup on original OCC| E["OCC Curve (Saturated)"] C -->|Lookup on shifted OCC| F["Shifted OCC (origin O')"] F -->|Horizontal shift = Delta I_f| G["Origin O' (1.5 A, 70 V)"] G -->|Original Origin O = 0,0| H["Origin O"] G -->|Vertical side = I_a X_p = 70 V| I["Potier Triangle: side PQ"] G -->|Horizontal side = Delta I_f = 1.5 A| J["Potier Triangle: side OO'"] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Experimental Setup ```mermaid graph LR subgraph DRIVE["Prime Mover (DC Motor or 3-phase IM)"] M1["DC Shunt Motor / Induction Motor"] end subgraph MACHINE["Synchronous Generator (Salient Pole)"] S1["Stator: Armature winding"] F1["Rotor: DC Field winding with slip rings"] end subgraph FIELD_EXC["Field Excitation System"] DC1["DC Voltmeter"] DC2["DC Ammeter (I_f)"] RHEO["Field Rheostat"] DC3["DC Supply (230 V)"] end subgraph LOAD_BANK["Variable Load (for ZPF)"] L1["Three-Phase Reactor Bank (Pure Inductive)"] AMM["AC Ammeter (I_a)"] VOLT["AC Voltmeter (V)"] end M1 -->|Mechanical Coupling| S1 F1 --> DC2 DC2 --> RHEO RHEO --> DC3 S1 --> AMM AMM --> L1 S1 --> VOLT subgraph TESTS["Three Test Modes"] T1["OCC: Open switch, vary I_f, read V"] T2["SCC: Close switch, vary I_f, read I_sc"] T3["ZPF: Load = Reactor, vary I_f, read V (at I_a = rated)"] end ``` ## 4.3 Potier Triangle Construction Flowchart ```mermaid graph TD START["Start: ZPF curve, OCC, SCC obtained"] --> P1["Step 1: Locate Point P on ZPF curve at V = V_rated"] P1 --> P2["Step 2: From P, draw horizontal line PQ of length = V_rated"] P2 --> P3["Step 3: From P, draw line PR parallel to Air-Gap line"] P3 --> P4["Step 4: Mark intersection R = new origin O'"] P4 --> P5["Step 5: Measure horizontal OO' = Delta I_f (armature reaction field current)"] P5 --> P6["Step 6: Measure vertical PQ = I_a * X_p voltage drop"] P6 --> P7["Step 7: Calculate X_p = PQ / I_a (rated)"] P7 --> P8["Step 8: Shift OCC by OO' horizontally and PQ vertically"] P8 --> P9["Step 9: Obtain shifted OCC (E' vs I_f)"] P9 --> P10["Step 10: Calculate E' using phasor formula"] P10 --> P11["Step 11: Read I_f' from shifted OCC at E'"] P11 --> P12["Step 12: Compute I_f,total = I_f' + Delta I_f"] P12 --> P13["Step 13: Read E_0 from original OCC at I_f,total"] P13 --> P14["Step 14: Compute VR = E_0 - V / V x 100%"] P14 --> END["Final Voltage Regulation Result"] ``` ## 4.4 Decision Tree — Method Selection ```mermaid graph TD Q["Need to find Voltage Regulation?"] --> Q1{"Is the machine non-salient
(cylindrical rotor)?"} Q1 -->|Yes| A1["Use EMF or MMF Method"] Q1 -->|No - Salient Pole| A2["Use Potier (ZPF) Method - KTU Preferred"] A2 --> A3["Conduct OCC, SCC, ZPF Tests"] A3 --> A4{"Power Factor of Load?"} A4 -->|Lagging| A5["Use + I_a X_p in quadrature component"] A4 -->|Leading| A6["Use - I_a X_p in quadrature component"] A4 -->|Unity| A7["Set sin phi = 0; X_p drop only in quadrature"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Exam Paper]** > **Q:** List the **three tests** required to determine the voltage regulation of a 3-phase synchronous generator by the Potier method. What quantities are measured in each test? **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Potier method requires the following three tests, all performed on the synchronous machine driven at synchronous speed by a prime mover: 1. **Open-Circuit Test (OCC):** Terminals are kept open. Field current is varied from zero to a value that gives ~125% of rated voltage. The **open-circuit terminal voltage (V_OC)** is recorded against **field current (I_f)**. 2. **Short-Circuit Test (SCC):** The armature terminals are short-circuated through ammeters. Field current is gradually increased from zero to a value that gives ~125% of rated current. The **short-circuit armature current (I_sc)** is recorded against **I_f**. 3. **Zero Power Factor (ZPF) Test:** The machine is loaded on a **pure inductive load** (e.g., three-phase reactor bank) adjusted to keep the armature current at the **rated value** throughout. The **terminal voltage (V)** is recorded against **I_f**. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** > **Q:** Define **Potier reactance (X_p)**. How is it physically interpreted, and how does it differ from synchronous reactance (X_s)? **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Potier reactance (X_p)** is the **armature leakage reactance** of the synchronous machine, determined experimentally from the vertical side of the Potier triangle constructed on the ZPF curve. $$ X_p = \frac{I_a \cdot \text{Potier voltage drop}}{I_a} = \frac{PQ}{I_{rated}} $$ **Physical interpretation:** X_p represents the leakage flux component that links only the armature winding and does not cross the air gap. Unlike X_s, it is **largely independent of saturation** because leakage flux paths remain in the unsaturated iron / air region. **Difference from X_s:** - **X_s = X_AR + X_p** (the synchronous reactance combines armature reaction + leakage effects, and is **saturation-dependent**). - **X_p** is **only the leakage component** and is treated as a **constant** (Ohmic value) for all operating points. - X_s is used in EMF/MMF methods; X_p is used (with a shifted OCC) in the Potier method to correctly model the saturated machine behaviour. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Module-Locked Pattern: Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and offers **internal choice** between two alternatives. Each alternative is divided into **two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks**, both compulsory if chosen. --- ### 🔹 Choice A — Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Exam Paper]** > **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat phasor diagram, derive the expression for the internal EMF **E'** (EMF behind Potier reactance) in a synchronous generator operating at a lagging power factor. Clearly state all assumptions. > **(b) [7 Marks]** The following test data are obtained for a 3-phase, 7.5 kVA, 415 V, 50 Hz, Y-connected salient-pole synchronous generator: > > | I_f (A) | 0.5 | 1.0 | 1.5 | 2.0 | 2.5 | 3.0 | 3.5 | > |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| > | E_0 (OCC, V line) | 80 | 165 | 245 | 305 | 350 | 380 | 405 | > | I_sc (SCC, A) | 2.8 | 5.6 | 8.4 | 11.2 | 14.0 | 16.8 | 19.6 | > | V (ZPF at I_rated, V line) | – | – | 60 | 145 | 215 | 270 | 315 | > > The rated armature current is **10.45 A** (line), and the rated ZPF test field current is **3.5 A** at V = 315 V (line). Compute the voltage regulation at **0.8 p.f. lagging** using the Potier method. Take R_a = 0.8 Ω/phase. --- ### 🔹 Choice A — Question A: Model Solution #### (a) Derivation of E' (7 marks) **Step 1 — Phasor Setup (2 marks)** Consider one phase of the synchronous generator. Let the terminal phase voltage be V, load current I_a at lagging power factor angle φ. The phasor diagram has: - **V** along the reference axis - **I_a** lagging V by angle φ - **I_a R_a** in phase with I_a (in-phase drop) - **I_a X_p** leading I_a by 90° (quadrature drop) - **E' = V + I_a R_a + j I_a X_p** (phasor sum) **Step 2 — Real Component (2 marks)** The horizontal (real) component of E': $$E'_x = V\cos\phi + I_a R_a \cos\phi - I_a X_p \sin\phi$$ **Step 3 — Quadrature Component (2 marks)** The vertical (imaginary) component of E': $$E'_y = V\sin\phi + I_a R_a \sin\phi + I_a X_p \cos\phi$$ **Step 4 — Magnitude and Simplification (1 mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} E' &= \sqrt{(E'_x)^2 + (E'_y)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi + I_a X_p)^2} \end{aligned} $$ **Assumptions:** - Rₐ is small (Rₐ ≪ X_p) but not zero. - The Potier reactance X_p is constant (saturation-independent). - Phasor diagram is drawn for steady-state balanced sinusoidal operation. - The "lagging" sign in $V\sin\phi + I_a X_p$ becomes "−" for leading p.f. --- #### (b) Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Step 1 — Convert to per-phase values (0.5 mark)** $$V_{ph} = \frac{415}{\sqrt{3}} = 239.6 \, V, \quad I_a = 10.45 \, A$$ **Step 2 — Find I_f for rated I_sc (1 mark)** SCC is linear: $I_f$ for $I_{sc} = 10.45 \, A$ → by interpolation between 8.4 A @ 1.5 A and 11.2 A @ 2.0 A: $$I_{f,sc} = 1.5 + (10.45 - 8.4) \times \frac{0.5}{2.8} = 1.5 + 0.366 = 1.866 \, A$$ **Step 3 — Find V_OC (linear) at I_f = 1.866 A (0.5 mark)** OCC linear: between 80 V @ 0.5 A and 165 V @ 1.0 A → slope = 170 V/A. At 1.866 A: $$V_{OC,\text{linear}} = 165 + (1.866 - 1.0) \times 170 = 165 + 147.2 = 312.2 \, V/\text{line}$$ **Per phase:** $312.2 / \sqrt{3} = 180.2$ V **Step 4 — Construct Potier Triangle from ZPF data (2 marks)** At I_a = 10.45 A, ZPF curve gives V = 315 V (line) at I_f = 3.5 A. - Point P = (3.5 A, 315 V) - Horizontal line PQ of length 315 V on Y-axis. - From P, line PR parallel to air-gap line. Air-gap line slope (from linear OCC): 170 V/A. - The horizontal shift gives ΔI_f = 315/170 = 1.853 A (≈ 1.85 A). Thus: $\Delta I_f = 1.85 \, A$ and Potier drop = ... *by graphical construction: assume PQ = 73 V/phase*. So: $X_p = 73 / 10.45 = 6.99 \, \Omega/\text{phase}$. **Step 5 — Calculate E' (1 mark)** For 0.8 p.f. lagging: cos φ = 0.8, sin φ = 0.6. $$ \begin{aligned} E' &= \sqrt{(239.6 \times 0.8 + 10.45 \times 0.8)^2 + (239.6 \times 0.6 + 10.45 \times 6.99)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{(191.7 + 8.36)^2 + (143.76 + 73.05)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{(200.06)^2 + (216.81)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{40024 + 47007} = \sqrt{87031} \\ &= 295.0 \, V/\text{phase} \end{aligned} $$ Line value: $E'_{line} = 295.0 \times \sqrt{3} = 511.0 \, V$ **Step 6 — Total field current and E₀ (1 mark)** On shifted OCC (origin O' = (1.85, 0)), E' = 295 V/ph → E' − drop = 295 − 73 = 222 V on original OCC scale. From OCC, E = 222 V (line: 384 V) corresponds to I_f1 ≈ 2.6 A. I_f' = 2.6 + 1.85 = 4.45 A. I_f,total = 4.45 + 1.85 = **6.30 A**. From original OCC, E₀ at I_f = 6.30 A → extrapolate from 405 V @ 3.5 A. Saturation slope ≈ 30 V/A beyond knee → E₀ ≈ 405 + 2.8 × 30 = 489 V/line. E₀,ph = 489/√3 = 282.3 V/phase. **Step 7 — Regulation (0.5 mark)** $$VR(\%) = \frac{282.3 - 239.6}{239.6} \times 100 = 17.8\%$$ > ✅ **VR ≈ +17.8%** (typical value for 0.8 p.f. lagging) --- ### 🔹 Choice B — Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Sessional Question Paper]** > **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch, the **construction of the Potier triangle** on the ZPF curve. What are the two important properties of the ZPF curve that enable this construction? > **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **Potier method** with the **EMF method** and the **MMF method** of voltage regulation. State two advantages and two disadvantages of the Potier method. --- ### 🔹 Choice B — Question B: Model Solution #### (a) Potier Triangle Construction (7 marks) **Two properties of the ZPF curve (2 marks):** 1. **Property 1 — Parallelism:** The ZPF characteristic (at rated I_a) is a curve that is **parallel to the air-gap line** (the linear portion of the OCC, extended as a straight line). This is because the field current on the ZPF curve is the sum of (a) the field current required to produce the internal EMF on the air-gap line, and (b) a constant field current ΔI_f required to overcome the armature reaction, which is constant at fixed I_a. 2. **Property 2 — Constant horizontal shift:** The horizontal distance between the ZPF curve and the air-gap line is **constant and equal to ΔI_f** (the field current equivalent of armature reaction at rated current) — this is because at fixed I_a, the armature reaction is fixed. **Construction Steps (5 marks):** 1. On the ZPF curve (plotted at I_a = I_rated), mark point **P** where V = V_rated. 2. From **P**, draw a **horizontal line PQ** of length = V_rated (so that Q is on the X-axis, directly above the origin O). 3. From **P**, draw a line **PR** *parallel to the air-gap line* (linear OCC tangent), extending back toward the Y-axis. 4. Mark the intersection **R** — this is the **shifted origin O'**. 5. Measure **OO'** (horizontal) = field current ΔI_f needed to overcome armature reaction. 6. Measure **PQ** (vertical) = I_rated × X_p = voltage drop across Potier reactance. 7. The triangle **O P R** (with R = O') is the **Potier triangle**. **Key Insight (1 mark):** The Potier triangle has constant dimensions (for a fixed I_a) and can be "slid" along the OCC to construct the internal EMF characteristic. This is the foundation of the Potier method's accuracy. --- #### (b) Comparison Table (7 marks) | Aspect | EMF Method | MMF Method | **Potier Method** | |---|---|---|---| | **Data Required** | X_s (saturated) | X_AR | OCC, SCC, ZPF tests | | **Approach** | Uses single reactance X_s | Uses equivalent MMF for AR | Separates X_p and AR MMF | | **Accuracy** | Poor (over-estimates VR) | Better, but uses X_s | **Best, especially for salient pole** | | **Saturation** | Approximated by X_s variation | Ignored in MMF domain | **Accurately handled** (uses shifted OCC) | | **Complexity** | Simple, fast | Moderate | **High (needs ZPF test)** | | **Field Current** | Not directly obtained | Obtained | **Directly obtained** | | **KTU Preference** | ❌ | ⚠️ | ✅ | **Advantages of Potier Method (2 marks):** 1. **Highest accuracy** among classical methods, especially for salient-pole machines where AR is not purely demagnetizing. 2. **Directly yields the no-load excitation** required for a given load, which is essential for **voltage regulator and AVR design**. **Disadvantages of Potier Method (2 marks):** 1. Requires **three separate tests** (OCC, SCC, ZPF), making it more time-consuming and equipment-intensive. 2. **ZPF load** (pure inductive load) is often difficult to arrange at high power ratings in a lab; pure reactor banks are bulky and must be carefully tuned to maintain **exactly 0 p.f. lagging**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Sign Error in Lagging/Leading p.f.:** The term $V\sin\phi \pm I_a X_p$ uses **+ for lagging** and **− for leading**. Mixing these up will produce a wrong E' and incorrect regulation. (2–3 marks lost) > > 2. **Per-Phase vs Line-Value Confusion:** The OCC and ZPF data are often given as **line values**, but the phasor formula uses **phase values**. Always divide line-to-line voltage by √3 before applying the formula. Many students forget and end up with a regulation value that's 73% too high. (3–4 marks lost) > > 3. **Rₐ Neglect vs. Inclusion:** For a **5 kVA lab machine**, Rₐ is ~1 Ω and CANNOT be neglected. The examiner often marks down when Rₐ is ignored. Include Rₐ in the real-axis term. (1–2 marks lost) > > 4. **Shifted OCC Origin Confusion:** Students often look up E' on the **original** OCC instead of the **shifted** OCC. This gives a *smaller* field current and *under-estimates* the regulation. (3 marks lost) > > 5. **Wrong ΔI_f Direction:** ΔI_f is the **horizontal** shift to the **right**, not the left. The shifted OCC is offset to the right because the field current must first overcome AR before producing E' on the air-gap line. (1 mark lost) > > 6. **Forgetting the second ΔI_f:** The total field current is $I_{f,total} = I_{f,OCC}(E') + \Delta I_f$ — the $\Delta I_f$ is added **only once** (not twice). Some students add it twice and over-estimate the regulation. (2 marks lost) > > 7. **Units in X_p Calculation:** X_p = (vertical drop in V) / (rated I_a in A) gives Ω/phase. Mixing A and V without division will yield a wrong X_p. (1 mark lost) --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Potier Method** > > ✅ **Three Tests:** OCC, SCC, ZPF (all at synchronous speed). > > ✅ **ZPF Curve Properties:** (1) Parallel to air-gap line; (2) Constant horizontal shift = ΔI_f. > > ✅ **Potier Triangle Sides:** Horizontal = ΔI_f (armature reaction field current); Vertical = I_a X_p (Potier reactance drop). > > ✅ **X_p Calculation:** $X_p = \text{vertical side (V)} / I_{rated} (A)$, in Ω/phase. > > ✅ **E' Formula (lagging):** $E' = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi + I_a X_p)^2}$ > > ✅ **E' Formula (leading):** Replace `+ I_a X_p` with `− I_a X_p` in the quadrature term. > > ✅ **Shifted OCC Origin O':** Located at (ΔI_f, I_a X_p) from the original origin. > > ✅ **Two field-current components:** I_f' (from shifted OCC at E') + ΔI_f (to overcome AR) = I_f,total. > > ✅ **E₀ Lookup:** Always from the **original** OCC at I_f,total. > > ✅ **Regulation Formula:** $VR = (E_0 - V)/V \times 100\%$, where E₀ and V must be in the **same units** (both per-phase or both line). > > ✅ **Sign of VR:** +ve for lagging p.f. (terminal voltage rises when load removed); -ve for leading p.f. (terminal voltage falls). > > ✅ **Why preferred for salient-pole machines:** AR in salient-pole machines is not purely demagnetizing (has cross-magnetizing component too); Potier method's empirical ZPF approach captures this better than analytical methods. > > ✅ **Lab Equipment:** DC motor or 3-phase IM as prime mover; 3-phase variable auto-transformer; 3-phase reactor bank for ZPF; DC field supply with rheostat; measuring instruments (V, I, p.f. meters). > > ✅ **Safety:** Ensure field circuit is energized only after prime mover reaches synchronous speed; never open-field while running (overvoltage hazard); never operate at voltages above nameplate rating.

topics.title: V curves and Inverted V curves of synchronous machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL507 - AC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** V curves and Inverted V curves of synchronous machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **V-curve** of a synchronous machine is the locus of the armature current $I_a$ plotted against the DC field current $I_f$ at a **constant real mechanical load** (motor) or **constant real power output** (generator), with the machine operating at **synchronous speed** and at a **constant terminal voltage**. The shape of the curve resembles the letter **"V"**, hence the name.

An **Inverted V-curve** (also called the **Compounding Curve** or **Reactive Power Capability Curve**) is the locus of the field current $I_f$ plotted against the armature current $I_a$ for various constant values of **load power factor (cos φ)** at a fixed terminal voltage and synchronous speed. Equivalently, it is the family of curves of constant $I_a$ plotted against $I_f$ representing the boundary between the **underexcited (lagging pf)** and **overexcited (leading pf)** regions of operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim, learn this for the 3-mark question):**
> *"The V-curve of a synchronous machine is the variation of armature current with field current at constant load, while the inverted V-curve is the variation of field current with armature current at constant power factor, both at rated terminal voltage and synchronous speed."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tightrope Walker" Picture

Imagine a person walking on a tightrope while holding a balancing pole. The **tightrope is the real power (P)** being transferred — it must be held constant. The **horizontal position of the pole** is the field current $I_f$, and the **tilt of the pole** represents reactive power $Q$:

- When the pole is **vertical** (centered) → no tilt, no reactive power → **Unity Power Factor** → the walker needs **minimum effort** to balance → armature current $I_a$ is **minimum**.
- When the pole is **tilted forward** → it pulls reactive power from the system (overexcited, leading pf).
- When the pole is **tilted backward** → it pushes reactive power into the system (underexcited, lagging pf).

The walker's muscles (armature current) must work **harder** the more the pole is tilted → $I_a$ rises on either side of the vertical position. Plotting $I_a$ vs $I_f$ gives a **V-shape**. The "deepest point of the V" corresponds to **unity pf** — the **Normal Excitation Point**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> - **Synchronous reactance** $X_s$ — assumed constant in the unsaturated region; **per-phase value in Ohms (Ω)**.
> - **Rated terminal voltage** $V_L$ (line-to-line) and $V_{ph}$ (phase voltage) — typically **400 V / 230 V** for laboratory machines.
> - **Rated armature current** $I_{a(rated)}$ in **Amperes (A)**.
> - **DC field current** $I_f$ in **Amperes (A)**, separately excited.
> - **Power factor** $\cos \phi$ — dimensionless, range $-1$ to $+1$.
> - **Pull-out torque limit** — defines the steady-state stability boundary on the inverted V-curve.
> - **Stator copper loss** = $3 I_a^2 R_a$ — the reason for "minimum-loss" operation at unity pf.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Phasor Theory

For a synchronous machine operating at synchronous speed, the **internal EMF** $E_f$ (induced by the DC field $I_f$) and the **terminal voltage** $V$ are related by the armature equation:

$$E_f = V + I_a (R_a + j X_s)$$

At no-load, $I_a \approx 0$, so $E_f \approx V$. As the field is increased from zero, $E_f$ rises along the **Open-Circuit Characteristic (OCC)** — a saturation curve.

Under load, with $I_f$ varying at constant mechanical power, $E_f$ varies in magnitude (but the load angle $\delta$ also adjusts) so that the real-power balance is maintained.

### 2.1.1 The Power Constraint (Constant P)

For a round-rotor synchronous motor, ignoring $R_a$:

$$P = \frac{3 \, V_{ph} \, E_f}{X_s} \sin \delta \quad \text{(per-phase equivalent)}$$

Equivalently in three-phase form:

$$P = \frac{V_L \, E_{fL}}{X_s} \sin \delta$$

If $P$ is held **constant**, then as $E_f$ (i.e., $I_f$) is changed, the load angle $\delta$ must change in the inverse sense: **larger $E_f$ → smaller $\delta$**, and vice versa.

### 2.1.2 The Reactive Power Expression

The reactive power $Q$ delivered/absorbed by the machine (per-phase, motor convention) is:

$$Q = \frac{V_{ph} \, E_f}{X_s} \cos \delta - \frac{V_{ph}^2}{X_s}$$

When $\cos \phi = 1$ (unity pf at the terminals), the armature current is purely along the voltage phasor, so the demagnetising/armature-reaction component of $I_a$ (which is 90° to $V$) is zero. This defines the **Normal Excitation Condition**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the V-Curve Construction

1. **Fix the mechanical load** on the synchronous motor (e.g., $P = P_1$).
2. **Run the machine at synchronous speed** $N_s = 120 f / P$ (with $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$ in India).
3. **Apply rated terminal voltage** $V_L$ to the stator.
4. **Vary the DC field current $I_f$** in small steps (say, from $0.5 I_{f(nom)}$ to $1.5 I_{f(nom)}$).
5. At each value of $I_f$, **read the armature current $I_a$** and the **power factor $\cos \phi$** (from wattmeter + voltmeter + ammeter).
6. **Plot $I_a$ (Y-axis) vs $I_f$ (X-axis)** — the curve has a **V-shape** with minimum at unity pf.
7. **Repeat for other loads** ($P_2, P_3, \ldots$) — a **family of V-curves** is obtained, each shifted upward and to the right with increasing load.

## 2.3 The Inverted V-Curve — Stability Boundary

The **inverted V-curve** is obtained by plotting the locus of points of **constant power factor** on the $I_f$–$I_a$ plane for a fixed terminal voltage. The salient features are:

- **Locus of unity pf** → the **Normal Excitation Curve**.
- **Locus of constant $P$** → the **V-curves themselves** (each parabola-like).
- **Constant pf lines** are **radial straight lines** through the origin.
- The **envelope of stable operation** (steady-state stability limit, $\delta = 90°$ for cylindrical rotor) defines the right-hand boundary of the inverted-V region.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear in every KTU problem on this topic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = 120 f / P$ | rpm |
| 2 | Armature EMF equation | $E_f = V + I_a(R_a + j X_s)$ | per-phase, V |
| 3 | Real power (cylindrical rotor, ignore $R_a$) | $P = \dfrac{3 V_{ph} E_f}{X_s} \sin \delta$ | W (per-phase); $\times 3$ for 3-φ |
| 4 | Reactive power (motor) | $Q = \dfrac{3 V_{ph} E_f}{X_s} \cos \delta - \dfrac{3 V_{ph}^2}{X_s}$ | VAR |
| 5 | Real power (3-phase, line values) | $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_a \cos \phi$ | W |
| 6 | Reactive power (3-phase) | $Q = \sqrt{3} V_L I_a \sin \phi$ | VAR |
| 7 | Armature current at given $P,Q$ | $I_a = \dfrac{\sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L}$ | A |
| 8 | Condition for unity pf | $E_f \cos \delta = V_{ph}$ (ignoring $R_a$) | — |
| 9 | Field-current to EMF relation (OCC) | $E_f \propto I_f$ (linear, unsaturated region) | V/A |
| 10 | Stability limit | $\delta_{\max} = 90°$ (cylindrical rotor) | degrees |
| 11 | Power angle at normal excitation | $\cos \delta_0 = V_{ph} / E_{f0}$ | — |
| 12 | Synchronous impedance | $Z_s = \sqrt{R_a^2 + X_s^2}$ | Ω |
| 13 | Pull-out (max) power | $P_{\max} = 3 V_{ph} E_f / X_s$ | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Caution on table syntax:** All absolute-value bars have been rendered as vertical separators only. The reader is expected to treat $V \cos \delta$ etc. as scalar products, not as pipes.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power-factor correction:** Synchronous motors operated **over-excited** behave as synchronous condensers, supplying leading reactive power to industrial loads (steel plants, arc furnaces). The V-curve helps the operator **select the correct $I_f$** to achieve a target pf.
- **Reactive power management in grid substations** — the inverted V-curve defines the **P-Q capability chart** of a generator.
- **Voltage regulation** of long transmission lines.
- **Industrial drives** where constant-speed, variable-pf operation is required (e.g., rubber mills, cement kilns).
- **Laboratory demonstration** of the duality of real vs reactive power control in a single machine.
