## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between the `ADD` and `ADC` instructions of the 8086 microprocessor. State one situation where `ADC` is mandatory.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | `ADD dest, src` | `ADC dest, src` |
|---|---|---|
| Operation performed | $\text{dest} \leftarrow \text{dest} + \text{src}$ | $\text{dest} \leftarrow \text{dest} + \text{src} + CF$ |
| Carry flag usage | Not added | Added explicitly |
| When mandatory | When no prior carry exists | **Multi-precision arithmetic** (e.g., adding two 32-bit numbers using 16-bit registers) — `ADC` is mandatory to propagate the carry from the low-word addition into the high-word addition. **(1 mark)** |

Marks breakdown: 1 mark for the difference table, 1 mark for the operation equation, 1 mark for the multi-precision example.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q2.** Three 16-bit numbers are stored in registers `BX = 8000H`, `CX = 8000H`, and `DX = 8001H`. What is the content of `AX` and the Carry Flag after executing: `MOV AX, BX / ADD AX, CX / ADD AX, DX`?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. After `MOV AX, BX`: `AX = 8000H`, `CF = 0`. **(1 mark)**
2. After `ADD AX, CX`: `AX = 8000H + 8000H = 0000H`, `CF = 1`. **(1 mark)**
3. After `ADD AX, DX`: `AX = 0000H + 8001H = 8001H`, `CF = 0`. **(1 mark)**

**Final:** `AX = 8001H`, `CF = 0`. True 17-bit sum = `1·2¹⁶ + 0 = 10000H` (the carry from step 2 is silently lost).

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** — *Module Experiment 8*

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the algorithm to add three 16-bit numbers stored in registers `BX`, `CX`, and `DX`, and store the result in `AX`. Show the state of the Carry Flag at each step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the 8086 assembly language program for the above task. Verify your program for the test data: `BX = FFFFH`, `CX = 0001H`, `DX = 0002H`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q A (a) — 7 marks

**Algorithm in pseudocode (3 marks):**
1. Load the first 16-bit number from `BX` into `AX` using `MOV AX, BX`.
2. Add the second number from `CX` into `AX` using `ADD AX, CX`; this may set `CF = 1`.
3. Add the third number from `DX` into `AX` using `ADD AX, DX`; this may set `CF` again.
4. Halt the processor with `HLT`.
5. The final answer resides in `AX`, and `CF` holds the carry from the last `ADD`.

**Flowchart (2 marks):** See *Section 4.2* above — Strategy A branch.

**State table for `BX=FFFFH, CX=0001H, DX=0002H` (2 marks):**

| Step | Instruction | AX (H) | CF |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `MOV AX, BX` | `FFFF` | 0 |
| 2 | `ADD AX, CX` | `0000` | 1 |
| 3 | `ADD AX, DX` | `0002` | 0 |

#### Model Solution to Q A (b) — 7 marks

**8086 Assembly Program (4 marks):**

```asm
; PROGRAM: ADD THREE 16-BIT NUMBERS
; ASSUMPTIONS: NUM1 IS IN BX, NUM2 IN CX, NUM3 IN DX
; RESULT WILL BE IN AX
; ASSEMBLER: TASM / MASM

.MODEL SMALL
.8086
.STACK 100H
.DATA
    PROMPT DB 'THE SUM IS: $'
.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV  AX, @DATA
    MOV  DS, AX

    MOV  AX, BX       ; AX <- NUM1     (1 mark)
    ADD  AX, CX       ; AX <- AX + NUM2  (1 mark)
    ADD  AX, DX       ; AX <- AX + NUM3  (1 mark)

    MOV  AH, 4CH      ; Return to DOS
    INT  21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN
```

**Verification (3 marks):**
- For `BX=FFFFH, CX=0001H, DX=0002H`:
- `AX = FFFFH → 0000H (CF=1) → 0002H (CF=0)`. ✓
- The program correctly computes the 16-bit sum, with `CF` reflecting the last operation's overflow.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram how the 8086 datapath performs a register-to-register `ADD` operation. Highlight the role of the **ALU**, the **Flags Register**, and the **temporary register** in this process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design and write an 8086 assembly language program that adds three 16-bit numbers stored in registers `SI`, `DI`, and `BP`, and stores the **full 17-bit result** in the register pair `DX:AX`. Explain why `ADC DX, 0` is required after each `ADD AX, ...` instruction. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q B (a) — 7 marks

**Block-diagram description (3 marks):** The 8086's internal datapath consists of:
- A **16-bit ALU** (built from ~6,000 transistors) that performs arithmetic and logic.
- Two **operand input latches** (often called the *temporary* and *accumulator* paths).
- The **FLAGS register** (16 bits, of which 9 are active) that latches ALU status at the end of every operation.
- A **Bus Interface Unit (BIU)** that fetches operands from the 16-bit internal data bus.

**Step-by-step role of each block during `ADD AX, BX` (3 marks):**
1. **BIU** fetches the `ADD` opcode from the instruction queue.
2. The source operand `BX` is placed on the **internal data bus** and latched into the **ALU's right-side input**.
3. The destination `AX` is latched into the **ALU's left-side input**.
4. **ALU** computes the sum bit-by-bit using an internal **ripple-carry adder**.
5. Result bits are simultaneously: (i) driven onto the bus to update `AX`, and (ii) fed into the **flags-generation logic** which sets `CF, ZF, SF, OF, PF, AF` accordingly.

**Why a temporary register is needed (1 mark):** In `ADD AX, BX`, both operands come from registers, but the 8086 uses a hidden internal **temporary register** so that the original `AX` value is preserved until the new value is computed (avoiding a race condition on the bus).

#### Model Solution to Q B (b) — 7 marks

**Program (4 marks):**

```asm
.MODEL SMALL
.8086
.STACK 100H
.CODE
MAIN PROC
    CLC                  ; Clear carry flag (1 mark)
    MOV  AX, SI          ; AX <- NUM1
    ADD  AX, DI          ; AX <- NUM1 + NUM2 (CF may be 1)
    ADC  DX, 0           ; DX <- 0 + 0 + CF  (capture spillover) (1 mark)
    ADD  AX, BP          ; AX <- previous + NUM3
    ADC  DX, 0           ; DX <- previous + new carry (1 mark)

    MOV  AH, 4CH
    INT  21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN
```

**Explanation of `ADC DX, 0` (3 marks):**
- After each 16-bit `ADD AX, ...`, the result fits in `AX` but the **carry out of bit 15** would be **discarded** if not captured.
- `ADC DX, 0` adds **zero plus the current carry flag** to `DX`. Since `DX` is the high word, this effectively **funnels the spilled bit upward** by exactly 2¹⁶ — the weight of one carry.
- Without `ADC DX, 0`, the final `DX:AX` would equal $\left((A+B+C) \bmod 2^{16}\right)$ rather than the full 17-bit sum.
- If three numbers each equal `FFFFH`, the true sum is `2FFFDH`. `AX = FFFDH` and `DX = 0002H` (two carries spilled out cumulatively). Verifying: `DX·2¹⁶ + AX = 2·65536 + 65533 = 196605 = 3·65535`. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting that `ADD` modifies `CF`:** Many students write three `ADD`s and assume `CF` retains the *first* addition's carry. It does not — the *second* `ADD` overwrites `CF` based on the *second* addition only. To chain carries, you must use `ADC` for every addition *after the first*.
> 2. **Treating the registers as signed 8-bit:** Some students compute in `AL` and forget that `AX` is **16 bits**, leading to truncation at 255 instead of 65,535.
> 3. **Omitting the `HLT` instruction:** Examiners deduct marks if the program does not include a termination directive (`HLT` in `.COM` programs or `INT 21H / AH=4CH` in `.EXE` programs).
> 4. **Reversing source/destination:** `MOV AX, BX` is valid; `MOV BX, AX` would overwrite `BX` and break the experiment if `BX` is needed later. Always preserve the original source.
> 5. **Not clearing `CF` before `ADC`:** If the previous operation left `CF = 1` (e.g., from `SUB` or a `CMP`), the first `ADC` will spuriously include that carry. Use `CLC` (Clear Carry) before starting.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- [x] **16-bit register** = range **0 to 65,535 unsigned**; range **-32,768 to +32,767 signed** (2's complement).
- [x] **Sum of three 16-bit numbers** mathematically requires up to **18 bits**; worst case is `2FFFDH`.
- [x] **`ADD dest, src`** performs `dest ← dest + src` and updates **all six** arithmetic flags.
- [x] **`ADC dest, src`** performs `dest ← dest + src + CF` — the **only** way to propagate carry.
- [x] To capture a carry, use `ADC DX, 0` (the canonical "carry-sniffer" idiom).
- [x] The 8086 ALU is a **ripple-carry adder** — carries propagate from bit 0 to bit 15.
- [x] **Result modulo 2¹⁶** is what stays in `AX`; the **Carry Flag** indicates whether the true sum exceeded 16 bits.
- [x] Always end the program with **`HLT`** (standalone) or **`INT 21H / AH=4CH`** (DOS terminate).
- [x] **General-purpose registers usable:** `AX, BX, CX, DX, SI, DI, BP`. Avoid `SP` (Stack Pointer) and `IP` (Instruction Pointer) — modifying them crashes the program.
- [x] The `MOV` instruction is **non-destructive** for the source, but `ADD`/`ADC` are **destructive** for the destination (the original value is overwritten).
- [x] **Exam hot-spot:** KTU often asks you to "predict the value of `AX` and the Carry Flag" for specific hex inputs. Practice the **byte-level addition** in hex: add the low bytes first, propagate any carry to the high bytes.
- [x] **Engineering context:** In an AC Machines Lab, three-register addition maps to **summing three phase currents or voltages** in a three-phase stator. The carry flag acts as a **saturation alarm** for over-current protection.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Add 2-BCD numbers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEEL507 - AC MACHINES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 8 > - **Topic:** Add 2-BCD numbers # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In Digital Arithmetic and Logic Systems, **BCD (Binary Coded Decimal)** is a class of binary encoding where each decimal digit (0–9) is represented by its own fixed **4-bit binary sequence**. When two valid BCD-encoded operands are added, the resulting binary sum may exceed the decimal equivalent of **9**, thereby producing an invalid BCD digit (any value from **1010** to **1111**). To restore numerical validity, a hardware/software correction routine must be applied — this is universally known as the **"Add 6 (0110) Correction"** algorithm. Mathematically, for any 4-bit BCD operand pair $(B_1, B_2)$: $$S_{binary} = B_1 + B_2$$ $$S_{BCD} = \begin{cases} S_{binary} & \text{if } S_{binary} \leq 9 \\ S_{binary} + 6 & \text{if } S_{binary} > 9 \text{ OR } C_{out} = 1 \end{cases}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** BCD arithmetic is foundational for digital panel meters, digital relays, motor protection relays, numerical control (NC) machine displays, and the decimal display logic embedded in modern Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) used in AC Machines Labs. Mastering this experiment demonstrates your ability to interface low-level arithmetic logic with industrial display hardware. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **mechanical abacus** that has only 4 beads per rod (instead of 10). - The abacus can only count from **0 to 9** per rod. - If you push **8 + 7 = 15** beads, the abacus physically cannot represent 15 on one rod (max is 9). - So, just like a human counting on fingers, you "carry over" 1 to the next rod (representing the tens place) and reset the current rod. - The remaining value is $15 - 10 = 5$. - However, the binary addition gave you $8 + 7 = 15$ (binary: $1000 + 0111 = 1111$). - To convert the binary sum 15 back to valid BCD 5 with a carry, you add **6 (0110)** because: $15 + 6 = 21 \rightarrow$ Lower nibble = 5, Higher nibble gets a carry. **Real-World Engineering Analogy:** Think of the digital display on a weighing machine in a grocery store. Even if the internal microcontroller speaks "pure binary," the display MUST show **"2.50 kg"** in proper decimal — not "2.8 kg" or some weird alphanumeric glitch. BCD addition ensures the displayed decimal number is always valid for human reading. > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition (Recall-Ready):** BCD addition = Binary addition of two 4-bit nibbles + conditional correction by **adding 6 (0110)** when the sum exceeds 9 or a carry is generated. ## 1.3 Boundary Constants - **Valid BCD Digit Range:** $0000_2$ to $1001_2$ (**0 to 9**) - **Invalid BCD Digit Range:** $1010_2$ to $1111_2$ (**10 to 15**) — requires correction - **Correction Constant:** $\mathbf{6_{10} = 0110_2}$ - **Largest Single-Digit BCD Sum:** $9 + 9 + 1_{carry} = 19_{10} = 10011_2$ — produces a 5-bit result - **Standard Decimal-Binary Equivalents Table:** | Decimal | BCD Nibble | Binary Equivalent | Decimal | BCD Nibble | Binary Equivalent | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **0** | 0000 | 0000 | **5** | 0101 | 0101 | | **1** | 0001 | 0001 | **6** | 0110 | 0110 | | **2** | 0010 | 0010 | **7** | 0111 | 0111 | | **3** | 0011 | 0011 | **8** | 1000 | 1000 | | **4** | 0100 | 0100 | **9** | 1001 | 1001 | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Theorem — The BCD Correction Algorithm The BCD addition correction logic follows a deterministic, repeatable 4-step procedure. Each step is non-negotiable and must be evaluated in sequence. ### Step 1 — Initialize Inputs Load two valid BCD numbers, each stored as a nibble (4-bit group). Verify that every nibble falls within the legal range $[0000_2, 1001_2]$. ### Step 2 — Perform Binary Addition Execute a standard 4-bit ripple-carry binary addition: $$S_3 S_2 S_1 S_0 = A_3 A_2 A_1 A_0 + B_3 B_2 B_1 B_0$$ Record both the **4-bit sum nibble** and the **carry-out bit** $C_{out}$. ### Step 3 — Apply Correction Conditions Evaluate **two independent conditions** simultaneously: **Condition X (Illegal Lower Nibble):** Is the resulting 4-bit sum $> 1001_2$? - $S_3 \cdot S_2 + S_3 \cdot S_1 = 1 \Rightarrow$ Add 6 **Condition Y (Carry Generated):** Did the addition generate a carry-out $C_{out} = 1$? - $C_{out} = 1 \Rightarrow$ Add 6 If **either** Condition X OR Condition Y is TRUE, the **correction flag** is set, and we add 6 (0110) to the 4-bit sum. ### Step 4 — Propagate Carry to Next Decade After correction, if the addition of 6 itself generates a new carry (this happens when pre-correction sum was between 10 and 15), this carry becomes the **tens-place BCD digit** of the final result. ## 2.2 Why Does Adding 6 Work? (The "How" Behind the Math) Binary addition inherently produces values from $0000_2$ (0) to $1111_2$ (15). - However, BCD demands each decade (units, tens, hundreds) wrap around at $1001_2$ (9), not at $1111_2$ (15). - The difference between these two wrap-around points is: $16 - 10 = \mathbf{6}$. - Therefore, adding 6 "skips" the 6 illegal codes (1010, 1011, 1100, 1101, 1110, 1111) and forces the binary counter into valid BCD territory. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Meaning | Range / Value | |:---:|:---|:---| | $A, B$ | Input BCD operands (nibbles) | $[0, 9]$ each | | $S_{bin}$ | Raw binary sum of A and B | $[0, 19]$ | | $C_{out}$ | Carry-out from 4-bit binary addition | $\{0, 1\}$ | | $K$ | Correction constant | $\mathbf{6_{10} = 0110_2}$ | | $S_{BCD}$ | Corrected BCD sum (lower nibble) | $[0, 9]$ | | $C_{final}$ | Carry propagated to next decade | $\{0, 1\}$ | **Master Correction Equation:** $$S_{BCD} = (A + B) + 6 \cdot (C_{out} \lor \text{ILL})$$ where $\text{ILL} = 1$ if $(A + B)_{bin} > 9$, else $\text{ILL} = 0$. **Correction Activation Boolean Function:** $$\text{CORRECT} = C_{out} + S_3 \cdot S_2 + S_3 \cdot S_1$$ ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application of BCD Addition | |:---|:---| | **AC Machines Lab** | Digital panel meters reading V, I, Hz, RPM (e.g., 230 V, 50 Hz displays) | | **Motor Protection Relays** | Digital overcurrent/overload setpoints displayed in decimal | | **VFD Keypads** | Operator enters frequency setpoints in decimal, internally stored as BCD | | **Digital Clocks / Timers** | Industrial process timers count seconds in BCD for LCD display | | **Positional Encoders** | Multi-digit absolute encoders output BCD for shaft position feedback | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example 1: 5 + 3 (No Correction Needed) **Given:** $A = 5_{10}$, $B = 3_{10}$ (both valid BCD) **Step 1 — Encode to BCD:** $$A = 0101_{BCD}, \quad B = 0011_{BCD}$$ **Step 2 — Perform binary addition:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\phantom{+}\ 0101 \\ &+\ 0011 \\ &\hline &\phantom{+}\ 1000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate correction conditions:** - Sum = $1000_2 = 8_{10} \leq 9$ → **Condition X = FALSE** - Carry-out = $0$ → **Condition Y = FALSE** **Step 4 — Apply correction:** No correction needed. Final answer: $0101_{BCD}$ representing **8**. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example 2: 7 + 8 (Correction by Lower Nibble) **Given:** $A = 7_{10}$, $B = 8_{10}$ **Step 1 — Encode to BCD:** $$A = 0111_{BCD}, \quad B = 1000_{BCD}$$ **Step 2 — Perform binary addition:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\phantom{+}\ 0111 \\ &+\ 1000 \\ &\hline &\phantom{+}\ 1111 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate correction conditions:** - Sum = $1111_2 = 15_{10} > 9$ → **Condition X = TRUE** - Carry-out = $0$ → **Condition Y = FALSE** - Overall: **Correction Required** **Step 4 — Apply correction by adding 6:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\phantom{+}\ 1111 \\ &+\ 0110 \\ &\hline &\ 1\ 0101 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Interpret result:** - Carry-out = **1** → tens place BCD digit - Lower nibble = $0101_2 = 5_{10}$ → units place BCD digit - Final BCD result: **$0001\ 0101_{BCD}$** = **15** ✓ ## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example 3: 9 + 9 (Maximum Single-Digit Case) **Given:** $A = 9_{10}$, $B = 9_{10}$ **Step 1 — Encode:** $$A = 1001_{BCD}, \quad B = 1001_{BCD}$$ **Step 2 — Binary addition:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\phantom{+}\ 1001 \\ &+\ 1001 \\ &\hline &\ 1\ 0010 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate conditions:** - Lower nibble sum = $0010_2 = 2 \leq 9$ → **Condition X = FALSE** - Carry-out = **1** → **Condition Y = TRUE** - Overall: **Correction Required (due to carry)** **Step 4 — Add 6 to lower nibble:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\phantom{+}\ 0010 \\ &+\ 0110 \\ &\hline &\phantom{+}\ 1000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Final result:** - Carry-out remains **1** → tens digit = $0001_{BCD}$ - Lower nibble = $1000_2 = 8_{10}$ → units digit = $1000_{BCD}$ - Final BCD result: **$0001\ 1000_{BCD}$** = **18** ✓ ## 3.4 Multi-Digit BCD Addition: 47 + 35 **Given:** Two 2-digit BCD numbers — A = 47, B = 35. **Step 1 — Encode into BCD nibbles:** $$A = 0100\ 0111_{BCD} \quad (4, 7)$$ $$B = 0011\ 0101_{BCD} \quad (3, 5)$$ **Step 2 — Add lower nibbles (units: 7 + 5):** $$ \begin{aligned} &\phantom{+}\ 0111 \\ &+\ 0101 \\ &\hline &\phantom{+}\ 1100 \end{aligned} $$ - Sum = $1100_2 = 12_{10} > 9$ → **Correct by adding 6:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\phantom{+}\ 1100 \\ &+\ 0110 \\ &\hline &\ 1\ 0010 \end{aligned} $$ - Units nibble result = $0010_{BCD}$ (= 2); Carry = **1** to tens place. **Step 3 — Add upper nibbles (tens: 4 + 3 + 1 carry):** $$ \begin{aligned} &\phantom{+}\ 0100 \\ &+\ 0011 \\ &+\ 0001 \\ &\hline &\phantom{+}\ 1000 \end{aligned} $$ - Sum = $1000_2 = 8_{10} \leq 9$ → **No correction needed.** - Tens nibble result = $1000_{BCD}$ (= 8); No further carry. **Step 4 — Assemble final result:** $$\text{Final BCD} = 1000\ 0010_{BCD} = \mathbf{82}$$ **Verification:** $47 + 35 = 82$ ✓ ## 3.5 Complete Correction Decision Matrix | Raw Sum (Decimal) | Raw Sum (Binary) | $C_{out}$ | Sum > 9? | Correction? | Add 6? | Final Result | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **0–9** | 0000–1001 | 0 | No | No | No | Same as raw | | **10** | 1010 | 0 | Yes | Yes | Yes | 1 0000 (= 10) | | **11** | 1011 | 0 | Yes | Yes | Yes | 1 0001 (= 11) | | **12** | 1100 | 0 | Yes | Yes | Yes | 1 0010 (= 12) | | **13** | 1101 | 0 | Yes | Yes | Yes | 1 0011 (= 13) | | **14** | 1110 | 0 | Yes | Yes | Yes | 1 0100 (= 14) | | **15** | 1111 | 0 | Yes | Yes | Yes | 1 0101 (= 15) | | **16** | 1 0000 | 1 | No* | Yes | Yes | 1 0110 (= 16) | | **17** | 1 0001 | 1 | No* | Yes | Yes | 1 0111 (= 17) | | **18** | 1 0010 | 1 | No* | Yes | Yes | 1 1000 (= 18) | | **19** | 1 0011 | 1 | No* | Yes | Yes | 1 1001 (= 19) | *Lower nibble ≤ 9, but $C_{out}$ alone triggers correction. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — BCD Addition Algorithm ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start: Load BCD Operands A and B]) --> validateA{Is A valid BCD
0 to 9?} validateA -->|No| errA[/Error: Invalid BCD Input/] validateA -->|Yes| validateB{Is B valid BCD
0 to 9?} validateB -->|No| errB[/Error: Invalid BCD Input/] validateB -->|Yes| binAdd[Perform 4-bit Binary Addition
S_bin = A + B] binAdd --> recordCO[Record Carry-Out C_out
and Lower Nibble S_3 S_2 S_1 S_0] recordCO --> checkCond{Check Correction Conditions:
C_out = 1 OR S > 9?} checkCond -->|No| noCorr[No Correction Needed
S_BCD = S_bin] checkCond -->|Yes| doCorr[Add 6 Correction
S_BCD = S_bin + 0110] doCorr --> genCarry{Does Add 6
Generate New Carry?} genCarry -->|Yes| setCarry[C_final = 1
Propagate to Next Decade] genCarry -->|No| noCarry[C_final = 0] noCorr --> output1[Output: BCD Result and Carry] setCarry --> output2[Output: BCD Result and Carry] noCarry --> output3[Output: BCD Result and Carry] output1 --> endNode([End]) output2 --> endNode output3 --> endNode ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hardware Implementation ```mermaid flowchart LR inpA[Input A: 4-bit BCD] --> adderUnit inpB[Input B: 4-bit BCD] --> adderUnit adderUnit[4-bit Binary
Ripple Carry Adder] --> rawSum[Raw Sum S: 4 bits] adderUnit --> coutLine[Carry-Out: 1 bit] rawSum --> detectBlock coutLine --> detectBlock detectBlock[Correction Detection Logic
C_out OR S3*S2 OR S3*S1] --> muxCtrl{MUX
Control Signal} const6[Constant Source
4-bit 0110] --> muxSel rawSum --> muxSel muxSel[2-to-1 Multiplexer] --> correctedSum[Corrected BCD Sum] muxCtrl --> muxSel detectBlock --> finalCO[Final Carry-Out
to Next Decade] ``` **Interpretation of Block Diagram:** 1. **4-bit Binary Ripple Carry Adder:** Accepts two BCD operands and produces a raw 4-bit sum plus carry-out. This is the "pure binary" path. 2. **Correction Detection Logic:** Implements the Boolean equation $C_{out} + S_3 S_2 + S_3 S_1$. Its output drives the multiplexer selector. 3. **2-to-1 Multiplexer:** Selects between the raw sum (no correction) and the raw sum + 6 (correction applied). The constant 0110 is hard-wired to one input. 4. **Final Carry-Out:** Propagates the tens-place digit to the next higher-order decade, enabling multi-digit BCD arithmetic (cascadable architecture). ## 4.3 Signal Flow Visualization — 7 + 8 Example ```mermaid flowchart LR A7[A = 0111
BCD 7] --> addStage[Binary Adder] B8[B = 1000
BCD 8] --> addStage addStage --> rawOut[Raw Sum: 1111
Carry-Out: 0] rawOut --> decision{Sum greater than 9?
YES} decision -->|Yes| addSix[Add Correction: 0110] addSix --> finalOut[Final BCD: 0001 0101
Equals 15] rawOut -.->|No| skipPath[Skip Correction] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: Define BCD. Why is a "correction by adding 6" necessary in BCD addition?** **Model Answer:** BCD (Binary Coded Decimal) is a numerical encoding system in which each decimal digit (0–9) is represented individually by a 4-bit binary code. The valid range for a single BCD nibble is $0000_2$ to $1001_2$ (0 to 9). When two BCD numbers are added using standard binary addition, the resulting 4-bit sum may take values from $0000_2$ to $1111_2$ (0 to 15). The codes $1010_2$ through $1111_2$ (10 to 15) are **invalid BCD codes**. To convert these illegal codes back into valid BCD and simultaneously generate a proper decimal carry, we add the binary constant $0110_2$ (decimal 6) to the result. This correction is necessary because the binary decade wraps at 16, while the BCD decade wraps at 10 — a difference of exactly **6**. --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: List the two conditions that trigger the BCD correction logic.** **Model Answer:** The BCD correction logic is triggered when **either** of the following two conditions is satisfied: 1. **Carry Condition:** A carry-out ($C_{out} = 1$) is generated from the 4-bit binary addition of the two BCD nibbles. 2. **Illegal Code Condition:** The 4-bit binary sum exceeds $1001_2$ (i.e., is in the range $1010_2$ to $1111_2$), making it an invalid BCD representation. If either condition is met, the constant $0110_2$ (decimal 6) is added to the lower nibble to obtain a valid BCD result. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: Perform the BCD addition of $A = 68_{10}$ and $B = 57_{10}$. Show all intermediate steps including binary encoding, nibble-wise addition, correction logic, and final verification.** #### Part (a) — Lower Nibble (Units Digit) Addition (7 Marks) **Solution:** **Step 1:** Encode units digits of A and B into 4-bit BCD: - Units of A = $8 \rightarrow 1000_{BCD}$ - Units of B = $7 \rightarrow 0111_{BCD}$ **Step 2:** Perform binary addition: $$ \begin{aligned} &\phantom{+}\ 1000 \\ &+\ 0111 \\ &\hline &\phantom{+}\ 1111 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Evaluate correction: - Sum = $1111_2 = 15_{10} > 9$ → **Correction Required** **Step 4:** Add correction constant 6: $$ \begin{aligned} &\phantom{+}\ 1111 \\ &+\ 0110 \\ &\hline &\ 1\ 0101 \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** Units nibble = $0101_{BCD}$ (= 5); Carry to tens = **1**. --- #### Part (b) — Upper Nibble (Tens Digit) Addition (7 Marks) **Solution:** **Step 1:** Encode tens digits: - Tens of A = $6 \rightarrow 0110_{BCD}$ - Tens of B = $5 \rightarrow 0101_{BCD}$ - Add incoming carry = $0001$ **Step 2:** Perform binary addition: $$ \begin{aligned} &\phantom{+}\ 0110 \\ &+\ 0101 \\ &+\ 0001 \\ &\hline &\phantom{+}\ 1100 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Evaluate correction: - Sum = $1100_2 = 12_{10} > 9$ → **Correction Required** **Step 4:** Add correction constant 6: $$ \begin{aligned} &\phantom{+}\ 1100 \\ &+\ 0110 \\ &\hline &\ 1\ 0010 \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** Tens nibble = $0010_{BCD}$ (= 2); Carry to hundreds = **1**. **Step 5:** Final assembled BCD result: $$\text{Result} = 0001\ 0010\ 0101_{BCD} = \mathbf{125_{10}}$$ **Verification:** $68 + 57 = 125$ ✓ --- ### Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: Design a combinational logic circuit that detects when BCD correction is required. Derive the Boolean expression for the correction signal and explain the function of each term.** #### Part (a) — Truth Table and Boolean Derivation (7 Marks) **Solution:** The correction signal $\text{CORRECT}$ must be HIGH when: 1. A carry-out is generated: $C_{out} = 1$, OR 2. The lower nibble sum $S = S_3 S_2 S_1 S_0$ is invalid (i.e., $> 1001_2$). **Truth Table for Invalid Sum Detection:** | $S_3$ | $S_2$ | $S_1$ | $S_0$ | Decimal Sum | Invalid? | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 10 | Yes | | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 11 | Yes | | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 12 | Yes | | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 13 | Yes | | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 14 | Yes | | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 15 | Yes | From K-map analysis, the **Invalid signal (ILL)** simplifies to: $$\text{ILL} = S_3 \cdot S_2 + S_3 \cdot S_1$$ **Complete Boolean Expression:** $$\boxed{\text{CORRECT} = C_{out} + S_3 \cdot S_2 + S_3 \cdot S_1}$$ --- #### Part (b) — Circuit Realization and Hardware Role (7 Marks) **Solution:** The combinational correction circuit consists of: - **One OR gate** (3-input): Combines $C_{out}$ with the two AND-gate outputs. - **Two AND gates** (2-input): Implement $S_3 \cdot S_2$ and $S_3 \cdot S_1$. The CORRECT signal drives the **select line of a 2-to-1 multiplexer**: - When CORRECT = 0: MUX passes raw sum directly → no correction. - When CORRECT = 1: MUX passes (raw sum + 0110) → corrected BCD output. **Engineering Significance:** In a real AC Machines Lab VFD or digital meter, this exact correction logic is implemented within the **DA (Decimal Adjust)** instruction of the microprocessor or as hardwired combinational logic in an ASIC. It guarantees that whatever decimal value the operator enters (e.g., 47 Hz frequency setpoint) is correctly displayed digit-by-digit on the 7-segment LCD without flicker or incorrect segments lighting up. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting to include the Carry condition:** Many students check only "$S > 9$" but miss that a carry-out alone (e.g., $9 + 9 = 18$, where lower nibble = 8 ≤ 9) also triggers correction. **Both** conditions must be OR-ed. > 2. **Skipping the add-6 step in multi-digit problems:** When correcting the upper nibble, students often forget that the **incoming carry from the lower nibble must be added BEFORE checking if correction is needed.** > 3. **Misinterpreting the final result:** BCD digits must be grouped as 4-bit nibbles. A common error is reading $10010_2$ as "18" in binary instead of treating it as BCD $0001\ 0010 = 12$. > 4. **Failing to verify:** Always perform a decimal sanity check at the end ($68 + 57 = 125$). Lost 1 mark per unchecked sub-question. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **BCD = 4-bit binary for each decimal digit.** Range: **0 to 9 only.** - ✅ **Correction constant = 6 (0110).** Triggered by carry-out OR sum > 9. - ✅ **Boolean Formula:** $\text{CORRECT} = C_{out} + S_3 S_2 + S_3 S_1$ - ✅ **Multi-digit BCD addition** is cascading: process least significant nibble first, propagate carry to next decade. - ✅ **Maximum single-digit BCD sum:** $9 + 9 + 1_{carry} = 19$, producing a 5-bit result that splits into two valid BCD nibbles. - ✅ **Hardware block:** Binary Adder → Correction Detector → Multiplexer (selects raw sum vs. sum + 6). - ✅ **Real-world relevance:** Digital panel meters, VFD displays, motor protection relays, industrial timers. - ✅ **Verification step:** Always cross-check your BCD result against expected decimal arithmetic. - ✅ **Common trap:** Carry-out alone (without sum > 9) still requires correction — e.g., $9+9$ case.

topics.title: Subtract two 8-bit numbers with or without borrow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL507 - AC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 8
> - **Topic:** Subtract two 8-bit numbers with or without borrow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **8-bit Subtraction** in microprocessor programming refers to the arithmetic operation of computing the difference between two unsigned or signed 8-bit operands using the **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** of the processor. In the **8085 microprocessor**, this is accomplished through instructions such as `SUB r`, `SUI data`, `SBB r`, and `SBI data`, where the presence or absence of a **borrow (incoming Carry Flag)** determines the result.

The **8085 microprocessor** is an **8-bit processor** built using **NMOS technology**, operating with a **+5V** supply, a **3.072 MHz** internal clock (derived from an external **6.144 MHz** crystal), and a 16-bit address bus capable of accessing **64 KB** of memory. Each internal register — Accumulator (`A`), `B`, `C`, `D`, `E`, `H`, `L` — is exactly **8 bits** wide, meaning any arithmetic operation natively operates on **8-bit operands**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two-Bucket Problem"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:** Imagine you have two buckets, one filled with **12 apples** and another with **5 apples**. Subtraction without borrow is simply: take 12, remove 5, you are left with 7. However, what if you only had **3 apples** in the first bucket and wanted to remove **7 apples**? You cannot remove 7 from 3. So you "borrow" 1 from the next higher place value (like the tens column in decimal, or the next bit position in binary), turning your 3 into 13 (in decimal) — only then can you subtract 7 to get 6. This "borrowing" is what the **Carry Flag (CF)** signals in the 8085.

In the **8085 architecture**, the **Carry Flag (CY)** is **set (CY = 1)** when a borrow occurs during subtraction, and **reset (CY = 0)** when no borrow is required. This single bit becomes the "memory" of whether a borrow was generated, and is the foundation for multi-byte (16-bit, 24-bit, 32-bit) subtraction.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key 8085 Microprocessor Specifications** relevant to this experiment:
> - **Data Bus Width:** **8 bits** (bidirectional)
> - **Address Bus Width:** **16 bits**
> - **Register Size:** **8 bits** each
> - **Operating Frequency:** **3.072 MHz** (internal)
> - **Supply Voltage:** **+5V DC**
> - **Memory Address Space:** **64 KB**
> - **Flags Register (F):** 8 bits, but only **5 bits are active** (S, Z, AC, P, CY)

## 1.4 Subtraction Modes in 8085

The 8085 supports **two distinct subtraction modes**:

| Mode | Instruction Family | Borrow In? | Typical Use |
|------|------------------|------------|-------------|
| **Without Borrow** | `SUB r`, `SUI data` | No | Single-byte subtraction, first byte of multi-byte |
| **With Borrow** | `SBB r`, `SBI data` | Yes (CY added) | Multi-byte subtraction (2nd, 3rd, ... byte) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8085 Flags Register — The Heart of Borrow Detection

The 8085 has a dedicated **8-bit Flags Register**, but only **5 flags** are implemented in hardware:

| Bit Position | Flag Name | Symbol | Relevance to Subtraction |
|--------------|-----------|--------|--------------------------|
| **D7** | Sign Flag | **S** | Set if result's MSB = 1 (negative in signed interpretation) |
| **D6** | Zero Flag | **Z** | Set if result = 00H |
| **D5** | Auxiliary Carry | **AC** | Set if borrow from bit 3 to bit 4 (for BCD correction) |
| **D4** | Parity Flag | **P** | Set if even number of 1s in result (not borrow-related) |
| **D3** | Carry Flag | **CY** | **Set if borrow generated from MSB (the borrow indicator!)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** In subtraction, the **Carry Flag acts as a Borrow Flag**. When the minuend is smaller than the subtrahend, the ALU cannot subtract directly — it must borrow from an imaginary 9th bit, and this borrow is recorded as **CY = 1**. This is the **inverse polarity** compared to addition, where CY = 1 indicates an overflow OUT, but in subtraction, CY = 1 indicates a borrow IN.

## 2.2 The Four Subtraction Instructions — Structured Logic

### **Group 1: Subtraction WITHOUT Borrow**

**Step 1 — `SUB r` (Subtract Register from Accumulator)**
- **Operation:** `A ← A − r` (where `r` can be B, C, D, E, H, L, or A)
- **Encoding:** 1 byte (register addressing mode)
- **Borrow Behavior:** If `A < r`, then **CY = 1**; else **CY = 0**
- **Example:** `MOV A, B` then `SUB C` → A = B − C

**Step 2 — `SUI data` (Subtract Immediate from Accumulator)**
- **Operation:** `A ← A − data` (8-bit constant follows opcode)
- **Encoding:** 2 bytes (opcode + immediate operand)
- **Use Case:** When subtrahend is a known constant
- **Example:** `SUI 05H` → subtracts 05H from A

### **Group 2: Subtraction WITH Borrow**

**Step 3 — `SBB r` (Subtract Register with Borrow)**
- **Operation:** `A ← A − r − CY`
- **Why "with borrow"?** The current state of CY is included in the subtraction. This is essential for **multi-byte arithmetic**.
- **Example (16-bit):** To subtract a 16-bit number in `HL` from a 16-bit number in `DE`:
  - Low byte: `MOV A, E` → `SBB L` (CY may become 1)
  - High byte: `MOV A, D` → `SBB H` (CY from previous step is auto-included)

**Step 4 — `SBI data` (Subtract Immediate with Borrow)**
- **Operation:** `A ← A − data − CY`
- **Use Case:** Multi-byte subtraction with a known constant

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Table — All Subtraction Operations and Their Effect on Flags**

| Operation | Mnemonic | Opcode | Bytes | Machine Cycles | Result Storage | CY after | Z after |
|-----------|----------|--------|-------|----------------|----------------|----------|---------|
| $A \leftarrow A - r$ | `SUB B` | `90` | 1 | 1 | A | Set if borrow | Set if A=0 |
| $A \leftarrow A - r$ | `SUB C` | `91` | 1 | 1 | A | Set if borrow | Set if A=0 |
| $A \leftarrow A - r$ | `SUB D` | `92` | 1 | 1 | A | Set if borrow | Set if A=0 |
| $A \leftarrow A - r$ | `SUB E` | `93` | 1 | 1 | A | Set if borrow | Set if A=0 |
| $A \leftarrow A - r$ | `SUB H` | `94` | 1 | 1 | A | Set if borrow | Set if A=0 |
| $A \leftarrow A - r$ | `SUB L` | `95` | 1 | 1 | A | Set if borrow | Set if A=0 |
| $A \leftarrow A - A$ | `SUB A` | `97` | 1 | 1 | A | Always **0** | Always **1** |
| $A \leftarrow A - \text{data}$ | `SUI 8-bit` | `D6 xx` | 2 | 2 | A | Set if borrow | Set if A=0 |
| $A \leftarrow A - r - CY$ | `SBB B` | `98` | 1 | 1 | A | Set if borrow | Set if A=0 |
| $A \leftarrow A - r - CY$ | `SBB C` | `99` | 1 | 1 | A | Set if borrow | Set if A=0 |
| $A \leftarrow A - \text{data} - CY$ | `SBI 8-bit` | `DE xx` | 2 | 2 | A | Set if borrow | Set if A=0 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** `SUB A` does NOT produce a "negative of A." It produces **0** and **always sets Z = 1** and **CY = 0**, because A − A = 0 with no borrow.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in Industry:** Subtraction with borrow is the backbone of every **digital signal processor (DSP)**, **cryptographic engine (AES, RSA)**, **checksum verifier**, and **Banking Transaction System**. For instance, every **16-bit, 32-bit, or 64-bit subtraction** in a CPU is decomposed into a chain of 8-bit subtractions, where the borrow flag from byte *N* is propagated to byte *N+1*. Without proper borrow handling, financial systems would produce **silent arithmetic errors** that could go undetected for years.

## 2.5 The Borrow Algebra — Mathematical Foundation

For any two 8-bit numbers $X$ and $Y$:

$$X - Y = X + (\overline{Y}) + 1$$

Where $\overline{Y}$ is the **bitwise NOT** (1's complement) of $Y$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 2's Complement Trick:** The 8085's ALU does NOT have a dedicated subtractor circuit. Instead, it uses a single adder circuit and inverts the subtrahend's bits, then adds 1 (using CY as the input carry). This is mathematically equivalent to true subtraction. The **Carry Flag output** is inverted by the hardware: a CY=0 from the adder means a borrow occurred in true subtraction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Algorithm — Single 8-bit Subtraction WITHOUT Borrow

**Problem Statement:** Subtract the contents of memory location `2001H` from the contents of memory location `2000H`. Store the result in `2002H`. Assume the minuend ≥ subtrahend (no borrow case).

### **Step-by-Step Algorithm**

| Step | Action | Reasoning |
|------|--------|-----------|
| 1 | `LXI H, 2000H` | Load HL pair with address of minuend (HL = 2000H) |
| 2 | `MOV A, M` | Load minuend from `M` (memory pointed by HL) into A |
| 3 | `INX H` | Increment HL to point to `2001H` (subtrahend) |
| 4 | `SUB M` | Subtract subtrahend (memory at HL) from A; result in A |
| 5 | `INX H` | Increment HL to point to `2002H` (result location) |
| 6 | `MOV M, A` | Store result from A into memory `2002H` |
| 7 | `HLT` | Halt the processor (end of program) |

### **Detailed Hand-Traced Execution**

Let Minuend = `65H` (at `2000H`), Subtrahend = `27H` (at `2001H`).

**Decimal equivalent check:** $65H = 6 \times 16 + 5 = 101$, $27H = 2 \times 16 + 7 = 39$. Expected result: $101 - 39 = 62 = 3 \times 16 + 14 = 3EH$.

**Binary Subtraction (Bit-by-Bit):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 0110\,0101 \quad (65H) \\
M &= 0010\,0111 \quad (27H) \\
\hline
A - M &= 0110\,0101 - 0010\,0111
\end{aligned}
$$

Performing column-wise subtraction from LSB (D0) to MSB (D7):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{D0: } & 1 - 1 = 0 \quad \text{(no borrow)} \\
\text{D1: } & 0 - 1 = 1 \quad \text{(borrow from D2)} \\
\text{D2: } & 1 - 1 - 1(\text{borrowed}) = 1 \quad \text{(borrow from D3)} \\
\text{D3: } & 0 - 1 - 1(\text{borrowed}) = 0 \quad \text{(borrow from D4)} \\
\text{D4: } & 1 - 0 - 1(\text{borrowed}) = 0 \\
\text{D5: } & 1 - 0 = 1 \\
\text{D6: } & 1 - 0 = 1 \\
\text{D7: } & 0 - 0 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Final result: $A = 0011\,1110 = 3EH$. ✓ Verified.

**Flag State after `SUB M`:**
- **CY = 0** (no borrow from MSB)
- **Z = 0** (result ≠ 0)
- **S = 0** (MSB of result is 0)
- **AC = 1** (borrow from bit 3 to bit 4, since D3 borrowed)
- **P = 0** (5 ones in result — odd parity)

## 3.2 Algorithm — Single 8-bit Subtraction WITH Borrow

**Problem Statement:** Subtract two 8-bit numbers and store both the **difference** and the **borrow status** (1 if borrow occurred, 0 otherwise).

### **Step-by-Step Algorithm**

| Step | Action | Reasoning |
|------|--------|-----------|
| 1 | `LXI H, 2000H` | HL points to minuend |
| 2 | `MOV A, M` | A ← minuend |
| 3 | `INX H` | HL points to subtrahend |
| 4 | `SUB M` | A ← A − subtrahend; **CY is set if borrow occurs** |
| 5 | `INX H` | HL points to result location |
| 6 | `MOV M, A` | Store difference at `2002H` |
| 7 | `MVI A, 00H` | Clear A (default borrow = 0) |
| 8 | `JNC SKIP` | If CY = 0 (no borrow), jump to SKIP |
| 9 | `INR A` | If borrow, set A = 01H (A becomes 1) |
| 10 | `SKIP:` `INX H` | HL points to `2003H` |
| 11 | `MOV M, A` | Store borrow flag (00H or 01H) at `2003H` |
| 12 | `HLT` | Halt |

### **Hand-Traced Example with Borrow**

Let Minuend = `27H` (39 dec), Subtrahend = `65H` (101 dec).

**Decimal check:** $39 - 101 = -62$ → in 8-bit unsigned, this is **borrow case**, result = $256 - 62 = 194 = \text{C2H}$, and **CY = 1**.

**Binary Subtraction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 0010\,0111 \quad (27H) \\
M &= 0110\,0101 \quad (65H) \\
\hline
\end{aligned}
$$

Since A < M, a borrow from bit 8 (imaginary) occurs.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Result: } A &= 256 + (27H) - (65H) \\
&= 256 + 39 - 101 \\
&= 194 = \text{C2H}
\end{aligned}
$$

In binary: $A = 1100\,0010$ = **C2H**, with **CY = 1** (borrow occurred).

**Flag State after `SUB M`:**
- **CY = 1** ← **(BORROW DETECTED)**
- **Z = 0**
- **S = 1** (MSB = 1)
- **AC = 0**
- **P = 0** (3 ones — odd)

## 3.3 16-bit Subtraction Using Borrow (Multi-byte Application)

**Problem:** Subtract a 16-bit number stored at `2000H–2001H` from another 16-bit number at `2002H–2003H`. Store the 16-bit difference at `2004H–2005H`.

**Key Concept:** The 8085's ALU is 8-bit, so 16-bit subtraction is split into:
- **Low byte:** Subtract low bytes; CY may be set
- **High byte:** Subtract high bytes **WITH borrow** (using `SBB`)

**Assembly Program:**

| Address | Label | Mnemonic | Comment |
|---------|-------|----------|---------|
| `2000H` | START | `LHLD 2000H` | Load HL with low and high bytes of minuend |
| `2003H` | | `XCHG` | Move HL ↔ DE (DE now has minuend) |
| `2004H` | | `LHLD 2002H` | Load HL with low and high bytes of subtrahend |
| `2007H` | | `MOV A, E` | A ← low byte of minuend |
| `2008H` | | `SUB L` | A ← E − L (low bytes) |
| `2009H` | | `STA 2004H` | Store low byte of result |
| `200CH` | | `MOV A, D` | A ← high byte of minuend |
| `200DH` | | `SBB H` | A ← D − H − CY (high bytes with borrow) |
| `200EH` | | `STA 2005H` | Store high byte of result |
| `2011H` | | `HLT` | Halt |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why `SBB` and not `SUB` for the high byte?** If the low-byte subtraction generated a borrow (CY = 1), we must subtract one more from the high byte. `SBB` automatically includes CY in the operation. Using plain `SUB` would ignore the carry and produce a **wrong result**.

**Verification by example:** Minuend = `1234H` (DE), Subtrahend = `0567H` (HL).
- Low byte: $34H - 67H$. Since $34H < 67H$, borrow occurs. $34H + 100H - 67H = \text{CDH}$. CY = 1.
- High byte: $12H - 05H - 1(\text{CY}) = 0CH$.
- Final 16-bit result: `0CH CDH` = `(0C \times 256) + 205 = 3072 + 205 = 3277 = 0CCDH`.

Decimal check: $4660 - 1383 = 3277 = 0CCDH$. ✓ Verified.

## 3.4 The Borrow Propagation — Recursive Logic

For an $N$-byte subtraction (where bytes are indexed $0$ to $N-1$, with byte 0 being the LSB):

$$
\text{Result}_i = \text{Minuend}_i - \text{Subtrahend}_i - \text{Borrow}_{i-1}
$$

Where:

$$\text{Borrow}_{i} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \text{Minuend}_i - \text{Subtrahend}_i - \text{Borrow}_{i-1} < 0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

And $\text{Borrow}_{-1} = 0$ (initial condition; no incoming borrow for the LSB).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The 8085 ALU

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ACC[Accumulator A] -->|Operand 1| ALU[ALU Core]
    REG[Register r or data] -->|Operand 2| ALU
    ALU -->|Result| ACC
    ALU -.->|Update| FLAG[Flags Register F]
    FLAG -->|CY bit| ALU
    ALU -->|Borrow Detection| CY[Carry Flag CY]
    CY -.->|Feeds back for SBB/SBI| ALU
    ALU -->|Result A| BUS[Internal Data Bus]
    BUS --> MEM[Memory or Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this diagram as:** The Accumulator and the second operand both feed the ALU. The ALU produces the result back to the Accumulator and **updates the Flags Register**. Critically, the **CY flag feeds back into the ALU** for chained operations like `SBB` (subtract with borrow). This feedback loop is the hardware foundation of multi-byte subtraction.

## 4.2 Flowchart — 8-bit Subtraction WITHOUT Borrow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1([Start]) --> node2[Load HL with 2000H]
    node2 --> node3[Load A from Memory M]
    node3 --> node4[Increment HL to 2001H]
    node4 --> node5[Execute SUB M]
    node5 --> node6[Increment HL to 2002H]
    node6 --> node7[Store A into Memory M]
    node7 --> node8([Halt])
```

## 4.3 Flowchart — 8-bit Subtraction WITH Borrow Detection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA([Start]) --> stepB[Load HL with 2000H]
    stepB --> stepC[Load A from Memory M]
    stepC --> stepD[Increment HL to 2001H]
    stepD --> stepE[Execute SUB M]
    stepE --> stepF[Increment HL to 2002H]
    stepF --> stepG[Store Difference in Memory]
    stepG --> stepH[Clear A to 00H]
    stepH --> stepI{CY = 1?}
    stepI -->|Yes| stepJ[Increment A to 01H]
    stepI -->|No| stepK[Skip A remains 00H]
    stepJ --> stepL
    stepK --> stepL[Increment HL to 2003H]
    stepL --> stepM[Store Borrow Flag in Memory]
    stepM --> stepN([Halt])
```

## 4.4 16-bit Subtraction Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1([Start]) --> s2[Load DE with 16-bit Minuend]
    s2 --> s3[Load HL with 16-bit Subtrahend]
    s3 --> s4[Move E to A]
    s4 --> s5[Execute SUB L]
    s5 --> s6[Store low-byte result]
    s6 --> s7[Move D to A]
    s7 --> s8[Execute SBB H with borrow]
    s8 --> s9[Store high-byte result]
    s9 --> s10([Halt])
```

## 4.5 Borrow Propagation Diagram — Multi-byte Case

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BYTE0[Byte 0 LSB] -->|CY out| BYTE1[Byte 1]
    BYTE1 -->|CY out| BYTE2[Byte 2]
    BYTE2 -->|CY out| BYTE3[Byte 3 MSB]
    BYTE3 -->|Final CY| RESULT[Final Borrow Indicator]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This ripple-carry-style borrow chain is the EXACT reason** why every modern processor (even 64-bit) decomposes large subtractions into 8-bit (or 64-bit) chunks and uses an internal **carry-lookahead adder** with the **2's complement trick** to compute the borrow in parallel.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define the Carry Flag's role in 8085 subtraction.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
In 8085 subtraction, the **Carry Flag (CY)** acts as the **Borrow Flag**. When subtracting, if the minuend is smaller than the subtrahend, the ALU must borrow from an imaginary 9th bit. This borrow is recorded by setting **CY = 1**. If no borrow is required, **CY = 0**. The CY flag's state after a subtraction is used by the `SBB` and `SBI` instructions to extend subtraction across multiple bytes, making 16-bit, 24-bit, and larger subtractions possible. The CY flag is bit **D3** of the Flags Register, and it is also affected by the **Auxiliary Carry (AC)** flag for BCD correction purposes.

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between `SUB r` and `SBB r` instructions in 8085.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `SUB r` | `SBB r` |
|--------|---------|---------|
| Full Form | Subtract Register | Subtract Register with Borrow |
| Operation | $A \leftarrow A - r$ | $A \leftarrow A - r - CY$ |
| Borrow | Does **not** include CY | **Includes** current CY |
| Opcode (for r=B) | `90` | `98` |
| Bytes | 1 | 1 |
| Typical Use | First byte of multi-byte subtraction | Subsequent bytes (with borrow) |
| Example | `SUB C` | `SBB C` |

The `SBB` instruction's inclusion of CY makes it the only correct choice for the high byte of a 16-bit subtraction.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks): Full Assembly Program with Explanation** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Write an 8085 assembly language program to subtract two 8-bit numbers stored at memory locations `2050H` and `2051H`. Store the result at `2052H` and the borrow status (00H for no borrow, 01H for borrow) at `2053H`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of the **Carry Flag** in this subtraction with a suitable example showing both the **borrow** and **no-borrow** cases. Also state the effect on all five flags. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

| Address | Label | Opcode | Mnemonic | Comment |
|---------|-------|--------|----------|---------|
| `2000H` | START | `21 50 20` | `LXI H, 2050H` | HL ← address of minuend |
| `2003H` | | `7E` | `MOV A, M` | A ← minuend |
| `2004H` | | `23` | `INX H` | HL ← 2051H |
| `2005H` | | `96` | `SUB M` | A ← A − subtrahend |
| `2006H` | | `23` | `INX H` | HL ← 2052H |
| `2007H` | `LOOP` | `77` | `MOV M, A` | Store difference at 2052H |
| `2008H` | | `3E 00` | `MVI A, 00H` | A ← 00H (default no borrow) |
| `200AH` | | `D2 0F 20` | `JNC AHEAD` | If CY=0, jump to AHEAD |
| `200DH` | | `3C` | `INR A` | A ← 01H (borrow occurred) |
| `200EH` | | `00` | `NOP` | (no operation) |
| `200FH` | `AHEAD` | `23` | `INX H` | HL ← 2053H |
| `2010H` | | `77` | `MOV M, A` | Store borrow flag |
| `2011H` | | `76` | `HLT` | Halt processor |

**Memory Before Execution:**
- `2050H` = `80H`
- `2051H` = `30H`

**Memory After Execution:**
- `2052H` = `50H` (i.e., $80H - 30H = 50H$)
- `2053H` = `00H` (no borrow)

**Alternative Test Case (Borrow Case):**
- `2050H` = `20H`, `2051H` = `90H`
- `2052H` = `90H` (i.e., $20H - 90H = 256 + 32 - 144 = 144 = 90H$, with borrow)
- `2053H` = `01H` (borrow occurred)

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carry Flag Role in 8085 Subtraction:**
> The 8085's ALU performs subtraction using **2's complement addition**: $A - B = A + \overline{B} + 1$. The hardware automatically sets **CY = 1** when the subtraction requires a borrow (i.e., when $A < B$ in unsigned terms) and **CY = 0** otherwise.

**Example 1 — No Borrow Case:**
$A = 65H$, $B = 27H$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
65H - 27H &= 3EH \\
CY &= 0 \quad (\text{no borrow}) \\
Z &= 0 \quad (\text{result is non-zero}) \\
S &= 0 \quad (\text{MSB of } 3EH = 0) \\
AC &= 1 \quad (\text{borrow from D3 to D4}) \\
P &= 0 \quad (\text{odd parity})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example 2 — Borrow Case:**
$A = 27H$, $B = 65H$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
27H - 65H &= \text{C2H} \quad (\text{with borrow from bit 8}) \\
CY &= 1 \quad (\text{BORROW occurred}) \\
Z &= 0 \\
S &= 1 \quad (\text{MSB of C2H} = 1) \\
AC &= 0 \\
P &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

The CY flag's value can be **tested** by the `JC` (Jump if Carry) and `JNC` (Jump if No Carry) instructions, enabling **conditional branching** — a critical feature for implementing compare-and-branch logic, error detection, and multi-precision arithmetic.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice — 16-bit Subtraction** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Write an 8085 ALP to perform 16-bit subtraction: subtract the 16-bit number stored at memory locations `2040H` (low) and `2041H` (high) from the 16-bit number stored at `2042H` (low) and `2043H` (high). Store the 16-bit result at `2044H` (low) and `2045H` (high). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a numerical example how the **borrow propagates** from the low byte to the high byte. Why is `SBB` (not `SUB`) used for the high byte? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

| Address | Label | Mnemonic | Comment |
|---------|-------|----------|---------|
| `2000H` | | `LXI H, 2042H` | HL points to low byte of minuend |
| `2003H` | | `MOV A, M` | A ← low byte of minuend |
| `2004H` | | `INX H` | HL ← 2043H |
| `2005H` | | `MOV B, A` | B ← save low byte of minuend |
| `2006H` | | `MOV A, M` | A ← high byte of minuend |
| `2007H` | | `INX H` | HL ← 2044H (not used) |
| `2008H` | | `MOV C, A` | C ← save high byte of minuend |
| `2009H` | | `LXI H, 2040H` | HL points to low byte of subtrahend |
| `200CH` | | `MOV A, M` | A ← low byte of subtrahend |
| `200DH` | | `INX H` | HL ← 2041H |
| `200EH` | | `MOV D, A` | D ← save low byte of subtrahend |
| `200FH` | | `MOV A, M` | A ← high byte of subtrahend |
| `2010H` | | `MOV E, A` | E ← save high byte of subtrahend |
| `2011H` | | `MOV A, B` | A ← low byte of minuend |
| `2012H` | | `SUB D` | A ← minuend_low − subtrahend_low |
| `2013H` | | `STA 2044H` | Store low byte of result |
| `2016H` | | `MOV A, C` | A ← high byte of minuend |
| `2017H` | | `SBB E` | A ← minuend_high − subtrahend_high − CY |
| `2018H` | | `STA 2045H` | Store high byte of result |
| `201BH` | | `HLT` | Halt |

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Numerical Example:**
- Minuend (at 2042H–2043H): `2042H = 34H`, `2043H = 12H` → 16-bit = `1234H`
- Subtrahend (at 2040H–2041H): `2040H = 67H`, `2041H = 05H` → 16-bit = `0567H`

**Step 1 — Low-byte subtraction:** $34H - 67H$

Since $34H < 67H$, a borrow occurs. $34H - 67H = 256 + 52 - 103 = 205 = CDH$, with **CY = 1**.

**Step 2 — High-byte subtraction with borrow:** $12H - 05H - 1 = 0CH$

The CY = 1 from the low byte is **automatically subtracted** by the `SBB` instruction. Final result = `0CH CDH` = `0CCDH`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students use `SUB` for the high byte, ignoring the CY flag. This produces `12H - 05H = 0DH` instead of `0CH`, a **1-bit off-by-one error** that loses 1 mark per occurrence.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Students forget that `SUB` and `SBB` only operate on the **Accumulator**. The minuend must first be moved into A using `MOV A, ...`.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Students reverse the order of bytes (high and low) when storing 16-bit values in memory. The convention is **low byte at the lower address**.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing `CMA` (complement accumulator) with `CMC` (complement carry flag) — these are unrelated to subtraction and will result in **wrong answer if misused**.
> - **Mark Deduction:** A correct program without comments typically loses **1–2 marks**. KTU examiners require **comments for every instruction**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Remember for KTU Exam:**

- The 8085 supports **4 subtraction instructions**: `SUB r`, `SUI data`, `SBB r`, `SBI data`.
- In 8085 subtraction, the **Carry Flag = Borrow Flag**. **CY = 1** means a borrow occurred.
- `SUB r` and `SUI data` perform subtraction **without** considering the current CY.
- `SBB r` and `SBI data` perform subtraction **with** the current CY included in the operation.
- `SUB A` always yields **A = 0**, **Z = 1**, **CY = 0** (used as a fast way to clear the accumulator).
- For **16-bit subtraction**, use `SUB` for the low byte and `SBB` for the high byte to propagate borrow correctly.
- The 8085 ALU uses the **2's complement trick**: $A - B = A + \overline{B} + 1$, with CY inverted to indicate borrow.
- The **Auxiliary Carry (AC)** flag indicates a borrow from bit 3 to bit 4 — important for **BCD subtraction** and **DAA correction**.
- The **Sign Flag (S)** reflects the MSB of the result and is used in **signed arithmetic** (2's complement interpretation).
- The **Zero Flag (Z)** is set when the result is exactly `00H`, often used as a **loop terminator** in counting operations.
- Common **exam trap**: confusing `SUB` (subtract) with `CMP` (compare). `CMP r` performs `A − r` internally but **discards the result** and only affects flags. Use `CMP` when you only need to test conditions like "is A > r?" without modifying A.
- For **multi-byte subtraction**, after the highest byte's `SBB`, the final CY indicates the **overall borrow** of the entire N-byte operation.
- Always include **comments** in your 8085 assembly programs — KTU examiners deduct marks for uncommented code.
- The **memory addressing convention** for 16-bit data is: **low byte at the lower address**, high byte at the higher address (little-endian).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Divide two 16-bit numbers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEEL507 - AC MACHINES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 8 > - **Topic:** Divide two 16-bit numbers # ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 📘 Formal Academic Definition In the **8086 microprocessor architecture**, dividing two 16-bit numbers is performed using the **`DIV` (Unsigned Divide)** instruction. When dividing a 16-bit dividend by a 16-bit divisor, the dividend is implicitly treated as a **32-bit value located in the DX:AX register pair**, the divisor must be a 16-bit operand (register or memory), and the result is stored with the **16-bit quotient in AX** and the **16-bit remainder in DX**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** The `DIV` instruction is the only hardware-supported division operation in 8086. There is **no dedicated divide instruction for signed numbers** in 8086 — signed division is performed using `IDIV` after ensuring the operands are in proper two's complement form. The general syntax is: $$ \texttt{DIV source} $$ Where the `source` operand determines the size of the divisor. For a 16-bit divisor: $$ \text{Dividend (32-bit)} = \text{DX:AX}, \quad \text{Divisor} = \text{source (16-bit)} $$ $$ \text{Quotient} \rightarrow \text{AX}, \quad \text{Remainder} \rightarrow \text{DX} $$ ## 🌎 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are at a market distributing **4660 apples** (the dividend) into bags, with each bag holding **18 apples** (the divisor). The total number of bags you can fully fill is the **quotient**, and the leftover apples that don't fit into a complete bag form the **remainder**. $$4660 \div 18 = 258 \text{ bags}, \text{ with } 16 \text{ apples left over}$$ In the 8086 microprocessor: - The **DX:AX register pair** = the big basket holding the dividend (DX = high word, AX = low word). - The **`DIV` instruction** = the act of distributing the apples into bags. - **AX** = the number of full bags (quotient). - **DX** = the leftover apples (remainder). > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for Beginners:** A 16-bit divisor forces the 8086 to assume a 32-bit dividend. Since a single 16-bit register can only hold 16 bits, the processor concatenates DX and AX to form the 32-bit dividend. This is why DX must be **cleared to zero** when the dividend is genuinely 16-bit (i.e., fits entirely in AX). ## 🎯 Key Constants and Parameters - **8086 Data Bus Width:** **16 bits** - **8086 Address Bus Width:** **20 bits** (1 MB addressable memory) - **Maximum 16-bit Unsigned Value:** **65535** (FFFFH) - **Maximum 32-bit Unsigned Value:** **4,294,967,295** (FFFFFFFFH) - **Type 0 Interrupt Vector Address:** **00000H – 00003H** (Divide Error Trap) - **Clock Frequency (8086-1):** **5 MHz** (200 ns cycle time) - **Clock Frequency (8086-2):** **8 MHz** # ⚡ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 🔬 Operational Breakdown of the `DIV` Instruction The `DIV` instruction is a **non-iterative, hardware-executed operation**. Internally, the 8086 performs a **binary long-division algorithm** using shifts and subtracts, but the programmer only sees a single instruction. The operational logic proceeds in structured stages: 1. **Operand Size Detection:** The assembler examines the `source` operand. - If 8-bit → Dividend is 16-bit (AX), Quotient is 8-bit (AL), Remainder is 8-bit (AH). - If 16-bit → Dividend is 32-bit (DX:AX), Quotient is 16-bit (AX), Remainder is 16-bit (DX). 2. **Implicit DX:AX Concatenation:** The processor physically combines the contents of DX (high word) and AX (low word) into a single 32-bit internal register for the dividend. 3. **Division Execution:** The binary long-division algorithm runs internally over multiple clock cycles (typically **80–90 clock cycles** for 16-bit division on the original 8086). 4. **Result Storage:** The quotient and remainder are stored in pre-determined registers based on operand size. 5. **Error Check:** If the quotient is too large to fit in the destination register, a **Type 0 interrupt (Divide Error)** is triggered. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Trap:** If the quotient exceeds the destination register size, the 8086 does NOT store a partial result — it generates a Type 0 interrupt and program execution halts. For example, dividing 0xFFFF0000 (32-bit) by 0x0001 (16-bit) produces a 32-bit quotient that cannot fit in 16-bit AX, causing a **Divide Error**. ## 📐 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Operation Type | Dividend Location | Divisor Location | Quotient Location | Remainder Location | Clock Cycles (8086) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 8-bit ÷ 8-bit | AX (16-bit) | r/m8 (8-bit) | AL (8-bit) | AH (8-bit) | 80 – 90 | | 16-bit ÷ 16-bit | DX:AX (32-bit) | r/m16 (16-bit) | AX (16-bit) | DX (16-bit) | 144 – 162 | | 16-bit ÷ 8-bit | AX (16-bit) | r/m8 (8-bit) | AL (8-bit) | AH (8-bit) | 80 – 90 | | 32-bit ÷ 16-bit | DX:AX (32-bit) | r/m16 (16-bit) | AX (16-bit) | DX (16-bit) | 144 – 162 | ### 🧮 Key Mathematical Relationships **Quotient-Remainder Theorem:** $$ \text{Dividend} = (\text{Divisor} \times \text{Quotient}) + \text{Remainder} $$ **Constraint for Valid Operation:** $$ 0 \leq \text{Quotient} < 2^{n} $$ **Constraint for Remainder:** $$ 0 \leq \text{Remainder} < \vert \text{Divisor} \vert $$ **Verification of Division (cross-check formula):** $$ \text{AX}_{\text{quotient}} \times \text{BX}_{\text{divisor}} + \text{DX}_{\text{remainder}} = \text{Original Dividend in DX:AX} $$ ### 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility The 16-bit division operation is fundamental in: - **Digital Signal Processing (DSP):** Computing signal averages, normalization, and filter coefficient scaling. - **Motor Control Algorithms:** Computing RPM-to-frequency conversion ratios in AC drives (especially relevant to the **PCEEL507 course**). - **Embedded System Firmware:** Industrial automation, sensor calibration, and real-time clock arithmetic. - **Data Acquisition Systems:** Converting ADC readings into engineering units. # ⚡ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 📝 Division Algorithm (Repeated Subtraction Method — Educational Foundation) Although the 8086 executes division in hardware using shift-subtract logic, the underlying **mathematical algorithm** can be expressed as repeated subtraction. This builds conceptual clarity expected in KTU lab viva questions. ### Algorithm Pseudocode ``` 1. Initialize: Count Quotient (Q) = 0, Accumulator (A) = Dividend 2. While A >= Divisor: a. A = A - Divisor b. Q = Q + 1 3. Remainder = A 4. Quotient = Q ``` ### 📊 Worked Example: 0x1234 ÷ 0x0012 **Given:** - Dividend (in AX, with DX cleared) = **0x1234** (Decimal: **4660**) - Divisor (in BX) = **0x0012** (Decimal: **18**) **Step 1:** Load dividend into AX and clear DX to form the 32-bit dividend. $$ \text{DX:AX} = \texttt{0000H:1234H} $$ **Step 2:** Load divisor into BX. $$ \text{BX} = \texttt{0012H} $$ **Step 3:** Execute `DIV BX`. Internally, the 8086 computes: $$ \frac{\texttt{0x00001234}}{\texttt{0x0012}} $$ **Step 4:** Mathematical verification. $$ \begin{aligned} 4660 \div 18 &= 258 \text{ with remainder } 16 \\ \text{In hexadecimal:} \quad 258_{10} &= \texttt{0x0102} \\ \text{Remainder:} \quad 16_{10} &= \texttt{0x0010} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** Verify using the quotient-remainder theorem. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Divisor} \times \text{Quotient} + \text{Remainder} &= \text{Dividend} \\ 18 \times 258 + 16 &= 4660 \\ 4644 + 16 &= 4660 \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6:** Confirm register contents after `DIV BX`. $$ \text{AX} = \texttt{0x0102} \quad \text{(Quotient)}, \qquad \text{DX} = \texttt{0x0010} \quad \text{(Remainder)} $$ ## 💻 Complete 8086 Assembly Language Program ```assembly ;============================================================ ; PROGRAM : Divide two 16-bit numbers using DIV instruction ; COURSE : PCEEL507 - AC Machines Lab (KTU 2024 Scheme) ; DETERMINISTIC BEHAVIOR : Unsigned 16-bit division ;============================================================ ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA DATA SEGMENT DIVIDEND DW 1234H ; 16-bit dividend DIVISOR DW 0012H ; 16-bit divisor QUOTIENT DW ? ; Result storage REMAINDER DW ? ; Remainder storage MSG_OK DB 'Division Successful!$' DATA ENDS CODE SEGMENT START: MOV AX, @DATA MOV DS, AX ; ---- Load dividend and clear DX ---- MOV AX, DIVIDEND ; AX <- 0x1234 XOR DX, DX ; DX <- 0x0000 (forms 32-bit dividend 0000:1234H) CMP DX, 0 ; Sanity check JNZ DIV_ERROR ; Jump if dividend overflows 16-bit upper half ; ---- Load divisor ---- MOV BX, DIVISOR ; BX <- 0x0012 CMP BX, 0 ; Prevent division by zero JZ DIV_ERROR ; ---- Perform division ---- DIV BX ; DX:AX / BX => AX=quotient, DX=remainder ; ---- Store results ---- MOV QUOTIENT, AX MOV REMAINDER, DX ; ---- Display success message ---- MOV AH, 09H LEA DX, MSG_OK INT 21H ; ---- Terminate program ---- MOV AH, 4CH INT 21H DIV_ERROR: ; Division by zero or overflow handler MOV AH, 4CH INT 21H CODE ENDS END START ``` ## 🧩 Register Interaction Matrix | Step | Instruction | AX | DX | BX | Effect | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `MOV AX, DIVIDEND` | 1234H | (old) | (old) | Dividend loaded into AX | | 2 | `XOR DX, DX` | 1234H | 0000H | (old) | DX cleared → 32-bit dividend formed | | 3 | `MOV BX, DIVISOR` | 1234H | 0000H | 0012H | Divisor loaded into BX | | 4 | `DIV BX` | 0102H | 0010H | 0012H | Division performed | | 5 | `MOV QUOTIENT, AX` | 0102H | 0010H | 0012H | Quotient stored in memory | | 6 | `MOV REMAINDER, DX` | 0102H | 0010H | 0012H | Remainder stored in memory | ## 🧪 Alternate Implementation: 16-bit ÷ 8-bit Division When dividing a 16-bit number by an 8-bit number, the dividend fits in **AX** and the divisor is 8-bit: $$ \text{Dividend} = \text{AX (16-bit)}, \quad \text{Divisor} = \text{r/m8 (8-bit)} $$ $$ \text{Quotient} \rightarrow \text{AL (8-bit)}, \quad \text{Remainder} \rightarrow \text{AH (8-bit)} $$ **Example:** 0x1234 ÷ 0x12 $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Quotient} &= \texttt{0x0102} \text{ (decimal 258)} \\ \text{Remainder} &= \texttt{0x0010} \text{ (decimal 16)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > This 16÷8 variant is **commonly tested in KTU lab exams** because it shows whether the student understands the **register-pair concatenation principle**. # ⚡ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 🗺️ Flowchart: 16-bit ÷ 16-bit Division Process ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["START: Load Dividend into AX"] node2["Clear DX to 0000H"] node3["Load Divisor into BX"] node4{"Is BX equal to 0?"} node5["Trigger DIV_ERROR handler"] node6["Execute DIV BX"] node7{"Quotient fits in AX?"} node8["Store AX in QUOTIENT"] node9["Store DX in REMAINDER"] node10["Display success message"] node11["Terminate program via INT 21H"] node12["END"] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 -- Yes --> node5 node4 -- No --> node6 node6 --> node7 node7 -- No --> node5 node7 -- Yes --> node8 node8 --> node9 node9 --> node10 node10 --> node11 node5 --> node11 node11 --> node12 ``` ## 🏗️ Block-Level Functional Architecture of the 8086 DIV Operation ```mermaid flowchart LR regDX["DX Register
High Word of Dividend"] regAX["AX Register
Low Word of Dividend"] regBX["BX Register
16-bit Divisor"] concat["32-bit Concatenation
Unit"] divider["Hardware Division
Engine Binary Long Division"] resultQ["AX Register
Stores 16-bit Quotient"] resultR["DX Register
Stores 16-bit Remainder"] overflow["Type 0 Interrupt
Divide Error"] regDX --> concat regAX --> concat concat --> divider regBX --> divider divider --> resultQ divider --> resultR divider -.-> overflow ``` ## 📊 Memory Layout Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD mem1["Memory Address 2000H
DIVIDEND = 1234H"] mem2["Memory Address 2002H
DIVISOR = 0012H"] mem3["Memory Address 2004H
QUOTIENT = ?"] mem4["Memory Address 2006H
REMAINDER = ?"] mem1 --> mem2 mem2 --> mem3 mem3 --> mem4 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** The above memory map represents the standard layout expected in KTU lab manuals. Each `DW` (Define Word) directive occupies 2 bytes. The data segment begins at an even address for proper word-aligned access. # ⚡ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **What is the role of the DX register when performing 16-bit ÷ 16-bit unsigned division using the `DIV` instruction in 8086?** **Model Answer:** When the divisor is 16-bit, the `DIV` instruction assumes a 32-bit dividend located in the **DX:AX register pair**, where DX holds the upper 16 bits and AX holds the lower 16 bits. If the actual dividend fits in 16 bits, DX must be explicitly cleared (e.g., `XOR DX, DX`) before executing `DIV`. After the instruction, **AX stores the quotient** and **DX stores the remainder**. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What happens in the 8086 microprocessor if a divide error occurs during the execution of the `DIV` instruction?** **Model Answer:** If the quotient is too large to fit in the destination register, the 8086 triggers a **Type 0 interrupt (Divide Error)**. The processor automatically pushes the **CS, IP, and flags** onto the stack and transfers control to the interrupt vector stored at memory location **00000H – 00003H**. Program execution is interrupted unless the user has installed a custom ISR (Interrupt Service Routine). --- ## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU Pattern Note:** All Part B questions in the 2024 scheme offer an internal choice between two sub-questions. You must answer either **Question A** or **Question B** in full. ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **(a)** Write an 8086 assembly language program to **divide two 16-bit unsigned numbers**. The dividend is stored at memory location `2000H` and the divisor at `2002H`. Store the quotient at `2004H` and remainder at `2006H`. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the internal register behavior during the execution of the `DIV` instruction. What precaution must be taken to avoid a divide error? **(7 Marks)** #### 🔹 Model Solution for Part (a): ```assembly ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA DATA SEGMENT ORG 2000H DIVIDEND DW 1234H DIVISOR DW 0012H QUOTIENT DW ? REMAINDER DW ? DATA ENDS CODE SEGMENT START: MOV AX, DATA MOV DS, AX MOV AX, DIVIDEND XOR DX, DX MOV BX, DIVISOR DIV BX MOV QUOTIENT, AX MOV REMAINDER, DX MOV AH, 4CH INT 21H CODE ENDS END START ``` #### 🔹 Model Solution for Part (b): The `DIV` instruction operates based on the size of the divisor: - If the divisor is **16-bit**, the dividend is implicitly read from **DX:AX** (32-bit). - After execution, the **16-bit quotient** is stored in **AX** and the **16-bit remainder** in **DX**. - The BX register is unaffected by the instruction. **Precautions to avoid divide error:** 1. **Clear DX** before division if the dividend is truly 16-bit, to avoid unintended upper word influence. 2. **Verify divisor ≠ 0** before executing `DIV` (8086 does not perform automatic zero-check). 3. **Ensure the quotient fits** in the destination register. For 16-bit ÷ 16-bit, the quotient must be ≤ 65535. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Alternative]** **(a)** Develop an 8086 ALP to perform **32-bit ÷ 16-bit division** where the 32-bit dividend is stored in two consecutive memory locations (`4000H` and `4002H`) and the divisor is at `4004H`. Store the 16-bit quotient at `4006H` and remainder at `4008H`. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare the `DIV` and `IDIV` instructions of 8086 with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)** #### 🔹 Model Solution for Part (a): ```assembly ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA DATA SEGMENT ORG 4000H DIV_HI DW 0001H ; High word of 32-bit dividend DIV_LO DW 8000H ; Low word of 32-bit dividend DIVISOR DW 00FFH ; 16-bit divisor QUOT DW ? REM DW ? DATA ENDS CODE SEGMENT START: MOV AX, DATA MOV DS, AX MOV DX, DIV_HI ; DX <- High word MOV AX, DIV_LO ; AX <- Low word MOV BX, DIVISOR DIV BX ; DX:AX / BX MOV QUOT, AX MOV REM, DX MOV AH, 4CH INT 21H CODE ENDS END START ``` #### 🔹 Model Solution for Part (b): | Feature | `DIV` (Unsigned) | `IDIV` (Signed) | |---|---|---| | Operand Type | Unsigned integers | Signed two's complement integers | | Sign of Result | Always positive | Sign follows mathematical rules | | Quotient Sign | Always non-negative | Same sign as dividend | | Remainder Sign | Always non-negative | Same sign as dividend | | Use Case | Address calculations, counters | Arithmetic with negative values | | Example | `0xFFFF ÷ 0x0002 = 0x7FFF r 0x0001` | `(-1) ÷ (+2) = 0 r -1` | **Example of IDIV:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{AX} &= \texttt{0xFFFE} \; (-2 \text{ in two's complement}) \\ \text{BX} &= \texttt{0x0002} \; (+2) \\ \text{After IDIV BX:} \quad \text{AX} &= \texttt{0xFFFF} \; (-1), \quad \text{DX} = \texttt{0x0000} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to clear DX before `DIV BX` → Undefined behavior, wrong result. > - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing `DIV` and `IDIV` results when the dividend contains sign bits. Always check whether the data is signed or unsigned. > - **Common Mistake #3:** Assuming the quotient is stored in BX. The quotient is **always** in AL (for 8-bit divisor) or AX (for 16-bit divisor). > - **Common Mistake #4:** Division by zero → Causes Type 0 interrupt and program crash. > - **Mark Deduction:** 1 mark is typically deducted for not including proper comments in assembly code. --- ## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - 🔹 The `DIV` instruction in 8086 is the **only hardware division operation** for unsigned numbers. - 🔹 For 16-bit ÷ 16-bit division, the dividend is always **32-bit (DX:AX)**, the quotient goes to **AX**, and the remainder to **DX**. - 🔹 Always **clear DX** (`XOR DX, DX`) before dividing if the dividend fits in 16 bits. - 🔹 A **divide error (Type 0 interrupt)** occurs if the quotient is too large to fit in the destination register or if the divisor is zero. - 🔹 The `IDIV` instruction is used for **signed division**; the rest of the register behavior is identical to `DIV`. - 🔹 The 8086 division operation takes approximately **80–162 clock cycles** depending on operand size — it is one of the **slowest arithmetic operations**. - 🔹 **Memory layout** in KTU lab programs must use the `DW` directive for 16-bit data storage. - 🔹 The 8086 has **no flag results** for `DIV` — flags like CF, OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF are **undefined** after division. - 🔹 Standard program structure: `ASSUME` directive → `DATA SEGMENT` → `CODE SEGMENT` → `START` label → `INT 21H` for termination. - 🔹 Always include **sanity checks** for zero divisor and overflow conditions in viva-worthy programs.

topics.title: Swap two numbers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEEL507 - AC MACHINES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 8 > - **Topic:** Swap two numbers # 📘 Module Experiment 8: Swap Two Numbers ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of the **C Programming Lab / Computational Problem Solving** component of the KTU 2024 Scheme, **Swapping two numbers** is formally defined as a fundamental data manipulation operation wherein the values stored in two memory locations (variables) are interchanged such that the original value of the first variable is transferred to the second, and the original value of the second variable is transferred to the first, **without losing any of the original data**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"A swap operation is the bidirectional exchange of values between two operands, typically executed in O(1) time complexity using a third temporary variable, or in O(1) time and O(1) auxiliary space using arithmetic or bitwise XOR logic."* The two operands involved are conventionally denoted as `a` and `b`. The transformation can be expressed mathematically as: $$ (a, b) \longrightarrow (b, a) $$ ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plain English Analogy:** Imagine you are holding a **red ball in your left hand** and a **blue ball in your right hand**. You want to swap them so that the left hand now holds the blue ball and the right hand holds the red ball. The naive (and wrong) approach is to simply move the left ball to the right hand — but as soon as you do that, the left hand is empty and you no longer remember where the red ball was! > > The correct approach is to use a **third container** (a small table) — place the red ball there, then move the blue ball to your left hand, then pick up the red ball from the table into your right hand. In programming, that "third container" is called a **temporary variable**. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following standard metrics are used to evaluate swap algorithms in the KTU 2024 Scheme: | Metric | Value/Standard | Importance | |---|---|---| | **Time Complexity** | **O(1)** | Constant time — independent of operand size | | **Space Complexity** | **O(1)** (basic), **O(1)** (XOR), **O(1)** (temp) | Constant auxiliary space | | **Integer Overflow Boundary** | **2,147,483,647** (for `int` on 32-bit) | Critical for arithmetic swap | | **Sign Bit Position** | **Bit 31** (for 32-bit int) | Critical for XOR swap safety | > [!NOTE] > The **swap operation is one of the foundational algorithms** in KTU's PCEEL507 (and PBL101/PBL201) programming experiments. Mastering all three swap techniques (temporary variable, arithmetic, XOR) is **mandatory** for the 2024 scheme lab examinations. --- # 🧠 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Primary Swap Techniques ### 2.1.1 Method 1: Using a Temporary Variable (The Classical Approach) This is the **most readable, safe, and universally recommended** method for swapping two numbers. **Step-by-step logic:** - **Step 1:** Initialize a temporary variable `temp` with the value of `a`. This **preserves** the value of `a` before it gets overwritten. - **Step 2:** Assign the value of `b` to `a`. Now `a` contains the original value of `b`. - **Step 3:** Assign the value of `temp` (which holds the original value of `a`) to `b`. Now `b` contains the original value of `a`. - **Result:** The values of `a` and `b` have been successfully interchanged. > [!TIP] > **KTU Tip:** This method works for **all data types** (int, float, char, struct) without any edge-case failures.
