Examiners often award full marks when this method is presented with a clear dry-run table. ### 2.1.2 Method 2: Using Arithmetic Operations (Addition/Subtraction) This method eliminates the need for a third variable, but introduces **overflow risk** and is **not safe for all data types**. **Step-by-step logic:** - **Step 1:** `a = a + b` — now `a` holds the **sum** of the original values. - **Step 2:** `b = a - b` — since `a` contains `original_a + original_b`, subtracting `b` (which is `original_b`) gives `original_a`. So `b = original_a`. - **Step 3:** `a = a - b` — now `a` contains `original_a + original_b`, and `b` contains `original_a`. Subtracting gives `original_b`. So `a = original_b`. > [!WARNING] > **Overflow Hazard:** If `original_a` and `original_b` are both very large positive numbers, the sum `a + b` may **exceed the maximum representable integer**, leading to **undefined behavior** in C. This is a common KTU exam pitfall. ### 2.1.3 Method 3: Using Bitwise XOR (The Clever Trick) This method is the **most elegant** and avoids overflow entirely because it operates at the bit level. **Step-by-step logic:** - **Step 1:** `a = a ^ b` — `a` now holds the **bitwise XOR** of the original values. - **Step 2:** `b = a ^ b` — XOR-ing `(a ^ b)` with `b` cancels out `b`, leaving `original_a` in `b`. - **Step 3:** `a = a ^ b` — XOR-ing `(a ^ b)` with `original_a` cancels out `a`, leaving `original_b` in `a`. > [!CAUTION] > **XOR Swap Pitfall:** If you mistakenly write `a = a ^ b ^ a ^ b ^ a` (chained incorrectly) or use the **same memory address** for both `a` and `b` (e.g., calling `swap(x, x)`), the variable gets **zeroed out** instead of swapped. This is a classic KTU viva question. ## 2.2 The Three-Way Swap (Rotating Three Variables) Sometimes KTU questions ask you to **rotate three variables** `a → b → c → a`. This requires a **4th temporary variable** because the naive two-temp approach fails. **Logic:** - **Step 1:** `temp = a` (save `a`) - **Step 2:** `a = c` (`a` becomes old `c`) - **Step 3:** `c = b` (`c` becomes old `b`) - **Step 4:** `b = temp` (`b` becomes old `a`) ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Method | Core Equations | Time | Space | Overflow Risk | Data Type Limitation | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Temporary Variable** | $temp = a;\ a = b;\ b = temp$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | **None** | **None** (universal) | | **Arithmetic (+, −)** | $a = a+b;\ b = a-b;\ a = a-b$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | **High** (sum may overflow) | Only **integer/float** types | | **XOR (Bitwise)** | $a = a \oplus b;\ b = a \oplus b;\ a = a \oplus b$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | **None** | Only **integer** types (not float) | | **In-Place (Destructuring in C via struct)** | $a = a+b-(b=a)*0$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | **High** | Undefined behavior in C | ### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering > [!IMPORTANT] > The swap operation is **not merely an academic exercise**. It is foundational to: > - **Sorting algorithms** (Bubble Sort, Quick Sort, Selection Sort) — every comparison-based sort involves millions of swaps. > - **Memory management** in operating systems (page replacement algorithms like LRU). > - **Signal processing** (e.g., FFT butterflies in DSP — AC machines lab context!). > - **AC Machine control** (parking transform in Field-Oriented Control swaps the $d$-axis and $q$-axis currents). --- # 🔬 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Dry-Run Derivation: All Three Methods (with a = 7, b = 9) ### Method 1: Temporary Variable Derivation Let the initial state be $a = 7$ and $b = 9$. **Step 1: Preserve `a` in `temp`** $$ temp = a $$ Substituting $a = 7$: $$ temp = 7 $$ State after Step 1: $a = 7$, $b = 9$, $temp = 7$. **Step 2: Overwrite `a` with `b`** $$ a = b $$ Substituting $b = 9$: $$ a = 9 $$ State after Step 2: $a = 9$, $b = 9$, $temp = 7$. **Step 3: Overwrite `b` with `temp`** $$ b = temp $$ Substituting $temp = 7$: $$ b = 7 $$ Final State: $a = 9$, $b = 7$. ✅ **Swap successful.** ### Method 2: Arithmetic Derivation Let $a = 7$, $b = 9$. **Step 1: Sum into `a`** $$ a = a + b = 7 + 9 = 16 $$ State: $a = 16$, $b = 9$. **Step 2: Recover `original_a` into `b`** $$ b = a - b = 16 - 9 = 7 $$ State: $a = 16$, $b = 7$. **Step 3: Recover `original_b` into `a`** $$ a = a - b = 16 - 7 = 9 $$ Final State: $a = 9$, $b = 7$. ✅ **Algebraic Proof of Correctness:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad a_1 &= a_0 + b_0 \\ \text{Step 2:} \quad b_1 &= a_1 - b_0 = (a_0 + b_0) - b_0 = a_0 \\ \text{Step 3:} \quad a_2 &= a_1 - b_1 = (a_0 + b_0) - a_0 = b_0 \end{aligned} $$ Where $a_0, b_0$ are the original values and $a_2, b_1$ are the swapped values. QED. ### Method 3: XOR Bitwise Derivation Let $a = 7$ (binary: `0111`), $b = 9$ (binary: `1001`). **Recall:** The XOR truth table is $0 \oplus 0 = 0$, $0 \oplus 1 = 1$, $1 \oplus 0 = 1$, $1 \oplus 1 = 0$. **Step 1: $a = a \oplus b$** $$ a = 0111 \oplus 1001 = 1110 \ (\text{decimal } 14) $$ State: $a = 14$, $b = 9$. **Step 2: $b = a \oplus b$** $$ b = 1110 \oplus 1001 = 0111 \ (\text{decimal } 7) $$ State: $a = 14$, $b = 7$. **Step 3: $a = a \oplus b$** $$ a = 1110 \oplus 0111 = 1001 \ (\text{decimal } 9) $$ Final State: $a = 9$, $b = 7`. ✅ **Algebraic Proof using XOR Properties:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Property 1:} \quad & x \oplus x = 0 \\ \text{Property 2:} \quad & x \oplus 0 = x \\ \text{Property 3:} \quad & x \oplus y = y \oplus x \quad \text{(commutative)} \\ \text{Property 4:} \quad & (x \oplus y) \oplus z = x \oplus (y \oplus z) \quad \text{(associative)} \end{aligned} $$ Applying these: $$ \begin{aligned} a_1 &= a_0 \oplus b_0 \\ b_1 &= a_1 \oplus b_0 = (a_0 \oplus b_0) \oplus b_0 = a_0 \oplus (b_0 \oplus b_0) = a_0 \oplus 0 = a_0 \\ a_2 &= a_1 \oplus b_1 = (a_0 \oplus b_0) \oplus a_0 = (a_0 \oplus a_0) \oplus b_0 = 0 \oplus b_0 = b_0 \end{aligned} $$ QED — the swap is mathematically rigorous. ## 3.2 Swap-by-Reference (Using Pointers in C) In C, you can write a function that swaps two numbers **by reference** using pointers. This is a high-frequency KTU exam question. ```c #include void swap(int *x, int *y) { int temp = *x; // Dereference x, store in temp *x = *y; // Assign y's value to x *y = temp; // Assign temp to y } int main() { int a = 7, b = 9; printf("Before swap: a = %d, b = %d\n", a, b); swap(&a, &b); // Pass addresses printf("After swap: a = %d, b = %d\n", a, b); return 0; } ``` **Output:** ``` Before swap: a = 7, b = 9 After swap: a = 9, b = 7 ``` ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: All Three Methods Side-by-Side (for $a = 7, b = 9$) | Step | Method 1 (temp) | State (a, b, temp) | Method 2 (arith) | State (a, b) | Method 3 (XOR) | State (a, b) in binary | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Initial** | $a=7, b=9$ | (7, 9, —) | $a=7, b=9$ | (7, 9) | $a=0111, b=1001$ | (0111, 1001) | | **Step 1** | `temp = a` | (7, 9, 7) | `a = a+b` | (16, 9) | `a = a^b` | (1110, 1001) | | **Step 2** | `a = b` | (9, 9, 7) | `b = a-b` | (16, 7) | `b = a^b` | (1110, 0111) | | **Step 3** | `b = temp` | (9, 7, 7) | `a = a-b` | (9, 7) | `a = a^b` | (1001, 0111) | | **Final** | ✅ | (9, 7) | ✅ | (9, 7) | ✅ | (1001, 0111) = (9, 7) | --- # 🗺️ 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: The Temporary Variable Method (Most Common in KTU Exams) ```mermaid flowchart TD A0([Start]) --> A1[/Read values of a and b/] A1 --> A2[Display original values of a and b] A2 --> B1[Initialize: temp is allocated in memory] B1 --> B2["temp = a
Save a into temp"] B2 --> C1["a = b
Overwrite a with b"] C1 --> C2["b = temp
Overwrite b with original a"] C2 --> D1[Display swapped values of a and b] D1 --> E1([End]) style A0 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 style E1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000 style B2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 style C1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 style C2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Memory Allocation During Swap (a = 7, b = 9) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Initial_State["STAGE 1: Initial"] ADDR_A1["Memory Address of a: 1000
Value: 7"] ADDR_B1["Memory Address of b: 1004
Value: 9"] ADDR_T1["Memory Address of temp: 1008
Value: UNDEFINED"] end subgraph After_Step1["STAGE 2: After temp = a"] ADDR_A2["Memory Address of a: 1000
Value: 7"] ADDR_B2["Memory Address of b: 1004
Value: 9"] ADDR_T2["Memory Address of temp: 1008
Value: 7"] end subgraph After_Step2["STAGE 3: After a = b"] ADDR_A3["Memory Address of a: 1000
Value: 9"] ADDR_B3["Memory Address of b: 1004
Value: 9"] ADDR_T3["Memory Address of temp: 1008
Value: 7"] end subgraph After_Step3["STAGE 4: After b = temp FINAL"] ADDR_A4["Memory Address of a: 1000
Value: 9"] ADDR_B4["Memory Address of b: 1004
Value: 7"] ADDR_T4["Memory Address of temp: 1008
Value: 7"] end Initial_State --> After_Step1 --> After_Step2 --> After_Step3 style ADDR_A4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 style ADDR_B4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of the Swap Experiment ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Input["INPUT MODULE"] I1[Keyboard / Scanf] I2[Variables a and b] end subgraph Processing["PROCESSING MODULE (Three Methods)"] P1["METHOD 1
Temporary Variable
temp = a; a = b; b = temp"] P2["METHOD 2
Arithmetic
a = a+b; b = a-b; a = a-b"] P3["METHOD 3
Bitwise XOR
a = a^b; b = a^b; a = a^b"] end subgraph Validation["VALIDATION MODULE"] V1[Check for Integer Overflow] V2[Check for Data Type Compatibility] V3[Check for Aliasing in XOR] end subgraph Output["OUTPUT MODULE"] O1[Display Swapped Values] O2[Return 0 to OS] end I1 --> I2 I2 --> P1 I2 --> P2 I2 --> P3 P1 --> V2 P2 --> V1 P3 --> V3 V1 --> O1 V2 --> O1 V3 --> O1 O1 --> O2 style Input fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064 style Processing fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17 style Validation fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100 style Output fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20 ``` --- # 🎯 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define the term "swap" in the context of C programming. Why is a third variable necessary when swapping two numbers using the most common method?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **swap** is an operation that interchanges the values of two variables. For variables `a` and `b`, after the swap operation, the original value of `a` is stored in `b` and vice versa. A third variable is necessary because, in a computer's memory, assigning one variable to another **overwrites** the original value. Without a backup copy stored in a temporary variable, the original value of `a` would be permanently lost when we try to assign `b` to `a`. **Example:** $$ a = 5, \ b = 10 \\ \text{Without temp: } a = b \implies a = 10 \ \text{(but original 5 is lost!)} \\ \text{With temp: } temp = a;\ a = b;\ b = temp \implies a = 10,\ b = 5 \ ✅ $$ ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *List any three methods to swap two numbers without using a function. Mention one limitation of the XOR method.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three methods are: 1. **Using a temporary variable** (most common). 2. **Using arithmetic operations** ($a = a + b$, $b = a - b$, $a = a - b$). 3. **Using bitwise XOR** ($a = a \oplus b$, $b = a \oplus b$, $a = a \oplus b$). **Limitation of XOR method:** If `a` and `b` point to the **same memory location** (i.e., aliasing), the value gets zeroed out instead of swapped. Example: `swap(x, x)` produces $x = 0$. Also, XOR only works on **integer data types** and cannot be applied directly to floating-point numbers without converting them to their bit representations. --- ## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students answer ONE of the two alternatives. ### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a)** Write a C program to swap two numbers using a temporary variable. Explain the working with a suitable example. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a C program to swap two numbers using **call by reference** (using pointers). Draw a memory diagram showing the addresses and values before and after the swap. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Question A: ### **Part (a) — Temporary Variable Swap (7 marks)** ```c #include int main() { int a, b, temp; printf("Enter two integers: "); scanf("%d %d", &a, &b); printf("\nBefore swap: a = %d, b = %d\n", a, b); temp = a; // Step 1: Save a in temp a = b; // Step 2: Copy b into a b = temp; // Step 3: Copy temp (original a) into b printf("After swap: a = %d, b = %d\n", a, b); return 0; } ``` **Sample Run (with a=7, b=9):** | Step | Code Executed | a | b | temp | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Initial** | — | 7 | 9 | undefined | | **Step 1** | `temp = a;` | 7 | 9 | 7 | | **Step 2** | `a = b;` | 9 | 9 | 7 | | **Step 3** | `b = temp;` | 9 | 7 | 7 | **Output:** ``` Before swap: a = 7, b = 9 After swap: a = 9, b = 7 ``` ### **Part (b) — Call by Reference (Pointer-Based) Swap (7 marks)** ```c #include void swap(int *x, int *y) { int temp = *x; *x = *y; *y = temp; } int main() { int a = 7, b = 9; printf("Before swap: a = %d, b = %d\n", a, b); swap(&a, &b); // Pass addresses printf("After swap: a = %d, b = %d\n", a, b); return 0; } ``` **Memory Diagram Explanation:** | Stage | Address 1000 (a) | Address 1004 (b) | Address 2000 (pointer x) | Address 2004 (pointer y) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Before swap** | 7 | 9 | 1000 | 1004 | | **Inside swap: temp = *x** | 7 | 9 | 1000 | 1004 (temp=7) | | **Inside swap: *x = *y** | 9 | 9 | 1000 | 1004 (temp=7) | | **Inside swap: *y = temp** | 9 | 7 | 1000 | 1004 (temp=7) | | **After function returns** | 9 | 7 | — | — | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Many students write `swap(a, b)` instead of `swap(&a, &b)`. This is **CALL BY VALUE** and the swap will **NOT work** outside the function. You will lose **3 marks immediately**. Always pass the **address** (`&a`, `&b`) when using pointer-based swap. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** Write a C program to swap two integers **without using a third variable**, using the **arithmetic method**. Explain why this method is not preferred for very large numbers. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a C program to swap two integers using the **XOR method**. Prove mathematically (using XOR properties) that the swap is correct. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Question B: ### **Part (a) — Arithmetic Swap (7 marks)** ```c #include int main() { int a, b; printf("Enter two integers: "); scanf("%d %d", &a, &b); printf("Before swap: a = %d, b = %d\n", a, b); a = a + b; // Step 1: a holds the sum b = a - b; // Step 2: b = (a+b) - b = original a a = a - b; // Step 3: a = (a+b) - original a = original b printf("After swap: a = %d, b = %d\n", a, b); return 0; } ``` **Dry Run (a = 7, b = 9):** | Step | a | b | Calculation | |---|---|---|---| | Initial | 7 | 9 | — | | `a = a + b` | 16 | 9 | 7 + 9 = 16 | | `b = a - b` | 16 | 7 | 16 - 9 = 7 | | `a = a - b` | 9 | 7 | 16 - 7 = 9 | **Why this fails for large numbers:** In C, an `int` is typically **32 bits**, with a maximum value of $2{,}147{,}483{,}647$. If `a = 1{,}500{,}000{,}000` and `b = 1{,}500{,}000{,}000`, the sum `a + b = 3{,}000{,}000{,}000` **exceeds the range** of a signed 32-bit integer, causing **integer overflow**. The result wraps around to a negative value due to two's complement representation, and the subsequent subtraction produces **wrong results**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** The arithmetic method **must include the overflow discussion** in your answer. Examiners specifically award **2 marks** for explaining the overflow pitfall. Students who omit this lose significant marks. ### **Part (b) — XOR Swap (7 marks)** ```c #include int main() { int a, b; printf("Enter two integers: "); scanf("%d %d", &a, &b); printf("Before swap: a = %d, b = %d\n", a, b); a = a ^ b; b = a ^ b; // b = (a^b) ^ b = a ^ (b^b) = a ^ 0 = a a = a ^ b; // a = (a^b) ^ a = (a^a) ^ b = 0 ^ b = b printf("After swap: a = %d, b = %d\n", a, b); return 0; } ``` **Mathematical Proof of Correctness:** Using the four properties of XOR: - **P1:** $x \oplus x = 0$ - **P2:** $x \oplus 0 = x$ - **P3:** Commutativity: $x \oplus y = y \oplus x$ - **P4:** Associativity: $(x \oplus y) \oplus z = x \oplus (y \oplus z)$ Let $a_0$ and $b_0$ be the original values. $$ \begin{aligned} a_1 &= a_0 \oplus b_0 \\ b_1 &= a_1 \oplus b_0 = (a_0 \oplus b_0) \oplus b_0 \\ &= a_0 \oplus (b_0 \oplus b_0) \quad \text{(by P4)} \\ &= a_0 \oplus 0 \quad \text{(by P1)} \\ &= a_0 \quad \text{(by P2)} \\ \\ a_2 &= a_1 \oplus b_1 = (a_0 \oplus b_0) \oplus a_0 \\ &= (a_0 \oplus a_0) \oplus b_0 \quad \text{(by P3 and P4)} \\ &= 0 \oplus b_0 \quad \text{(by P1)} \\ &= b_0 \quad \text{(by P2)} \end{aligned} $$ **Final state:** $a_2 = b_0$ and $b_1 = a_0$. The values are swapped. ∎ **QED.** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #3:** The XOR method **does NOT work** when `a` and `b` refer to the same memory location (e.g., `swap(x, x)`). In that case, $a \oplus a = 0$, and the variable becomes **0** instead of being swapped. This is a favorite KTU viva question — be prepared. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme** - ✅ The **swap operation** interchanges values of two variables without data loss. - ✅ **Method 1 (Temporary Variable)** is the **safest, most universal, and most recommended** method — works for **all data types** including `int`, `float`, `char`, and `struct`. - ✅ **Method 2 (Arithmetic)** uses $a = a+b$, $b = a-b$, $a = a-b$ — **fast** but **risky** due to **integer overflow** for large values. - ✅ **Method 3 (XOR)** uses $a = a \oplus b$, $b = a \oplus b$, $a = a \oplus b$ — **overflow-safe** but **only works on integers** and **fails on aliasing** (same memory address). - ✅ **Call by reference** (using pointers) is **mandatory** to swap variables across functions. Use `swap(&a, &b)` — never `swap(a, b)` for pointer-based swap. - ✅ The **time complexity** of all three methods is **O(1)** — constant time. - ✅ The **space complexity** of all three methods is **O(1)** — constant auxiliary space. - ✅ The **three-way rotation** (a→b→c→a) requires **4 variables** including one temporary, since naive 2-temp rotation fails. - ✅ **XOR swap is not suitable for floating-point numbers** — `float` and `double` require type-punning via `union` or `memcpy` to swap at the bit level. - ✅ The KTU 2024 examiner awards **2 bonus marks** for including a **dry-run table** with concrete values in your answer. - ✅ **Viva favorite:** *"What happens if you call `swap(x, x)` using the XOR method?"* — Answer: $x$ becomes **0** due to self-XOR. ---

topics.title: Add numbers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL507 - AC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 8
> - **Topic:** Add numbers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PCEEL507 – AC Machines Laboratory**, *Module Experiment 8* fundamentally relies on the arithmetic operation of **Addition** of numerical quantities obtained from analog/digital meters, oscilloscope traces, and tabulated experimental observations. Addition is the binary mathematical operation of combining two or more quantities ($a$ and $b$) to obtain their **Sum** ($S$), expressed as:

$$S = a + b$$

In AC machine experiments, this operation is used to aggregate **wattmeter readings**, **ammeter readings** (line currents), **voltmeter readings**, and to compute **total losses** (iron loss + copper loss + friction & windage loss), **total power input**, and **average values** across multiple loading conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** In every laboratory experiment of the AC Machines Lab, students are expected to tabulate at least **5 to 6 sets of readings** and perform arithmetic addition to compute total quantities. The University examiner often verifies the "Sum" column and "Average" column during record evaluation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are at a marketplace buying three items: rice worth ₹50, vegetables worth ₹30, and fruits worth ₹20. To find the total amount of money you must pay, you simply **add** all three prices: $50 + 30 + 20 = 120$ rupees.

In an AC Machines Lab, consider a **three-phase induction motor test** where you measure the input power using the **two-wattmeter method**. Wattmeter $W_1$ reads **1200 W** and Wattmeter $W_2$ reads **800 W**. The *total input power* is the **sum**:

$$P_{total} = W_1 + W_2 = 1200 + 800 = 2000 \text{ W}$$

The "Add numbers" operation is the foundation upon which every derived quantity (efficiency, regulation, slip, power factor) is calculated in your lab record.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Properties

The addition operation obeys the following algebraic properties which are critical for organizing lab calculations:

> [!NOTE]
> - **Commutative Property:** $a + b = b + a$ (Order of summation does not change the result)
> - **Associative Property:** $(a + b) + c = a + (b + c)$ (Grouping does not affect the sum)
> - **Identity Element:** $a + 0 = a$ (Zero is the additive identity)
> - **Inverse Element:** $a + (-a) = 0$ (The negative of a number is its additive inverse)
> - **Unit Consistency:** All quantities being added **must** have the same SI unit (e.g., all wattmeter readings in **Watts (W)**)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic in AC Machines Lab Context

In Module Experiment 8, the addition of numbers appears in the following typical computational tasks:

- **Task 1 — Total Input Power Calculation (Two-Wattmeter Method):**
  The total power consumed by a three-phase load is the sum of the two wattmeter readings:
  $$P_{total} = W_1 + W_2$$
- **Task 2 — Total Losses Aggregation:**
  The total losses in a machine are the sum of individual loss components:
  $$P_{loss,total} = P_{iron} + P_{copper} + P_{friction} + P_{stray}$$
- **Task 3 — Average Reading Computation:**
  The arithmetic mean of $n$ observations is computed by adding them and dividing by $n$:
  $$\bar{x} = \frac{x_1 + x_2 + \cdots + x_n}{n}$$
- **Task 4 — Summation of Tabulated Columns:**
  At the bottom of every observation table, students write a "Total" or "Sum" row obtained by column-wise addition.
- **Task 5 — Slip Calculation (Indirect Addition):**
  Slip is often computed using the difference of synchronous and rotor speeds, which themselves may be derived from summed averages:
  $$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

1. **Identify the quantities** to be added (e.g., $W_1 = 1200$ W, $W_2 = 800$ W).
2. **Verify unit consistency** — all quantities must be in the same unit (Watts, Volts, Amperes, etc.).
3. **Align decimal points** when adding values with different decimal precision.
4. **Perform column-wise addition** starting from the rightmost (least significant) digit.
5. **Apply carry-over** wherever the column sum exceeds 9.
6. **Record the final sum** in the designated "Total" row of the observation table.
7. **Use the sum** in subsequent formulas (efficiency, regulation, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Addition underpins the computation of **efficiency** ($\eta = \frac{Output}{Input} = \frac{Input - Losses}{Input}$), which is the single most important performance metric reported in every AC Machines Lab experiment.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Operation | Formula | Typical Application in Lab | Units |
|---|-----------|---------|----------------------------|-------|
| 1 | Sum of two wattmeter readings | $P = W_1 + W_2$ | Two-wattmeter method for 3-φ power | W (Watts) |
| 2 | Total losses | $P_L = P_i + P_c + P_{fw} + P_{stray}$ | Efficiency computation | W (Watts) |
| 3 | Arithmetic mean | $\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n}$ | Averaging meter readings | Same as $x$ |
| 4 | Total 3-φ power (sum) | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ | Cross-verification of two-wattmeter result | W (Watts) |
| 5 | Sum of line currents (balanced) | $I_R + I_Y + I_B$ | Verification of balance condition | A (Amperes) |
| 6 | Output power sum | $P_{out} = P_{in} - P_{loss,total}$ | Efficiency = Output / Input | W (Watts) |
| 7 | Slip (using difference) | $s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} \times 100$ | Induction motor performance | % (percentage) |
| 8 | Sum of per-phase quantities | $P_{total} = 3 \times P_{phase}$ | Star-connected load | W (Watts) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All summed quantities **must** be expressed in **SI base or derived units**. Mixing units (e.g., adding Watts with kiloWatts) without conversion will lead to **valuation deduction** during KTU practical record verification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Total Power in Two-Wattmeter Method

**Problem Statement:**
In a no-load test of a three-phase induction motor, the two-wattmeter method gives the following readings:
- Wattmeter $W_1 = 1250$ W
- Wattmeter $W_2 = 750$ W
- Voltmeter $V_L = 415$ V
- Ammeter $I_L = 4.5$ A

Find the **total input power** and the **power factor**.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Apply the addition operation to wattmeter readings.**

The total real power consumed by a three-phase load, as measured by the two-wattmeter method, is the arithmetic sum:

$$P_{total} = W_1 + W_2$$

**Step 2: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$P_{total} = 1250 + 750$$

**Step 3: Perform the addition.**

$$P_{total} = 2000 \text{ W} = 2.0 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Compute the apparent power using the formula $S = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L$.**

$$S = \sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 4.5$$

$$S = 1.732 \times 415 \times 4.5$$

$$S = 3234.21 \text{ VA} \approx 3.234 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 5: Compute the power factor as the ratio of real power to apparent power.**

$$\cos\phi = \frac{P_{total}}{S} = \frac{2000}{3234.21}$$

$$\cos\phi = 0.6184 \text{ (lagging)}$$

**Step 6: State the final result.**

$$\boxed{P_{total} = 2.0 \text{ kW}, \quad \cos\phi = 0.6184 \text{ lagging}}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Total Losses in a Transformer

**Problem Statement:**
The following losses were measured during the open-circuit and short-circuit tests of a single-phase transformer:
- Iron (core) loss $P_i = 85$ W
- Full-load copper loss $P_{cu,FL} = 120$ W
- Friction and windage loss $P_{fw} = 40$ W
- Stray load loss $P_{stray} = 15$ W

Compute the **total losses** at full load.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Identify all loss components to be added.**

$$P_{loss,total} = P_i + P_{cu,FL} + P_{fw} + P_{stray}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$P_{loss,total} = 85 + 120 + 40 + 15$$

**Step 3: Apply associative property of addition to simplify grouping.**

$$P_{loss,total} = (85 + 120) + (40 + 15)$$

$$P_{loss,total} = 205 + 55$$

**Step 4: Final addition.**

$$\boxed{P_{loss,total} = 260 \text{ W}}$$

**Step 5: Verification (alternative grouping).**

$$P_{loss,total} = (85 + 40) + (120 + 15) = 125 + 135 = 260 \text{ W} \quad \checkmark$$

The associative property confirms the result is independent of the order of summation.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Average of Tabulated No-Load Readings

**Problem Statement:**
The no-load current of a three-phase induction motor was measured for 5 successive trials and the following values were obtained (in Amperes):
$2.4, 2.5, 2.3, 2.6, 2.4$

Compute the **average no-load current**.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Sum all observations using the addition operation.**

$$\sum I_{0,i} = I_{0,1} + I_{0,2} + I_{0,3} + I_{0,4} + I_{0,5}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$\sum I_{0,i} = 2.4 + 2.5 + 2.3 + 2.6 + 2.4$$

**Step 3: Group using associative property.**

$$\sum I_{0,i} = (2.4 + 2.6) + (2.5 + 2.5) + 2.3$$

$$\sum I_{0,i} = 5.0 + 5.0 + 2.3 = 12.2 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4: Divide by the number of observations $n = 5$.**

$$\bar{I_0} = \frac{\sum I_{0,i}}{n} = \frac{12.2}{5}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{I_0} = 2.44 \text{ A}}$$

**Step 5: Cross-check using decimal alignment.**

| Observation | Value (A) |
|:-----------:|:---------:|
| 1 | 2.4 |
| 2 | 2.5 |
| 3 | 2.3 |
| 4 | 2.6 |
| 5 | 2.4 |
| **Sum** | **12.2** |
| **Average** | **2.44** |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Experiment | Quantities Added | Resultant Quantity | Typical Value Range |
|------------|------------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| No-load test of induction motor | $W_1 + W_2$ | $P_{in,NL}$ | 100 – 500 W |
| Blocked-rotor test | $W_1 + W_2$ | $P_{in,BR}$ | 500 – 2000 W |
| OC & SC test of transformer | $P_i + P_{cu}$ | Total losses | 50 – 300 W |
| Slip test on synchronous machine | Sum of phase voltages | Average excitation | 100 – 250 V |
| Regulation of alternator | Sum of $I_a R_a + I_a X_s$ terms | Total regulation | 5 % – 25 % |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Role of "Add Numbers" in Lab Data Processing

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **data flow architecture** of a typical AC Machines Lab experiment, highlighting where the "Add numbers" operation is invoked.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Experimental Setup AC Machine] --> B[Analog and Digital Meters]
    B --> C[Raw Observations Table]
    C --> D{Operation Required}
    D -- Add --> E[Sum Operation a plus b]
    D -- Subtract --> F[Difference Operation]
    D -- Multiply --> G[Product Operation]
    D -- Divide --> H[Quotient Operation]
    E --> I[Aggregated Quantities]
    I --> J[Efficiency Calculation]
    I --> K[Regulation Calculation]
    I --> L[Power Factor Calculation]
    J --> M[Final Lab Result]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[KTU Record Submission]
```

**Description of Flow:**

- **Node A** represents the AC machine under test (induction motor, transformer, alternator, or synchronous motor).
- **Node B** represents the metering instruments that generate raw numerical data.
- **Node C** is the observation table where readings are tabulated.
- **Node D** is the **decision node** that routes the data to the appropriate arithmetic operation. The **"Add"** branch leads to **Node E**, the summation block.
- **Nodes I through M** represent the downstream computations (efficiency, regulation, power factor) that consume the summed values.
- **Node N** is the final record submission to the KTU examiner.

## 4.2 Column-wise Summation Workflow in Observation Table

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Row 1 Values] --> S1[Column Sum Step 1]
    P2[Row 2 Values] --> S1
    P3[Row 3 Values] --> S2[Column Sum Step 2]
    P4[Row 4 Values] --> S2
    P5[Row 5 Values] --> S3[Column Sum Step 3]
    S1 --> T[Total Row]
    S2 --> T
    S3 --> T
    T --> AVG[Divide by n for Average]
    AVG --> R[Reported Result]
```

**Interpretation:** This diagram shows how individual row values are progressively accumulated into a column sum, which is then divided by the number of observations $n$ to yield the average. This is the **canonical procedure** for processing tabulated meter readings in KTU lab records.

> [!NOTE]
> The examiner verifies that the **"Total"** and **"Average"** rows at the bottom of every observation table are **arithmetically correct**. A simple addition error (e.g., $2.4 + 2.5 = 4.8$ written as $4.9$) will attract **valuation deduction** in the KTU 2024 Scheme continuous evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain the commutative and associative properties of addition with reference to AC Machines Lab calculations.**

**Model Answer:**
The **commutative property** states that the order of addition does not affect the sum: $a + b = b + a$. In a two-wattmeter method, the total power is $W_1 + W_2$ or $W_2 + W_1$, and both yield the same result. The **associative property** states that the grouping of numbers does not affect the sum: $(a + b) + c = a + (b + c)$. This is useful when summing multiple loss components, e.g., $P_{iron} + P_{copper} + P_{friction}$ can be grouped in any order. Both properties ensure **numerical consistency** in tabulated lab calculations.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is the addition of wattmeter readings performed in the two-wattmeter method? What is the underlying principle?**

**Model Answer:**
In a three-phase, three-wire system, the total instantaneous power is given by $p(t) = v_{RN} i_R + v_{YN} i_Y + v_{BN} i_B$. By a mathematical identity (Blondel's theorem), the total power can be measured using only **two wattmeters**, with their readings $W_1$ and $W_2$ added algebraically:

$$P_{total} = W_1 + W_2$$

The addition operation directly follows from the principle of **conservation of energy** — the total power supplied to the load must equal the sum of the individual wattmeter measurements. This avoids the need for a third wattmeter and is the **standard KTU-approved method** for three-phase power measurement.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** In a load test on a three-phase induction motor, the following observations were recorded. Compute the **total input power**, **output power**, and **efficiency** for each load condition. **(7 marks)**

| Load | $V_L$ (V) | $I_L$ (A) | $W_1$ (W) | $W_2$ (W) | Speed (rpm) | Torque (N·m) |
|:----:|:---------:|:---------:|:---------:|:---------:|:-----------:|:------------:|
| 1/4  | 415       | 3.2       | 480       | 220       | 1440        | 4.5          |
| 1/2  | 415       | 4.5       | 1250      | 750       | 1420        | 9.2          |
| 3/4  | 415       | 6.1       | 2100      | 1400      | 1400        | 13.8         |
| Full | 415       | 7.8       | 3100      | 2100      | 1380        | 18.5         |

**(b)** From the above results, comment on the **variation of efficiency with load** and identify the load point at which efficiency is maximum. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1: Compute total input power using addition of wattmeter readings.**

The fundamental formula is:

$$P_{in} = W_1 + W_2$$

**For each load:**

$$\text{1/4 Load: } P_{in} = 480 + 220 = 700 \text{ W}$$

$$\text{1/2 Load: } P_{in} = 1250 + 750 = 2000 \text{ W}$$

$$\text{3/4 Load: } P_{in} = 2100 + 1400 = 3500 \text{ W}$$

$$\text{Full Load: } P_{in} = 3100 + 2100 = 5200 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2: Compute output power using the formula $P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$.**

$$P_{out} = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60}$$

$$\text{1/4 Load: } P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \times 1440 \times 4.5}{60} = \frac{40703.5}{60} = 678.4 \text{ W}$$

$$\text{1/2 Load: } P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \times 1420 \times 9.2}{60} = \frac{82068.5}{60} = 1367.8 \text{ W}$$

$$\text{3/4 Load: } P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \times 1400 \times 13.8}{60} = \frac{121369.5}{60} = 2022.8 \text{ W}$$

$$\text{Full Load: } P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \times 1380 \times 18.5}{60} = \frac{160443.3}{60} = 2674.1 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3: Compute efficiency $\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$.**

$$\eta_{1/4} = \frac{678.4}{700} \times 100 = 96.9\%$$

$$\eta_{1/2} = \frac{1367.8}{2000} \times 100 = 68.4\%$$

$$\eta_{3/4} = \frac{2022.8}{3500} \times 100 = 57.8\%$$

$$\eta_{Full} = \frac{2674.1}{5200} \times 100 = 51.4\%$$

**Step 4: Tabulate the final results.**

| Load | $P_{in}$ (W) | $P_{out}$ (W) | $\eta$ (%) |
|:----:|:------------:|:-------------:|:----------:|
| 1/4  | 700          | 678.4         | 96.9       |
| 1/2  | 2000         | 1367.8        | 68.4       |
| 3/4  | 3500         | 2022.8        | 57.8       |
| Full | 5200         | 2674.1        | 51.4       |

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

From the computed values, the efficiency **decreases as the load increases**. This is because the input power grows approximately linearly with load (dominated by the $I^2R$ copper losses which scale as the square of the current), while the output power increases at a slower relative rate at higher loads due to **increased slip and rotor copper losses**.

In this particular dataset, the efficiency is **maximum at 1/4 load (96.9%)**, which is unusual for a real machine but is a consequence of the small torque values in the given data. In a practical induction motor, efficiency is typically maximum at **3/4 to full load** where the variable (copper) losses equal the constant (iron + friction) losses.

The **loss-minimization principle** states that efficiency is maximum when:

$$P_{variable} = P_{constant}$$

That is:

$$I^2 R_{copper} = P_{iron} + P_{friction}$$

This is the **design criterion** used by KTU examiners to justify the location of peak efficiency on the performance curve.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** During the **open-circuit (OC) and short-circuit (SC) tests** of a single-phase 2 kVA, 230 V / 115 V transformer, the following data was recorded:

**OC Test (LV side open, HV side supplied):**
- $V_{oc} = 230$ V, $I_{oc} = 1.2$ A, $W_{oc} = 65$ W

**SC Test (HV side shorted, LV side supplied):**
- $V_{sc} = 18$ V, $I_{sc} = 8.7$ A, $W_{sc} = 95$ W

Compute the **efficiency at full load and 0.8 power factor (lagging)** by adding the appropriate loss components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the transformer supplies a load current of **10 A at 0.85 p.f. lagging**, compute the **total losses** and the **percentage efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1: Identify the loss components from the test data.**

From the OC test: $W_{oc} = P_{iron} = 65$ W (this is the core loss, assumed constant at all loads).

From the SC test: $W_{sc} = P_{cu,FL} = 95$ W (this is the full-load copper loss).

**Step 2: Compute the total losses at full load using addition.**

$$P_{loss,total} = P_{iron} + P_{cu,FL}$$

$$P_{loss,total} = 65 + 95 = 160 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3: Compute the output power at full load and 0.8 p.f.**

For a 2 kVA transformer at full load:

$$S = 2000 \text{ VA}$$

$$P_{out} = S \times \cos\phi = 2000 \times 0.8 = 1600 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4: Compute the input power using addition of output and losses.**

$$P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{loss,total}$$

$$P_{in} = 1600 + 160 = 1760 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5: Compute the efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{1600}{1760} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{FL, 0.8\,pf} = 90.91\%}$$

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Determine the load fraction.**

Rated full-load current (HV side, since LV side shorted during SC test and $I_{sc} = 8.7$ A corresponds to rated HV current):

$$x = \frac{I_{load}}{I_{FL}} = \frac{10}{8.7} = 1.149$$

**Step 2: Compute copper loss at this load (scaling as $I^2 R$).**

$$P_{cu} = x^2 \times P_{cu,FL} = (1.149)^2 \times 95$$

$$P_{cu} = 1.320 \times 95 = 125.4 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3: Add iron loss and copper loss to get total losses.**

$$P_{loss,total} = P_{iron} + P_{cu}$$

$$P_{loss,total} = 65 + 125.4 = 190.4 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4: Compute output power.**

$$P_{out} = V \times I \times \cos\phi = 115 \times 10 \times 0.85 = 977.5 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5: Compute input power using addition.**

$$P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{loss,total} = 977.5 + 190.4 = 1167.9 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6: Compute efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{977.5}{1167.9} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 83.70\%}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Penalty:** Adding a value in Watts to a value in kiloWatts (e.g., $0.5 \text{ kW} + 200 \text{ W}$) without unit conversion is a **fatal error** worth 1–2 mark deduction.
> 2. **Missing "Total" Row:** Forgetting to add a "Total" or "Sum" row at the bottom of the observation table results in **incomplete record** — KTU evaluators deduct up to **2 marks** during continuous assessment.
> 3. **Algebraic vs Arithmetic Sign Error in Two-Wattmeter Method:** When one wattmeter reads negative (low power factor), students must subtract it, not add it. Adding a negative wattmeter reading without proper sign handling is a **frequently penalized mistake**.
> 4. **Decimal Misalignment:** Adding 2.4 + 2.5 incorrectly as 4.10 (instead of 4.9) is a careless addition error. Always align decimal points vertically.
> 5. **Omitting Associative Grouping Step:** For sums of 4+ quantities, KTU expects students to show **intermediate grouping** (e.g., $(a+b) + (c+d)$) to demonstrate computational transparency.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Add Numbers" in AC Machines Lab:**
> - The addition operation $S = a + b$ is the **cornerstone of all lab computations** in PCEEL507.
> - The **two-wattmeter method** for three-phase power measurement **mandates** the addition: $P_{total} = W_1 + W_2$.
> - **Total losses** are always the sum: $P_{loss,total} = P_{iron} + P_{copper} + P_{friction} + P_{stray}$.
> - **Input power** is the sum of output power and total losses: $P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{loss,total}$.
> - The **arithmetic mean** of $n$ observations is obtained by summing them and dividing by $n$: $\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n}$.
> - Addition obeys **commutative** ($a+b = b+a$) and **associative** ($(a+b)+c = a+(b+c)$) properties — both are routinely exploited in lab arithmetic.
> - **Unit consistency** is non-negotiable: convert all quantities to a common SI unit **before** addition.
> - The **"Total"** and **"Average"** rows of the observation table are the **first things** verified by the KTU examiner — errors here propagate to every downstream calculation.
> - For the **slip-ring induction motor**, the sum of rotor copper losses and mechanical output equals the air-gap power transfer: $P_{ag} = P_{cu,rotor} + P_{mech}$.
> - In **regulation of alternators**, the total voltage drop is the phasor sum (not arithmetic sum) of $I_a R_a$ and $I_a X_s$ components — distinguish carefully between **arithmetic** and **phasor** addition.
> - Always **show intermediate steps** of addition in your record — this earns **method marks** even if the final answer has a minor arithmetic slip.
> - **Negative wattmeter readings** in the two-wattmeter method at low power factors require **algebraic** (signed) addition, not blind arithmetic summation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
