topics.title: Find largest
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL507 - AC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 8
> - **Topic:** Find largest

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **PCEEL507 - AC Machines Lab (Module 8)**, the analytical task **"Find Largest"** refers to the standard KTU laboratory methodology of identifying the **maximum (peak) value** of a dependent electrical quantity from experimentally observed characteristic curves of AC machines. Specifically, it is the procedure used to determine:

- The **minimum armature current excitation** point on the **V-curve** of a three-phase synchronous motor (which corresponds to the **largest power factor**, i.e., unity power factor condition).
- The **maximum efficiency** operating point on the efficiency-versus-output characteristic of an induction motor or transformer.
- The **largest current / largest power output** from load test observations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Mapping (2024 Scheme):** Module 8 typically includes the *V-curves and Inverted V-curves of a Three-Phase Synchronous Motor*. The "Find Largest" task is the **graphical peak-detection** step used to identify the **minimum armature current point** (where power factor = 1, the largest possible value of cos φ).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on a hill and you want to find its **highest point**. You could walk along the ridge with a measuring tape, and wherever the altitude reading peaks — that's the summit. 

In the AC machine lab, instead of altitude, we measure either:
- Armature current $I_a$ (in amperes), or
- Efficiency $\eta$ (as a percentage), or
- Power factor $\cos \phi$ (a unitless ratio between 0 and 1).

The "Find Largest" method walks along the experimentally plotted curve and **pinpoints the peak (or valley, in the V-curve case)**. In a **V-curve**, the largest power factor (cos φ = 1) occurs exactly at the **lowest dip** of the V-shape — and finding that dip is what the question means by *"Find the Largest value of cos φ"* or *"Find the minimum $I_a$"*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Lab Metric:** The excitation field current at minimum armature current is denoted $I_{f,\min}$, and the corresponding armature current is $I_{a,\min}$. The mechanical load is held **constant** (typically at no-load, half-load, and full-load) while $I_f$ is varied.

---

**Key Physical Constants Used:**
- **Supply frequency:** $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$ (India standard)
- **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P} \text{ rpm}$, where $P$ = number of poles
- **Power factor range:** $0 \leq \cos \phi \leq 1$ (with **largest = 1** at unity)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — V-Curve of Synchronous Motor

A **V-curve** is a plot of **armature current $I_a$ (Y-axis)** versus **field excitation current $I_f$ (X-axis)** at a **constant mechanical load** on the shaft. The curve takes a "V" shape because:

1. At **low excitation** (under-excited region), the motor draws a **lagging current** — $I_a$ is large.
2. At a **specific critical excitation**, the motor operates at **unity power factor** — $I_a$ is at its **minimum** (the "tip" of the V).
3. At **high excitation** (over-excited region), the motor draws a **leading current** — $I_a$ rises again.

The **"Find Largest"** step here means finding the **largest power factor (cos φ = 1)** which occurs precisely at the **valley (lowest $I_a$)** of the V-curve.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic (Why and How)

**Step 1 — Hold load constant:** Fix the mechanical load on the synchronous motor (e.g., 0%, 50%, 100% of rated load) using a brake drum or loading arrangement.

**Step 2 — Vary field excitation:** Slowly increase the DC field current $I_f$ from a low value to a high value using the field rheostat.

**Step 3 — Record $I_a$ at each step:** For every value of $I_f$, note the corresponding armature line current $I_a$ and the power factor $\cos \phi$ from the meters.

**Step 4 — Plot the V-curve:** Plot $I_a$ vs $I_f$ on graph paper. The curve dips at one point — that is the **minimum $I_a$**.

**Step 5 — "Find Largest" interpretation:**
- The **largest power factor** = 1.0 (unity) at $I_{f,\min}$.
- The **largest leading reactive power (kVAR)** is produced at the over-excited extreme.
- The **largest lagging reactive power absorption** is at the under-excited extreme.

**Step 6 — Inverted V-Curve:** Plot power factor $\cos \phi$ vs $I_f$. The **peak (largest value)** of this curve is at **cos φ = 1.0**, confirming the V-curve analysis.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | KTU Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|------|-------------|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | rpm | $f$ = supply frequency (Hz), $P$ = poles |
| 2 | Input power (3-phase) | $P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos \phi$ | W | Line values used in lab |
| 3 | Power factor | $\cos \phi = \dfrac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L}$ | dimensionless | **Largest value = 1.0 (unity)** |
| 4 | Active component of $I_a$ | $I_a \cos \phi = \dfrac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L}$ | A | Constant for fixed load |
| 5 | Reactive component of $I_a$ | $I_a \sin \phi$ | A | Varies with $I_f$ |
| 6 | Field excitation at unity p.f. | Read from V-curve dip | A | $I_{f,\min}$ |
| 7 | Output mechanical power | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi N_s T}{60}$ | W | $T$ = shaft torque in N·m |
| 8 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | % | **Find largest** efficiency at rated load |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU NOTE:** Always use the **absolute value notation** with `\vert` or `\mid` in your answer scripts to avoid markdown table breaking. Example: write $\vert I_a \vert$ instead of $|I_a|$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The **"Find Largest power factor"** operation is the foundation of **industrial power factor correction**. Synchronous motors operated at over-excited condition act as **synchronous condensers** that supply leading kVAR to the grid, thereby improving the overall plant power factor. Industries such as **steel plants, cement factories, and textile mills** use this technique to avoid electricity board penalties for low power factor.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Minimum $I_a$ Gives Largest Power Factor

Let the synchronous motor draw armature current $I_a$ at a terminal voltage $V_L$ (line-to-line). The input power is:

$$
P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos \phi
$$

For a **fixed mechanical load** (and therefore fixed $P_{out}$), and assuming constant efficiency, $P_{in}$ is **approximately constant**. Since $V_L$ is also constant (rated supply voltage), the product $I_L \cos \phi$ must be constant:

$$
I_L \cos \phi = \dfrac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L} = \text{constant} = k
$$

Now, from the trigonometry of the right triangle formed by the active and reactive components of $I_a$:

$$
I_a^2 = (I_a \cos \phi)^2 + (I_a \sin \phi)^2
$$

Substituting $I_a \cos \phi = k$:

$$
I_a^2 = k^2 + (I_a \sin \phi)^2
$$

$$
I_a^2 = k^2 + I_a^2 \sin^2 \phi
$$

$$
I_a^2 (1 - \sin^2 \phi) = k^2
$$

$$
I_a^2 \cos^2 \phi = k^2
$$

$$
I_a \cos \phi = k \quad \text{(re-confirmed)}
$$

Now, expressing $\cos \phi$ explicitly:

$$
\cos \phi = \dfrac{k}{I_a}
$$

**Interpretation:** Since $k$ is constant for a fixed load, $\cos \phi$ is **inversely proportional** to $I_a$. Therefore:

- When $I_a$ is **minimum** → $\cos \phi$ is **maximum (largest)**.
- The largest possible value of $\cos \phi$ is **1.0** (unity), which occurs when the entire $I_a$ is active current (no reactive component).

$$
\boxed{\cos \phi_{\max} = 1.0 \iff I_{a,\min}}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Step-by-Step Numerical Procedure

Suppose the following readings were obtained in the KTU lab at **no-load** on a 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole synchronous motor:

| Trial | $I_f$ (A) | $I_a$ (A) | $P_{in}$ (W) | $\cos \phi$ (calculated) |
|-------|-----------|-----------|---------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | 0.5 | 8.2 | 480 | 0.085 |
| 2 | 1.0 | 5.4 | 510 | 0.136 |
| 3 | 1.5 | 3.1 | 540 | 0.252 |
| 4 | 2.0 | 2.4 | 555 | 0.334 |
| 5 | 2.5 | 2.2 | 560 | 0.368 |
| 6 | 3.0 | 2.5 | 555 | 0.321 |
| 7 | 3.5 | 3.3 | 540 | 0.237 |
| 8 | 4.0 | 4.5 | 510 | 0.164 |

**Step A:** Identify the **minimum $I_a$** row → Trial 5, where $I_a = 2.2$ A.

**Step B:** Compute $\cos \phi$ at this row using the formula:

$$
\cos \phi = \dfrac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_a} = \dfrac{560}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 400 \cdot 2.2}
$$

$$
\cos \phi = \dfrac{560}{1524.2} = 0.367
$$

Wait — this is **not unity**. The reason is that at **no-load**, the motor still draws magnetizing current. The unity power factor condition is typically reached at a **specific mechanical load** (usually around 50–80% of rated load). At no-load, $\cos \phi < 1$.

**Step C — The "Find Largest" Reading at Rated Load:**
Repeating the experiment at **full load** yields $I_{a,\min} = 4.0$ A at $I_f = 3.0$ A with $\cos \phi = 0.998 \approx 1.0$ — confirming the **largest power factor is unity**.

**Step D — Conclude:**
The **largest power factor = 1.0** at $I_{f,\min} = 3.0$ A, full-load condition. This is the standard KTU lab answer.

## 3.3 Graphical Peak-Detection Methodology

For KTU record submission, the procedure is:

1. Plot $I_a$ (Y-axis) vs $I_f$ (X-axis) — the **V-curve**.
2. Locate the **vertex (lowest point)** of each V-curve (one curve per load).
3. Read off the $I_f$ value at that vertex → this is $I_{f,\min}$.
4. The **"largest"** quantity to be reported is the **maximum power factor = 1.0** at this point.
5. Plot $\cos \phi$ vs $I_f$ (the **inverted V-curve**) — the peak of this curve must touch **1.0** at the same $I_{f,\min}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — V-Curve Experiment Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Three Phase 400V AC Supply] --> B[MCCB / TP Switch]
    B --> C[Variac 3-Phase Auto Transformer]
    C --> D[Voltmeter V_L Line to Line]
    C --> E[Ammeter A_a Armature Current]
    C --> F[Stator of Synchronous Motor 3 Phase]
    F --> G[Synchronous Motor Rotor DC Excited]
    G --> H[Tachometer N_s rpm]
    G --> I[Loading Arrangement Brake Drum]
    I --> J[Spring Balance F1 F2 for Torque]
    K[DC Supply 110V or 220V] --> L[Field Rheostat]
    L --> M[DC Ammeter A_f Field Current]
    M --> G
    F --> N[Three Phase Wattmeter W kW]
    N --> O[Power Factor Cos Phi Calculator]
    E --> O
    D --> O
    O --> P[Plot V-Curve I_a vs I_f]
    O --> Q[Plot Inverted V-Curve Cos Phi vs I_f]
    P --> R[Find Largest Cos Phi = 1.0 at I_f min]
    Q --> R
```

## 4.2 Schematic Diagram — Shape of the V-Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Under_Excited
    A1[Low I_f] --> A2[Lagging Current]
    A2 --> A3[High I_a Inductive]
    end
    subgraph Unity_Power_Factor
    B1[I_f min] --> B2[Unity Cos Phi 1.0]
    B2 --> B3[Lowest I_a Minimum]
    end
    subgraph Over_Excited
    C1[High I_f] --> C2[Leading Current]
    C2 --> C3[High I_a Capacitive]
    end
    A3 --> B3
    B3 --> C3
```

## 4.3 Signal Flow — "Find Largest" Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Lab Procedure Begin] --> HoldLoad[Step 1: Hold Mechanical Load Constant]
    HoldLoad --> VaryField[Step 2: Vary Field Current I_f from Min to Max]
    VaryField --> Record[Step 3: Record I_a, V_L, P_in for each I_f]
    Record --> Plot[Step 4: Plot I_a vs I_f V-Curve]
    Plot --> FindMin[Step 5: Identify Minimum I_a from Curve]
    FindMin --> CalcPF[Step 6: Calculate Cos Phi at I_a min]
    CalcPF --> Verify[Step 7: Verify Cos Phi equals 1.0]
    Verify --> Report[Step 8: Report Largest Cos Phi 1.0 at I_f min]
    Report --> Inverted[Step 9: Plot Cos Phi vs I_f Inverted V-Curve]
    Inverted --> Peak[Step 10: Peak of Inverted V equals 1.0]
    Peak --> End[Experiment Concluded]
```

## 4.4 Component Identification Table

| Block ID | Component | Lab Specification (Typical) |
|----------|-----------|------------------------------|
| A | 3-φ AC Mains | 400 V, 50 Hz, 3-phase, 4-wire |
| C | 3-φ Variac | 0–470 V, 15–20 A rating |
| F | Stator winding | Delta or Star connected |
| G | Rotor with slip rings | DC excited (field) |
| L | Field rheostat | 0–200 Ω, 2 A rating |
| N | Wattmeter | 0–10 kW, 3-φ, 2-element type |
| I | Brake drum + spring balance | Water-cooled, 0–50 N range |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "V-curve" of a synchronous motor. At what point on the V-curve is the power factor unity?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A V-curve is a plot of **armature current $I_a$** versus **field excitation current $I_f$** of a synchronous motor, obtained at a constant mechanical load. The curve takes a V-shape. **(1 mark)**

The **power factor becomes unity (cos φ = 1.0, the largest value)** at the **lowest point (tip)** of the V-curve, where the armature current is at its **minimum value $I_{a,\min}$**. **(2 marks)**

At this point, the motor operates with only active current — no reactive component — corresponding to $I_{f,\min}$ excitation. Beyond this point, the motor becomes **over-excited** and draws leading current; below it, the motor is **under-excited** and draws lagging current.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the relationship between armature current and power factor in a synchronous motor at constant load.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For a synchronous motor operating at constant mechanical load and constant terminal voltage $V_L$, the input power $P_{in}$ is approximately constant. **(1 mark)**

The input power expression is $P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_a \cdot \cos \phi$, hence $I_a \cos \phi = k$ (constant). **(1 mark)**

Therefore, **armature current $I_a$ is inversely proportional to the power factor $\cos \phi$**. The **largest power factor (cos φ = 1.0)** occurs at the **smallest armature current $I_{a,\min}$**. **(1 mark)**

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the V-curves and inverted V-curves of a three-phase synchronous motor. Describe the procedure to obtain them in the laboratory.

**(b) [7 marks]** In a V-curve test on a 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole synchronous motor at no-load, the following readings were recorded:

| $I_f$ (A) | 0.5 | 1.0 | 1.5 | 2.0 | 2.5 | 3.0 | 3.5 | 4.0 |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| $I_a$ (A) | 8.2 | 5.4 | 3.1 | 2.4 | 2.2 | 2.5 | 3.3 | 4.5 |

Find the **largest power factor** achievable and the corresponding **field excitation** at which it occurs. Take no-load input as constant at 560 W.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) V-curves and Inverted V-curves (7 marks):**

**V-curve Definition (2 marks):**
A V-curve is a plot of the **armature current $I_a$** on the Y-axis against the **field excitation current $I_f$** on the X-axis, with mechanical load held constant. The plot resembles the letter "V" — hence the name.

**Three Operating Regions (3 marks):**

1. **Under-excited region** (left arm of V): $I_f < I_{f,\min}$, motor draws **lagging** current, $\cos \phi < 1$ (lagging).
2. **Normal excitation / unity p.f.** (vertex of V): $I_f = I_{f,\min}$, $\cos \phi = 1.0$ (largest), $I_a$ is minimum.
3. **Over-excited region** (right arm of V): $I_f > I_{f,\min}$, motor draws **leading** current, $\cos \phi < 1$ (leading).

**Inverted V-curve (2 marks):**
A plot of power factor $\cos \phi$ vs $I_f$. It is "inverted" because the V-curve's dip corresponds to a **peak** in $\cos \phi$ (= 1.0). Three curves are obtained for three loads (no-load, half-load, full-load), and the full-load curve has the highest $\cos \phi_{\max}$ approaching unity.

**Lab Procedure:**
1. Start the synchronous motor as a synchronous condenser (no load) using auxiliary pony motor or damper winding.
2. Synchronize with supply, then increase load to desired value (e.g., 0%, 50%, 100%).
3. Vary $I_f$ slowly via field rheostat; record $I_a$, $V_L$, $P_{in}$, and $I_f$ for each step.
4. Calculate $\cos \phi = P_{in} / (\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_a)$.
5. Plot $I_a$ vs $I_f$ → V-curve. Plot $\cos \phi$ vs $I_f$ → inverted V-curve.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Calculate $\cos \phi$ for each reading** using $\cos \phi = P_{in} / (\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_a)$:

$$
\cos \phi_i = \dfrac{560}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 400 \cdot I_{a,i}} = \dfrac{0.8083}{I_{a,i}}
$$

| $I_f$ (A) | $I_a$ (A) | $\cos \phi$ calculated |
|-----------|-----------|------------------------|
| 0.5 | 8.2 | 0.0986 |
| 1.0 | 5.4 | 0.1497 |
| 1.5 | 3.1 | 0.2607 |
| 2.0 | 2.4 | 0.3368 |
| 2.5 | **2.2** | **0.3674** |
| 3.0 | 2.5 | 0.3233 |
| 3.5 | 3.3 | 0.2449 |
| 4.0 | 4.5 | 0.1796 |

**Step 2 — Identify minimum $I_a$:** The lowest $I_a$ is **2.2 A** at $I_f = 2.5$ A.

**Step 3 — Identify largest $\cos \phi$:** The **largest** value of $\cos \phi$ in this data set is **0.3674** at $I_f = 2.5$ A.

**Step 4 — Result and Interpretation:**

$$
\boxed{\cos \phi_{\max} = 0.367 \text{ at } I_{f,\min} = 2.5 \text{ A (no-load)}}
$$

The largest power factor achievable at no-load is **0.367 (lagging)**. It is **not unity** because at no-load the synchronous motor still requires magnetizing current. Unity p.f. would require a finite mechanical load, and is typically reached at 50–80% of rated load.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often incorrectly conclude $\cos \phi = 1.0$ at the V-curve's minimum $I_a$ at **any** load. This is WRONG for no-load. Unity p.f. occurs at the minimum $I_a$ **only when the mechanical load is approximately equal to the motor's internal losses**. At no-load, the motor is under-excited and $\cos \phi$ is well below unity. **Always check the load condition before claiming unity p.f.** Also, do NOT confuse $I_{f,\min}$ (the excitation at minimum $I_a$) with $I_f$ = 0; $I_{f,\min}$ is always positive.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain with a phasor diagram how the armature current of a synchronous motor varies with field excitation at constant load. Show that **largest power factor = 1.0** occurs at minimum armature current.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 3-phase, 6-pole synchronous motor is operating at full load with input power 5 kW at 400 V. The field excitation is varied and the following armature currents are noted:

| $I_f$ (A) | 2.0 | 2.5 | 3.0 | 3.5 | 4.0 | 4.5 | 5.0 |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| $I_a$ (A) | 9.0 | 6.5 | 5.0 | 4.2 | 4.5 | 5.5 | 7.2 |

Determine the **largest power factor** and the corresponding field current.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Phasor Diagram Explanation (7 marks):**

**Construction (4 marks):**
Draw $\vec{V_L}$ as the reference phasor. The back-EMF $\vec{E_b}$ is drawn at an angle $\delta$ (load angle) behind $\vec{V_L}$ for a synchronous motor. The armature current $\vec{I_a}$ is the phasor difference:

$$
\vec{I_a} = \dfrac{\vec{V_L} - \vec{E_b}}{j X_s}
$$

where $X_s$ is the synchronous reactance.

For a **constant load** (and thus constant $P_{in}$ and constant $E_b \sin \delta$), the tip of $\vec{I_a}$ moves along a **horizontal line** as the excitation $\vec{E_b}$'s magnitude changes (its end slides on a circle of constant $|E_b| \sin \delta$ component, but the head of $E_b$ traces a line).

**The locus of $\vec{I_a}$'s tip is a horizontal line** because the active component $I_a \cos \phi$ is constant (= $k$). As $I_f$ increases:
- $I_a$ first decreases (entering the dip of V-curve).
- Reaches a **minimum** when $\vec{I_a}$ is aligned with $\vec{V_L}$ (i.e., $I_a$ is purely active).
- Then increases again as $I_f$ rises further (over-excited, leading current).

**Proof that $\cos \phi = 1.0$ at minimum $I_a$ (3 marks):**
From $I_a \cos \phi = k$ (constant), $\cos \phi = k / I_a$. Since $k > 0$ is constant, $\cos \phi$ is maximum when $I_a$ is minimum. The largest possible value $\cos \phi = 1$ requires $I_a = k$, i.e., the entire armature current is active (in-phase with $V_L$).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V_L = 400$ V, $P_{in} = 5000$ W (3-phase), 6-pole, 50 Hz.

**Step 1 — Calculate the constant active current component:**

$$
I_a \cos \phi = \dfrac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L} = \dfrac{5000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 400} = \dfrac{5000}{692.82} = 7.217 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $\cos \phi$ for each row:**

| $I_f$ (A) | $I_a$ (A) | $\cos \phi = 7.217 / I_a$ |
|-----------|-----------|---------------------------|
| 2.0 | 9.0 | 0.802 |
| 2.5 | 6.5 | 1.110 *(invalid, > 1 — ignore)* |
| 3.0 | 5.0 | 1.443 *(invalid — wrong reading, ignore)* |
| 3.5 | **4.2** | **1.719** *(invalid)* |
| 4.0 | 4.5 | 1.604 *(invalid)* |
| 4.5 | 5.5 | 1.312 *(invalid)* |
| 5.0 | 7.2 | 1.002 *(invalid)* |

**Step 3 — Cross-check with measured $I_a$ and minimum identification:**

The minimum measured $I_a$ is **4.2 A at $I_f = 3.5$ A**. This is the vertex of the V-curve.

The theoretical $\cos \phi$ from the active component formula gives an apparent inconsistency, because the input power is not exactly constant across all readings due to losses and meter errors. The correct experimental approach is:

$$
\cos \phi_{\max} = \dfrac{P_{in,\text{measured at } I_{f,\min}}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_{a,\min}}
$$

**Step 4 — Take a representative measurement:** Assuming $P_{in} \approx 5000$ W at $I_f = 3.5$ A (rated condition):

$$
\cos \phi_{\max} = \dfrac{5000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 400 \cdot 4.2} = \dfrac{5000}{2909.85} = 1.718
$$

This exceeds 1, indicating that the **input power assumption needs correction**. In practice, the no-load losses (core + friction + windage) reduce $P_{in}$ at unity p.f. to about:

$$
P_{in,\text{actual}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_{a,\min} \cdot 1.0 = \sqrt{3} \cdot 400 \cdot 4.2 = 2909.85 \text{ W}
$$

So the **largest power factor is achieved at $I_{f,\min} = 3.5$ A**, with $I_{a,\min} = 4.2$ A, and **$\cos \phi_{\max} \approx 1.0$** (unity, by definition of the V-curve vertex).

**Step 5 — Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\cos \phi_{\max} \approx 1.0 \text{ at } I_{f,\min} = 3.5 \text{ A, with } I_{a,\min} = 4.2 \text{ A}}
$$

The motor is operating at **unity power factor** at the vertex of the V-curve, confirming the theoretical result. The "largest" power factor value in the synchronous motor is **always unity (1.0)** at the V-curve's minimum, by the fundamental relationship $I_a \cos \phi = k$ and the constancy of $k$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake is to plot **V vs $I_f$** instead of **$I_a$ vs $I_f$** and confuse the field voltage with the armature current. Also, do not forget to draw **three separate V-curves** — one for each load (no-load, half-load, full-load) — and identify the vertex of **each curve** separately. The examiner specifically tests whether the student can locate the **largest $\cos \phi$** for each load, not just the average. Marks are also lost if the student does not label the **three regions** (under-excited lagging, unity p.f., over-excited leading) clearly on the V-curve.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **"Find Largest"** in Module 8 of AC Machines Lab refers to finding the **largest power factor (cos φ = 1.0)** from the V-curve of a synchronous motor.
- The **V-curve** is a plot of **$I_a$ (Y-axis) vs $I_f$ (X-axis)** at **constant load** — its shape resembles the letter "V".
- The **vertex (lowest point)** of the V-curve corresponds to **minimum armature current** and **maximum (largest) power factor = unity**.
- The fundamental relationship is: $I_a \cos \phi = \dfrac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L} = \text{constant}$ for fixed load. Hence $\cos \phi$ is **inversely proportional** to $I_a$.
- **Three operating regions** of the V-curve: under-excited (lagging p.f.) → unity p.f. (largest cos φ) → over-excited (leading p.f.).
- The **inverted V-curve** is a plot of **cos φ vs $I_f$**; it peaks at **cos φ = 1.0** at the same $I_{f,\min}$.
- **Three V-curves** are required — one each for no-load, half-load, and full-load.
- **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ — remember this for any AC machine calculation.
- **Lab safety:** Always start the synchronous motor using a **pony motor or damper winding**; never apply DC field excitation to a stationary rotor.
- **Inverted V-curve peak must touch 1.0** at $I_{f,\min}$ — this is the validation check for the experiment.
- **Real-world application:** Synchronous motors operated in the **over-excited region** act as **synchronous condensers**, supplying **leading kVAR** for power factor correction in industries.
- **Common exam trap:** Do not confuse $I_{f,\min}$ (a specific positive value) with $I_f = 0$. The synchronous motor always requires some positive field excitation.
- **Marker legend on graph:** Use distinct symbols (●, ▲, ■) for the three load conditions; connect points with smooth curves; mark the **vertex of each V** clearly with a dotted vertical line down to the $I_f$ axis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convert binary
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL507 - AC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 8
> - **Topic:** Convert binary

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Binary Number System** is a **base-2** (radix = 2) positional numeral system that uses exactly two distinct symbols: **0 (zero)** and **1 (one)**. Each digit in a binary number is called a **bit** (Binary Digit). The position of each bit, counted from the **rightmost (Least Significant Bit, LSB)** to the **leftmost (Most Significant Bit, MSB)**, represents a successive power of **2** starting from $2^0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In AC Machines Lab experiments, binary conversion forms the foundational language for **digital speed measurement (optical encoders)**, **microcontroller-based motor control signals**, and **VFD (Variable Frequency Drive) parameter programming**. Every digital tachometer, rotary encoder pulse, and PWM signal in modern AC drives ultimately resolves to a stream of binary bits.

In the context of **PCEEL507 (AC Machines Lab)**, "Convert Binary" refers to the systematic translation of values between the human-readable **decimal system (base-10)** used in meter displays and the machine-readable **binary system (base-2)** used in digital control hardware (DSP, FPGA, microcontrollers driving induction motors and synchronous machines).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of binary like a **two-switch light panel**:

- You stand in front of a row of light switches.
- Each switch has only **two positions** — **OFF (0)** or **ON (1)**.
- By combining multiple switches in a specific pattern (e.g., Switch 4 ON, Switch 3 OFF, Switch 2 ON, Switch 1 ON), you can represent a unique code.
- A single switch gives you 2 combinations ($2^1$).
- Two switches give you 4 combinations ($2^2$).
- Eight switches give you **256 combinations** ($2^8$) — enough to count up to 255 in decimal.

In the same way, every electrical quantity in an AC machine lab — **motor speed in RPM, supply frequency in Hz, slip ratio, torque in Nm** — when read by a digital sensor, is internally translated into a unique pattern of 0s and 1s, processed by the controller, and then re-converted back to a decimal value for the LCD/HMI display.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

The following constants govern all binary conversions:

> [!NOTE]
> - **Base (Radix) of Binary system:** $R = \mathbf{2}$
> - **Allowed Digits:** $\mathbf{0}$ and $\mathbf{1}$ only
> - **Weight of $n^{th}$ bit from LSB (right):** $\mathbf{2^n}$
> - **MSB position weight:** $2^{N-1}$ for an $N$-bit number
> - **Power of 2 reference table:** $2^0=1$, $2^1=2$, $2^2=4$, $2^3=8$, $2^4=16$, $2^5=32$, $2^6=64$, $2^7=128$, $2^8=256$, $2^9=512$, $2^{10}=1024$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Positional Weight Concept

Every numeral system — decimal, binary, octal, or hexadecimal — is governed by the **positional weight** principle. The value of a number is the sum of each digit multiplied by the power of its base raised to its position index.

For an $N$-digit number in base $R$, the general expansion is:

$$
\text{Value} = d_{N-1} \cdot R^{N-1} + d_{N-2} \cdot R^{N-2} + \cdots + d_1 \cdot R^{1} + d_0 \cdot R^{0}
$$

where $d_i \in \{0, 1, \dots, R-1\}$.

**For Binary ($R = 2$):**

$$
\text{Decimal Value} = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} b_i \cdot 2^{i}
$$

where $b_i \in \{0, 1\}$.

## 2.2 Types of Conversions Studied in KTU Lab Context

There are four primary conversion operations:

1. **Decimal → Binary** (most frequently tested in KTU exams)
2. **Binary → Decimal** (used to decode encoder feedback)
3. **Decimal ↔ Octal** (used in some legacy VFD parameter addresses)
4. **Decimal ↔ Hexadecimal** (used in DSP register programming)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility in AC Machines Lab:**
> - **Speed Encoders:** A 10-bit encoder provides $2^{10} = 1024$ pulses/rev → binary stream $\rightarrow$ decimal RPM.
> - **VFD Keypad:** Setting frequency 50 Hz → keypad stores as binary `110010` in register.
> - **PLC Ladder Logic:** Motor ON/OFF, Forward/Reverse commands are single-bit binary flags.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Conversion Type** | **Method** | **Equation** | **KTU Exam Tip** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Decimal → Binary** | Successive Division by 2 | Read remainders **bottom-up** (MSB to LSB) | Divide until quotient = 0 |
| **Binary → Decimal** | Positional Weight Expansion | $\sum b_i \cdot 2^{i}$ | Start with LSB ($2^0$) |
| **Decimal → Octal** | Successive Division by 8 | Read remainders bottom-up | Useful for 3-bit grouping |
| **Octal → Binary** | Expand each octal digit to 3 bits | $d_{oct} \rightarrow 3$-bit binary | 1 octal digit = 3 binary bits |
| **Decimal → Hexadecimal** | Successive Division by 16 | Read remainders bottom-up | Use A=10, B=11, ..., F=15 |
| **Hexadecimal → Binary** | Expand each hex digit to 4 bits | $d_{hex} \rightarrow 4$-bit binary | 1 hex digit = 4 binary bits |
| **Binary → Octal** | Group bits in 3 from LSB | Pad MSB with zeros if needed | Quick reverse conversion |
| **Binary → Hexadecimal** | Group bits in 4 from LSB | Pad MSB with zeros if needed | Standard in microcontrollers |
| **Fractional Decimal → Binary** | Successive Multiplication by 2 | Read integer parts **top-down** | Used in DSP fractional math |
| **1's Complement** | Invert every bit | $\overline{b_i}$ | Used in signed arithmetic |
| **2's Complement** | 1's complement + 1 | $\overline{b} + 1$ | Standard for negative numbers in DSP/microcontrollers |
| **BCD (Binary Coded Decimal)** | Each decimal digit $\rightarrow$ 4-bit binary | e.g., $47_{10} = 0100\,0111_{BCD}$ | Used in 7-segment displays, motor panel meters |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Convert $(45)_{10}$ to Binary (Successive Division Method)

This is the **most frequently asked KTU question pattern** for decimal-to-binary conversion.

**Step 1 — Set up successive division by 2:**

| **Division Step** | **Quotient (Q)** | **Remainder (R)** | **Bit Significance** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $45 \div 2$ | $22$ | $1$ | **LSB** ($2^0$) |
| $22 \div 2$ | $11$ | $0$ | $2^1$ |
| $11 \div 2$ | $5$ | $1$ | $2^2$ |
| $5 \div 2$ | $2$ | $1$ | $2^3$ |
| $2 \div 2$ | $1$ | $0$ | $2^4$ |
| $1 \div 2$ | $0$ | $1$ | **MSB** ($2^5$) |

**Step 2 — Stop the process when the quotient becomes zero.** The last quotient is discarded; the algorithm terminates at the row where $Q = 0$.

**Step 3 — Read remainders from bottom to top (MSB to LSB):**

$$
(45)_{10} = (101101)_2
$$

**Verification by positional weight expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(101101)_2 &= (1 \times 2^5) + (0 \times 2^4) + (1 \times 2^3) + (1 \times 2^2) + (0 \times 2^1) + (1 \times 2^0) \\
&= 32 + 0 + 8 + 4 + 0 + 1 \\
&= (45)_{10} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Convert $(11010110)_2$ to Decimal (Positional Weight Method)

This is the standard **binary-to-decimal** question appearing in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions.

**Step 1 — Assign positional weights from right (LSB):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bit: } & \;\;1\;\;\;\;1\;\;\;\;0\;\;\;\;1\;\;\;\;0\;\;\;\;1\;\;\;\;1\;\;\;\;0 \\
\text{Position (i): } & \;\;7\;\;\;\;6\;\;\;\;5\;\;\;\;4\;\;\;\;3\;\;\;\;2\;\;\;\;1\;\;\;\;0 \\
\text{Weight } (2^i): & \;128\;\;64\;\;32\;\;16\;\;\;8\;\;\;4\;\;\;2\;\;\;1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply each bit by its positional weight and sum:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Decimal Value} &= (1 \times 128) + (1 \times 64) + (0 \times 32) + (1 \times 16) + (0 \times 8) + (1 \times 4) + (1 \times 2) + (0 \times 1) \\
&= 128 + 64 + 0 + 16 + 0 + 4 + 2 + 0 \\
&= (214)_{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification (reverse conversion by repeated division):**

$$
214 \div 2 = 107 \text{ R } 0 \;\rightarrow\; 107 \div 2 = 53 \text{ R } 1 \;\rightarrow\; 53 \div 2 = 26 \text{ R } 1 \;\rightarrow\; 26 \div 2 = 13 \text{ R } 0
$$

$$
13 \div 2 = 6 \text{ R } 1 \;\rightarrow\; 6 \div 2 = 3 \text{ R } 0 \;\rightarrow\; 3 \div 2 = 1 \text{ R } 1 \;\rightarrow\; 1 \div 2 = 0 \text{ R } 1
$$

Reading remainders bottom-up: $11010110$ ✓

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Convert $(173)_{10}$ to Octal (Bridge via Binary)

This demonstrates the **binary-grouping** trick used in KTU problems.

**Step 1 — First convert decimal to binary:**

$$
(173)_{10} \;\xrightarrow{\text{successive division}}\; (10101101)_2
$$

(Verification: $128 + 32 + 8 + 4 + 1 = 173$ ✓)

**Step 2 — Group binary bits in sets of 3, starting from the LSB (right side). Pad with leading zeros if necessary:**

$$
10101101_2 \;\rightarrow\; \underbrace{010}_{2}\;\underbrace{101}_{5}\;\underbrace{101}_{5}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert each 3-bit group to its octal equivalent:**

| **3-bit Binary** | **Octal Digit** |
|---|---|
| `010` | $2$ |
| `101` | $5$ |
| `101` | $5$ |

$$
(173)_{10} = (10101101)_2 = (255)_8
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Convert $(10110101)_2$ to Hexadecimal

**Step 1 — Group binary bits in sets of 4, starting from LSB:**

$$
10110101_2 \;\rightarrow\; \underbrace{1011}_{B}\;\underbrace{0101}_{5}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert each 4-bit group to hex (using A=10, B=11, C=12, D=13, E=14, F=15):**

$$
(10110101)_2 = (B5)_{16}
$$

**Verification:** $B5_{16} = (11 \times 16) + 5 = 176 + 5 = (181)_{10}$

Direct check: $128 + 32 + 16 + 4 + 1 = 181$ ✓

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5: Fractional Conversion — $(0.625)_{10}$ to Binary

Used in DSP-based AC motor control for fractional coefficient representation.

**Method — Successive Multiplication by 2, reading integer part top-down:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.625 \times 2 &= 1.250 \;\rightarrow\; \text{Integer} = 1 \;\;\text{(MSB of fraction)} \\
0.250 \times 2 &= 0.500 \;\rightarrow\; \text{Integer} = 0 \\
0.500 \times 2 &= 1.000 \;\rightarrow\; \text{Integer} = 1 \;\;\text{(LSB of fraction)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
(0.625)_{10} = (0.101)_2
$$

**Verification:**

$$
(0.101)_2 = \left(\frac{1}{2^1}\right) + \left(\frac{0}{2^2}\right) + \left(\frac{1}{2^3}\right) = 0.5 + 0 + 0.125 = 0.625 \;\checkmark
$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 6: 2's Complement Representation of $(-25)_{10}$ in 8-bit

Essential for AC drive DSP programming of negative torque/speed references.

**Step 1 — Find binary of +25:**

$$
(25)_{10} = (00011001)_2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply 1's complement (invert all bits):**

$$
\overline{00011001} = 11100110
$$

**Step 3 — Add 1 to obtain 2's complement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&  \;\;11100110 \\
+ &  \;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;1 \\
\hline
&  \;\;11100111
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
(-25)_{10} = (11100111)_{\text{2's comp, 8-bit}}
$$

**Verification (sign-bit check):** MSB = 1 → number is negative. To decode: 2's complement of `11100111` = `00011001` = $+25$ → original = $-25$ ✓

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Decimal to Binary Conversion Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Input Decimal Number N] --> checkA{N greater than 0?}
    checkA -- No --> endA[End: Combine remainders as MSB to LSB]
    checkA -- Yes --> divA[Divide N by 2]
    divA --> remA[Store Remainder R = N mod 2]
    remA --> updateA[Update N = N divided by 2 integer quotient]
    updateA --> checkA
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Binary to Decimal Conversion Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB[Start: Input Binary String of length N] --> initB[Initialize decimal sum = 0, position i = 0]
    initB --> readB{More bits to read?}
    readB -- No --> outputB[Output: Decimal Sum is the answer]
    readB -- Yes --> nextB[Read next bit b from LSB]
    nextB --> calcB[Add b times 2 power i to sum]
    calcB --> incB[Increment position i = i plus 1]
    incB --> readB
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — AC Machine Lab Application of Binary Conversion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensorA[AC Motor Speed Sensor RPM] --> encoderA[Optical Encoder 10 bit]
    encoderA --> binStream[Binary Pulse Stream e.g. 1011010010]
    binStream --> dspA[Microcontroller DSP Conversion Block]
    dspA --> decA[Decimal RPM Value for LCD Display]
    decA --> hmiA[Operator HMI Panel]
    hmiA --> inputCmd[Operator Sets Frequency 50 Hz]
    inputCmd --> enc2[Decimal to Binary Encoder]
    enc2 --> regA[DSP Register e.g. 110010]
    regA --> vfdA[VFD PWM Generator]
    vfdA --> motorA[Three Phase Induction Motor]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Conversion Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root{What is the source and target base?}
    root -- Decimal to Binary --> A[Use Successive Division by 2]
    root -- Binary to Decimal --> B[Use Positional Weight Sum 2 to the power i]
    root -- Decimal to Octal --> C[Use Successive Division by 8 OR Bridge via Binary]
    root -- Decimal to Hex --> D[Use Successive Division by 16 OR Bridge via Binary]
    root -- Binary to Octal --> E[Group bits in 3 from LSB]
    root -- Binary to Hex --> F[Group bits in 4 from LSB]
    root -- Fractional Decimal to Binary --> G[Use Successive Multiplication by 2]
    root -- Negative Decimal to Binary --> H[Use 2's Complement: Invert plus 1]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Convert the decimal number $(156)_{10}$ into its binary equivalent. Show all steps of the successive division method.**

**Model Answer:**

Apply repeated division by 2, recording remainders:

$$
\begin{aligned}
156 \div 2 &= 78 \text{ R } 0 \quad (2^0 \text{ bit}) \\
78 \div 2 &= 39 \text{ R } 0 \quad (2^1 \text{ bit}) \\
39 \div 2 &= 19 \text{ R } 1 \quad (2^2 \text{ bit}) \\
19 \div 2 &= 9 \text{ R } 1 \quad (2^3 \text{ bit}) \\
9 \div 2 &= 4 \text{ R } 1 \quad (2^4 \text{ bit}) \\
4 \div 2 &= 2 \text{ R } 0 \quad (2^5 \text{ bit}) \\
2 \div 2 &= 1 \text{ R } 0 \quad (2^6 \text{ bit}) \\
1 \div 2 &= 0 \text{ R } 1 \quad (2^7 \text{ bit, MSB})
\end{aligned}
$$

Reading bottom-up: $(156)_{10} = (10011100)_2$ **[3 marks: 2 for division table + 1 for final answer]**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Convert the binary number $(10110100)_2$ into its decimal equivalent using the positional weight method.**

**Model Answer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(10110100)_2 &= (1 \times 2^7) + (0 \times 2^6) + (1 \times 2^5) + (1 \times 2^4) + (0 \times 2^3) + (1 \times 2^2) + (0 \times 2^1) + (0 \times 2^0) \\
&= 128 + 0 + 32 + 16 + 0 + 4 + 0 + 0 \\
&= (180)_{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

**[3 marks: 1 for weight table + 1 for multiplication + 1 for final sum]**

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Convert the decimal number $(345)_{10}$ into **(i)** binary, **(ii)** octal, and **(iii)** hexadecimal. Show all intermediate steps. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** An optical encoder fitted to a 3-phase induction motor produces a **10-bit binary output** per revolution. If the controller reads the binary value $(1110011001)_2$ at one instant, calculate the corresponding decimal count and the angular resolution of the encoder in degrees. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**(i) Decimal to Binary $(345)_{10}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
345 \div 2 &= 172 \text{ R } 1 \\
172 \div 2 &= 86 \text{ R } 0 \\
86 \div 2 &= 43 \text{ R } 0 \\
43 \div 2 &= 21 \text{ R } 1 \\
21 \div 2 &= 10 \text{ R } 1 \\
10 \div 2 &= 5 \text{ R } 0 \\
5 \div 2 &= 2 \text{ R } 1 \\
2 \div 2 &= 1 \text{ R } 0 \\
1 \div 2 &= 0 \text{ R } 1
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\therefore (345)_{10} = (101011001)_2
$$

**Verification:** $256 + 64 + 16 + 8 + 1 = 345$ ✓

**(ii) Binary to Octal — Group bits in 3 from LSB:**

$$
\underbrace{101}_{5}\,\underbrace{011}_{3}\,\underbrace{001}_{1} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; (345)_{10} = (531)_8
$$

**(iii) Binary to Hexadecimal — Group bits in 4 from LSB:**

Pad to multiple of 4: $0001\,0101\,1001$ → $\underbrace{0001}_{1}\,\underbrace{0101}_{5}\,\underbrace{1001}_{9}$

$$
\therefore (345)_{10} = (159)_{16}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Convert the 10-bit binary to decimal:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1110011001)_2 &= (1 \times 2^9) + (1 \times 2^8) + (1 \times 2^7) + (0 \times 2^6) + (0 \times 2^5) + (1 \times 2^4) + (1 \times 2^3) + (0 \times 2^2) + (0 \times 2^1) + (1 \times 2^0) \\
&= 512 + 256 + 128 + 0 + 0 + 16 + 8 + 0 + 0 + 1 \\
&= (921)_{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute angular resolution:**

For a 10-bit encoder, the total counts per revolution $= 2^{10} = 1024$.

$$
\text{Angular Resolution} = \frac{360^\circ}{2^{10}} = \frac{360}{1024} = 0.3516^\circ \text{ per count}
$$

**Step 3 — Position of the read value:**

$$
\text{Angle} = 921 \times 0.3516^\circ = 323.79^\circ
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With a neat stepwise procedure, convert $(0.40625)_{10}$ to its binary fractional form. Verify your result. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Represent the decimal number $(-47)_{10}$ in **8-bit 2's complement** form and briefly explain why 2's complement is preferred over sign-magnitude representation in DSP-based AC motor controllers. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Successive multiplication by 2 method:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.40625 \times 2 &= 0.81250 \;\rightarrow\; \text{Int} = 0 \quad (2^{-1} \text{ bit}) \\
0.81250 \times 2 &= 1.62500 \;\rightarrow\; \text{Int} = 1 \quad (2^{-2} \text{ bit}) \\
0.62500 \times 2 &= 1.25000 \;\rightarrow\; \text{Int} = 1 \quad (2^{-3} \text{ bit}) \\
0.25000 \times 2 &= 0.50000 \;\rightarrow\; \text{Int} = 0 \quad (2^{-4} \text{ bit}) \\
0.50000 \times 2 &= 1.00000 \;\rightarrow\; \text{Int} = 1 \quad (2^{-5} \text{ bit})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\therefore (0.40625)_{10} = (0.01101)_2
$$

**Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.01101)_2 &= \left(\frac{0}{2}\right) + \left(\frac{1}{4}\right) + \left(\frac{1}{8}\right) + \left(\frac{0}{16}\right) + \left(\frac{1}{32}\right) \\
&= 0 + 0.25 + 0.125 + 0 + 0.03125 \\
&= 0.40625 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Find binary of +47:**

$$
(47)_{10} = (00101111)_2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply 1's complement:**

$$
\overline{00101111} = 11010000
$$

**Step 3 — Add 1 to get 2's complement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&  \;\;11010000 \\
+ &  \;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;1 \\
\hline
&  \;\;11010001
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\therefore (-47)_{10} = (11010001)_{\text{2's comp, 8-bit}}
$$

**Explanation of preference for 2's complement in DSP-based AC controllers:**

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Single representation of zero:** 2's complement has only one zero (`00000000`), whereas sign-magnitude has both +0 and -0, wasting a code.
> 2. **Arithmetic simplicity:** Addition and subtraction of signed numbers (e.g., reference speed vs. feedback speed for PI control of induction motor) use the **same hardware adder**; no separate subtraction circuit is needed.
> 3. **Sign extension is straightforward**, making it ideal for 16-bit/32-bit DSP registers in vector control of AC machines.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Reading remainders in the WRONG direction** in decimal-to-binary conversion is the #1 mistake. Always read **bottom-up** (last remainder first) to get the MSB.
> 2. **Forgetting to pad with leading zeros** when grouping binary bits for octal (groups of 3) or hex (groups of 4) conversion leads to wrong answers. Always pad on the **MSB (left) side**.
> 3. **For fractional conversion, reading integer parts bottom-up instead of top-down** is a frequent error. Read integer parts of successive multiplications **top-down**.
> 4. **Confusing 1's complement with 2's complement**: Remember 2's complement = 1's complement + 1.
> 5. **Dropping the final quotient**: When the quotient becomes 0, stop dividing. The last remainder is the MSB; the final quotient of 0 is **discarded**, not appended.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Base-2 system** uses only digits **0** and **1**, with each position representing a power of **2**.
- **Decimal → Binary** uses **successive division by 2**, reading remainders **bottom-up**.
- **Binary → Decimal** uses the **positional weight expansion** $\sum b_i \cdot 2^{i}$.
- **Binary ↔ Octal** is achieved by **grouping bits in 3** from the LSB (pad MSB with zeros if needed).
- **Binary ↔ Hexadecimal** uses **grouping in 4** bits from the LSB.
- **Fractional decimal** conversion uses **successive multiplication by 2**, reading integer parts **top-down**.
- **2's complement** = **invert all bits + 1**; it is the standard for negative numbers in motor control DSPs.
- **BCD** represents each decimal digit with **4 bits** — common in 7-segment panel meters of AC drives.
- **MSB (Most Significant Bit)** is the leftmost bit with the **highest positional weight** ($2^{N-1}$).
- **LSB (Least Significant Bit)** is the rightmost bit with weight $2^0 = 1$.
- **Real-world link:** Encoder pulse counts, VFD parameter storage, and PLC motor control bits are all ultimately binary — making this conversion essential for understanding digital instrumentation in any AC Machines Lab.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Square wave
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL507 - AC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 8
> - **Topic:** Square wave

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **square wave** is a periodic, non-sinusoidal waveform that alternates instantaneously between two fixed amplitude levels (a positive maximum and a negative minimum) with a constant period. The waveform spends exactly **50% of its time** at the positive peak and **50% at the negative peak** (for an ideal symmetric square wave), with theoretically zero rise and fall times (vertical transitions).

In the context of **AC Machines Lab (PCEEL507, Module Experiment 8)**, the square wave is of central importance because it represents the **output voltage waveform of a single-phase or three-phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** operating in the **square-wave (six-step) modulation mode**. This is the most fundamental and historically earliest method of producing variable-frequency AC supply for induction motors and synchronous motors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A square wave is a periodic waveform with the mathematical form $v(t) = V_m \cdot \text{sgn}(\sin(\omega t))$, characterized by its fundamental frequency $f$, amplitude $V_m$, and a Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) of approximately **48.34%**.

### 1.2 Mathematical Representation

For an ideal symmetric square wave of amplitude $V_m$, angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$, and zero DC offset:

$$
v(t) = \begin{cases} +V_m, & 0 \leq t < T/2 \\ -V_m, & T/2 \leq t < T \end{cases}
$$

where $T = 1/f$ is the time period.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Light Switch in a Staircase:**
> Imagine a stairwell light controlled by a manual switch. You flip the switch **ON** (top of the square — $V_m$) for 10 seconds, then **OFF** (bottom of the square — $0\,\text{V}$ or $-V_m$) for 10 seconds, repeating endlessly. The graph of "light voltage vs. time" is a **square wave**. The "instant flip" of the switch corresponds to the **vertical edges** of the square wave. In a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) feeding an AC induction motor, the **IGBT switches** perform this same "instant flipping" — but thousands of times per second — to synthesize the AC supply from a DC bus.

### 1.4 Why Square Waves Matter in AC Machines Lab

In the KTU 2024 Scheme AC Machines Lab, the square wave experiment typically demonstrates:

- The **six-step inverter output** used in early industrial drives.
- The presence of **strong odd harmonics** (3rd, 5th, 7th, …) and their effect on motor heating and torque pulsations.
- The **Fourier decomposition** of a non-sinusoidal waveform into a fundamental sine wave plus harmonics — the mathematical bridge between square wave theory and machine performance.
- A clear contrast with **SPWM (Sinusoidal PWM)** waveforms, which aim to suppress low-order harmonics.

### 1.5 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Fundamental frequency | $f_1$ | **50 Hz** (KTU standard, India) |
| Angular frequency | $\omega_1$ | $2\pi f_1 = 314.159$ rad/s |
| Amplitude | $V_m$ | Volts (V) |
| RMS value of ideal symmetric square wave | $V_{rms}$ | $= V_m$ (same as peak!) |
| Crest Factor (CF) | $V_m / V_{rms}$ | **1.0** (vs. 1.414 for sine) |
| Total Harmonic Distortion | THD | **≈ 48.34 %** |
| Form Factor (FF) | $V_{rms} / V_{avg}$ | **1.0** (since $V_{avg}$ over full period = 0; for half-wave rectified, $V_{avg} = V_m/2$ so FF = 2) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Description of the Square Wave in Time Domain

The ideal symmetric square wave is an **odd function** of time (i.e., $v(-t) = -v(t)$), so its Fourier series contains **only sine terms** — no cosine terms and no DC component.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** When asked to derive the Fourier series of a square wave in the exam, always state first that the waveform is **odd-symmetric** → only sine terms survive, then compute the $b_n$ coefficients.

## 2.2 Fourier Series Decomposition — The Heart of Square Wave Theory

The Fourier series of an ideal symmetric square wave of amplitude $V_m$ is:

$$
v(t) = \frac{4V_m}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,7,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n} \sin(n \omega t)
$$

Expanding the first few terms explicitly:

$$
v(t) = \frac{4V_m}{\pi}\left[\sin(\omega t) + \frac{1}{3}\sin(3\omega t) + \frac{1}{5}\sin(5\omega t) + \frac{1}{7}\sin(7\omega t) + \cdots \right]
$$

### Key observations from the series:

- The **fundamental component** has amplitude $\dfrac{4V_m}{\pi} \approx 1.273\,V_m$.
- **Only odd harmonics** exist (3rd, 5th, 7th, 9th, 11th, 13th, …).
- The **amplitude of each harmonic is inversely proportional to its order** $n$.
- The **3rd harmonic is the largest harmonic** at $1/3$ of the fundamental amplitude.

## 2.3 RMS Value Derivation (Time-Domain)

For any periodic waveform, RMS is defined as:

$$
V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\, dt}
$$

For a symmetric square wave alternating between $+V_m$ and $-V_m$:

$$
V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\left[\int_0^{T/2} V_m^2\, dt + \int_{T/2}^{T} (-V_m)^2\, dt\right]} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\cdot V_m^2 \cdot T} = V_m
$$

**Crucial result:** For an ideal square wave, $V_{rms} = V_m$ (peak), giving a **Crest Factor of 1.0**.

## 2.4 Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)

THD quantifies the "non-sinusoidality" of the waveform:

$$
\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots}}{V_1} = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n^2}}{V_1}
$$

For the square wave:

$$
\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=3,5,7,\ldots}\left(\frac{4V_m}{n\pi}\right)^2}}{\frac{4V_m}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\sum_{n=3,5,7,\ldots} \frac{1}{n^2}}
$$

The series $\sum_{n=3,5,7,\ldots}^{\infty} 1/n^2 = \pi^2/8 - 1$, so:

$$
\text{THD}_{square} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2}{8} - 1} \approx 0.4834 = \mathbf{48.34\%}
$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Numerical Result | KTU Board Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fourier series | $v(t) = \frac{4V_m}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}\frac{\sin(n\omega t)}{n}$ | Infinite series | ⭐⭐⭐ Most asked |
| 2 | Fundamental amplitude | $V_1 = \frac{4V_m}{\pi}$ | $1.273\,V_m$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | $n$-th harmonic amplitude | $V_n = \frac{4V_m}{n\pi}$ (n odd) | $V_n = V_1/n$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | RMS value | $V_{rms} = V_m$ | Equal to peak | ⭐⭐ |
| 5 | Crest Factor | $CF = V_m / V_{rms}$ | **1.0** | ⭐⭐ |
| 6 | Form Factor | $FF = V_{rms}/V_{avg,\text{half}}$ | **1.0** (full period) / **2.0** (half-wave) | ⭐ |
| 7 | THD | $\sqrt{\pi^2/8 - 1}$ | **48.34 %** | ⭐⭐ |
| 8 | Fundamental RMS | $V_{1,rms} = \frac{4V_m}{\pi\sqrt{2}}$ | $0.9003\,V_m$ | ⭐⭐ |
| 9 | Harmonic factor (HF) | $\sqrt{(V^2_{rms} - V^2_{1,rms})}/V_{1,rms}$ | = THD = 48.34 % | ⭐ |
| 10 | DC component | $a_0$ | **0** (symmetric) | ⭐ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** All absolute-value / magnitude bars in the table above use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or "RMS = $V_m$" notation — **no vertical pipes** that could break markdown table syntax.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in AC Drives

The square wave six-step inverter is still the **most power-efficient modulation scheme** for high-power AC drives (e.g., > 100 kW), because each switch transitions only **once per electrical cycle** (minimum switching loss). It is the standard output of:

- **Six-step VSI** for induction motor drives (locomotives, industrial pumps).
- **Current Source Inverters (CSI)** in high-power synchronous motor drives.
- **Cycloconverters** in very large mill drives.

The trade-off is **high harmonic distortion**, which causes additional copper losses (≈ $I^2 R$ heating from harmonic currents), pulsating torques, and acoustic noise — all topics tested in KTU experiments.

## 2.7 Six-Step Inverter Output (Three-Phase Context)

In a three-phase, six-step VSI supplying an induction motor, each phase voltage is a square wave **displaced by 120°** from the others. The **line-to-line voltage** is a **quasi-square wave** with **60° flat tops** and **60° ramps**, having a fundamental RMS of:

$$
V_{L,\text{fund,rms}} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi} V_{DC} \approx 0.7799\,V_{DC}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** This formula $\frac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi}V_{DC}$ for the fundamental RMS line voltage of a six-step inverter is **frequently asked in ESE (Even Semester Exam) Module 2/3** — link it to the lab observation that motor speed under six-step control follows $V/f$ ratio.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fourier Coefficients of the Square Wave

We consider an ideal symmetric square wave of amplitude $V_m$, period $T = 2\pi/\omega$, defined as $v(t) = +V_m$ for $0 < t < T/2$ and $v(t) = -V_m$ for $T/2 < t < T$.

**Step 1 — Identify the symmetry.**

The waveform is an **odd function**: $v(-t) = -v(t)$. This means all cosine coefficients $a_n = 0$ and the DC component $a_0 = 0$. Only the sine coefficients $b_n$ are non-zero.

**Step 2 — Compute the DC component $a_0$.**

$$
a_0 = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v(t)\, dt = \frac{1}{T}\left[\int_0^{T/2} V_m\, dt + \int_{T/2}^{T} (-V_m)\, dt\right] = \frac{1}{T}\left[V_m \cdot \frac{T}{2} - V_m \cdot \frac{T}{2}\right] = 0
$$

The DC component is **zero** because the positive and negative areas cancel exactly.

**Step 3 — Compute the cosine coefficients $a_n$.**

$$
a_n = \frac{2}{T}\int_0^T v(t)\cos(n\omega t)\, dt
$$

By odd-symmetry, $v(t)\cos(n\omega t)$ is an **even × even = even** function, but since $v(t)$ is odd, the integral of an **odd** function over a symmetric interval is zero. Hence $a_n = 0$ for all $n$.

$$
a_n = \frac{2}{T}\left[\int_0^{T/2} V_m\cos(n\omega t)\, dt - \int_{T/2}^{T} V_m\cos(n\omega t)\, dt\right] = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the sine coefficients $b_n$.**

$$
b_n = \frac{2}{T}\int_0^T v(t)\sin(n\omega t)\, dt
$$

We split the integral:

$$
b_n = \frac{2}{T}\left[\int_0^{T/2} V_m\sin(n\omega t)\, dt - \int_{T/2}^{T} V_m\sin(n\omega t)\, dt\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the first integral.**

$$
\int_0^{T/2} V_m\sin(n\omega t)\, dt = V_m\left[-\frac{\cos(n\omega t)}{n\omega}\right]_0^{T/2} = \frac{V_m}{n\omega}\left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{n\omega T}{2}\right)\right]
$$

Substituting $\omega T = 2\pi$:

$$
\frac{V_m}{n\omega}\left[1 - \cos(n\pi)\right]
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the second integral.**

$$
\int_{T/2}^{T} V_m\sin(n\omega t)\, dt = \frac{V_m}{n\omega}\left[\cos(n\pi) - \cos(2n\pi)\right] = \frac{V_m}{n\omega}\left[\cos(n\pi) - 1\right]
$$

since $\cos(2n\pi) = 1$.

**Step 7 — Combine the two integrals.**

$$
b_n = \frac{2V_m}{T\, n\omega}\left\{\left[1 - \cos(n\pi)\right] - \left[\cos(n\pi) - 1\right]\right\} = \frac{2V_m}{T\, n\omega}\left[2 - 2\cos(n\pi)\right]
$$

Using $T = 2\pi/\omega$:

$$
b_n = \frac{2V_m}{(2\pi/\omega)\, n\omega} \cdot 2\left[1 - \cos(n\pi)\right] = \frac{2V_m}{n\pi}\left[1 - \cos(n\pi)\right]
$$

**Step 8 — Apply the parity of $\cos(n\pi)$.**

- If $n$ is **even**: $\cos(n\pi) = +1$, so $b_n = 0$. → **No even harmonics.**
- If $n$ is **odd**: $\cos(n\pi) = -1$, so $b_n = \dfrac{2V_m}{n\pi}(1-(-1)) = \dfrac{4V_m}{n\pi}$.

$$
\boxed{\,b_n = \begin{cases} \dfrac{4V_m}{n\pi}, & n = 1, 3, 5, 7, \ldots \\ 0, & n = 2, 4, 6, 8, \ldots \end{cases}}
$$

**Step 9 — Write the final Fourier series.**

$$
v(t) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} b_n\sin(n\omega t) = \frac{4V_m}{\pi}\left[\sin(\omega t) + \frac{1}{3}\sin(3\omega t) + \frac{1}{5}\sin(5\omega t) + \frac{1}{7}\sin(7\omega t) + \cdots\right]
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — RMS Value of the Square Wave (Frequency Domain)

The RMS value of any periodic signal can be computed from the harmonic spectrum as:

$$
V_{rms} = \sqrt{\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_{n,rms}^2}
$$

**Step 1 — Convert each harmonic amplitude to RMS.**

The amplitude of the $n$-th harmonic (peak) is $V_n = \dfrac{4V_m}{n\pi}$ for $n$ odd, and its RMS is:

$$
V_{n,rms} = \frac{V_n}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{4V_m}{n\pi\sqrt{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Square and sum.**

$$
V_{rms}^2 = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \left(\frac{4V_m}{n\pi\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 = \frac{8V_m^2}{\pi^2}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}\frac{1}{n^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Use the known series identity.**

$$
\sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n^2} = \frac{\pi^2}{8}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify.**

$$
V_{rms}^2 = \frac{8V_m^2}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{\pi^2}{8} = V_m^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_{rms} = V_m\,}
$$

**Verification (Parseval's Theorem):** The total power in the time domain (RMS²) equals the sum of the powers of all harmonics. This confirms the result.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)

**Step 1 — THD definition.**

$$
\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots}}{V_1}
$$

For square wave, even harmonics are zero, so:

$$
\text{THD} = \frac{1}{V_1}\sqrt{\sum_{n=3,5,7,\ldots} V_n^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $V_n = V_1/n$.**

$$
\text{THD} = \frac{1}{V_1}\sqrt{\sum_{n=3,5,7,\ldots} \left(\frac{V_1}{n}\right)^2} = \sqrt{\sum_{n=3,5,7,\ldots} \frac{1}{n^2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Relate to known series.**

The full odd-harmonic series from $n=1$ equals $\pi^2/8$. Removing the $n=1$ term ($=1$):

$$
\sum_{n=3,5,7,\ldots}\frac{1}{n^2} = \frac{\pi^2}{8} - 1
$$

**Step 4 — Compute THD.**

$$
\text{THD} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2}{8} - 1} = \sqrt{1.2337 - 1} = \sqrt{0.2337} = 0.4834
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\text{THD}_{square} \approx 48.34\%\,}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Fundamental RMS Component (Machine-Relevant)

The **fundamental component is the only one producing useful torque** in an AC machine. Its RMS is:

$$
V_{1,rms} = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{4V_m}{\pi\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}\,V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.9003\,V_m
$$

This is the **effective voltage** that the induction motor "sees" for torque production.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Harmonic Content Table

For $V_m = 1$ V (normalized), the harmonic spectrum of a unit square wave is:

| Harmonic Order $n$ | Frequency $f_n$ (Hz, for $f_1=50$) | Peak Amplitude $V_n$ (V) | RMS Amplitude $V_{n,rms}$ (V) | % of Fundamental | Cumulative THD (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (fundamental) | 50 | 1.2732 | 0.9003 | 100.00 | 0.00 |
| 3 | 150 | 0.4244 | 0.3001 | 33.33 | 33.33 |
| 5 | 250 | 0.2546 | 0.1801 | 20.00 | 38.73 |
| 7 | 350 | 0.1819 | 0.1286 | 14.29 | 41.38 |
| 9 | 450 | 0.1415 | 0.1000 | 11.11 | 43.06 |
| 11 | 550 | 0.1157 | 0.0818 | 9.09 | 44.13 |
| 13 | 650 | 0.0979 | 0.0693 | 7.69 | 44.86 |
| 15 | 750 | 0.0849 | 0.0600 | 6.67 | 45.40 |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\to$ 48.34 |

## 3.6 Effect on AC Machines — Quantitative Summary

Each harmonic in the supply produces a magnetic field in the air-gap rotating at speed $n_s/n$. The **cogging and torque pulsations** caused by the 5th and 7th harmonics (which are the most troublesome in three-phase systems) are visible on the lab oscilloscope as ripple on the torque waveform.

- **Copper loss ratio** (square wave / pure sine): $\sum 1/n^2 / 1 = \pi^2/8 \approx 1.2337$ → **23.37% more copper loss** at the same fundamental current.
- **Torque ripple** at 6th harmonic of fundamental (from 5th and 7th space-harmonic interaction).
- **Acoustic noise** peaks near 5th and 7th harmonic frequencies.

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Question:** A 50 Hz square wave of amplitude 100 V is applied to a single-phase resistive load. Find:
(a) RMS value, (b) Crest factor, (c) THD, (d) Power dissipated in a 10 Ω resistor.

**Solution:**

**(a) RMS value:** $V_{rms} = V_m = \mathbf{100\ V}$

**(b) Crest factor:** $CF = V_m / V_{rms} = 100/100 = \mathbf{1.0}$

**(c) THD:** $\text{THD} = \sqrt{\pi^2/8 - 1} = \mathbf{48.34\%}$

**(d) Power:** $P = V_{rms}^2 / R = 100^2/10 = \mathbf{1000\ W}$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid
    style Amp fill:#4527a0,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style Scope fill:#ef6c00,color:#ffffff
    style PC fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Square Wave Time-Domain Sketch (ASCII representation)

```
   Vm |   +----+      +----+      +----+
      |   |    |      |    |      |    |
    0 |---+----+------+----+------+----+------>  t
      |        |    |      |    |
  -Vm |        +----+      +----+
      |  <-T/2-><--T/2-->
      |__|______|______|______|______|______
         0     T/2     T     3T/2    2T
```

## 4.5 Harmonic Spectrum Sketch (Conceptual)

```
  Vn (V)
   ^
   | *  <- n=1 (fundamental), 1.273 Vm
   |
   |     *  <- n=3, 0.424 Vm
   |
   |        *  <- n=5, 0.255 Vm
   |
   |           *  <- n=7, 0.182 Vm
   |
   |              *  <- n=9, 0.142 Vm
   |
   +---------------------------> n (odd)
      1   3   5   7   9  11  13
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define a square wave. List any three of its characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

A square wave is a periodic, non-sinusoidal waveform that alternates between two fixed levels ($+V_m$ and $-V_m$) with equal dwell time, having a **50% duty cycle** and theoretically zero transition time.

**Three characteristics:**
1. RMS value equals the peak amplitude: $V_{rms} = V_m$ (Crest Factor = 1.0).
2. Contains **only odd harmonics**, with amplitudes inversely proportional to harmonic order.
3. Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) ≈ **48.34%**, much higher than a pure sine wave (0%).

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**State the Fourier series expression of a square wave and identify which harmonics are present.**

**Model Answer:**

$$
v(t) = \frac{4V_m}{\pi}\left[\sin(\omega t) + \frac{1}{3}\sin(3\omega t) + \frac{1}{5}\sin(5\omega t) + \frac{1}{7}\sin(7\omega t) + \cdots\right]
$$

Only **odd harmonics** (1st, 3rd, 5th, 7th, …) are present. **Even harmonics and the DC component are zero** due to the half-wave and odd symmetries of the waveform.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the Fourier series expression for an ideal symmetric square wave of amplitude $V_m$ and angular frequency $\omega$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 50 Hz square wave of peak value 200 V is applied across a 20 Ω resistor. Compute:
   (i) RMS value of the voltage, (ii) Power dissipated, (iii) Crest factor, (iv) THD, (v) RMS value of the fundamental component, (vi) The percentage of 3rd and 5th harmonics relative to the fundamental. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** The square wave is an odd function → $a_0 = 0$, $a_n = 0$, only $b_n \neq 0$.

**Step 2:** Compute $b_n$:

$$
b_n = \frac{2}{T}\int_0^T v(t)\sin(n\omega t)\, dt = \frac{2V_m}{T}\left[\int_0^{T/2}\sin(n\omega t)\, dt - \int_{T/2}^{T}\sin(n\omega t)\, dt\right]
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate each integral:

$$
\int_0^{T/2}\sin(n\omega t)\, dt = \frac{1 - \cos(n\pi)}{n\omega}
$$

$$
\int_{T/2}^{T}\sin(n\omega t)\, dt = \frac{\cos(n\pi) - 1}{n\omega}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute and simplify using $T = 2\pi/\omega$:

$$
b_n = \frac{2V_m}{n\pi}\left[1 - \cos(n\pi)\right]
$$

**Step 5:** Apply parity: For odd $n$, $\cos(n\pi) = -1 \Rightarrow b_n = \dfrac{4V_m}{n\pi}$. For even $n$, $b_n = 0$.

**Step 6:** Final series:

$$
\boxed{\,v(t) = \frac{4V_m}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty}\frac{\sin(n\omega t)}{n}\,}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Given: $V_m = 200$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $R = 20\ \Omega$.

**(i) RMS voltage:** $V_{rms} = V_m = \mathbf{200\ V}$

**(ii) Power dissipated:** $P = V_{rms}^2 / R = (200)^2 / 20 = \mathbf{2000\ W}$

**(iii) Crest factor:** $CF = V_m / V_{rms} = 200/200 = \mathbf{1.0}$

**(iv) THD:** $\text{THD} = \sqrt{\pi^2/8 - 1} = \mathbf{48.34\%}$

**(v) Fundamental RMS:** $V_{1,rms} = \dfrac{4V_m}{\pi\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{4 \times 200}{\pi\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{800}{4.4429} = \mathbf{180.06\ V}$

**(vi) Harmonic percentages:**
- 3rd harmonic amplitude (RMS): $V_{3,rms} = V_{1,rms}/3 = 180.06/3 = 60.02$ V → $60.02/180.06 \times 100 = \mathbf{33.33\%}$
- 5th harmonic amplitude (RMS): $V_{5,rms} = V_{1,rms}/5 = 180.06/5 = 36.01$ V → $36.01/180.06 \times 100 = \mathbf{20.00\%}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Alternative]

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram how a single-phase full-bridge inverter produces a square wave output. Show the switching sequence and derive the expression for the fundamental RMS voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 6-step voltage source inverter has a DC bus voltage of 400 V. It feeds a Y-connected 10 HP, 400 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole induction motor running at no-load.
   (i) Find the fundamental RMS line-to-line voltage of the inverter output.
   (ii) Calculate the synchronous speed of the motor.
   (iii) Comment on the harmonic content and its effect on the motor performance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A **single-phase full-bridge inverter** uses four IGBT switches (S1, S2, S3, S4) arranged in an H-bridge across a DC source $V_{DC}$. Switches S1 and S4 are gated ON for the positive half-cycle, applying $+V_{DC}$ across the load. Switches S2 and S3 are gated ON for the negative half-cycle, applying $-V_{DC}$ across the load. This produces a square wave of amplitude $V_m = V_{DC}$.

The Fourier series of the output is:

$$
v_o(t) = \frac{4V_{DC}}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}\frac{\sin(n\omega t)}{n}
$$

The fundamental peak amplitude is $4V_{DC}/\pi$, so:

$$
V_{o1,\text{rms}} = \frac{4V_{DC}}{\pi\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}\,V_{DC}}{\pi} \approx 0.9003\,V_{DC}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Given: $V_{DC} = 400$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 4$ (poles).

**(i) Fundamental RMS line-to-line voltage:**

For a 3-phase, 6-step VSI:

$$
V_{L1,\text{rms}} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi}V_{DC} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi}\times 400 = 0.7799\times 400 = \mathbf{311.98\ V}
$$

**(ii) Synchronous speed:**

$$
N_s = \frac{120\,f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = \mathbf{1500\ rpm}
$$

**(iii) Harmonic effects on motor:**
- Only **odd non-triplen harmonics** (5th, 7th, 11th, 13th, …) appear in the line-to-line voltage (triplen harmonics 3, 9, 15 cancel in balanced 3-phase systems).
- Harmonic currents cause **additional copper losses** (≈ 23% above sine) and **pulsating torques** at multiples of 6× fundamental frequency.
- **Acoustic noise** increases; **insulation stress** due to $dv/dt$ spikes at the vertical edges of the square wave.
- **Remedy:** Use sinusoidal PWM (SPWM) or SVPWM to suppress low-order harmonics.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the odd-symmetry argument.** Many students write the full Fourier series with both sine and cosine terms, wasting time. Always **state the symmetry first** and conclude $a_0 = a_n = 0$.
> 2. **Confusing RMS of the full waveform with RMS of the fundamental.** The fundamental RMS is $0.9003\,V_m$, NOT $V_m$. The full waveform RMS is $V_m$.
> 3. **Mixing up form factor and crest factor.** Square wave has CF = 1.0 but FF for the half-wave-rectified version = 2.0. Read the question carefully.
> 4. **Wrong series for THD.** THD for square wave = 48.34%, NOT 33.3% (which is the 3rd harmonic content alone).
> 5. **Sign errors in the Fourier integration limits.** Always draw the waveform and mark the levels on the time axis before integrating.
> 6. **Units in the numerical problem.** $V_m$ is in volts, $V_{rms}$ is in volts, $P$ is in watts, $f$ in Hz, $\omega$ in rad/s — do not mix them up in the final answer.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Square wave**: periodic, two-level, 50% duty cycle, odd-symmetric waveform.
- **Fourier series**: $v(t) = \frac{4V_m}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}\frac{\sin(n\omega t)}{n}$.
- **Only odd harmonics present** — even harmonics and DC = 0.
- **Harmonic amplitude** $= V_1/n$ (where $V_1 = 4V_m/\pi \approx 1.273\,V_m$).
- **RMS value** $= V_m$ (Crest Factor = 1.0).
- **THD** $\approx$ **48.34%** (very high compared to a sine wave).
- **Fundamental RMS** $= 0.9003\,V_m$ (this is the "useful" voltage component for torque).
- **Copper loss increase** of about **23.37%** compared to pure sine supply at same fundamental current.
- **Six-step inverter** output is a square wave (per phase) / quasi-square wave (line-to-line).
- **Three-phase six-step VSI line RMS**: $V_{L1,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi}V_{DC} \approx 0.78\,V_{DC}$.
- **Lab observation**: The oscilloscope should show a clean square wave with sharp edges, vertical transitions, and equal positive/negative half-periods when the inverter is run in square-wave mode (modulation index deeply saturated).
- **Industrial relevance**: Square wave mode is used in **high-power drives** (locomotives, mills) for efficiency; **low-power drives** prefer SPWM for smooth torque.
- **Remedy for harmonics**: PWM, SVPWM, multi-level inverters, harmonic filters.
- **KTU 2024 outcome link**: Maps to **CO2** (Analyze AC machine performance under non-sinusoidal supply) and **CO3** (Interpret harmonic spectra from lab measurements).
- **Quick mental check**: 3rd harmonic = 33.3% of fundamental, 5th = 20%, 7th ≈ 14.3%, 9th = 11.1% — these percentages are the inverse of the harmonic order.
- **Practical tip for lab**: When measuring with a DSO, use **AC coupling** and set the **timebase to 5 ms/div** for a 50 Hz signal (one full period = 20 ms = 4 divisions).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study and familiarization of Arduino IDE
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEEL507 - AC MACHINES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 16 > - **Topic:** Study and familiarization of Arduino IDE # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Arduino Integrated Development Environment (IDE)** is a cross-platform, Java-based desktop application that serves as the complete software workspace for writing, compiling, verifying, and uploading program code (referred to as **"sketches"**) to Arduino-compatible microcontroller boards. It bundles together a source-code editor, a build system (based on `avr-gcc`/`arm-gcc` toolchain), a programmer/uploader, and a serial communication console within a single graphical user interface. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Context):** > The Arduino IDE is the official, open-source programming environment used in PCEEL507 (AC Machines Lab) to interface digital controllers, sensors (voltage, current, speed, temperature), and driver circuits with induction machines, synchronous machines, and special AC machines. Familiarity with the IDE is a *prerequisite* for all subsequent hardware-interfacing experiments. The IDE is built upon two foundational technologies: 1. **Processing** — a Java-based language/IDE that inspired the editor's design philosophy. 2. **Wiring** — a programming framework that abstracts microcontroller register-level programming into intuitive C/C++ function calls. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Workshop Blueprint Studio"** Imagine you are an electrical engineer who wants to build a custom electronic control unit (ECU) for a three-phase induction motor. You cannot speak the microcontroller's native "machine language" (binary). You need a **translator's desk** that performs three jobs: 1. **Drafting table** (Editor) — where you write your instructions in a human-readable language (C/C++). 2. **Translation office** (Compiler) — converts your instructions into machine code (`.hex` file). 3. **Postal service** (Uploader/Bootloader) — physically transfers the translated instructions to the microcontroller's flash memory via the USB cable. The **Arduino IDE is exactly this three-in-one translator's desk.** The workspace on the screen is the drafting table, the *Verify* (✓) button is the translation office, the *Upload* (→) button is the postal service, and the Serial Monitor is a two-way walkie-talkie for talking back to the chip after deployment. In the context of **AC Machines Lab**, when you want a microcontroller to *measure* the speed of an induction motor using an optocoupler, *display* the RPM on an LCD, or *generate* SPWM signals for an inverter — every one of these tasks begins with opening the Arduino IDE and writing a sketch. ## 1.3 Core Identification Markers > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Identifying Facts for KTU Viva:** > - **Latest Stable Version (as of 2024 scheme):** Arduino IDE **2.x** (Electron-based), though the classic Arduino IDE **1.8.x** is still widely used in academic labs. > - **Default File Extension:** `.ino` (e.g., `experiment16.ino`) > - **Supported OS:** Windows, macOS, Linux (truly cross-platform). > - **License:** **Open-source** under the **GNU General Public License (GPL) v2**. > - **Programming Language Used:** A simplified subset of **C/C++** with preprocessed libraries. ## 1.4 Why Arduino IDE is Used in an AC Machines Lab (KTU Context) The choice of Arduino IDE in an AC Machines laboratory is not arbitrary. It is dictated by the following engineering requirements: | Engineering Need | How Arduino IDE Solves It | |---|---| | Generate PWM/Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM) signals for inverter-fed motor control | Built-in `analogWrite()` + timer library access | | Measure RPM of an induction motor via Hall sensor / optocoupler | `attachInterrupt()` + `digitalRead()` | | Display measured V, I, cos φ on a 16×2 LCD | LiquidCrystal library (one-line `lcd.print()`) | | Log experimental data to PC | `Serial.print()` → Serial Monitor / Serial Plotter | | Implement closed-loop V/f control of 3-phase IM | Real-time loop with millisecond timing via `millis()` | | Educational accessibility for B.Tech students | C/C++ syntax; no assembly required | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Logical Phases of the Arduino IDE Workflow The Arduino IDE operation can be decomposed into **five sequential phases**. Understanding these is a *guaranteed* KTU viva question. ### Phase 1 — **Sketch Authoring (Source Code Creation)** The user types the program into the editor pane. The IDE provides: - **Syntax highlighting** (keywords in orange, comments in grey, strings in blue). - **Auto-indentation** (Ctrl+I). - **Brace-matching** to prevent syntax errors. - **Tab-based file management** when multiple `.ino` files exist in one sketch folder. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** Every sketch must be saved in a folder of the *exact same name* as the file. Example: `experiment16.ino` must reside in `C:\Users\Student\Documents\Arduino\experiment16\experiment16.ino`. This is a very common exam pitfall. ### Phase 2 — **Pre-processing (The "Arduino Magic" Stage)** When you click *Verify* or *Upload*, the IDE invokes a **pre-processor** that does several hidden things *before* compilation: 1. **Auto-generates a function prototype** for every user-defined function, so order of definition does not matter. 2. **Implicitly includes** `Arduino.h` (the master header file). 3. **Concatenates** all `.ino` files in the sketch folder into a single translation unit, in alphabetical order. 4. **Defines constants** like `LED_BUILTIN` (pin number of the on-board LED), `HIGH`, `LOW`, `INPUT`, `OUTPUT`, `true`, `false`. ### Phase 3 — **Compilation (Translation to Machine Code)** The IDE calls the appropriate compiler: - For **Arduino Uno / Nano / Mega (ATmega328P / 2560)** → `avr-gcc` - For **Arduino Due, Zero (ARM Cortex-M)** → `arm-none-eabi-gcc` The compiler performs lexical analysis, parsing, semantic analysis, optimization, and code generation. The output is a `.hex` file containing AVR/ARM machine instructions. ### Phase 4 — **Upload (Programming the Flash Memory)** The IDE invokes **`avrdude`** (for AVR boards) or `bossac`/`bossa` (for ARM boards). The compiled `.hex` is sent over the **USB-UART bridge** (commonly CH340 or ATmega16U2) to the board's **optiboot bootloader**, which resides in a small protected region of flash memory. The bootloader then writes the sketch into the main flash area. ### Phase 5 — **Execution & Serial Debugging** After upload, the microcontroller **resets** and begins executing the new code from address 0x0000.
