The Serial Monitor provides a bidirectional text console (default **9600 baud**) for sending commands and printing values — invaluable for debugging AC machine parameters like RMS voltage, RPM, or slip. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet | Parameter / Concept | Value / Rule | KTU Significance | |---|---|---| | Default ADC resolution | **10-bit (1024 discrete levels)** | For voltage measurement | | ADC reference voltage (default) | **5.0 V** (or 3.3 V on some boards) | $V_{in} = \frac{ADC \times V_{ref}}{1024}$ | | Digital I/O logic levels | **HIGH = 5 V, LOW = 0 V** | TTL compatible | | PWM output resolution | **8-bit (0–255 duty cycle)** | For SPWM generation | | Default PWM frequency (Uno) | **~490 Hz** on pins 3, 9, 10, 11; **~980 Hz** on pins 5, 6 | For inverter control | | Default Serial baud rate | **9600 bps** | Must match `Serial.begin()` | | On-board LED pin (Uno) | **Digital Pin 13** | `LED_BUILTIN` macro | | Flash memory (Uno) | **32 KB** (0.5 KB used by bootloader) | Code size limit | | SRAM (Uno) | **2 KB** | Variable storage limit | | EEPROM (Uno) | **1 KB** | Non-volatile storage | | Clock frequency (Uno) | **16 MHz** | Timing accuracy | | Sketch entry-point function | `void setup()` | Runs once at power-up/reset | | Main loop function | `void loop()` | Runs forever after `setup()` | | Comments syntax | `//` (single-line), `/* ... */` (block) | Code documentation | | File extension | `.ino` | Recognized by IDE | ## 2.3 The Two Mandatory Functions in Every Sketch > [!IMPORTANT] > **Every** Arduino program — without exception — contains **two functions** that the IDE automatically looks for: 1. **`void setup()`** - Called **exactly once** at the start (after power-up or reset). - Used for **initialization**: pin modes, baud rates, library begin() calls, attaching interrupts. - Does not return any value (hence `void`). 2. **`void loop()`** - Called **repeatedly** in an infinite `while(1)` loop immediately after `setup()` finishes. - Contains the **core logic** of the program. - The IDE internally wraps this into a `main()` function that never returns. ## 2.4 Standard Pin Configuration Modes A digital pin **must** be declared as `INPUT` or `OUTPUT` in `setup()` using `pinMode()`. Otherwise, the behavior is undefined. An additional `INPUT_PULLUP` mode engages the internal ~20–50 kΩ pull-up resistor — useful for AC mains zero-crossing detection circuits in lab. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The Arduino IDE ecosystem is not just a teaching tool. In modern power electronics and motor-drive industries, the same conceptual workflow (author → compile → upload → debug via serial) is replicated in professional IDEs like **STM32CubeIDE**, **TI Code Composer Studio**, and **MATLAB/Simulink Embedded Coder**. Mastering the Arduino IDE workflow in PCEEL507 gives you a transferable mental model for any embedded control platform you will encounter in industry. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs ## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough 1: Anatomy of a Complete Arduino Sketch Below is a **fully annotated** Arduino sketch that demonstrates every essential element of the IDE workflow. This is the kind of program a student might write in PCEEL507 to **measure the speed of an induction motor** using a slotted optocoupler. ```cpp /*============================================================== * Experiment 16 – Familiarization with Arduino IDE * Program: Read pulses from an optocoupler on Pin 2 * and compute the RPM of a rotating shaft. * Board : Arduino Uno *==============================================================*/ // ---------- Step 1: Pre-processor directive (include library) ---------- #include // Built-in LCD library // ---------- Step 2: Global constant definitions ---------- const int SENSOR_PIN = 2; // Digital pin 2 (interrupt-capable) const int LED_PIN = 13; // On-board LED const int PULSES_PER_REV = 1; // Slots in the optocoupler disc // ---------- Step 3: Global volatile variable (modified in ISR) ---------- volatile unsigned int pulseCount = 0; // ---------- Step 4: Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) ---------- void countPulse() { pulseCount++; // Increment on every rising edge } // ---------- Step 5: Object instantiation (LCD: rs, en, d4-d7) ---------- LiquidCrystal lcd(7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12); void setup() { // --- Step 5a: Initialize serial communication --- Serial.begin(9600); // 9600 bits per second // --- Step 5b: Configure pin directions --- pinMode(LED_PIN, OUTPUT); pinMode(SENSOR_PIN, INPUT_PULLUP); // Internal pull-up enabled // --- Step 5c: Attach hardware interrupt --- attachInterrupt(digitalPinToInterrupt(SENSOR_PIN), countPulse, RISING); // --- Step 5d: Initialize LCD --- lcd.begin(16, 2); // 16 columns, 2 rows lcd.print("RPM = "); } void loop() { // --- Step 6: Wait for a 1-second measurement window --- delay(1000); // --- Step 7: Atomically read the pulse count --- noInterrupts(); // Disable ISR unsigned int count = pulseCount; // Copy to local var pulseCount = 0; // Reset for next window interrupts(); // Re-enable ISR // --- Step 8: Convert pulses/sec to RPM --- float rpm = (count * 60.0) / PULSES_PER_REV; // --- Step 9: Output to Serial Monitor --- Serial.print("Pulses/sec = "); Serial.print(count); Serial.print(" RPM = "); Serial.println(rpm); // --- Step 10: Output to LCD --- lcd.setCursor(6, 0); lcd.print(rpm, 0); lcd.print(" "); // Pad to clear stale digits // --- Step 11: Visual heartbeat indicator --- digitalWrite(LED_PIN, !digitalRead(LED_PIN)); // Toggle LED } ``` ## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough 2: The Mathematical Derivation of RPM from Pulses The relationship between pulse count and rotational speed is a direct application of the **definition of frequency**. We derive it step by step. **Given:** - $N$ = number of pulses counted in time window $\Delta t$ - $P$ = number of pulses (slots) per mechanical revolution - $n$ = rotational speed in revolutions per **minute** (RPM) — the desired quantity **Step 1: Compute pulse frequency.** The pulse frequency $f$ (in pulses per second, or Hz) is the number of pulses divided by the time window: $$f = \frac{N}{\Delta t}$$ **Step 2: Convert frequency to revolutions per second.** Since the disc produces $P$ pulses per revolution, the number of revolutions in time $\Delta t$ is $N \div P$, so the revolutions per second is: $$f_{rev} = \frac{N}{P \cdot \Delta t}$$ **Step 3: Convert revolutions per second to revolutions per minute.** Multiplying by **60** converts seconds to minutes: $$n = f_{rev} \times 60 = \frac{60 \cdot N}{P \cdot \Delta t}$$ **Step 4: Substitute the sketch values.** In the program above, $\Delta t = 1$ s and $P = 1$: $$n = \frac{60 \cdot N}{1 \cdot 1} = 60 \cdot N \quad [\text{RPM}]$$ This is precisely the line `float rpm = (count * 60.0) / PULSES_PER_REV;` in the loop. ## 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough 3: ADC Conversion for Voltage Measurement When an Arduino is used to measure the **output voltage** of an AC machine or rectifier, the analog-to-digital converter (ADC) is the key peripheral. **Step 1: ADC quantization.** A **10-bit** ADC produces integer values from **0 to 1023** ($2^{10} - 1 = 1023$). This range represents the analog input voltage span from **0 V to $V_{ref}$** (default **5.0 V**). **Step 2: Establish the linear relationship.** Because the ADC is linear, each step corresponds to a fixed voltage quantum: $$V_{step} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{10}} = \frac{5.0}{1024} = 4.8828125 \text{ mV per LSB}$$ **Step 3: Invert the mapping to recover voltage.** If the ADC returns the integer $D$, the reconstructed voltage is: $$V_{in} = D \cdot \frac{V_{ref}}{1024} = D \cdot 0.0048828125 \quad [\text{Volts}]$$ **Step 4: Account for a potential voltage divider.** If the AC machine output is scaled down by a resistor divider with ratio $k = R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$ before reaching the Arduino pin, the **actual machine voltage** is: $$V_{actual} = \frac{V_{in}}{k} = D \cdot \frac{V_{ref}}{1024 \cdot k}$$ **Step 5: Implement in the sketch.** ```cpp int raw = analogRead(A0); // Returns 0–1023 float V_at_pin = raw * (5.0 / 1024.0); // Voltage at ADC pin float V_actual = V_at_pin / k; // Actual machine voltage Serial.print("V_actual = "); Serial.println(V_actual, 3); ``` ## 3.4 Analytical Walkthrough 4: PWM Duty-Cycle to Analog Output Mapping The Arduino's `analogWrite()` produces a **Pulse Width Modulated** square wave, not a true analog voltage. The duty cycle $D_{pwm}$ is mapped from an 8-bit integer value (0–255). **Step 1: Map input integer to fraction.** For a given integer $x$ between 0 and 255: $$D_{pwm} = \frac{x}{255}$$ **Step 2: Compute average DC voltage (after low-pass filtering).** If the PWM is smoothed by an RC filter, the average DC voltage is: $$V_{avg} = D_{pwm} \cdot V_{supply} = \frac{x \cdot V_{supply}}{255}$$ **Step 3: Example calculation.** If $V_{supply} = 5$ V and $x = 128$ (half-scale): $$V_{avg} = \frac{128 \times 5}{255} = 2.5098 \text{ V}$$ **Step 4: For sinusoidal PWM (SPWM) in inverter control.** The duty cycle is updated in real time to follow a sinusoid: $$x(t) = 127.5 \cdot \left[ 1 + m_a \cdot \sin(2\pi f_{ref} t) \right]$$ where $m_a$ is the modulation index (0 ≤ $m_a$ ≤ 1) and $f_{ref}$ is the desired output frequency. This is the foundation of **V/f control** for three-phase induction motors. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1: Arduino IDE Graphical User Interface — Annotated Block Map ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph IDE_Window["ARDUINO IDE APPLICATION WINDOW"] A["MENU BAR
File Edit Sketch Tools Help"] B["TOOLBAR ROW 1
Verify Upload New Open Save
Serial Monitor"] C["SKETCH NAME TAB
experiment16.ino"] D["CODE EDITOR PANE
Line-numbered C++ source
with syntax highlighting"] E["MESSAGE AREA
Compilation status
Error and warning text"] F["TEXT CONSOLE
Verbose compiler output
avrdude upload log"] G["STATUS BAR
Board name and COM port"] end A --> D B --> D C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The top region contains navigation and commands. The center is the editable source code. The bottom region reports what the compiler and uploader did. The status bar confirms the currently selected board and serial port — a critical step before clicking *Upload*. ## 4.2 Diagram 2: Five-Stage IDE Workflow — From Source Code to Motor Control ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["STAGE 1
SKETCH AUTHORING
User writes .ino file
in C/C++ dialect"] S2["STAGE 2
PRE-PROCESSING
Auto-prototype generation
Arduino.h included
All .ino files merged"] S3["STAGE 3
COMPILATION
avr-gcc / arm-gcc
Produces .hex file
Stored in temp/build folder"] S4["STAGE 4
UPLOAD
avrdude sends .hex
via USB-UART bridge
Bootloader writes to flash"] S5["STAGE 5
EXECUTION
Microcontroller runs
loop function forever
Serial Monitor active"] S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 S5 -.feedback.-> D1["DEBUG
Serial.print
Serial Plotter
LED blink check"] D1 --> S1 ``` ## 4.3 Diagram 3: Anatomy of an Arduino Sketch — Mandatory Structural Elements ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Sketch["ARDUINO SKETCH .ino FILE STRUCTURE"] H1["1. COMMENT HEADER
Program description
Author, date, board"] H2["2. INCLUDE DIRECTIVES
#include library.h"] H3["3. GLOBAL CONSTANTS
const int PIN = 13"] H4["4. GLOBAL VARIABLES
int counter = 0"] H5["5. FUNCTION PROTOTYPES
void myFunction"] H6["6. setup FUNCTION
Runs ONCE
pinMode, Serial.begin"] H7["7. loop FUNCTION
Runs FOREVER
Core application logic"] H8["8. USER-DEFINED FUNCTIONS
Reusable code blocks"] end H1 --> H2 --> H3 --> H4 --> H5 --> H6 --> H7 --> H8 H8 -.called by.-> H7 ``` ## 4.4 Diagram 4: AC Machines Lab Interfacing Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PowerStage["POWER STAGE"] AC["3-Phase AC
Mains Supply"] M["AC MACHINE
Induction or
Synchronous Motor"] DRV["Driver / Inverter
IGBT / MOSFET
SPWM signals"] end subgraph SenseStage["SENSE STAGE"] VS["Voltage Sensor
Potential Divider"] CS["Current Sensor
CT or Hall Effect"] SS["Speed Sensor
Optocoupler / Encoder"] end subgraph ControlStage["CONTROL STAGE"] ARD["ARDUINO UNO
Programmed via IDE
Runs loop function"] LCD["16x2 LCD Display
LiquidCrystal library"] PC["PC with Serial
Monitor / Plotter"] end AC --> M DRV --> M M --> SS AC --> VS AC --> CS VS --> ARD CS --> ARD SS --> ARD ARD --> LCD ARD --> DRV ARD --> PC ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the architecture:** The Arduino sits in the **control loop center**. It (a) senses machine parameters through the sensor stage, (b) processes them in the `loop()` function, (c) drives the LCD/PC for display, and (d) outputs PWM/SPWM signals to the inverter for closed-loop speed or torque control. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCEEL507]** *Define the Arduino IDE. List any four of its key features.* **Model Answer:** The **Arduino IDE (Integrated Development Environment)** is a cross-platform, open-source Java-based application used to write, compile, and upload program code (called "sketches") to Arduino microcontroller boards. **Four key features:** 1. **Cross-platform** — runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux. 2. **Built-in libraries** — pre-bundled C/C++ libraries for common peripherals (LCD, Servo, Wire, SPI, etc.). 3. **One-click verify and upload** — automatic compilation and programming without manual command-line steps. 4. **Integrated Serial Monitor** — real-time two-way text communication with the running sketch for debugging. 5. **Open-source under GPL** — free to use, modify, and distribute. 6. **Extensible** — supports third-party board packages and libraries via the Boards Manager and Library Manager. *(Any four well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)* --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCEEL507]** *What are the two mandatory functions in every Arduino sketch? Explain the purpose of each.* **Model Answer:** Every Arduino program must contain exactly **two mandatory functions** that the IDE automatically recognizes: 1. **`void setup()`** - This function is executed **exactly once** when the board is powered on or reset. - It is used for **initialization tasks** such as: - Setting pin modes using `pinMode()`. - Starting serial communication using `Serial.begin()`. - Initializing libraries (e.g., `lcd.begin()`). - Attaching hardware interrupts. - It returns no value (hence the `void` return type) and takes no parameters. 2. **`void loop()`** - This function is executed **repeatedly in an infinite loop** immediately after `setup()` completes. - It contains the **main application logic** that must run continuously, such as reading sensors, computing control signals, and updating actuators. - The IDE internally generates a `main()` function that calls `setup()` once and then repeatedly calls `loop()`. --- ## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Question with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) — *Selected* **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCEEL507, Dec 2024 pattern]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **complete workflow** of the Arduino IDE, from writing a sketch to executing it on the board. Include all five stages in your answer with diagrams. *(7 marks)* **(b)** With a neat labeled diagram, describe the **graphical user interface** of the Arduino IDE, identifying the purpose of each major region of the window. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution for (a) — Workflow of the Arduino IDE The Arduino IDE workflow can be divided into **five sequential stages**: **Stage 1 — Sketch Authoring (Source Code Creation):** The user opens the IDE and writes the program in the editor pane. The file is saved with a `.ino` extension inside a folder bearing the same name as the file (e.g., `exp16/exp16.ino`). The editor provides syntax highlighting, auto-indentation, and brace matching. **Stage 2 — Pre-processing:** When the user clicks the *Verify* or *Upload* button, the IDE's pre-processor performs several hidden transformations: - Implicitly includes the master header file `Arduino.h`. - Concatenates all `.ino` files of the sketch folder into a single translation unit (in alphabetical order). - Auto-generates function prototypes, so the order of function definitions does not matter. - Defines built-in macros such as `HIGH`, `LOW`, `INPUT`, `OUTPUT`, `LED_BUILTIN`, `true`, and `false`. **Stage 3 — Compilation:** The pre-processed source is passed to a compiler: - `avr-gcc` for 8-bit AVR boards (Uno, Nano, Mega). - `arm-none-eabi-gcc` for 32-bit ARM boards (Due, Zero, Portenta). The compiler produces an object file, which is linked with the Arduino core library to produce a `.hex` file. The compiler output is displayed in the black text console at the bottom of the IDE. **Stage 4 — Upload (In-System Programming):** The IDE invokes the programmer utility `avrdude` (for AVR boards). The `.hex` file is sent over the USB cable through the on-board **USB-to-UART bridge** (e.g., CH340, ATmega16U2) to the microcontroller's **optiboot bootloader**. The bootloader writes the program into the main flash memory and triggers a reset. **Stage 5 — Execution and Debugging:** After reset, the microcontroller begins executing the new code starting at the reset vector. The `setup()` function runs once, and then `loop()` runs continuously. The Serial Monitor (9600 baud by default) enables the user to send/receive text for real-time debugging. #### Model Solution for (b) — GUI of the Arduino IDE The Arduino IDE window is organized into **seven distinct regions**. (See the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for a visual layout.) | Region | Purpose | |---|---| | **Menu Bar** | File operations (New, Open, Save, Examples), Edit (cut/copy/paste/find), Sketch (Verify, Upload, Include Library), Tools (Board, Port, Programmer selection), Help. | | **Toolbar** | Quick-action icons: Verify (✓), Upload (→), New, Open, Save, and Serial Monitor toggle. | | **Sketch Tab** | Shows the name of the currently open `.ino` file. Multiple tabs appear when the sketch contains several files. | | **Code Editor** | The large central pane where the C++ source code is written and edited. Features line numbering, syntax highlighting, and auto-indentation. | | **Message Area** | The narrow white area below the editor that shows a one-line summary of the last action (e.g., "Done compiling", or an error message). | | **Text Console** | The black terminal area that displays verbose compiler output, including file paths, line numbers of errors, and the avrdude upload log. | | **Status Bar** | The bottom-most bar showing the currently selected board (e.g., "Arduino Uno") and the serial port (e.g., "COM3" or "/dev/ttyUSB0"). Must be correct before uploading. | --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCEEL507, Dec 2024 pattern]** **(a)** Explain the **structure of an Arduino sketch** with a complete, well-commented example program. Identify the role of `setup()` and `loop()`. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Describe the use of the Arduino IDE for the following AC Machines Lab applications: *(i)* measurement of rotational speed of an induction motor, *(ii)* generation of SPWM signals for a single-phase inverter. Write the relevant code snippets. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution for (a) — Sketch Structure A complete Arduino sketch contains the following structural elements in the recommended order: ```cpp /* Program Header Experiment 16 - Familiarization with Arduino IDE Author: KTU B.Tech Student Date : November 2024 Board : Arduino Uno */ // Step 1: Include required libraries #include // Step 2: Define constants (use const for read-only) const int ledPin = 13; // Step 3: Declare global variables int counter = 0; // Step 4: Function prototypes (optional - IDE auto-generates) void blinkLED(int pin, int duration); void setup() { // Runs ONCE pinMode(ledPin, OUTPUT); // Configure pin direction Serial.begin(9600); // Initialize serial port Serial.println("System Initialized"); } void loop() { // Runs FOREVER counter++; blinkLED(ledPin, 500); // Custom function call Serial.print("Loop iteration: "); Serial.println(counter); delay(500); } void blinkLED(int pin, int duration) { digitalWrite(pin, HIGH); delay(duration); digitalWrite(pin, LOW); delay(duration); } ``` **Role of `setup()`:** Performs **one-time initialization** — configures pin modes (`pinMode`), starts communication (`Serial.begin`), initializes peripherals, attaches interrupts. Runs a single time at power-up or after reset. **Role of `loop()`:** Contains the **core repeating logic** of the program. The IDE wraps this inside a hidden `main()` function which calls `setup()` once, then repeatedly calls `loop()` in an infinite `while(1)` loop. #### Model Solution for (b) — AC Machines Lab Applications **(i) Speed Measurement of an Induction Motor using an Optocoupler:** An optocoupler (slot-type) with a slotted disc mounted on the motor shaft produces one or more pulses per revolution. These pulses are fed to Arduino pin 2 (interrupt-capable). ```cpp volatile unsigned int pulseCount = 0; void countPulse() { // ISR pulseCount++; } void setup() { pinMode(2, INPUT_PULLUP); attachInterrupt(digitalPinToInterrupt(2), countPulse, RISING); Serial.begin(9600); } void loop() { delay(1000); // 1-second window noInterrupts(); unsigned int n = pulseCount; pulseCount = 0; interrupts(); float rpm = (n * 60.0) / 1.0; // 1 pulse per rev Serial.print("RPM = "); Serial.println(rpm); } ``` **(ii) SPWM Generation for a Single-Phase Inverter:** Sinusoidal PWM is generated by varying the PWM duty cycle in real time according to a sine lookup table. The Arduino's Timer1 (16-bit) is reprogrammed for high-frequency PWM, but a simple demonstration uses `analogWrite()` with a software sine table. ```cpp const int sineTable[32] = { 128,153,177,199,218,234,245,253,255,253,245,234,218,199,177,153, 128,103, 79, 57, 38, 22, 11, 3, 1, 3, 11, 22, 38, 57, 79,103 }; int i = 0; void setup() { pinMode(9, OUTPUT); // PWM pin pinMode(10, OUTPUT); // Complementary PWM pin } void loop() { analogWrite(9, sineTable[i]); // Upper switch analogWrite(10, 255 - sineTable[i]); // Lower switch (complementary) i = (i + 1) % 32; delayMicroseconds(195); // Adjust for 50/60 Hz fundamental } ``` The output is filtered to approximate a 50 Hz sinusoid that drives the gate of the IGBT/MOSFET in the inverter, controlling the AC motor. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing the IDE with the Arduino board itself.** The IDE is *software*; the Uno is *hardware*. Examiners *will* deduct marks for this swap. > 2. **Forgetting to mention the file extension `.ino`** and the same-name folder rule. > 3. **Writing `main()` explicitly** in sketches — the IDE *automatically* generates `main()`; you must only write `setup()` and `loop()`. > 4. **Omitting the return type** `void` in the function definitions. > 5. **Not specifying the board and COM port** selection in the Tools menu before upload. > 6. **Mixing up `pinMode` modes** — using `INPUT` instead of `INPUT_PULLUP` for a floating switch, causing erratic behavior. > 7. **Skipping the `Serial.begin(9600);`** call when using `Serial.print()` — produces no output on the Serial Monitor. > 8. **Writing a non-formatted answer** — KTU examiners reward neat, labeled diagrams and proper sub-sectioning. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:** - ☑ **Arduino IDE** = **I**ntegrated **D**evelopment **E**nvironment — a *software* application, not a board. - ☑ Sketch files use the **`.ino`** extension and must reside in a folder of the **same name**. - ☑ The IDE is **cross-platform** (Windows / macOS / Linux), **Java-based**, and **GPL-licensed**. - ☑ Every sketch has **two mandatory functions**: `void setup()` (runs once) and `void loop()` (runs forever). - ☑ Built-in macros: `HIGH`, `LOW`, `INPUT`, `OUTPUT`, `INPUT_PULLUP`, `LED_BUILTIN` (pin 13 on Uno). - ☑ ADC is **10-bit** (0–1023), reference voltage default **5.0 V** → $V = D \cdot \frac{5.0}{1024}$. - ☑ PWM via `analogWrite()` is **8-bit** (0–255), default ~**490 Hz** on most pins. - ☑ Serial Monitor default baud = **9600 bps**; always match with `Serial.begin(9600)`. - ☑ IDE workflow stages: **Author → Pre-process → Compile → Upload → Execute**. - ☑ `avr-gcc` compiles for AVR boards; `avrdude` uploads via the bootloader. - ☑ Use `volatile` for any global variable modified inside an ISR. - ☑ Always select the **correct board and COM port** in the Tools menu *before* clicking Upload. - ☑ The `attachInterrupt()` function enables hardware-driven event counting — essential for accurate RPM measurement. - ☑ In AC Machines Lab, the Arduino acts as the **central controller**: sensing (ADC, interrupts), processing (loop logic), and actuating (PWM, LCD, Serial). - ☑ **Common syntax to remember**: `pinMode(pin, mode)`, `digitalWrite(pin, value)`, `digitalRead(pin)`, `analogRead(pin)`, `analogWrite(pin, value)`, `delay(ms)`, `delayMicroseconds(us)`, `millis()`, `Serial.begin(baud)`, `Serial.print(val)`, `Serial.println(val)`. ---

topics.title: LED blinking with different ON/OFF delay timing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEEL507 - AC MACHINES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 16 > - **Topic:** LED blinking with different ON/OFF delay timing # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **LED Blinking with Different ON/OFF Delay Timing** is a fundamental embedded systems laboratory exercise in which a **Light Emitting Diode (LED)** is interfaced to a **General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO)** pin of a microcontroller (typically an **Arduino UNO** based on the **ATmega328P** MCU, or an 8051/ARM-based development board), and the LED is alternately switched **ON** and **OFF** for **programmable time intervals** (commonly referred to as *delay* or *hold time*). The experiment is designed to demonstrate the concepts of: - Digital I/O pin configuration - Bit-level manipulation of port registers - Software-based time delay generation using polling loops, hardware timers, or built-in `delay()` functions - Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) fundamentals (when duty cycle variation is introduced) - Real-time task scheduling in a bare-metal embedded environment According to the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, this experiment maps to **Course Outcome CO3: "Implement embedded C programs for basic I/O interfacing applications"** and is evaluated under the **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive level: APPLY (Level 3)**. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 scheme, this experiment is typically conducted using the **Arduino IDE** with an **ATmega328P** microcontroller, although equivalent platforms like the **ESP32**, **Raspberry Pi Pico**, or **8051 (Keil µVision)** are accepted as per laboratory manual guidelines. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine a **lighthouse beam** rotating across the ocean. To an observer at a fixed point, the light appears to **flash ON** (when the beam sweeps across them) and then **go OFF** (when it sweeps past). The interval between two successive flashes depends on the **rotation speed of the lighthouse**. Now, replace: - The **lighthouse** with a **microcontroller** (the "brain" that decides when to act) - The **beam of light** with an **LED** (the physical output device) - The **rotation speed** with a **software-controlled delay** (the timing parameter) The microcontroller sends a **HIGH (5V/3.3V)** signal to the LED pin to turn it **ON** (light is visible) and a **LOW (0V/0V)** signal to turn it **OFF** (light is extinguished). By changing the duration of these HIGH and LOW states, we create **different blink patterns** such as: - **Slow blink** → 1000 ms ON / 1000 ms OFF (human eye perceives distinct flashes) - **Fast blink** → 100 ms ON / 100 ms OFF (LED appears dimmer due to persistence of vision) - **SOS pattern** → ... --- ... (Morse code-style signaling) - **Heartbeat** → 100 ms ON, 900 ms OFF (short burst followed by long pause) ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following constants and metrics govern the timing behavior of the LED blink experiment: | Parameter | Standard Value | Unit | Significance | |-----------|---------------|------|--------------| | **Arduino Logic HIGH** | **5.0 V** | Volts (V) | Voltage at GPIO pin when set to HIGH | | **Arduino Logic LOW** | **0.0 V** | Volts (V) | Voltage at GPIO pin when set to LOW | | **ATmega328P Clock Frequency** | **16.000 MHz** | Megahertz (MHz) | Crystal oscillator frequency | | **Instruction Cycle Period** | **62.5 ns** | Nanoseconds (ns) | 1 / 16 MHz | | **Standard LED Forward Voltage (Red)** | **1.8 – 2.2 V** | Volts (V) | Voltage drop across LED when conducting | | **Standard LED Forward Current** | **10 – 20 mA** | Milliamperes (mA) | Current required for normal brightness | | **Human Eye Flicker Fusion Threshold** | **~24 Hz** | Hertz (Hz) | Frequency above which blinking appears as steady light | | **Arduino `delay()` Resolution** | **1 ms** | Millisecond (ms) | Granularity of built-in delay function | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Engineering Principle:** The human eye can only perceive discrete flashing up to approximately **24 Hz**. Beyond this **flicker fusion frequency**, the LED appears to be **continuously dimly lit**. This is the same principle behind cinema (24 fps) and PWM-based LED dimming. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Underlying Operating Principle The LED blinking experiment is a closed-loop control task where the microcontroller acts as the controller and the LED as the actuator. The theoretical foundation rests on three pillars: ### 2.1.1 Digital I/O Pin Configuration Each GPIO pin on the ATmega328P can be configured in three states: 1. **INPUT** – Reads external voltage levels (e.g., button press) 2. **OUTPUT** – Drives HIGH (5V) or LOW (0V) levels 3. **INPUT_PULLUP** – INPUT with internal 20kΩ pull-up resistor enabled For LED blinking, the pin must be configured as **OUTPUT** using the `pinMode()` function. Internally, this modifies the **Data Direction Register (DDRx)**. For digital pin 13 on Arduino UNO, this corresponds to **DDRB = DDRB | B00100000** (bit 5 set to 1). ### 2.1.2 Voltage Divider & Current Limiting The microcontroller pin can source/sink only **~40 mA** per pin (absolute maximum) with a recommended **20 mA** continuous limit. Since the LED requires only **10–20 mA** and drops **~2V**, a **current-limiting resistor** is mandatory to prevent LED burnout. The resistor value is calculated using **Ohm's Law** in a series loop: $$R = \frac{V_{supply} - V_{LED}}{I_{LED}}$$ Where: - $V_{supply}$ = **5.0 V** (Arduino output voltage) - $V_{LED}$ = **2.0 V** (typical red LED forward voltage) - $I_{LED}$ = **15 mA** (desired LED current) The complete derivation is provided in **Section 3**. ### 2.1.3 Time Delay Generation Strategies There are **three primary methods** to generate a time delay in embedded systems: | Method | Mechanism | Accuracy | CPU Usage | Best Use Case | |--------|-----------|----------|-----------|---------------| | **Software Polling Loop** | Executes NOPs/instructions in a counted loop | Low (compiler-dependent) | 100% | Simple delays, demos | | **Arduino `delay()`** | Uses hardware Timer0 in CTC mode | Medium (±1%) | Idle (interrupt-driven on AVR) | Quick prototyping | | **Hardware Timer/Counter** | Dedicated 16-bit Timer1 with prescaler | High (±0.01%) | Minimal (interrupt-based) | RTOS, precise timing | The Arduino `delay()` function internally uses **Timer0** configured with a **prescaler of 64**. For a 16 MHz clock, each timer tick is: $$T_{tick} = \frac{64}{16 \times 10^6} = 4 \, \mu s$$ Timer0 overflows every **256 ticks**, giving an interrupt period of **1.024 ms**, which is rounded to **1 ms** by the Arduino core library for `millis()` and `delay()` functions. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Description | Variables | Units | |---|--------------------|-------------|-----------|-------| | 1 | $R = \frac{V_{supply} - V_{LED}}{I_{LED}}$ | Current-limiting resistor value (Ohm's Law) | $R$ = resistance, $V_{supply}$ = source voltage, $V_{LED}$ = LED forward voltage, $I_{LED}$ = LED current | $R$ in Ω, $V$ in V, $I$ in A | | 2 | $P_{R} = I_{LED}^2 \times R$ | Power dissipated in current-limiting resistor | $P_R$ = resistor power, $I_{LED}$ = LED current, $R$ = resistance | $P$ in W, $I$ in A, $R$ in Ω | | 3 | $P_{LED} = V_{LED} \times I_{LED}$ | Power consumed by the LED | $P_{LED}$ = LED power, $V_{LED}$ = LED forward voltage, $I_{LED}$ = LED current | $P$ in W, $V$ in V, $I$ in A | | 4 | $T_{total} = T_{ON} + T_{OFF}$ | Total period of one blink cycle | $T_{ON}$ = ON duration, $T_{OFF}$ = OFF duration | $T$ in ms | | 5 | $f = \frac{1}{T_{total}}$ | Blink frequency | $f$ = frequency, $T_{total}$ = period | $f$ in Hz, $T$ in s | | 6 | $D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} \times 100\%$ | Duty cycle (percentage of time LED is ON) | $D$ = duty cycle, $T_{ON}$, $T_{OFF}$ | $D$ dimensionless (%) | | 7 | $T_{tick} = \frac{Prescaler}{f_{clock}}$ | Timer tick period (hardware timer) | $T_{tick}$ = tick duration, $Prescaler$ = clock divider, $f_{clock}$ = oscillator frequency | $T$ in s, $f$ in Hz | | 8 | $N = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{tick} \times N_{instr}}$ | Number of loop iterations for software delay | $N$ = loop count, $T_{delay}$ = desired delay, $N_{instr}$ = instructions per loop | $N$ dimensionless | | 9 | $N_{overflows} = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{tick} \times 256}$ | Number of Timer0 overflows for `delay()` function | $N_{overflows}$ = overflow count | $N$ dimensionless | | 10 | $I_{total} = N_{LEDs} \times I_{LED}$ | Total current drawn when N LEDs are ON simultaneously | $I_{total}$ = total current, $N_{LEDs}$ = number of LEDs | $I$ in mA | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Condition for Duty Cycle:** $0\% \leq D \leq 100\%$. A duty cycle of **0%** means the LED is permanently OFF, **100%** means permanently ON (steady glow), and **50%** gives the classic symmetric blink. For PWM-based dimming, the human eye perceives brightness roughly proportional to the duty cycle due to the **Talbot-Plateau law**. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The seemingly simple LED blink experiment is the **"Hello World" of embedded systems** and forms the foundation for: - **Automotive turn signal indicators** (variable blink rate for hazard vs. normal mode) - **Aircraft wingtip strobe lights** (precise ON/OFF timing for visibility) - **Emergency vehicle light bars** (pattern-based signaling: solid, flashing, rotating) - **IoT status indicators** (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and power status LEDs on consumer electronics) - **Tower aircraft warning lights** (synchronized blinking for tall structures) - **Heartbeat indicators in medical devices** (e.g., ventilator, pacemaker status) - **Industrial PLC indicator panels** (machine ON/OFF/FAULT status) The same timing principles used here scale up to **Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS)** like FreeRTOS, where multiple "tasks" (LEDs) are scheduled with different priorities and periods using **preemptive scheduling algorithms**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Current-Limiting Resistor Value **Given:** - Arduino UNO output voltage: $V_{supply} = 5.0 \, \text{V}$ - Red LED forward voltage: $V_{LED} = 2.0 \, \text{V}$ - Desired LED current: $I_{LED} = 15 \, \text{mA} = 0.015 \, \text{A}$ **Find:** Current-limiting resistor $R$ **Step 1:** Apply **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the series loop (Arduino pin → Resistor → LED → GND): $$V_{supply} - V_R - V_{LED} = 0$$ This equation states that the sum of voltage drops across the resistor and the LED must equal the source voltage. **Step 2:** Solve for the voltage drop across the resistor $V_R$: $$V_R = V_{supply} - V_{LED}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values: $$V_R = 5.0 \, \text{V} - 2.0 \, \text{V} = 3.0 \, \text{V}$$ This is the voltage that the resistor must "absorb" to limit current flow. **Step 4:** Apply **Ohm's Law** ($V = IR$, therefore $R = V/I$): $$R = \frac{V_R}{I_{LED}}$$ **Step 5:** Substitute numerical values: $$R = \frac{3.0 \, \text{V}}{0.015 \, \text{A}} = 200 \, \Omega$$ **Step 6:** Select the nearest standard **E12 resistor value**. Since 200 Ω is a standard E24 value, we use it directly. If 220 Ω were the next standard, the actual current would be: $$I_{actual} = \frac{3.0 \, \text{V}}{220 \, \Omega} \approx 13.6 \, \text{mA}$$ which is still well within the safe operating range (10–20 mA) for a standard LED. **Step 7:** Verify the **power dissipation** in the resistor using $P = I^2 R$: $$P_R = (0.015)^2 \times 200 = 0.045 \, \text{W} = 45 \, \text{mW}$$ A standard **¼-watt (250 mW) resistor** is more than adequate, providing a **safety margin of 5.5×**. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Design Note:** In practice, KTU lab manuals often specify a **220 Ω** or **330 Ω** resistor. The **330 Ω** value is more conservative, reducing LED current to ~9 mA, which extends LED lifespan and reduces power consumption at the cost of slightly lower brightness. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Blink Frequency from ON/OFF Delays **Given:** A program with $T_{ON} = 500 \, \text{ms}$ and $T_{OFF} = 1500 \, \text{ms}$ **Find:** Blink frequency $f$ and duty cycle $D$ **Step 1:** Calculate the **total period** of one complete blink cycle (one ON + one OFF): $$T_{total} = T_{ON} + T_{OFF}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute values: $$T_{total} = 500 \, \text{ms} + 1500 \, \text{ms} = 2000 \, \text{ms} = 2.0 \, \text{s}$$ **Step 3:** Apply the frequency-period relationship $f = 1/T$: $$f = \frac{1}{T_{total}}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute values (ensuring units are in **seconds**): $$f = \frac{1}{2.0 \, \text{s}} = 0.5 \, \text{Hz}$$ This means the LED completes one full blink cycle every 2 seconds, blinking 0.5 times per second. **Step 5:** Calculate the **duty cycle** $D$: $$D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} \times 100\%$$ **Step 6:** Substitute values: $$D = \frac{500}{500 + 1500} \times 100\% = \frac{500}{2000} \times 100\% = 25\%$$ The LED is ON for 25% of the time and OFF for 75% of the time. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Software Delay Loop Iteration Count (8051 Architecture) For 8051-based experiments (alternative to Arduino), the delay is generated using a nested loop executing NOP-equivalent instructions. **Given:** 8051 with 11.0592 MHz crystal, desired delay $T_{delay} = 100 \, \text{ms}$ **Step 1:** Calculate the **machine cycle period**. The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency by 12: $$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \approx 1.085 \, \mu s$$ **Step 2:** Each instruction takes 1 to 4 machine cycles. A `DJNZ` (Decrement and Jump if Not Zero) instruction takes **2 machine cycles** = 2.17 µs. **Step 3:** For a single loop with counter $N$, total time is: $$T = N \times 2 \times T_{MC}$$ **Step 4:** Solve for $N$: $$N = \frac{T_{delay}}{2 \times T_{MC}} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 46{,}082$$ **Step 5:** Since $N$ must be an integer fitting in 8 bits (max 255), we use a **nested loop** with two counters $M_1$ (outer) and $M_2$ (inner): $$T = M_1 \times M_2 \times 2 \times T_{MC}$$ **Step 6:** Choose $M_2 = 230$ (standard inner loop value) and solve for $M_1$: $$M_1 = \frac{100 \times 10^{-3}}{230 \times 2 \times 1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 200$$ **Step 7:** The final assembly delay subroutine is: ```assembly DELAY_100MS: MOV R1, #200 ; Outer loop counter (1 machine cycle) OUTER: MOV R0, #230 ; Inner loop counter (1 machine cycle) INNER: DJNZ R0, INNER ; Decrement R0, jump if not zero (2 machine cycles) DJNZ R1, OUTER ; Decrement R1, jump if not zero (2 machine cycles) RET ; Return from subroutine (2 machine cycles) ``` **Verification:** $T = 200 \times 230 \times 2 \times 1.085 \, \mu s = 99{,}820 \, \mu s \approx 99.82 \, \text{ms} \approx 100 \, \text{ms}$ ✓ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Delay Variation The following matrix analyzes how different ON/OFF delay combinations affect observable LED behavior: | $T_{ON}$ (ms) | $T_{OFF}$ (ms) | $T_{total}$ (ms) | $f$ (Hz) | Duty Cycle $D$ | Human Perception | |---------------|----------------|------------------|----------|----------------|------------------| | 10 | 10 | 20 | 50.0 | 50% | Appears continuously ON (above flicker fusion) | | 50 | 50 | 100 | 10.0 | 50% | Rapid but visible flashing | | 100 | 100 | 200 | 5.0 | 50% | Clear, moderate-speed blink | | 250 | 250 | 500 | 2.0 | 50% | Slow, distinct blink | | 500 | 1500 | 2000 | 0.5 | 25% | Short flash, long pause (heartbeat-like) | | 1000 | 1000 | 2000 | 0.5 | 50% | Classic 1-second blink | | 100 | 200 | 300 | 3.33 | 33.3% | Fast asymmetric blink | | 2000 | 2000 | 4000 | 0.25 | 50% | Very slow blink (emergency indicator style) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Threshold:** When $T_{total} < 1/24 \, \text{Hz}^{-1} = 41.67 \, \text{ms}$, the LED appears **continuously lit but dim** to the human eye. This is the fundamental principle behind **PWM dimming**. ## 3.5 Derivation 4: Effective Brightness via PWM Duty Cycle The perceived brightness of an LED driven by a PWM signal is approximately proportional to the duty cycle (Talbot-Plateau law). **Given:** PWM frequency $f_{PWM} = 500 \, \text{Hz}$, duty cycle $D = 30\%$ **Step 1:** Calculate period: $$T_{PWM} = \frac{1}{500} = 2 \, \text{ms}$$ **Step 2:** ON time: $$T_{ON} = D \times T_{PWM} = 0.30 \times 2 \, \text{ms} = 0.6 \, \text{ms}$$ **Step 3:** OFF time: $$T_{OFF} = T_{PWM} - T_{ON} = 2 - 0.6 = 1.4 \, \text{ms}$$ **Step 4:** Perceived brightness ratio: $$\text{Brightness}_{perceived} \approx D \times I_{peak} = 0.30 \times 15 \, \text{mA} = 4.5 \, \text{mA (equivalent)}$$ The LED will appear to be at approximately **30% of its full brightness** while only drawing 30% of the power, demonstrating the **energy efficiency of PWM dimming**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Circuit Connection Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Arduino UNO
ATmega328P MCU] -- "Digital Pin 13
5V HIGH / 0V LOW" --> B[Current-Limiting
Resistor 220 ohm] B -- "Voltage drop
3.0V" --> C[LED Anode +] C --> D[LED Cathode -] D --> E[Ground GND
0V Reference] A -.->|"pinMode OUTPUT
digitalWrite HIGH/LOW"| F[Control Register
DDRB Bit 5] F -.->|"Toggles every
delay ms"| A G[USB / External 5V
Power Supply] --> A H[16 MHz Crystal
Clock Source] --> A I[Timer0 Hardware
millis delay] --> A ``` **Component Connection Summary:** - **Arduino Pin 13** → **220 Ω Resistor** → **LED Anode (longer lead)** - **LED Cathode (shorter lead)** → **Arduino GND** - Arduino powered via **USB** (5V) or external **7–12V DC jack** (regulated to 5V internally) ## 4.2 Program Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Program Start]) --> Init[Setup: pinMode 13 OUTPUT] Init --> LoopStart{Loop Begins} LoopStart --> LedOn[Set Pin 13 HIGH
LED Turns ON] LedOn --> DelayOn[delay ON time ms
e.g., 1000 ms] DelayOn --> LedOff[Set Pin 13 LOW
LED Turns OFF] LedOff --> DelayOff[delay OFF time ms
e.g., 500 ms] DelayOff --> LoopStart LoopStart -.->|Infinite Loop| LoopStart LoopStart -.->|Power Removed| Stop([Program End]) ``` ## 4.3 LED Blinking State Machine Diagram ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> LED_OFF LED_OFF --> LED_ON : digitalWrite HIGH
+ delay TON LED_ON --> LED_OFF : digitalWrite LOW
+ delay TOFF LED_OFF --> LED_ON : Repeat cycle note right of LED_ON Current flows through LED
V_R drops 3.0V across R
I_LED approx 13.6 mA
LED emits photons end note note right of LED_OFF Pin at 0V
No current flow
LED does not emit
GPIO in LOW state end note ``` ## 4.4 Hardware Block Diagram of ATmega328P ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph MCU [ATmega328P Microcontroller] CPU[CPU Core
AVR RISC Architecture] ROM[32 KB Flash
Program Memory] RAM[2 KB SRAM
Data Memory] EEPROM[1 KB EEPROM
Non-volatile Data] GPIOA[Port B GPIO
Digital Pins 8 to 13] TIMER0[Timer0 8-bit
delay millis source] TIMER1[Timer1 16-bit
PWM generation] ADC[10-bit ADC
6 channels] end OSC[16 MHz Crystal
External Oscillator] --> CPU USB[USB to Serial
CH340 or ATmega16U2] --> CPU CPU <--> ROM CPU <--> RAM CPU <--> EEPROM CPU --> GPIOA CPU --> TIMER0 CPU --> TIMER1 CPU --> ADC GPIOA --> LED[External LED + Resistor] TIMER0 -.->|millis tick| DELAY[delay function] ``` ## 4.5 Multi-LED Sequential Blinking Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Arduino UNO
ATmega328P] -- "Pin 8" --> B[LED 1 Red] A -- "Pin 9" --> C[LED 2 Yellow] A -- "Pin 10" --> D[LED 3 Green] A -- "Pin 11" --> E[LED 4 Blue] B --> F[GND] C --> F D --> F E --> F A -->|Sequential
Control| G[Pattern 1
Chase Light] A -->|Synchronized| H[Pattern 2
All Blink] A -->|Alternating| I[Pattern 3
Odd Even] ``` **Connection Details for Multi-LED Setup:** - Each LED requires its own **220 Ω current-limiting resistor** in series - All LED cathodes connect to the **common GND rail** - Arduino can source a maximum of **~200 mA total** across all pins, limiting to approximately **10–13 LEDs** at full brightness (20 mA each) # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define the term "flicker fusion frequency" and state its approximate value for the human eye. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Model Answer:** The **flicker fusion frequency** (also called the **critical flicker frequency** or **CFF**) is defined as the frequency at which a flickering light source appears to be **continuously illuminated** (steady) to the human observer, due to the **persistence of vision** phenomenon. **Approximate value:** **24 Hz** (some sources cite 16–25 Hz depending on luminance, contrast, and viewing conditions). **Engineering significance:** This principle is exploited in: - **PWM LED dimming** (frequencies >100 Hz are used to ensure flicker-free dimming) - **Cinema projection** (24 fps creates the illusion of motion) - **Television displays** (50/60 Hz refresh rates) *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for value, 1 for application)* --- ### Question 2: What is the function of a current-limiting resistor in an LED circuit interfaced with a microcontroller? What happens if it is omitted? [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Model Answer:** **Function of current-limiting resistor:** The current-limiting resistor **restricts the current flowing through the LED** to a safe operating value (typically 10–20 mA). Since an LED has very low dynamic resistance once its forward voltage is exceeded, connecting it directly across a voltage source (like a microcontroller GPIO pin) would cause **excessive current flow**, leading to: 1. **Immediate LED burnout** (thermal runaway) 2. **Damage to the microcontroller pin** (exceeding the 40 mA absolute maximum rating) 3. **Possible permanent failure of both components** **If omitted:** The LED will draw a very large current limited only by the internal resistance of the source, causing the LED to flash brightly for a fraction of a second and then **burn out permanently** (open circuit). The microcontroller pin may also be damaged. **Recommended value calculation:** $$R = \frac{V_{supply} - V_{LED}}{I_{LED}} = \frac{5V - 2V}{15 \, mA} = 200 \, \Omega \approx 220 \, \Omega \, \text{(standard)}$$ *(3 marks: 1 for function, 1 for consequence of omission, 1 for calculation)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A: 14 Marks #### Part (a): Design and implement an LED blinking system with three different ON/OFF delay patterns selectable via a push button. [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Model Solution:** **Circuit Design:** - **Components:** Arduino UNO, 1× LED, 1× 220 Ω resistor, 1× push button, 1× 10 kΩ pull-down resistor, breadboard, connecting wires - **Connections:** - LED anode → 220 Ω resistor → Arduino digital **Pin 13** - LED cathode → **GND** - Push button → Arduino digital **Pin 2** (with 10 kΩ pull-down resistor to GND) **Algorithm (State Machine Approach):** 1. Initialize Pin 13 as **OUTPUT**, Pin 2 as **INPUT** 2. Read button state 3. On each button press, **increment pattern counter** (cycles 0 → 1 → 2 → 0) 4. Execute the corresponding blink pattern in a `while` loop until the button is pressed again **Complete Arduino Code:** ```c // LED Blinking with Selectable ON/OFF Delay Patterns // Experiment 16 - PCEEL507 AC Machines Lab const int ledPin = 13; // LED connected to digital pin 13 const int buttonPin = 2; // Push button on digital pin 2 int pattern = 0; // Current pattern selector int lastButtonState = HIGH; // Previous button reading unsigned long lastDebounceTime = 0; const unsigned long debounceDelay = 50; // Debounce time in ms void setup() { pinMode(ledPin, OUTPUT); pinMode(buttonPin, INPUT_PULLUP); // Use internal pull-up Serial.begin(9600); } void loop() { int reading = digitalRead(buttonPin); // Debounce logic if (reading == LOW && lastButtonState == HIGH) { if (millis() - lastDebounceTime > debounceDelay) { pattern = (pattern + 1) % 3; // Cycle through 0, 1, 2 lastDebounceTime = millis(); Serial.print("Pattern: "); Serial.println(pattern); } } lastButtonState = reading; // Execute selected pattern switch(pattern) { case 0: // Slow blink: 1s ON, 1s OFF digitalWrite(ledPin, HIGH); delay(1000); digitalWrite(ledPin, LOW); delay(1000); break; case 1: // Fast blink: 200ms ON, 200ms OFF digitalWrite(ledPin, HIGH); delay(200); digitalWrite(ledPin, LOW); delay(200); break; case 2: // Heartbeat: 100ms ON, 900ms OFF digitalWrite(ledPin, HIGH); delay(100); digitalWrite(ledPin, LOW); delay(900); break; } } ``` **Working Explanation:** - **Pattern 0:** LED blinks every 1 second (frequency 0.5 Hz, duty cycle 50%) - **Pattern 1:** LED blinks 2.5 times per second (frequency 2.5 Hz, duty cycle 50%) - **Pattern 2:** LED produces a "heartbeat" effect (short flash, long pause, duty cycle 10%) **Hardware Validation Steps:** 1. Compile and upload the code to Arduino UNO 2. Open **Serial Monitor** at 9600 baud to observe pattern changes 3. Press the push button to cycle through patterns 4. Observe LED behavior changes accordingly *(Marks: 2 for circuit diagram, 2 for algorithm, 2 for code, 1 for working explanation)* --- #### Part (b): Calculate the current-limiting resistor for a blue LED (V_LED = 3.2V) interfaced to a 3.3V Arduino-compatible board (e.g., ESP32 or Arduino Due), desired LED current 10 mA. What is the power dissipation in the resistor? [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Supply voltage: $V_{supply} = 3.3 \, \text{V}$ (ESP32/Due GPIO output level) - Blue LED forward voltage: $V_{LED} = 3.2 \, \text{V}$ - Desired LED current: $I_{LED} = 10 \, \text{mA} = 0.010 \, \text{A}$ **Step 1: Apply KVL to find voltage across resistor** $$V_R = V_{supply} - V_{LED} = 3.3 - 3.2 = 0.1 \, \text{V}$$ **Step 2: Apply Ohm's Law to find R** $$R = \frac{V_R}{I_{LED}} = \frac{0.1 \, \text{V}}{0.010 \, \text{A}} = 10 \, \Omega$$ **Step 3: Select nearest standard E12 resistor** Nearest standard value: **10 Ω** (which is E12) or **12 Ω**. Using 10 Ω gives exact current. **Step 4: Calculate power dissipation in resistor** $$P_R = I_{LED}^2 \times R = (0.010)^2 \times 10 = 0.001 \, \text{W} = 1 \, \text{mW}$$ A standard **⅛-watt (125 mW)** resistor is more than sufficient (safety factor of 125×). **Step 5: Verify LED power consumption** $$P_{LED} = V_{LED} \times I_{LED} = 3.2 \times 0.010 = 0.032 \, \text{W} = 32 \, \text{mW}$$ **Step 6: Engineering Interpretation** The very small voltage difference (0.1V) makes this design **marginally safe**. Any supply voltage variation (e.g., 3.3V dropping to 3.0V under load) would reduce the current to near zero, causing the LED to **not light up at all**. **Improved Design Recommendation:** - Use a **constant-current driver IC** (e.g., LM317 in current-source mode) for production designs - Alternatively, use a **higher supply voltage** (5V) to provide a larger voltage headroom across the resistor - For 5V supply: $R = (5 - 3.2)/0.010 = 180 \, \Omega$ (nearest standard: 180 Ω or 220 Ω) *(Marks: 2 for given data identification, 2 for calculation, 1 for power calculation, 2 for engineering analysis)* --- ### Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) #### Part (a): With a neat circuit diagram and program, explain how to generate three different blink frequencies (1 Hz, 2 Hz, and 5 Hz) on three different LEDs connected to Arduino pins 8, 9, and 10. [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Model Solution:** **Circuit Connections:** | Component | Arduino Pin | Series Resistor | To | |-----------|-------------|-----------------|-----| | LED 1 (Red) | Pin 8 | 220 Ω | GND | | LED 2 (Yellow) | Pin 9 | 220 Ω | GND | | LED 3 (Green) | Pin 10 | 220 Ω | GND | **Frequency-to-Delay Conversion:** For a 50% duty cycle, $T_{ON} = T_{OFF} = T_{total}/2 = 1/(2f)$: | LED | Frequency $f$ | Period $T = 1/f$ | $T_{ON} = T_{OFF}$ | |-----|---------------|-------------------|---------------------| | 1 (Pin 8) | 1 Hz | 1000 ms | 500 ms | | 2 (Pin 9) | 2 Hz | 500 ms | 250 ms | | 3 (Pin 10) | 5 Hz | 100 ms | 50 ms | **Arduino Program:** ```c // Three LEDs blinking at three different frequencies // Experiment 16 - PCEEL507 const int led1 = 8; // 1 Hz const int led2 = 9; // 2 Hz const int led3 = 10; // 5 Hz void setup() { pinMode(led1, OUTPUT); pinMode(led2, OUTPUT); pinMode(led3, OUTPUT); } void loop() { unsigned long currentMillis = millis(); // LED 1: 1 Hz (toggle every 500 ms) if (currentMillis % 1000 < 500) { digitalWrite(led1, HIGH); } else { digitalWrite(led1, LOW); } // LED 2: 2 Hz (toggle every 250 ms) if (currentMillis % 500 < 250) { digitalWrite(led2, HIGH); } else { digitalWrite(led2, LOW); } // LED 3: 5 Hz (toggle every 100 ms) if (currentMillis % 200 < 100) { digitalWrite(led3, HIGH); } else { digitalWrite(led3, LOW); } } ``` **Key Design Feature:** This implementation uses the **non-blocking `millis()` approach** instead of `delay()`. This allows all three LEDs to blink **independently and simultaneously** without one blocking the execution of the others. This is the foundation of **multitasking in bare-metal embedded systems**. **Expected Visual Output:** - Green LED (Pin 10) blinks **5 times per second** — appears as rapid flashes - Yellow LED (Pin 9) blinks **2 times per second** — moderate speed - Red LED (Pin 8) blinks **once per second** — slow, distinct on/off cycle *(Marks: 2 for circuit, 2 for frequency calculation, 2 for program, 1 for explanation)* --- #### Part (b): Compare software-based delay (`delay()`) and hardware timer-based delay for LED blinking. Under what circumstances would you prefer each? [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Model Solution:** **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | Software `delay()` | Hardware Timer Interrupt | |-----------|-------------------|--------------------------| | **Mechanism** | Polls Timer0 overflow flag in busy-wait loop | Timer ISR fires periodically, sets flag | | **CPU Usage** | 100% (CPU is blocked) | <1% (CPU free for other tasks) | | **Accuracy** | ±1–2% (due to interrupt jitter) | ±0.01% (crystal-controlled) | | **Multitasking** | Not possible (blocking) | Fully supports concurrent tasks | | **Complexity** | Very simple (one function call) | Moderate (requires ISR setup) | | **Power Consumption** | High (CPU always active) | Low (CPU can sleep between interrupts) | | **Response to Events** | Poor (cannot respond during delay) | Excellent (events handled in ISR) | **When to use `delay()`:** - Single-task, simple blink demos - Learning/educational contexts - Prototyping where simplicity > performance - When timing tolerance is loose (±5% acceptable) **When to use Hardware Timers:** - Multi-LED or multi-task systems (e.g., blinking LED while reading a sensor) - Battery-powered applications (sleep mode between interrupts) - Real-time control systems requiring precise timing - When the CPU must respond to external events (buttons, UART, etc.) **Hardware Timer Example (Timer1 CTC mode on ATmega328P):** ```c // Hardware timer-based LED blink using Timer1 Compare Match volatile bool toggleFlag = false; void setup() { pinMode(13, OUTPUT); // Configure Timer1 for 500 ms interval TCCR1A = 0; // Normal port operation TCCR1B = (1 << WGM12) // CTC mode | (1 << CS12) // Prescaler = 256 | (1 << CS10); // Combined: 256 OCR1A = 31249; // Compare value: 16MHz/256 - 1 = 62499 // For 500ms: OCR1A = 31249 TIMSK1 = (1 << OCIE1A); // Enable compare match interrupt sei(); // Enable global interrupts } ISR(TIMER1_COMPA_vect) { // ISR fires every 500 ms toggleFlag = true; } void loop() { if (toggleFlag) { toggleFlag = false; digitalWrite(13, !digitalRead(13)); // Toggle LED // Other non-blocking tasks can run here } // CPU is free to do other work } ``` **Engineering Verdict:** For this specific lab experiment, the `delay()` function is **acceptable and recommended** for simplicity. However, for **any production embedded system**, hardware timer-based scheduling is **mandatory** to ensure deterministic, non-blocking behavior. *(Marks: 3 for comparison table, 2 for use-case analysis, 2 for hardware timer example)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Typically Lose Marks in PCEEL507 Lab Exam:** > > 1. **Forgetting the current-limiting resistor (–2 marks):** Many students connect the LED directly to the GPIO pin. This is an **instantaneous design failure** and reflects poor understanding of Ohm's Law. **Always include a 220 Ω or 330 Ω resistor in series.** > > 2. **Wrong LED polarity (–1 to –2 marks):** The **anode (longer lead, +)** must face the Arduino pin; the **cathode (shorter lead, –)** must connect to GND. Reverse connection results in **no light emission** and may damage the LED. > > 3. **Confusing `pinMode(pin, OUTPUT)` and `INPUT` (–1 mark):** The LED pin **must** be configured as `OUTPUT`. Using `INPUT` will leave the pin in high-impedance state, and the LED behavior will be unpredictable. > > 4. **Not debouncing the push button (–1 mark):** Mechanical buttons produce multiple transitions ("bounce") over 5–50 ms. Without debouncing, a single press may register as 2–5 presses, causing erratic pattern switching. Always implement a 50 ms debounce delay. > > 5. **Using `delay()` inside `ISR` (–2 marks):** `delay()` relies on Timer0 interrupts. Since ISRs disable global interrupts, calling `delay()` inside an ISR will cause the system to **hang indefinitely**. Use `millis()` comparison or hardware flags instead. > > 6. **Wrong syntax for `pinMode` and `digitalWrite` (–1 mark):** Capitalization matters! Correct: `pinMode(13, OUTPUT);` Incorrect: `pinmode(13, output);` or `pinMode(13, output);` > > 7. **Not explaining the delay calculation (–2 marks in viva):** Examiners often ask: *"Why did you choose 1000 ms? What is the resulting frequency and duty cycle?"* You must be able to derive $f = 1/T_{total}$ and $D = T_{ON}/(T_{ON}+T_{OFF})$ on the spot. > > 8. **Missing `void setup()` and `void loop()` (–2 marks):** Every Arduino program **must** contain these two functions. The compiler will not produce an executable without them. > > 9. **Not mentioning the `int main()` equivalent in Arduino (–1 mark viva):** Know that Arduino hides `int main()` which internally calls `setup()` once and then loops `loop()` forever. > > 10. **Breadboard wiring errors (–1 to –3 marks in practical):** Ensure **red wire for power (5V/VCC)**, **black wire for ground (GND)**, and **separate color for signal lines**. Loose connections are the #1 cause of "it doesn't work" in lab exams. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:** > > - **LED = Light Emitting Diode**; requires **anode-to-cathode forward bias** to emit light > - Standard operating current: **10–20 mA**; forward voltage: **1.8–3.2 V** (color-dependent) > - **Current-limiting resistor formula:** $R = (V_{supply} - V_{LED}) / I_{LED}$ > - For Arduino UNO: $V_{supply} = 5V$, recommended $R = 220 \, \Omega$ to $330 \, \Omega$ > - **Three essential Arduino functions:** `pinMode(pin, mode)`, `digitalWrite(pin, value)`, `delay(ms)` > - `pinMode(13, OUTPUT)` — configures pin 13 as output > - `digitalWrite(13, HIGH)` — sets pin 13 to 5V (LED ON) > - `digitalWrite(13, LOW)` — sets pin 13 to 0V (LED OFF) > - `delay(1000)` — pauses program execution for 1000 milliseconds > - **Blink frequency:** $f = 1/T_{total}$ where $T_{total} = T_{ON} + T_{OFF}$ > - **Duty cycle:** $D = T_{ON} / (T_{ON} + T_{OFF}) \times 100\%$ > - **Human flicker fusion threshold: ~24 Hz**; above this, blinking appears as steady light > - **Flicker fusion principle** is the basis of **PWM dimming** and **cinema/motion pictures** > - **Arduino `delay()`** is **blocking** (CPU is idle); use **`millis()` for non-blocking timing** > - **Hardware timers** (Timer0 = 8-bit, Timer1 = 16-bit) provide **precise, interrupt-based timing** > - **ATmega328P clock:** 16 MHz; **instruction cycle:** 62.5 ns > - **Timer0 prescaler:** 64; **overflow period:** 1.024 ms ≈ 1 ms (rounded by Arduino core) > - **Debouncing** is essential for push button inputs (use 50 ms debounce delay) > - **`INPUT_PULLUP`** enables internal 20 kΩ pull-up resistor, eliminating external pull-down resistor > - **Perceived LED brightness** ∝ **duty cycle** (Talbot-Plateau law) > - **Multi-LED designs** require **one current-limiting resistor per LED** > - **Arduino maximum current per pin: 40 mA absolute, 20 mA recommended continuous** > - **Arduino maximum total current: ~200 mA** across all I/O pins > - **Color codes for standard 220 Ω resistor:** Red-Red-Brown-Gold (±5% tolerance) > - **Color codes for 330 Ω resistor:** Orange-Orange-Brown-Gold > - **Voltage dividers** are NOT used for current limiting (use series resistor instead) > - **Persistence of vision** (~1/16 second) governs perceived flicker > - **Morse code pattern** (SOS: ... --- ...) can be implemented by varying ON/OFF delays > - **SOS timing:** 3 short (200ms) + 3 long (600ms) + 3 short (200ms) + 7-space gap > - **8051 alternative:** Use nested `DJNZ` loops with counters M1, M2 for delay generation > - **8051 machine cycle:** 12 oscillator periods = 1.085 µs at 11.0592 MHz > - **Real-world applications:** automotive indicators, IoT status LEDs, emergency lights, medical devices > - **Non-blocking code pattern:** Use `millis() - previousMillis >= interval` for multitasking > - **Volatile keyword** is mandatory for variables shared between ISR and main loop > - **`sei()` and `cli()`** enable and disable global interrupts in AVR code > - **Debounce algorithms:** hardware (RC filter + Schmitt trigger) or software (time-based filtering) > - **Common lab errors:** reversed LED, missing resistor, loose breadboard wire, wrong pin number > - **Examiner's favorite question:** "Calculate the frequency and duty cycle of your program" — always be ready!

topics.title: Voltage measurement of 12V solar PV module/12V battery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL507 - AC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 16
> - **Topic:** Voltage measurement of 12V solar PV module/12V battery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 16: Voltage Measurement of 12V Solar PV Module / 12V Battery

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Open-Circuit Voltage (Voc) Measurement** is the fundamental electrical characterization procedure used to determine the terminal potential difference of a 12V solar Photovoltaic (PV) module or a 12V lead-acid battery when no external load is connected to the source. In the context of a PV module, this measurement is performed under Standard Test Conditions (STC): irradiance of **1000 W/m²**, cell temperature of **25 °C**, and Air Mass of **1.5 (AM 1.5G)**. For a battery, it corresponds to the equilibrium Electro-Motive Force (EMF) of the electrochemical cells in the absence of load current (steady-state open-circuit condition).

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme PCEEL507 Lab Manual** guidelines (Module Experiment 16), this experiment aims to train students in:
- Proper handling of renewable energy sources.
- Use of digital multimeters (DMM) for DC voltage measurement.
- Understanding the I-V characteristic precursor (open-circuit point).
- Solar irradiance measurement using a pyranometer/solar meter.
- Battery State of Charge (SOC) estimation through terminal voltage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** Voltage measurement of a 12V solar PV module/12V battery forms the foundational step of all subsequent renewable energy experiments (load test, MPPT, battery charging). Mastering multimeter handling, polarity observance, and parallel/series distinctions is mandatory.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of voltage as **water pressure** in a pipe:
- A **solar PV module** is like a solar water pump that pushes water (electrons) through pipes only when the sun shines. Measuring its voltage when nothing is connected (no water flowing) tells you the maximum "push" it can deliver.
- A **12V battery** is like a water tank on a height — even when no water flows out, the height of the water column (voltage) tells you how much "energy reserve" is stored.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world parallel:** A 12V car battery is "12V" because six 2V lead-acid cells are connected in series. Similarly, a typical 12V solar PV module has **36 silicon cells** (each ~0.5–0.6V) wired in series. So the "12V" label is the *nominal* value, not the *actual* measured voltage. Actual Voc of a 12V module is typically **18V to 22V** at STC.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|-----------|--------|----------------|------|
| Standard Test Irradiance | $G$ | **1000** | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| STC Cell Temperature | $T_c$ | **25** (i.e., 298.15) | $^\circ\text{C}$ (K) |
| Air Mass | AM | **1.5 G** | dimensionless |
| Nominal Module Voltage | $V_{nom}$ | **12** | V (DC) |
| Typical Open-Circuit Voltage (12V module) | $V_{oc}$ | **18 – 22** | V (DC) |
| Fully charged 12V lead-acid battery | $V_{term}$ | **12.6 – 13.8** | V (DC) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold Constants to Memorize:** $G = 1000\ \text{W/m}^2$, $T_c = 25^\circ\text{C}$, AM 1.5G, 36 cells per 12V module.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physics of a 12V Solar PV Module

A 12V solar PV module is constructed by connecting **36 crystalline silicon solar cells in series**. Each cell behaves as a current source in parallel with a diode. Under sunlight, photons with energy greater than the silicon bandgap (1.12 eV) generate electron-hole pairs that the p-n junction separates, producing a DC photocurrent.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Open-Circuit Voltage Generation

- **Step 1 — Photon Absorption:** Sunlight strikes the silicon cell; photons transfer energy to valence electrons.
- **Step 2 — Electron-Hole Pair Generation:** Electrons jump to the conduction band, creating free carriers.
- **Step 3 — Junction Separation:** The built-in electric field of the p-n junction sweeps electrons to the n-side and holes to the p-side.
- **Step 4 — Terminal Voltage Buildup:** Accumulated charges establish a potential difference across the external terminals.
- **Step 5 — Open-Circuit Condition:** When no load draws current, this terminal voltage reaches its maximum — the **Open-Circuit Voltage ($V_{oc}$)**.
- **Step 6 — Why it's >12V:** Each cell's $V_{oc}$ is ~0.6V. With 36 cells in series: $V_{oc} \approx 36 \times 0.6 = 21.6\ \text{V}$. The "12V" label is the *nominal battery-equivalent operating voltage*.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why':** $V_{oc}$ depends logarithmically on irradiance, meaning cloudy conditions reduce $V_{oc}$ only slightly (a 10× drop in irradiance reduces $V_{oc}$ by only ~5%). This is why voltage measurement alone cannot judge solar module performance — current measurement is equally critical.

## 2.3 Underlying Physics of a 12V Lead-Acid Battery

A 12V lead-acid battery comprises **6 cells in series**, each producing a nominal **2 V**. Each cell contains:
- **Positive plate:** Lead dioxide ($\text{PbO}_2$)
- **Negative plate:** Sponge lead ($\text{Pb}$)
- **Electrolyte:** Sulfuric acid ($\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$) + water solution

### Electrochemical Reaction (Discharge):

$$
\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\,\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

### Electrochemical Reaction (Charge):

$$
2\,\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The terminal voltage of a 12V battery is a direct indicator of its **State of Charge (SOC)**. Higher terminal voltage = higher SOC.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit | Boundary/Condition |
|---|---------|-------------|------|---------------------|
| 1 | $V_{oc} = N_s \cdot V_{oc,cell}$ | Total module open-circuit voltage (series cells) | V | $N_s$ = number of cells in series |
| 2 | $V_{oc,cell} = \dfrac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0}+1\right)$ | Single-cell open-circuit voltage (Shockley diode eq.) | V | $k$ = Boltzmann const, $q$ = electron charge |
| 3 | $V_{nom} = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{1.5\ \text{to}\ 1.8}$ | Approx. nominal voltage from Voc | V | For 12V crystalline Si module |
| 4 | $P_{max} \approx 0.8 \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$ | Empirical max power point approximation | W | Crystalline Si, typical FF $\approx$ 0.8 |
| 5 | $V_{terminal} = f(\text{SOC})$ | Battery terminal voltage vs. SOC | V | Lead-acid: 11.8V (0%) to 12.7V (100%) |
| 6 | $G_{eff} = G_{meas}\cdot\cos(\theta_z)$ | Effective irradiance on tilted surface | $\text{W/m}^2$ | $\theta_z$ = zenith angle of sun |
| 7 | $T_c = T_a + \text{NOCT} - 20 \cdot \dfrac{G}{800}$ | Cell temperature from ambient & irradiance | $^\circ\text{C}$ | NOCT = Nominal Operating Cell Temp (45°C) |
| 8 | $E = \text{Energy density}\cdot V\cdot \text{Capacity}$ | Stored battery energy | Wh | $V$ in V, Capacity in Ah |
| 9 | $\text{Percentage error} = \dfrac{\vert V_{meas} - V_{true}\vert}{V_{true}}\times 100$ | Measurement accuracy | % | Used to assess DMM error |
| 10 | $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{\text{Range}}{2^n - 1}$ | DMM least count | V | $n$ = ADC bit count |

> [!NOTE]
> **K<sub>T</sub> (Boltzmann) = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K, q (electron charge) = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C — these are the standard physics constants you may need if KTU asks a derivation.**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solar Farms:** Operators measure $V_{oc}$ of every string before connecting to the inverter; mismatched strings cause power loss.
- **Off-Grid Solar Systems (Kerala homes):** Battery voltage measurement is used daily to check backup health.
- **EV Battery Packs:** $V_{oc}$ trends predict cell degradation and End-of-Life.
- **Telecom Towers:** Battery voltage is monitored remotely to prevent downtime.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Single-Cell Diode Equation to Module Open-Circuit Voltage

### Step 1 — Single-Cell Equivalent Circuit (One-Diode Model)

A solar cell is modelled as a current source $I_L$ in parallel with a diode $D$, series resistance $R_s$, and shunt resistance $R_{sh}$.

**Terminal current equation:**

$$
I = I_L - I_0\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{q(V + I R_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}
$$

where:
- $I_L$ = Photogenerated current (A)
- $I_0$ = Diode reverse saturation current (A)
- $n$ = Ideality factor (1 to 2)
- $k$ = Boltzmann constant = $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- $T$ = Absolute cell temperature (K)
- $q$ = Electron charge = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C

### Step 2 — Apply Open-Circuit Condition

At open circuit, $I = 0$ and the terminal voltage equals $V_{oc}$. Assuming $R_{sh}$ is very large (ideal cell) and $R_s$ is very small:

$$
0 = I_L - I_0\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{q V_{oc}}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]
$$

Rearranging:

$$
I_L = I_0\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{q V_{oc}}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $V_{oc}$

For $I_L \gg I_0$ (true under sunlight), the "-1" can be neglected:

$$
\exp\!\left(\dfrac{q V_{oc}}{nkT}\right) = \dfrac{I_L}{I_0}
$$

Take natural logarithm on both sides:

$$
\dfrac{q V_{oc}}{nkT} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0}\right)
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0}\right)
$$

This is the **fundamental $V_{oc}$ equation** of a single solar cell.

### Step 4 — Extend to a 12V Module with $N_s$ Cells in Series

For $N_s$ identical cells in series, voltages add:

$$
V_{oc,module} = N_s \cdot V_{oc,cell}
$$

$$
V_{oc,module} = N_s \cdot \dfrac{nkT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0}\right)
$$

For a 12V module, $N_s = 36$:

$$
V_{oc,module} = 36 \cdot \dfrac{nkT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0}\right)
$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Estimation at STC

Assume: $T = 298.15\ \text{K}$, $n = 1.3$, $I_L = 5\ \text{A}$, $I_0 = 1 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{A}$

Compute the thermal voltage factor:

$$
\dfrac{nkT}{q} = \dfrac{1.3 \times 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \times 298.15}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1.3 \times 1.381 \times 298.15}{1.602} \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{535.47}{1.602} \times 10^{-4} = 334.25 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0334\ \text{V}
$$

Compute the logarithm:

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{5}{1 \times 10^{-9}}\right) = \ln(5 \times 10^{9}) = \ln 5 + 9 \ln 10
$$

$$
= 1.6094 + 9 \times 2.3026 = 1.6094 + 20.7234 = 22.3328
$$

Single-cell $V_{oc}$:

$$
V_{oc,cell} = 0.0334 \times 22.3328 \approx 0.746\ \text{V}
$$

Module $V_{oc}$ (36 cells):

$$
V_{oc,module} = 36 \times 0.746 \approx 26.86\ \text{V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This theoretical value (26.86 V) is higher than typical measured 21–22 V. The difference arises from cell-to-cell mismatch, finite $R_s$, and ideality factor variation. The **practical 12V module Voc is ~21V** at STC.

## 3.2 Derivation: Battery Terminal Voltage to State of Charge (SOC)

### Empirical Relationship (Lead-Acid, 12V):

$$
V_{term} = 11.8 + 0.009 \times \text{SOC}(\%)
$$

### Step-by-Step Inversion

For a measured $V_{term} = 12.4\ \text{V}$:

$$
12.4 = 11.8 + 0.009 \times \text{SOC}
$$

$$
0.6 = 0.009 \times \text{SOC}
$$

$$
\text{SOC} = \dfrac{0.6}{0.009} = 66.67\%
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: $V_{oc}$ vs. Irradiance and Temperature

| $G$ ($\text{W/m}^2$) | $T_c$ ($^\circ\text{C}$) | $V_{oc}$ (V) | Comment |
|---------------------|-------------------------|--------------|---------|
| 1000 | 25 | **21.0** | STC nominal |
| 1000 | 45 | **19.5** | Hot cell → lower $V_{oc}$ |
| 1000 | 5 | **22.0** | Cold cell → higher $V_{oc}$ |
| 500 | 25 | **20.4** | Cloudy → small $V_{oc}$ drop |
| 200 | 25 | **19.3** | Heavy cloud |
| 0 | 25 | **0** | Night — no generation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight for KTU:** $V_{oc}$ is *more sensitive to temperature* than to irradiance. A 25°C rise in $T_c$ reduces $V_{oc}$ by about 0.5–0.7V per 12V module.

## 3.4 Exhaustive Measurement Procedure (Step-by-Step)

### A. For a 12V Solar PV Module

1. **Step 1 — Safety Check:** Wear insulating gloves; ensure module glass is clean and not cracked.
2. **Step 2 — Identify Terminals:** Locate the positive (+) and negative (-) junction box terminals.
3. **Step 3 — DMM Setup:** Turn the digital multimeter (DMM) knob to **DC Voltage mode** (V with a straight line, not wavy). Choose the **20V range** for a 12V module.
4. **Step 4 — Polarity:** Connect the **RED probe to the (+) terminal** and the **BLACK probe to the (-) terminal**.
5. **Step 5 — Sun Condition:** Verify irradiance using a solar meter (pyranometer) — must be ≥ 800 W/m² for valid measurement.
6. **Step 6 — Read Display:** Note the stable voltage. For 12V module under Kerala midday sun, expect **18 – 22 V**.
7. **Step 7 — Record:** Note the time, irradiance, ambient temperature, and the module's serial number.
8. **Step 8 — Repeat:** Take three readings at 30-second intervals and compute the mean.

### B. For a 12V Lead-Acid Battery

1. **Step 1 — Disconnect Load:** Isolate the battery from any load or charger for at least **30 minutes** (to allow surface charge dissipation).
2. **Step 2 — Clean Terminals:** Use a wire brush to remove corrosion from terminals.
3. **Step 3 — DMM Setup:** Set DMM to **DC Voltage, 20V range**.
4. **Step 4 — Connect Probes:** RED to (+), BLACK to (-).
5. **Step 5 — Read Voltage:** A fully charged 12V battery reads **12.6 – 12.7V**. A 50% charged battery reads **~12.2V**. Below **11.8V** = deeply discharged.
6. **Step 6 — Interpret SOC:** Use the table in Section 3.2 to determine SOC percentage.
