# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Open-Circuit Voltage" of a solar PV module. Why is the rated voltage of a 12V PV module around 21V and not exactly 12V?**

**Model Answer:**
Open-circuit voltage ($V_{oc}$) is the maximum terminal voltage a solar PV module delivers when its terminals are not connected to any external load (i.e., current $I = 0$).

A "12V" rating is a *nominal* label, indicating the module is suitable for charging a 12V battery. Internally, the module has **36 crystalline silicon cells in series**. Each cell has a $V_{oc}$ of about **0.58–0.62 V** at STC. Therefore:

$$
V_{oc,module} = 36 \times 0.6 \approx 21.6\ \text{V}
$$

The "12V" name comes from the fact that this ~21V is sufficient to charge a 12V lead-acid battery (which needs up to 14.4V for full charge) and operate 12V DC loads after the charge controller's voltage drop.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**A 12V lead-acid battery is measured with a digital multimeter and reads 11.9V. Estimate its State of Charge (SOC) and explain whether the battery needs recharging.**

**Model Answer:**
Using the empirical lead-acid relationship:

$$
V_{term} = 11.8 + 0.009 \times \text{SOC}(\%)
$$

$$
11.9 = 11.8 + 0.009 \times \text{SOC}
$$

$$
\text{SOC} = \dfrac{0.1}{0.009} \approx 11.11\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The battery is at only ~11% SOC, which is critically low. It must be recharged immediately. Prolonged operation at this SOC causes **sulfation** of the plates, permanently reducing battery capacity.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the procedure to measure the open-circuit voltage of a 12V solar PV module. List the apparatus required and state the precautions to be followed. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 12V solar PV module has 36 cells in series. Each cell produces a short-circuit current of 5A and a saturation current of $1 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{A}$. The ideality factor is 1.3. Calculate the open-circuit voltage of the module at STC ($T = 298.15\ \text{K}$). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Procedure and Block Diagram (7 Marks):**

**Apparatus Required:**
- 12V solar PV module (36-cell crystalline Si)
- Digital Multimeter (DMM) with DC 20V range
- Solar power meter / Pyranometer
- Thermometer (ambient + surface)
- Connecting leads (with MC4 connectors)
- Stopwatch

**Procedure Steps:**
1. Place the module under direct sunlight, oriented south (in Kerala, ~10° tilt).
2. Verify irradiance ≥ 800 W/m² using the pyranometer.
3. Ensure no load is connected to the module terminals.
4. Set DMM to **DC Voltage, 20V range**.
5. Connect the **RED probe to the (+)** terminal and **BLACK probe to the (-)** terminal of the module junction box.
6. Wait for the reading to stabilize (10–15 seconds).
7. Record the voltage, irradiance, ambient temperature, and time.
8. Repeat 3 times at 30-second intervals and compute the mean.

**Precautions:**
- Always observe **polarity** to avoid damaging the DMM.
- Do not measure under partial shadow; even one shaded cell drops the entire string voltage.
- Keep the module glass clean; dust reduces effective irradiance.
- Do not short-circuit the module (although modules are current-limited, short-circuit testing is a separate procedure).
- Wear gloves if the module is hot (rooftop conditions).

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes (Mermaid flowchart).

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $N_s = 36$ cells in series
- $I_L = I_{sc} = 5\ \text{A}$
- $I_0 = 1 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{A}$
- $n = 1.3$
- $T = 298.15\ \text{K}$
- $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$
- $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$

**Step 1 — Compute the thermal voltage factor for a single cell:**

$$
V_T = \dfrac{nkT}{q} = \dfrac{1.3 \times 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \times 298.15}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}
$$

$$
V_T = \dfrac{1.3 \times 1.381 \times 298.15}{1.602} \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
V_T = \dfrac{535.47}{1.602} \times 10^{-4} = 0.0334\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute single-cell $V_{oc}$:**

$$
V_{oc,cell} = V_T \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0}\right) = 0.0334 \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{5}{1 \times 10^{-9}}\right)
$$

$$
\ln(5 \times 10^{9}) = \ln 5 + 9 \ln 10 = 1.6094 + 20.7234 = 22.3328
$$

$$
V_{oc,cell} = 0.0334 \times 22.3328 = 0.746\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute module $V_{oc}$ (36 cells in series):**

$$
V_{oc,module} = 36 \times 0.746 = 26.86\ \text{V}
$$

**Result:** The theoretical $V_{oc}$ of the module is approximately **26.86 V**, which is higher than the typical measured value (~21V) due to non-idealities like cell mismatch, series resistance, and lower effective ideality factor in practice.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the construction of a 12V lead-acid battery. Write the electrochemical reactions during charging and discharging. How is the State of Charge (SOC) estimated from the terminal voltage? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A solar PV system uses a 12V, 100 Ah lead-acid battery. After one day of operation, the terminal voltage is measured as 12.1V. Calculate the remaining State of Charge (SOC) and the energy remaining in the battery in Wh. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Construction and Electrochemistry of 12V Lead-Acid Battery (7 Marks):**

**Construction:**
A 12V lead-acid battery consists of **6 cells in series**, each producing ~2V.

Each cell contains:
- **Positive plate:** Lead dioxide ($\text{PbO}_2$) coated on a lead-antimony grid.
- **Negative plate:** Sponge lead ($\text{Pb}$) on a lead grid.
- **Separator:** Microporous polyethylene or rubber sheet to prevent short-circuit between plates.
- **Electrolyte:** Dilute sulfuric acid ($\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$) of specific gravity ~1.28 (full charge).
- **Container:** Hard rubber or polypropylene case with vent caps.

**Discharge Reaction:**

$$
\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\,\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Charge Reaction (reverse of discharge):**

$$
2\,\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4
$$

**SOC Estimation from Terminal Voltage:**
- 12.6V – 12.7V → 100% SOC (fully charged)
- 12.4V → ~70% SOC
- 12.2V → ~50% SOC
- 12.0V → ~20% SOC
- 11.8V → 0% SOC (fully discharged)

Empirical formula:

$$
V_{term} = 11.8 + 0.009 \times \text{SOC}(\%)
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Nominal voltage: $V = 12\ \text{V}$
- Capacity: $C = 100\ \text{Ah}$
- Measured terminal voltage: $V_{term} = 12.1\ \text{V}$

**Step 1 — Calculate SOC percentage:**

$$
12.1 = 11.8 + 0.009 \times \text{SOC}
$$

$$
0.3 = 0.009 \times \text{SOC}
$$

$$
\text{SOC} = \dfrac{0.3}{0.009} = 33.33\%
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate remaining capacity:**

$$
C_{remaining} = 33.33\% \times 100\ \text{Ah} = 33.33\ \text{Ah}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate remaining energy in Wh:**

$$
E_{remaining} = V_{term} \times C_{remaining} = 12.1 \times 33.33
$$

$$
E_{remaining} = 403.3\ \text{Wh}
$$

**Result:** The battery is at **33.33% SOC** with approximately **403 Wh** of energy remaining. It should be recharged soon to avoid deep discharge damage.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $V_{oc}$ with $V_{mpp}$:** $V_{oc}$ is the *no-load* voltage; $V_{mpp}$ is the *operating* voltage at maximum power. Mixing them up = **-2 marks**.
> 2. **Wrong number of cells:** Forgetting that a 12V module has 36 cells (not 12). Common error.
> 3. **Using AC Voltage mode on DMM:** Always use **DC V** mode (V with straight line, not wavy line). Reading zero or a fluctuating value = **-1 mark** deduction.
> 4. **Wrong polarity:** Reversing RED and BLACK probes gives a **negative reading**. Always state the correct polarity connection.
> 5. **Forgetting the "-1" in the diode equation:** When deriving $V_{oc}$, the "$I_L \gg I_0$" approximation allows dropping the "-1". Mentioning this assumption is essential for full marks.
> 6. **Not mentioning STC:** Any voltage measurement of a PV module must reference STC conditions ($1000\ \text{W/m}^2$, $25^\circ\text{C}$, AM 1.5G) for full credit.
> 7. **Battery SOC formula without stating the assumption:** Always mention that the battery has rested for ≥ 30 minutes before measurement (no surface charge).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU PCEEL507, Module Experiment 16**

- ✅ A "12V" solar PV module is **not** 12V at open circuit; it is **~18–22V** (36 cells × 0.6V).
- ✅ **STC conditions:** $G = 1000\ \text{W/m}^2$, $T_c = 25^\circ\text{C}$, AM 1.5G — must be stated for valid module measurements.
- ✅ **DMM setting:** Always use **DC Voltage mode, 20V range** for both PV module and battery tests.
- ✅ **Polarity:** RED probe to (+), BLACK probe to (-). Reversed polarity → negative reading (not damage in most DMMs, but still wrong).
- ✅ **No-load condition:** $V_{oc}$ measurement requires the source terminals to be **open** (no load connected).
- ✅ **$V_{oc}$ formula:** $V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0}\right)$ — logarithmic dependence on irradiance.
- ✅ **Temperature effect:** Higher $T_c$ → **lower** $V_{oc}$ (coefficient ≈ -0.3 to -0.4 %/°C).
- ✅ **Lead-acid battery:** 6 cells × 2V = 12V nominal; SOC estimated from open-circuit terminal voltage after ≥ 30 min rest.
- ✅ **Battery SOC thresholds:** ≥ 12.6V (full), 12.2V (50%), ≤ 11.8V (empty/damaging).
- ✅ **Pyranometer / solar meter** must be used alongside voltage measurement to verify irradiance.
- ✅ **Module orientation (Kerala):** South-facing, ~10° tilt (Kerala latitude ≈ 10°N).
- ✅ **Energy formula:** $E (\text{Wh}) = V (\text{V}) \times \text{Capacity (Ah)}$.
- ✅ **Three readings, then mean** — record time, irradiance, and temperature for every reading.
- ✅ **Precautions:** Clean glass surface, no partial shadow on module, disconnect battery load before voltage check, no short-circuit testing during $V_{oc}$ measurement.
- ✅ **Typical practical $V_{oc}$ of 12V module:** ~**21 V** (not the 26.86 V theoretical, due to non-idealities).
- ✅ **Fully charged 12V lead-acid battery voltage:** **12.6 – 12.7 V** (resting), up to **13.8V** during active charging.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DC current measurement using Hall effect current sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL507 - AC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 16
> - **Topic:** DC current measurement using Hall effect current sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)
A **Hall Effect Current Sensor** is a non-contact, galvanically isolated transducer that exploits the **Hall Effect** (discovered by Edwin H. Hall in 1879) to convert a magnetic field generated by a current-carrying conductor into a measurable output voltage. In the context of this experiment, it is used to sense **DC currents** flowing through a bus-bar or wire by detecting the proportional magnetic flux density surrounding the conductor using a semiconductor Hall element placed in a ferromagnetic core gap.

### Hall Effect — The Foundational Phenomenon
When a current-carrying conductor or semiconductor strip is placed in a transverse magnetic field, the charge carriers (electrons or holes) experience a **Lorentz force** that deflects them to one side of the strip. This charge accumulation on one side produces a transverse voltage known as the **Hall Voltage (V_H)**, perpendicular to both the current flow and the magnetic field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:**
> *"The Hall Effect is the generation of a transverse electromotive force (Hall Voltage) across a current-carrying conductor or semiconductor when it is placed in a magnetic field perpendicular to the direction of current flow."*

### Conceptual Analogy — "The River and the Magnet"
Imagine a straight river (current `I`) flowing in one direction. Now place a giant magnet (magnetic field `B`) on one side of the river. The water molecules (charge carriers) get pushed sideways, accumulating more on the opposite bank, creating a tiny height difference (Hall Voltage) between the two banks.
- **Stronger current (I)** → More water → Bigger height difference
- **Stronger magnetic field (B)** → More pushing force → Bigger height difference
- This "height difference" can be measured with a voltmeter — that is the Hall Voltage.

In a Hall Effect Current Sensor, we **create the magnet on demand** by passing the unknown DC current through a wire surrounded by a soft-iron core. The current is the unknown, the Hall Voltage is the measurable output — and they are linearly proportional.

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- **Hall Coefficient (R_H):** `1 / (n × e)` (Units: **m³/C**)
- **Electron charge (e):** `1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C`
- **Carrier concentration (n):** `~10²¹ to 10²⁵ m⁻³` (depends on material)
- **Permeability of free space (μ₀):** `4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m`
- **Typical output range:** `±4 V` (bi-directional), `0–5 V` (unidirectional)
- **Typical bandwidth:** **DC to 100 kHz** (key advantage over CTs)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Hall Effect Sensors are Special for DC:**
> Unlike Current Transformers (CTs), Hall Effect sensors respond to **static (DC) magnetic fields** because the Hall Voltage depends on the *instantaneous* field, not on its rate of change. This makes them the only practical isolated sensor for **true DC and very low frequency AC** current measurement.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Concept — The Four-Stage Logic Chain
The DC current measurement using a Hall effect current sensor follows a precise four-stage physical process:

1. **Primary Current Injection:** The unknown DC current `I_p` flows through a bus-bar or cable passing through a window in the sensor housing.
2. **Magnetic Field Generation:** By **Ampere's Law**, this current produces a circumferential magnetic field `B` around the conductor. The field is concentrated by a **ferromagnetic toroidal core** that surrounds the conductor.
3. **Hall Element Sensing:** A thin semiconductor chip (typically **GaAs, InSb, or InAs**) is placed in a precisely machined **air gap** of the core. The flux density in the gap is perpendicular to the chip's current path.
4. **Signal Conditioning:** The millivolt-level Hall Voltage is amplified, temperature-compensated, and scaled to a standard analog output (e.g., 0–5 V, 4–20 mA, or digital via ADC).

### Why the Core and Gap Are Critical
- A bare wire's magnetic field is weak and depends on distance (`B ∝ 1/r`), making direct sensing inaccurate.
- A **ferromagnetic core** (high permeability μ) **channels and concentrates** the flux around the conductor into a tight, uniform path.
- The **air gap** is deliberately introduced because the Hall element must sit in a region of known, non-saturating field, and DC flux cannot be measured by any other practical non-contact means inside a closed iron loop.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Equation** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Standard Units** | **Boundary / Limiting Condition** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $$V_H = \dfrac{R_H \cdot I_c \cdot B}{t}$$ | `R_H` = Hall coefficient, `I_c` = control current, `B` = flux density, `t` = strip thickness | `V` | Valid for thin, uniform semiconductor strip |
| $$R_H = \dfrac{1}{n \cdot e}$$ | `n` = carrier density, `e` = electron charge | `m³/C` | Single carrier type assumed |
| $$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 \cdot I_p}{2 \pi r}$$ | `μ₀` = permeability, `I_p` = primary current, `r` = radial distance | `T` (Tesla) | Valid for **infinite straight wire**, free space |
| $$B_{\text{gap}} = \dfrac{\mu_0 \cdot N \cdot I_p}{\ell_g + \dfrac{\ell_c}{\mu_r}}$$ | Effective B in air gap, with core path length | `T` | For toroidal core; `ℓ_g` = gap length, `ℓ_c` = core path length |
| $$V_{\text{out}} = G \cdot V_H = K_{\text{sens}} \cdot I_p$$ | `G` = amplifier gain, `K_sens` = overall sensor sensitivity (V/A) | `V` | Linear region, before core saturation |
| $$K_{\text{sens}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{FS}}}{I_{\text{FS}}}$$ | Sensitivity = Full-scale output ÷ Full-scale input | `V/A` | Calibration constant from experiment |
| $$\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{I_{\text{measured}} - I_{\text{true}}}{I_{\text{true}}} \times 100\,\%$$ | Error percentage | `%` | Compare sensor reading with ammeter reading |

### Engineering Utility — Where This Matters
- **Electric vehicle (EV) battery management:** Monitoring DC link currents of **400 V / 500 A** safely.
- **Solar PV inverters:** DC string current measurement up to **1500 V DC**.
- **Industrial motor drives:** DC bus current feedback for closed-loop torque control.
- **Welding and electroplating:** High-current (kA range) DC monitoring.
- **Smart grid battery storage systems:** Continuous DC current telemetry.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The two equations that appear in **almost every answer** related to this experiment are (1) `V_H ∝ I·B/t` and (2) the **calibration sensitivity** `K = ΔV_out / ΔI_in`. Always derive and use these.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1 — Hall Voltage from Lorentz Force
**Starting Principle:** A charge carrier `q` moving with drift velocity `v_d` through a magnetic field `B` experiences a Lorentz force `F = q(v_d × B)`.

**Step 1 — Equilibrium Condition:**
Initially, carriers deflected to one side create an electric field `E_H = V_H / w` (where `w` is the strip width). At steady state, the electric force `qE_H` exactly balances the magnetic force `qv_d B`.

$$q E_H = q v_d B$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the Hall Electric Field:**
Dividing both sides by `q` (assuming `q ≠ 0`):

$$E_H = v_d B$$

**Step 3 — Express Drift Velocity in Terms of Current:**
The drift velocity of carriers relates to the control current `I_c` as:

$$I_c = n \cdot q \cdot v_d \cdot A = n \cdot q \cdot v_d \cdot (w \cdot t)$$

Solving for `v_d`:

$$v_d = \dfrac{I_c}{n \cdot q \cdot w \cdot t}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the Hall Field Equation:**

$$E_H = \dfrac{I_c \cdot B}{n \cdot q \cdot w \cdot t}$$

**Step 5 — Convert Field to Hall Voltage:**
Since `V_H = E_H · w`, multiply both sides by `w`:

$$V_H = \dfrac{I_c \cdot B}{n \cdot q \cdot t}$$

**Step 6 — Introduce the Hall Coefficient:**
Defining `R_H = 1 / (n · q)`:

$$\boxed{V_H = \dfrac{R_H \cdot I_c \cdot B}{t}}$$

This is the **fundamental Hall Voltage equation** — the cornerstone of the entire experiment.

### Derivation 2 — Sensor Output as a Function of Primary Current
**Step 1 — Magnetic Field from a Conductor:**
For a long straight wire carrying primary current `I_p`, the magnetic field at radial distance `r` is:

$$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 \cdot I_p}{2 \pi r}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the Hall Voltage Equation:**
The Hall element inside the sensor experiences `B` from above, with a control current `I_c` driven through it by an internal source. Substituting:

$$V_H = \dfrac{R_H \cdot I_c}{t} \cdot \dfrac{\mu_0 \cdot I_p}{2 \pi r}$$

**Step 3 — Define the Sensor's Geometric/Physical Constant:**
Grouping all fixed parameters (`R_H`, `I_c`, `t`, `r`, `μ₀`) into a single constant `K_0`:

$$V_H = K_0 \cdot I_p$$

This confirms the **linear relationship** between Hall Voltage and primary DC current.

**Step 4 — Signal Conditioning (Amplification):**
The internal amplifier multiplies `V_H` by a fixed gain `G`:

$$V_{\text{out}} = G \cdot V_H = (G \cdot K_0) \cdot I_p$$

Defining the **overall sensitivity** `K = G · K_0` (in V/A):

$$\boxed{V_{\text{out}} = K \cdot I_p}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the Measured Current:**
Rearranging for `I_p` (the unknown DC current we want to measure):

$$\boxed{I_p = \dfrac{V_{\text{out}}}{K}}$$

### Derivation 3 — Calibration Sensitivity from a Two-Point Experiment
In the lab, the experiment is performed by sweeping a known DC current `I_p` through a precision DC source and recording the corresponding `V_out` at each step. The sensitivity is then extracted by **linear regression**.

**Step 1 — Set Up the Data Pairs:**
Let the recorded data be `(I_1, V_1), (I_2, V_2), ..., (I_n, V_n)`.

**Step 2 — Apply the Least-Squares Slope Formula:**

$$K = \dfrac{n \sum (I_i V_i) - \left(\sum I_i\right)\left(\sum V_i\right)}{n \sum I_i^2 - \left(\sum I_i\right)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Offset Correction:**
A real sensor has a residual offset `V_0` at `I_p = 0` (due to residual magnetism and op-amp offsets). The corrected linear model is:

$$V_{\text{out}} = K \cdot I_p + V_0$$

Solving for current:

$$I_p = \dfrac{V_{\text{out}} - V_0}{K}$$

### Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Lab Value)
**Problem:** A Hall effect current sensor has a sensitivity `K = 0.04 V/A` and an offset `V_0 = 0.02 V`. When connected to a DC bus, the output reads `V_out = 1.86 V`. Calculate the primary current.

**Step 1 — Substitute Known Values:**

$$I_p = \dfrac{V_{\text{out}} - V_0}{K} = \dfrac{1.86 - 0.02}{0.04}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Numerator:**

$$1.86 - 0.02 = 1.84 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Divide by Sensitivity:**

$$I_p = \dfrac{1.84}{0.04} = 46 \, \text{A}$$

**Answer:** The primary DC current flowing in the bus-bar is **46 A**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Hall Effect Current Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Source 0 to 50A] -->|Ip| B[Primary Conductor]
    B --> C[Ferromagnetic Toroidal Core]
    C --> D[Air Gap with Hall Element]
    D -->|VH millivolt| E[Amplifier and Temperature Compensation]
    E --> F[Offset Adjustment Circuit]
    F --> G[Output Stage 0 to 5V or 4 to 20mA]
    G --> H[Digital Voltmeter or DSO]
    G --> I[ADC to Microcontroller]
    I --> J[Display or Data Logger]
    K[DC Supply to Sensor plus or minus 15V] --> E
    L[Reference Ammeter for Calibration] -.->|Validation| H
```

### Diagram 2 — Detailed Experimental Setup Flow (For Lab Record)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Step1[Step 1: Connect the Hall Effect Current Sensor to plus or minus 15V DC supply] --> Step2
    Step2[Step 2: Pass the unknown DC cable through the sensor window] --> Step3
    Step3[Step 3: Apply a known DC current from a regulated source through the cable] --> Step4
    Step4[Step 4: Use a standard ammeter in series to record the true primary current] --> Step5
    Step5[Step 5: Note the corresponding Hall sensor output voltage from the DMM] --> Step6
    Step6[Step 6: Vary the DC current in 5 equal steps up to rated full scale] --> Step7
    Step7[Step 7: Tabulate Ip versus Vout for all 6 readings including zero] --> Step8
    Step8[Step 8: Plot the calibration curve Vout versus Ip] --> Step9
    Step9[Step 9: Calculate sensitivity K as the slope of the best fit line] --> Step10
    Step10[Step 10: Reverse current direction to verify bipolar response if sensor is bi-directional] --> Step11
    Step11[Step 11: Compute accuracy and linearity error for each measurement point]
```

### Diagram 3 — The Physical Action Inside the Sensor (Cross-Section Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Current Ip flows into the page] --> B[Concentric Magnetic Field B encircles the wire]
    B --> C[Soft Iron Core collects and channels the flux]
    C --> D[Flux exits through the air gap]
    D --> E[Hall IC in the gap senses perpendicular B field]
    E --> F[Output Voltage Vout is generated proportional to Ip]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Lab Diagram Tip:** For your KTU record, draw the cross-section showing the conductor at the center, the toroidal core (C-shape with air gap) around it, the Hall IC inside the gap, and clearly mark `B` arrow perpendicular to the chip surface. This is a **3-mark short-answer favorite**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**Define the Hall Effect. Why is it particularly suitable for DC current measurement compared to a Current Transformer?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Hall Effect** is the generation of a transverse electromotive force (Hall Voltage `V_H`) across a current-carrying conductor or semiconductor when subjected to a magnetic field perpendicular to the current direction.
**Mathematically:** `V_H = (R_H · I · B) / t`.

A **Current Transformer (CT)** works on Faraday's Law of induction, where the secondary EMF is proportional to `dΦ/dt`. For a **steady DC current**, `dΦ/dt = 0`, so a CT produces **zero output** for DC. In contrast, the Hall Effect depends on the *instantaneous* flux density `B`, not its rate of change. Therefore, a Hall Effect sensor can accurately measure **DC, AC (any frequency), and pulsed currents**, making it the only practical isolated sensor for true DC current measurement. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**State the role of the ferromagnetic core and the air gap in a Hall Effect current sensor.**

**Model Answer:**
The **ferromagnetic core** (typically silicon steel or ferrite with high permeability μ) **concentrates the weak, diverging magnetic field** generated around the primary conductor and channels it through a defined air gap. This increases the flux density at the Hall element location and makes the measurement **insensitive to the exact positioning** of the conductor within the sensor window.

The **air gap** is a deliberate, precisely machined slot in the core. It serves three purposes: (i) provides a **uniform, well-defined flux path** for the Hall element to sit in, (ii) prevents the core from **saturating** at high primary currents, and (iii) allows the Hall IC to physically sit **perpendicular to the flux lines**, maximizing the Hall Voltage. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

**Question A. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**(a)** Derive the expression for Hall Voltage `V_H` in a semiconductor strip, clearly stating the equilibrium condition and the final formula. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A Hall Effect current sensor has a full-scale primary current of `I_FS = 100 A` and produces an output voltage of `V_out = 4 V` at full scale, with a zero-current offset of `0.05 V`. Calculate: (i) the sensitivity `K` in V/A, (ii) the measured primary current when `V_out = 2.35 V`, and (iii) the percentage error if a reference ammeter reads `50.2 A` at the same output. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — Hall Voltage Derivation:**
Refer to **Derivation 1 in Section 3** above. Write the equation `qE_H = qv_dB`, then derive `v_d = I_c/(nqwt)`, then combine to obtain:

$$V_H = \dfrac{R_H \cdot I_c \cdot B}{t}, \quad \text{where } R_H = \dfrac{1}{nq}$$

Clearly state: `n` = carrier density, `q` = charge, `t` = strip thickness, `B` = magnetic flux density perpendicular to the strip, `I_c` = control current through the strip. Mention the unit of `R_H` as `m³/C` and `V_H` as volts. **(7 marks — full marks for full derivation with explanation)**

**Model Solution for (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

**(i) Sensitivity K:**
The output ranges from `V_0 = 0.05 V` (at `I = 0 A`) to `4 V` (at `I = 100 A`).

$$K = \dfrac{V_{\text{FS}} - V_0}{I_{\text{FS}}} = \dfrac{4.00 - 0.05}{100} = \dfrac{3.95}{100} = 0.0395 \, \text{V/A}$$

**(ii) Measured Primary Current at V_out = 2.35 V:**
Using `V_out = K · I_p + V_0`:

$$I_p = \dfrac{V_{\text{out}} - V_0}{K} = \dfrac{2.35 - 0.05}{0.0395} = \dfrac{2.30}{0.0395} \approx 58.23 \, \text{A}$$

**(iii) Percentage Error at the Reference Reading of 50.2 A:**
The sensor's reading at the corresponding output must be computed. But the reference ammeter reads `50.2 A`, and the sensor output at that point would be `V_out = K·50.2 + V_0 = 0.0395·50.2 + 0.05 = 1.9829 + 0.05 = 2.033 V`. So measuring the same output, sensor's computed `I_p = 58.23 A`. Error:

$$\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{58.23 - 50.2}{50.2} \times 100 = \dfrac{8.03}{50.2} \times 100 \approx 15.99\,\%$$

This indicates the sensor is not calibrated — proper KTU-style answer notes that **re-calibration** is required. **(7 marks — 2 for K, 3 for I_p, 2 for error)**

---

**Question B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model] — Internal Choice Alternative**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Hall Effect current sensor used for DC current measurement. State the function of each block. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A Hall sensor has the following calibration data: `(I_p, V_out) = (0, 0.02), (10, 0.42), (20, 0.81), (30, 1.21), (40, 1.60), (50, 2.00)`. Determine the sensitivity `K` using the least-squares method, and predict the output voltage for `I_p = 35 A`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — Block Diagram Explanation:**
Refer to **Diagram 1 in Section 4** above. Briefly describe each block:
1. **Primary conductor** — carries the unknown DC current `I_p`.
2. **Ferromagnetic core** — channels magnetic flux; provides a closed magnetic path.
3. **Air gap with Hall element** — site where Hall Voltage is generated.
4. **Amplifier** — boosts mV-level `V_H` to standard 0–5 V or 4–20 mA.
5. **Temperature compensation & offset adjustment** — corrects for thermal drift and residual offset.
6. **Output stage** — drives the display, ADC, or controller.
7. **Isolated DC supply** — powers the sensor electronics; isolation provides galvanic safety.
**(7 marks — 3 for diagram, 4 for explanation)**

**Model Solution for (b) — Least-Squares Sensitivity:**

**Step 1 — Tabulate the Required Sums:**
With `n = 6` data points:

| `I_i` | `V_i` | `I_i·V_i` | `I_i²` |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.02 | 0.00 | 0 |
| 10 | 0.42 | 4.20 | 100 |
| 20 | 0.81 | 16.20 | 400 |
| 30 | 1.21 | 36.30 | 900 |
| 40 | 1.60 | 64.00 | 1600 |
| 50 | 2.00 | 100.00 | 2500 |
| **ΣI=150** | **ΣV=6.06** | **ΣIV=220.70** | **ΣI²=5500** |

**Step 2 — Apply the Least-Squares Slope Formula:**

$$K = \dfrac{n \sum(IV) - (\sum I)(\sum V)}{n \sum I^2 - (\sum I)^2}$$

$$K = \dfrac{6 \times 220.70 - 150 \times 6.06}{6 \times 5500 - 150^2}$$

$$K = \dfrac{1324.20 - 909.00}{33000 - 22500} = \dfrac{415.20}{10500}$$

$$\boxed{K \approx 0.03954 \, \text{V/A}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Intercept V_0 (Optional but Professional):**

$$V_0 = \dfrac{\sum V - K \sum I}{n} = \dfrac{6.06 - 0.03954 \times 150}{6} = \dfrac{6.06 - 5.931}{6} = \dfrac{0.129}{6} \approx 0.0215 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Predict V_out at I_p = 35 A:**

$$V_{\text{out}} = K \cdot I_p + V_0 = 0.03954 \times 35 + 0.0215$$

$$V_{\text{out}} = 1.3839 + 0.0215 \approx 1.405 \, \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{out}} \approx 1.41 \, \text{V at } I_p = 35 \, \text{A}}$$

**(7 marks — 2 for setting up sums, 3 for slope calculation, 2 for prediction)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the offset voltage `V_0`:** Many students compute `I_p = V_out / K` directly and ignore the small offset. The correct KTU-style model is `I_p = (V_out − V_0) / K`. **Loses 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing control current `I_c` with primary current `I_p`:** `I_c` is the tiny internal current driven through the Hall semiconductor chip (fixed by the sensor design). `I_p` is the *external* current being measured. **Loses 1–2 marks.**
> 3. **Not drawing the air gap in the core diagram:** Examiners expect a clear gap with the Hall element inside. A closed core with no gap is a **structural error** in the diagram. **Loses 1 mark.**
> 4. **Skipping units in the final answer:** Always write `V/A` for sensitivity, `A` for primary current, and `V` for output. **Loses 0.5 mark per occurrence.**
> 5. **Using a CT explanation in a Hall Effect question:** A common mistake — explaining Faraday's law of induction instead of the Lorentz force deflection. This indicates **conceptual confusion** and may cost 2–3 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Hall Effect definition** → Transverse voltage generation due to Lorentz force on charge carriers in a magnetic field.
- **Hall Voltage formula** → `V_H = (R_H · I_c · B) / t`, where `R_H = 1/(n·q)`.
- **Primary current → Magnetic field** → `B = μ₀·I_p / (2πr)` (bare wire) or amplified through a ferromagnetic core.
- **Final linear relationship** → `V_out = K · I_p + V_0`; hence `I_p = (V_out − V_0) / K`.
- **Sensitivity `K` units** → **V/A** (calculated from calibration graph slope).
- **Why Hall for DC?** → CTs cannot sense static (DC) flux; Hall sensors depend only on instantaneous `B`.
- **Role of core** → Concentrate and channel flux; make measurement independent of conductor position.
- **Role of air gap** → Provide uniform flux zone for Hall IC; prevent core saturation.
- **Calibration method** → Sweep known `I_p`, record `V_out`, fit a line, extract `K` and `V_0` via least-squares.
- **Linearity check** → Plot `V_out` vs `I_p`; ideally a straight line; report maximum deviation as % nonlinearity.
- **Accuracy formula** → `((I_measured − I_true) / I_true) × 100%`.
- **Common applications** → EV battery monitoring, solar PV DC string sensing, welding current measurement, industrial motor drives.
- **Typical materials for Hall IC** → GaAs (high sensitivity), InSb (highest mobility), InAs (good temperature stability).
- **Isolated measurement** → Sensor is galvanically isolated; no physical contact with the high-current bus; **inherently safe**.
- **Draw the cross-section** → Conductor at center, C-shaped core, air gap with Hall chip inside, B arrows perpendicular to chip.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always pair the **definition** with the **diagram**, and the **diagram** with the **formula**. Examiners reward students who show *both* qualitative understanding (diagram + explanation) and quantitative skill (formula + calculation). Aim for **2 pages of crisp, structured answer** for a 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Potentiometer interfacing to control LED brightness by PWM signal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL507 - AC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 16
> - **Topic:** Potentiometer interfacing to control LED brightness by PWM signal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Potentiometer Interfacing with PWM-based LED Brightness Control** is a fundamental embedded systems experiment in which an analog potentiometer (a three-terminal variable resistor) is read by a microcontroller through its **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**, and the resulting digital value is mapped to the **duty cycle of a Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** signal generated on a GPIO pin, which in turn drives an LED. The apparent brightness of the LED varies smoothly and continuously as the user rotates the potentiometer knob, demonstrating the closed-loop concept of *analog input → digital processing → analog-equivalent output*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** This experiment bridges the gap between **analog signal acquisition** (potentiometer), **digital signal processing** (ADC and PWM in software), and **power delivery to a load** (LED). It is a prerequisite experiment for motor speed control labs, where the same principle is extended from an LED to a DC motor or a fan.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Terminology Decoded:**
> - **Potentiometer (Pot):** A three-terminal resistor with a sliding contact (wiper) that produces a variable voltage divider output.
> - **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter):** A peripheral that converts an analog voltage into a discrete digital integer.
> - **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation):** A digital signaling technique that emulates an analog level by varying the fraction of time the signal is HIGH within a fixed period (duty cycle).
> - **Duty Cycle (D):** The percentage of one PWM period for which the signal remains in the HIGH state, expressed as:
> 
> $$D = \frac{T_{HIGH}}{T_{HIGH} + T_{LOW}} \times 100\%$$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are filling a bucket with water using a **tap that you can turn on for a fixed duration of 1 second every cycle**. If you keep the tap fully open for the entire 1 second, the bucket gets a lot of water — this is **100% duty cycle**. If you keep the tap open for only 0.5 seconds, the bucket gets half the water — this is **50% duty cycle**. If you close the tap completely, **0% duty cycle**.

Now imagine the **bucket is the LED**, and **you control the tap duration with a knob (the potentiometer)**. As you rotate the knob:

- The wiper voltage changes.
- The microcontroller "reads" this voltage via ADC and converts it to a number (say, 0 to 1023 for a 10-bit ADC).
- The software converts that number into a duty cycle percentage.
- The PWM hardware rapidly turns the LED ON and OFF, and the **average voltage** delivered to the LED changes — making it look dimmer or brighter to your eyes.

Because the human eye cannot distinguish flicker above **~100 Hz**, and most microcontrollers run PWM at **500 Hz to 1 kHz** for LEDs, the eye perceives a smooth brightness gradient — not a blinking light.

> [!TIP]
> **Think of PWM as a "Fast Light Switch":** A chef who flips a light switch 1,000 times per second can dim a room by changing how long the light stays ON during each flip. Your microcontroller is the chef, the pot is the recipe dial, and the LED is the room light.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Block Architecture

The experiment is built on **three sequential sub-systems**. Understanding them in isolation makes the integrated behaviour obvious.

### A. The Potentiometer Voltage Divider

A potentiometer has three terminals: **VCC**, **GND**, and **WIPER**. Internally, it is a resistive track with a sliding contact. The wiper divides the track into two resistors, **R1** (top) and **R2** (bottom), such that:

$$R_1 + R_2 = R_{total}$$

The wiper voltage with respect to GND is given by the standard voltage-divider rule:

$$V_{wiper} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_{total}}$$

- When the wiper is at the **GND end**: R2 = 0, so **V_wiper = 0 V**.
- When the wiper is at the **VCC end**: R2 = R_total, so **V_wiper = VCC**.

Thus, the wiper produces a **continuous analog voltage between 0 V and VCC** as the knob is rotated.

### B. The Analog-to-Digital Conversion

The microcontroller's ADC samples V_wiper and produces a digital integer. The conversion formula is:

$$N_{ADC} = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{wiper}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^n - 1)\right)$$

Where:
- $N_{ADC}$ is the digital output code (integer)
- $V_{ref}$ is the ADC reference voltage (usually VCC)
- $n$ is the ADC resolution in bits (10 for Arduino UNO, 12 for ESP32/Arduino Due)

**Inverse formula** (to recover voltage from ADC reading):

$$V_{wiper} = \frac{N_{ADC}}{2^n - 1} \cdot V_{ref}$$

### C. The PWM Duty Cycle Mapping

The ADC value is then mapped linearly to the PWM duty cycle. For a PWM counter of resolution $m$ bits (e.g., 8-bit on Arduino UNO → 0–255):

$$D_{PWM} = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{N_{ADC}}{2^n - 1} \cdot (2^m - 1)\right)$$

Where:
- $D_{PWM}$ is the integer PWM compare value written to the timer register (0 → 0% duty, 255 → 100% duty for 8-bit)
- $m$ is the PWM resolution in bits

The **duty cycle percentage** is then:

$$\text{Duty \%} = \frac{D_{PWM}}{2^m - 1} \times 100\%$$

The **average DC voltage** seen by the LED is:

$$V_{avg} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{D_{PWM}}{2^m - 1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is brightness perceived as linear-ish?** Human perception of brightness is approximately logarithmic (Weber-Fechner law). A pure linear duty-cycle ramp will look "non-linear" to the eye. For LED dimming applications, a **logarithmic correction** (gamma correction) is sometimes applied:
> $$\gamma_{corrected} = \left(\frac{N_{ADC}}{2^n - 1}\right)^{\gamma}, \quad \gamma \approx 2.2$$
> 
> For a 4-hour lab experiment, **linear mapping is sufficient** and is what is expected in the KTU record.

---

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{wiper} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Voltage-divider output of potentiometer | V |
| 2 | $N_{ADC} = \dfrac{V_{wiper}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^n - 1)$ | ADC digital output code | integer (levels) |
| 3 | $V_{wiper} = \dfrac{N_{ADC}}{2^n - 1} \cdot V_{ref}$ | Reconstructed voltage from ADC reading | V |
| 4 | $D_{PWM} = \dfrac{N_{ADC}}{2^n - 1} \cdot (2^m - 1)$ | PWM compare value (0 to 2^m−1) | integer |
| 5 | $\text{Duty \%} = \dfrac{D_{PWM}}{2^m - 1} \times 100$ | Duty cycle percentage | % |
| 6 | $V_{avg} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{D_{PWM}}{2^m - 1}$ | Average voltage seen by LED | V |
| 7 | $T_{PWM} = \dfrac{1}{f_{PWM}}$ | PWM period | seconds |

| 9 | $I_{LED} = \dfrac{V_{CC} \cdot D - V_f}{R_s}$ | Average LED current | A |
| 10 | $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | Smallest detectable voltage change (LSB) | V |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The pot-to-PWM-to-LED chain is the **exact architectural skeleton** of countless commercial systems:

- **Inverter fan regulators:** A pot sets the speed; PWM drives the MOSFET that drives the BLDC motor.
- **Audio volume knobs:** A pot changes a digital code, which adjusts PWM duty cycle on a Class-D amplifier.
- **Dimmable LED bulb:** A pot or digital slider maps to PWM that drives a high-power MOSFET.
- **Industrial motor drives (VFD):** The reference signal from a pot/PLC is converted to a PWM pattern on the IGBT bridge.

In the AC Machines Lab context, this experiment is the **preliminary warm-up** that prepares students to understand how the **reference signal** in a closed-loop motor speed control system is generated — the same principle, but with a motor in place of the LED.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Pot Voltage → ADC Code → PWM Duty Cycle

We will work out the full chain **numerically** for an Arduino UNO-based setup (VCC = 5 V, n = 10 bits, m = 8 bits) at a specific pot position.

**Given:**
- $V_{CC} = 5\text{ V}$
- $V_{ref} = 5\text{ V}$
- ADC resolution: $n = 10$ bits → $2^{10} - 1 = 1023$
- PWM resolution: $m = 8$ bits → $2^8 - 1 = 255$
- Potentiometer position: $R_2 / R_{total} = 0.4$ (i.e., 40% rotation from GND)

**Step 1 — Compute the wiper voltage using the voltage divider rule:**

$$V_{wiper} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_{total}} = 5 \cdot 0.4 = 2.0\text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Convert the wiper voltage to an ADC digital code:**

$$N_{ADC} = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{wiper}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^n - 1)\right) = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{2.0}{5.0} \cdot 1023\right)$$

$$\frac{2.0}{5.0} = 0.4$$

$$0.4 \times 1023 = 409.2$$

$$N_{ADC} = \text{round}(409.2) = 409$$

**Step 3 — Convert the ADC code to an 8-bit PWM compare value:**

$$D_{PWM} = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{N_{ADC}}{2^n - 1} \cdot (2^m - 1)\right) = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{409}{1023} \cdot 255\right)$$

$$\frac{409}{1023} = 0.3998$$

$$0.3998 \times 255 = 101.95$$

$$D_{PWM} = \text{round}(101.95) = 102$$

**Step 4 — Compute the duty cycle percentage:**

$$\text{Duty \%} = \frac{D_{PWM}}{2^m - 1} \times 100\% = \frac{102}{255} \times 100\% = 40.0\%$$

**Step 5 — Compute the average DC voltage seen by the LED:**

$$V_{avg} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{D_{PWM}}{2^m - 1} = 5 \cdot \frac{102}{255} = 5 \cdot 0.4 = 2.0\text{ V}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the average LED current** (assume red LED with $V_f = 2.0\text{ V}$ and $R_s = 220\ \Omega$):

$$I_{LED} = \frac{V_{avg} - V_f}{R_s} = \frac{2.0 - 2.0}{220} = 0\ \text{mA}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** When $V_{avg}$ drops below $V_f$, the LED is effectively OFF. This is why LEDs are often driven through a **MOSFET** rather than directly from the GPIO pin in dimming applications — a MOSFET allows the LED to be switched between $V_{CC}$ and GND with full current capability, eliminating the resistive drop issue.

**Step 7 — Result Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Pot wiper voltage | $V_{wiper}$ | **2.0 V** |
| ADC code (10-bit) | $N_{ADC}$ | **409** |
| PWM compare (8-bit) | $D_{PWM}$ | **102** |
| Duty cycle | Duty | **40.0 %** |
| Average LED voltage | $V_{avg}$ | **2.0 V** |

## 3.2 Reference C Implementation (Arduino UNO)

The KTU lab record requires a working, well-commented C program. Below is the canonical implementation:

```c
/*-------------------------------------------------------
 Experiment 16: Potentiometer Interfacing to Control LED
                 Brightness by PWM Signal
 Platform : Arduino UNO (ATmega328P, 10-bit ADC, 8-bit PWM)
-------------------------------------------------------*/

const int POT_PIN   = A0;   // Potentiometer wiper → Analog pin A0
const int LED_PIN   = 9;    // LED (with 220Ω series resistor) → Digital pin 9 (PWM ~)

void setup() {
  pinMode(LED_PIN, OUTPUT);     // Configure LED pin as output
  // No pinMode needed for POT_PIN — analog pins are input by default
  Serial.begin(9600);           // Initialize serial monitor @ 9600 baud
}

void loop() {
  // 1. Read the analog value from the potentiometer (0 to 1023)
  int adcValue = analogRead(POT_PIN);

  // 2. Map the 10-bit ADC value (0–1023) to an 8-bit PWM value (0–255)
  int pwmValue = map(adcValue, 0, 1023, 0, 255);

  // 3. Write the PWM compare value to the LED pin
  analogWrite(LED_PIN, pwmValue);

  // 4. (Optional) Print values to Serial Monitor for the lab record
  Serial.print("ADC: ");
  Serial.print(adcValue);
  Serial.print("  |  PWM: ");
  Serial.print(pwmValue);
  Serial.print("  |  Duty: ");
  Serial.print((pwmValue * 100.0) / 255.0, 1);
  Serial.println(" %");

  // 5. Small delay to stabilize the Serial output
  delay(50);
}
```

**Key Code Analysis:**

- `analogRead(POT_PIN)` triggers the ATmega328P's ADC, samples the wiper voltage, and returns an integer in the range 0 to 1023 (10-bit).
- `map(value, fromLow, fromHigh, toLow, toHigh)` is Arduino's built-in linear interpolation function. The actual mathematical equivalent of `map(adcValue, 0, 1023, 0, 255)` is:

$$pwmValue = \frac{(adcValue - 0) \cdot (255 - 0)}{1023 - 0} + 0 = \frac{adcValue \cdot 255}{1023}$$

- `analogWrite(LED_PIN, pwmValue)` configures the ATmega328P's **Timer1** compare register. Pin 9 on Arduino UNO is the **OC1A** output, which produces a PWM signal whose HIGH time per period is:

$$T_{HIGH} = \frac{pwmValue}{255} \cdot T_{PWM}$$

## 4.4 Signal Path Explanation (Read this for full marks)

1. **User Action Layer** — The student rotates the potentiometer's slotted knob. This is a purely mechanical action that moves the wiper along the resistive track.
2. **Transduction Layer** — The mechanical motion is transduced into a continuously variable DC voltage between 0 V and VCC (5 V on Arduino UNO). This is the **analog signal**.
3. **Acquisition Layer** — The microcontroller's ADC samples this voltage, quantizes it into 1024 discrete levels (10-bit), and produces a digital integer $N_{ADC}$.
4. **Processing Layer** — The C program running in the `loop()` function mathematically maps the 10-bit ADC reading to an 8-bit PWM compare value via the linear map() function.
5. **Generation Layer** — The microcontroller's hardware timer/counter generates a PWM waveform on the assigned GPIO pin (pin 9 on UNO), with a duty cycle proportional to the compare value.
6. **Power Delivery Layer** — The PWM waveform drives the gate of a MOSFET (or directly the LED with a current-limiting resistor for low-power lab demos).
7. **Output Layer** — The LED receives a high-frequency pulsed current. The **average** current — and hence perceived brightness — is determined by the duty cycle.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
**"Define Pulse Width Modulation (PWM). With a neat sketch, explain the concept of duty cycle."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

PWM is a digital signaling technique in which a digital signal is switched between HIGH and LOW states at a fixed frequency, and the **fraction of time the signal remains HIGH** is varied to encode information or to deliver a controllable average power to a load.

The **duty cycle (D)** is defined as:

$$D = \frac{T_{HIGH}}{T_{HIGH} + T_{LOW}} \times 100\% = \frac{T_{HIGH}}{T_{period}} \times 100\%$$

For example:
- A square wave with $T_{HIGH} = T_{LOW}$ has a **50% duty cycle**.
- If $T_{HIGH}$ equals the full period, the duty cycle is **100%** (signal is constantly ON).

A duty cycle of 0% means the signal is constantly LOW; a duty cycle of 100% means the signal is constantly HIGH. By varying D from 0% to 100%, the **average DC voltage** seen by a load (such as an LED) varies continuously, allowing digital hardware to emulate an analog output.

*(Sketch: a square wave with HIGH portions shaded, indicating varying widths for different duty cycles — 25%, 50%, 75%.)*

---

### Question A2
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**"What is the function of a potentiometer in an embedded system? How is its wiper voltage related to its mechanical rotation?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A potentiometer is a **three-terminal variable resistor** used as an **analog input device** in embedded systems. It allows a user to manually set a voltage between 0 V and the supply voltage (VCC), which the microcontroller can read via its ADC.

**Wiper voltage relationship:**

$$V_{wiper} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}$$

Where $\theta$ is the angular rotation of the knob from the GND end and $\theta_{max}$ is the total rotation range (typically 270° for a standard rotary pot). In other words, the wiper voltage is **linearly proportional to the rotation angle** of the knob, assuming a linear-taper potentiometer (which is the standard type used in labs).

When the knob is at the **GND end** → $V_{wiper} = 0$ V → ADC reads 0.
When the knob is at the **VCC end** → $V_{wiper} = V_{CC}$ → ADC reads 1023 (for 10-bit ADC).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions in a 14-mark slot typically carry two sub-parts of **7 marks each** (a and b). Students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in the slot. Both options are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *ADC and PWM Theory-Heavy*

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`**
**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram how a potentiometer is interfaced to a microcontroller to control the brightness of an LED using PWM. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 10-bit ADC with a reference voltage of 5 V reads a value of 712 from a potentiometer. This value is fed to an 8-bit PWM generator. Calculate: (i) the corresponding input voltage, (ii) the PWM compare value, (iii) the duty cycle percentage, and (iv) the average voltage seen by the LED. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The block diagram and operation (refer to SECTION 4.1 above for the full diagram) consists of the following stages:

1. **Potentiometer voltage divider (2 marks):** The pot is connected between VCC (5 V) and GND, with the wiper providing a variable voltage $V_{wiper}$ to the microcontroller's analog input pin. The wiper voltage is given by $V_{wiper} = V_{CC} \cdot R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$.

2. **ADC conversion (2 marks):** The microcontroller's ADC samples $V_{wiper}$ and converts it to a 10-bit digital value $N_{ADC}$ in the range 0 to 1023. The conversion formula is $N_{ADC} = (V_{wiper} / V_{ref}) \cdot 1023$.

3. **Software mapping to PWM (1.5 marks):** The C program maps the 10-bit ADC value to an 8-bit PWM value using `map(adcValue, 0, 1023, 0, 255)`. The compare value $D_{PWM} = (N_{ADC} \cdot 255) / 1023$.

4. **PWM generation and LED drive (1.5 marks):** The hardware timer generates a PWM signal at pin 9 with the computed duty cycle. This drives the LED (with a 220 Ω series resistor) — the average current through the LED varies with the duty cycle, producing a continuously variable brightness.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $n = 10$ bits, $V_{ref} = 5$ V, $N_{ADC} = 712$, $m = 8$ bits, $V_{CC} = 5$ V.

**(i) Input voltage:**

$$V_{wiper} = \frac{N_{ADC}}{2^n - 1} \cdot V_{ref} = \frac{712}{1023} \cdot 5$$

$$\frac{712}{1023} = 0.6960$$

$$V_{wiper} = 0.6960 \cdot 5 = 3.48\ \text{V}$$

**Answer: $V_{wiper} = 3.48\ \text{V}$** *(1.5 marks)*

**(ii) PWM compare value:**

$$D_{PWM} = \frac{N_{ADC}}{2^n - 1} \cdot (2^m - 1) = \frac{712}{1023} \cdot 255$$

$$0.6960 \cdot 255 = 177.5$$

$$D_{PWM} = \text{round}(177.5) = 178$$

**Answer: $D_{PWM} = 178$** *(1.5 marks)*

**(iii) Duty cycle percentage:**

$$\text{Duty \%} = \frac{D_{PWM}}{2^m - 1} \times 100\% = \frac{178}{255} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Duty \%} = 0.6980 \times 100\% = 69.8\%$$

**Answer: Duty cycle $\approx 69.8\%$** *(2 marks)*

**(iv) Average LED voltage:**

$$V_{avg} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{D_{PWM}}{2^m - 1} = 5 \cdot \frac{178}{255} = 5 \cdot 0.6980 = 3.49\ \text{V}$$

**Answer: $V_{avg} = 3.49\ \text{V}$** *(2 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Duty Cycle Off-By-One:** Many students write $\text{Duty \%} = (D_{PWM} / 256) \times 100$ using $2^m = 256$ instead of $(2^m - 1) = 255$. While the difference is small (about 0.4%), it is a **common deduction** in KTU valuation. Always use the formula $\text{Duty \%} = D_{PWM} / (2^m - 1) \times 100$ — this is the technically correct version. (For example, $D_{PWM} = 255$ must yield exactly 100%, not 99.6%.)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The circuit diagram is the **Pin-Level Connection Schematic** shown in SECTION 4.2 above. The student must draw:

1. Arduino UNO board (1 mark)
2. Potentiometer with three terminals: Terminal 1 → 5V, Terminal 3 → GND, Wiper → A0 (2 marks)
3. LED with anode connected to Pin 9 and cathode connected through a 220 Ω resistor to GND (2 marks)
4. VCC and GND rails labeled clearly, with all wire connections drawn (2 marks)

**Connection Summary (for the record):**

| Component | Terminal 1 | Terminal 2 (Wiper) | Terminal 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Potentiometer | Arduino **5V** | Arduino **A0** | Arduino **GND** |

| Component | Anode (+) | Cathode (−) |
|---|---|---|
| LED | Arduino **D9** (via 220 Ω) | Arduino **GND** |

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

The C program is the one shown in SECTION 3.2 above. The student must provide both the code and a line-by-line explanation. Sample explanation:

- `const int POT_PIN = A0;` — Defines a constant for the analog input pin connected to the pot wiper. (0.5 marks)
- `const int LED_PIN = 9;` — Defines a constant for the PWM output pin. Pin 9 is chosen because it supports hardware PWM via Timer1. (0.5 marks)
- `pinMode(LED_PIN, OUTPUT);` — Configures the LED pin as an output. (0.5 marks)
- `int adcValue = analogRead(POT_PIN);` — Initiates an ADC conversion on A0 and stores the 10-bit result (0–1023) in `adcValue`. (1 mark)
- `int pwmValue = map(adcValue, 0, 1023, 0, 255);` — Linearly scales the 10-bit ADC value to an 8-bit PWM compare value. (1.5 marks)
- `analogWrite(LED_PIN, pwmValue);` — Writes the PWM compare value to the Timer1 hardware, generating a PWM waveform on pin 9 with the computed duty cycle. (1.5 marks)
- `Serial.print(...)` and `delay(50);` — Used to display the readings on the Serial Monitor for verification (optional but useful in the lab). (0.5 marks)
- The `loop()` function ensures the ADC is read and PWM updated continuously, providing real-time response to pot movement. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Forgetting `pinMode`:** A surprising number of students omit `pinMode(LED_PIN, OUTPUT);` in `setup()`. Although `analogWrite()` internally configures the pin as OUTPUT, the KTU record evaluator expects explicit `pinMode` for clarity and good practice. **Lose 0.5 mark** if omitted.
> 
> **Another Common Mistake:** Writing `analogWrite(9, 1023);` directly without scaling — this is wrong because the Arduino's `analogWrite()` expects a value in the range 0–255. Passing 1023 will be **clamped** to 255 internally, but it is conceptually incorrect and will lose marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — Print This and Pin to Your Lab Bench:**

- [ ] **Potentiometer (10 kΩ, linear taper)** is connected between VCC and GND; the **wiper** goes to the ADC input pin (e.g., A0).
- [ ] **Voltage Divider:** $V_{wiper} = V_{CC} \cdot R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$. Linear taper means wiper voltage is proportional to rotation angle.
- [ ] **Arduino UNO ADC is 10-bit** → 1024 levels (0 to 1023). Formula: $N_{ADC} = (V_{wiper} / V_{ref}) \cdot 1023$.
- [ ] **Arduino UNO default PWM is 8-bit** on pins 3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11. Formula: $D_{PWM} = (N_{ADC} / 1023) \cdot 255$.
- [ ] **Duty cycle formula:** $\text{Duty \%} = D_{PWM} / (2^m - 1) \times 100 = D_{PWM} / 255 \times 100$ (8-bit PWM).
- [ ] **Average LED voltage:** $V_{avg} = V_{CC} \cdot D_{PWM} / 255$.
- [ ] **Default PWM frequency** on Arduino UNO pins 9 and 10: **~490 Hz** (Timer1, 8-bit). Pins 5 and 6: **~980 Hz** (Timer0). Pin 3 and 11: **~490 Hz** (Timer2).
- [ ] **LED current limiting resistor:** $R_s = (V_{CC} - V_f) / I_f$. For red LED ($V_f \approx 2$ V, $I_f = 15$ mA, $V_{CC} = 5$ V): $R_s \approx 220\ \Omega$.
- [ ] **`analogRead()` returns 0–1023**; **`analogWrite()` accepts 0–255**. Always use `map()` to convert.
- [ ] **PWM is a digital signal** — it does NOT change the voltage amplitude, only the **average** voltage seen by the load.
- [ ] **Human eye flicker fusion threshold** is ~100 Hz; Arduino's ~490 Hz is well above this, so LED dimming appears smooth.
- [ ] **Real-world extension:** Same principle (pot → ADC → PWM) is used in **motor speed controllers** (replace LED with a DC motor via a MOSFET driver).
- [ ] **Lab viva questions to expect:**
  1. "What is the resolution of the Arduino UNO's ADC?" → 10 bits.
  2. "What is the formula for duty cycle?" → $D = T_{HIGH} / T_{period} \times 100\%$.
  3. "Why is PWM used instead of a variable resistor for LED dimming?" → PWM is more efficient (no heat loss in a series resistor) and is digitally controllable.
  4. "What is the difference between linear and logarithmic taper pots?" → Linear pot wiper voltage is linear with rotation; log pot is logarithmic (used in audio volume controls to match human hearing).
  5. "If the ADC reads 0, what is the LED state?" → OFF (Duty = 0%). If ADC reads 1023 → FULL brightness (Duty = 100%).
- [ ] **Common KTU Record Mistakes to Avoid:**
  - Forgetting to draw the **current-limiting resistor** in the circuit diagram.
  - Connecting the **LED with reverse polarity** (always check the longer lead is the anode).
  - Writing `analogRead(A0)` as `digitalRead(A0)` — wrong API.
  - Using `delay(1000)` instead of `delay(50)` — slows the response unacceptably.
  - Not labeling the **PWM pin number** in the circuit diagram.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DC motor speed control using MOSFET driven by PWM signal from Arduino
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL507 - AC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 16
> - **Topic:** DC motor speed control using MOSFET driven by PWM signal from Arduino

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔬 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 🎯 Aim of the Experiment
To control the speed of a **DC shunt motor** by varying the **average armature voltage** using a **PWM signal** generated by an **Arduino UNO** and switching the motor through a **power MOSFET** (IRF540N / IRLZ44N).

## 📘 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) speed control** of a DC motor is an *electronic commutation technique* in which the **average DC voltage** delivered to the motor armature is regulated by varying the **duty cycle (D)** of a fixed-frequency square wave. A **voltage-controlled semiconductor switch** (here, an N-channel enhancement-mode MOSFET) is operated in its **cut-off (OFF)** and **triode/ohmic (ON)** regions by the **PWM gate pulse** sourced from the Arduino's **Timer/Counter peripherals**.

## 🧠 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a ceiling fan with a mechanical regulator:** Traditional regulators use a series resistance (rheostat) which wastes power as heat. Imagine instead someone flicking the switch ON and OFF **50 times per second**. If the switch is ON for 30 ms and OFF for 20 ms (duty cycle = 60%), the fan "feels" an average of 60% of full supply. The motor never actually gets reduced voltage — it gets **full voltage sometimes and zero voltage other times** — but the inertia of the rotor smooths it out, and the speed settles to a value corresponding to 60% of rated.

| Concept | Mechanical Analogy | Electronic Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Rotating knob on regulator | Throttle in a car | PWM duty cycle |
| Heat loss in rheostat | Wasted fuel in idling | Eliminated (MOSFET is either fully ON or OFF) |
| Tap controlling water flow | Accelerator pedal | `analogWrite()` value (0–255) |

## 🔑 Key Subsystems Identified

1. **Controller Stage** – Arduino UNO (ATmega328P) generating PWM on Pin 9 (Timer1, ~490 Hz default).
2. **Switching Stage** – Logic-level N-channel MOSFET (IRLZ44N recommended) accepting 5 V gate drive.
3. **Power Stage** – DC motor (12 V / 24 V shunt type) with armature terminals.
4. **Protection Stage** – Flyback/ freewheeling diode (1N4007 or FR107) across motor terminals.
5. **Reference Stage** – 10 kΩ potentiometer on analog pin A0 producing 0–5 V (mapped to 0–1023 ADC value).
6. **Isolated Supply** – Separate 12 V SMPS for motor, 5 V USB for Arduino (common ground).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 📐 Theoretical Foundation — Four Pillars

### Pillar 1: Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) Mathematics

A PWM waveform is characterized by:
- **Period** $T$ (seconds) = $1 / f_{sw}$
- **ON-time** $T_{on}$ (seconds) when output is HIGH
- **OFF-time** $T_{off}$ (seconds) when output is LOW
- **Duty cycle** $D$ = ratio of ON time to total period

The fundamental property exploited in motor control is that the **average DC value** of a unipolar square wave is proportional to its duty cycle.

For Arduino UNO, the default `analogWrite(pin, x)` function maps an integer `x` in the range **0 to 255** to a duty cycle of **0 % to 100 %**. The standard default frequencies are:
- **Pins 5, 6** → 980 Hz (Timer0)
- **Pins 9, 10, 11** → 490 Hz (Timer1)
- **Pins 3, 11** → 490 Hz (Timer2)

### Pillar 2: MOSFET as an Electronic Switch

The IRF540N / IRLZ44N is an **N-channel enhancement-mode MOSFET**. Its three regions of operation on the $I_D$ vs $V_{DS}$ curve are:

| Region | Gate Condition | $V_{GS}$ vs $V_{th}$ | Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cut-off** | $V_{GS} < V_{th}$ | Below threshold | Switch OFF, $I_D \approx 0$ |
| **Triode (Ohmic)** | $V_{GS} >> V_{th}$ | Strongly enhanced | Switch ON, $R_{DS(on)}$ very small |
| **Saturation (Active)** | $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ and $V_{DS} > V_{GS}-V_{th}$ | Above threshold | Current source, used in amplifiers |

> [!TIP]
> In PWM motor control, the MOSFET is **saturated into triode** during ON state — meaning $R_{DS(on)}$ becomes very small (e.g., **22 mΩ** for IRLZ44N), so conduction loss $I_D^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)}$ is minimal. This is why MOSFETs are preferred over BJTs (no base current required).

### Pillar 3: DC Shunt Motor Speed Equation

The fundamental speed equation of a separately excited / shunt DC motor is:

$$
N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}
$$

For PWM-fed operation, the instantaneous voltage $V$ is either $V_s$ (during $T_{on}$) or $0$ (during $T_{off}$). Due to **mechanical inertia** and **armature inductance**, the motor responds to the **average** voltage:

$$
V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s
$$

Therefore the effective speed becomes:

$$
N = \frac{D \cdot V_s - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}
$$

### Pillar 4: Need for the Flyback (Freewheeling) Diode

The armature winding has significant inductance $L_a$. When the MOSFET abruptly turns OFF, the inductor attempts to maintain current $i_L(t)$ according to $V_L = L \cdot \frac{di}{dt}$. Without a path, the voltage across the MOSFET would spike to hundreds of volts ($-L \cdot di/dt$ becomes very large) — destroying the device.

The **flyback diode** (cathode to motor +, anode to motor −) provides a closed loop for the inductive current to decay safely through the motor's own resistance, clamping the spike to one diode drop (~0.7 V).

## 📊 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Expression | SI Unit |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| $D$ | Duty cycle | $D = T_{on} / T = \text{analogWrite}/255$ | dimensionless |
| $V_{avg}$ | Average armature voltage | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s$ | Volt (V) |
| $f_{sw}$ | PWM switching frequency | $f_{sw} = 1 / T$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| $T_{on}$ | MOSFET ON time | $T_{on} = D / f_{sw}$ | second (s) |
| $E_b$ | Back EMF | $E_b = V_{avg} - I_a R_a = k \Phi \omega$ | Volt (V) |
| $N$ | Motor speed | $N = (V_{avg} - I_a R_a) / (k\Phi)$ | rpm |
| $\omega$ | Angular speed | $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ | rad/s |
| $P_{sw}$ | Switching loss | $P_{sw} = 0.5 \cdot V_{DS} \cdot I_D \cdot (t_r + t_f) \cdot f_{sw}$ | Watt (W) |
| $P_{cond}$ | Conduction loss | $P_{cond} = I_D^{2} \cdot R_{DS(on)} \cdot D$ | Watt (W) |
| $V_{GS(th)}$ | Gate threshold voltage | Typically **1 V to 4 V** for logic-level MOSFET | Volt (V) |
| $\eta$ | Drive efficiency | $\eta = P_{out} / (P_{out} + P_{loss})$ | dimensionless |

## 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics and Drones** — Variable speed DC motor drivers in robot wheels and BLDC ESCs.
- **Electric Vehicles (EVs)** — Modern EV motor controllers use 10–20 kHz PWM through IGBTs/SiC MOSFETs.
- **HVAC Fans and Blowers** — Energy-efficient variable speed drives replace fixed-speed induction motors.
- **CNC Machines and 3D Printers** — Spindle and feed motor speed regulation.
- **Solar Water Pumps** — MPPT + PWM drive to match pump load to PV panel characteristics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 📘 Derivation 1: Average Voltage of a Unipolar PWM Wave

Let a square wave of amplitude $V_s$ have period $T$ and ON-time $T_{on} = D \cdot T$.

The instantaneous voltage function $v(t)$ is:
$$
v(t) = \begin{cases} V_s, & 0 \le t \le T_{on} \\ 0, & T_{on} < t \le T \end{cases}
$$

The average value over one period is computed by integrating and dividing by $T$:

$$
V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v(t) \, dt
$$

Substituting the piecewise definition:

$$
V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \left[ \int_{0}^{T_{on}} V_s \, dt + \int_{T_{on}}^{T} 0 \, dt \right]
$$

Evaluating the first integral:

$$
V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \left[ V_s \cdot T_{on} + 0 \right] = \frac{V_s \cdot T_{on}}{T}
$$

Recognizing that $D = T_{on} / T$:

$$
\boxed{V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The motor "sees" a value between 0 V (at $D=0$) and full $V_s$ (at $D=1$). The current is continuous due to armature inductance, and the torque ripple is filtered by rotor inertia.

---

## 📘 Derivation 2: Speed–Duty Cycle Linearized Relationship

Starting from the DC motor EMF equation:

$$
E_b = V_{avg} - I_a R_a
$$

But $E_b$ is also related to angular speed by Faraday's law:

$$
E_b = k \Phi \omega = k \Phi \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}
$$

Equating the two:

$$
k \Phi \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} = D \cdot V_s - I_a R_a
$$

Solving for $N$:

$$
N = \frac{60}{2\pi k \Phi} \cdot (D \cdot V_s - I_a R_a)
$$

$$
\boxed{N = \frac{60 \cdot (D V_s - I_a R_a)}{2\pi k \Phi}}
$$

**Physical interpretation of each term:**
- $D V_s$ — average supply voltage delivered to armature.
- $I_a R_a$ — ohmic voltage drop that always opposes applied voltage.
- $k \Phi$ — magnetic loading; for shunt motor $\Phi$ is **constant** (field supply is independent), so $N$ depends **linearly** on $D$.

---

## 📘 Derivation 3: Switching Power Loss in the MOSFET

During every transition from OFF to ON (rise time $t_r$) and ON to OFF (fall time $t_f$), the MOSFET simultaneously carries current and supports voltage, dissipating energy.

Energy dissipated in one turn-on transition:

$$
E_{on} = \int_{0}^{t_r} V_{DS}(t) \cdot I_D(t) \, dt
$$

Assuming linear transitions (worst case, both $V$ and $I$ change linearly):

$$
E_{on} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DS} \cdot I_D \cdot t_r
$$

Similarly for turn-off:

$$
E_{off} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DS} \cdot I_D \cdot t_f
$$

Total switching loss per cycle:

$$
E_{sw} = E_{on} + E_{off} = \frac{1}{2} V_{DS} I_D (t_r + t_f)
$$

Multiplying by switching frequency $f_{sw}$:

$$
\boxed{P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} V_{DS} I_D (t_r + t_f) f_{sw}}
$$

> **Design Insight:** Doubling the PWM frequency doubles the switching loss. This is why 20 kHz+ applications require fast-switching SiC or GaN devices.

---

## 📘 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Typical KTU Numerical)

> **Problem Statement:** A 12 V, 2 A DC shunt motor has armature resistance $R_a = 1 \, \Omega$ and flux $\Phi$ such that back EMF constant $k\Phi = 0.05 \text{ V·s/rad}$. The motor is fed via a MOSFET driven by Arduino at 60 % duty cycle from a 12 V supply. Calculate: **(a)** Average armature voltage, **(b)** Back EMF at load, **(c)** Speed in rpm.

### Given Data
- $V_s = 12 \text{ V}$
- $I_a = 2 \text{ A}$
- $R_a = 1 \, \Omega$
- $k\Phi = 0.05 \text{ V·s/rad}$
- $D = 0.60$

### (a) Average Voltage

$$
V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s = 0.60 \times 12 = \mathbf{7.2 \text{ V}}
$$

### (b) Back EMF

$$
E_b = V_{avg} - I_a R_a = 7.2 - (2)(1) = 7.2 - 2 = \mathbf{5.2 \text{ V}}
$$

### (c) Motor Speed

Using $E_b = k\Phi \omega$:

$$
\omega = \frac{E_b}{k\Phi} = \frac{5.2}{0.05} = 104 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Converting to rpm:

$$
N = \frac{60 \cdot \omega}{2\pi} = \frac{60 \times 104}{2\pi} = \frac{6240}{6.2832} \approx \mathbf{993 \text{ rpm}}
$$

### ✅ Verification
- At $D = 1$ (full voltage): $N_{rated} = 60(12 - 2)/(2\pi \times 0.05) = 60(10)/(0.3142) = 1909 \text{ rpm}$ — consistent with a small hobby motor.
- Ratio $993/1909 \approx 0.52$, slightly less than $D = 0.60$ because of the constant $I_a R_a$ drop. ✓

---

## 📘 Comparison Matrix: PWM vs Other Speed Control Methods

| Method | Energy Efficiency | Speed Range | Smoothness | Hardware Cost | Heat Dissipation |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Rheostatic (series R)** | Poor (30-50%) | 50-100% | Poor (stepped) | Low | **Very High** |
| **Armature voltage (linear reg.)** | Poor (40-60%) | 0-100% | Excellent | Medium | **High** |
| **PWM (MOSFET switch)** | **Excellent (90-95%)** | 0-100% | Excellent | Low | **Low** |
| **Field flux control** | Good (70-85%) | 100-Nmax% | Good | Medium | Medium |
| **Series-Parallel armature** | Good | Stepped | Poor | High | Low |

> [!TIP]
> The **PWM method** dominates all others because the MOSFET dissipates negligible power in either ON or OFF state — only during brief switching transitions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🖼️ 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 🔌 Diagram A — Complete Hardware Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    PC[USB LAPTOP FOR ARDUINO IDE] -->|Serial Upload| ARD[ARDUINO UNO ATMEGA328P]
    POT[10K OHM POTENTIOMETER] -->|Analog 0 to 5V| A0[ARDUINO A0 PIN]
    A0 -->|ADC 0 to 1023| ARD
    ARD -->|PWM 490Hz Pin 9| GD[GATE DRIVER OR DIRECT]
    GD -->|Gate Pulse Vgs| MOSG[MOSFET GATE TERMINAL]
    PS[12V DC SMPS SUPPLY] -->|Vplus| MOPP[MOTOR POSITIVE TERMINAL]
    MOPP --> MOTOR[DC SHUNT MOTOR ARMATURE]
    MOTOR --> MOMN[MOTOR NEGATIVE TERMINAL]
    MOSD[MOSFET DRAIN] --> MOMN
    MOSS[MOSFET SOURCE] --> GND[COMMON GROUND]
    DIODE[FLYBACK DIODE 1N4007] -.->|Freewheel Path| MOMN
    SHAFT[MOTOR SHAFT] --> MOTOR
    TACH[TACHOMETER OR OPTICAL SENSOR] -->|RPM Feedback| SHAFT
    ARD -.->|LCD Display 16x2| LCD[SPEED INDICATOR]
```

> **Node ID Note:** All node identifiers (`ARD`, `MOSG`, `MOPP`, `MOMN`, `MOSD`, `MOSS`, `PS`, `POT`, `GD`, `DIODE`, `SHAFT`, `TACH`, `LCD`, `PC`) are pure alphanumeric and prefixed with letters — no reserved Mermaid keywords are used.

---

## 🔁 Diagram B — PWM Switching Signal Behaviour Over Time (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    T0[T EQUALS 0] --> T1[Microcontroller Loads analogWrite Value]
    T1 --> T2[Timer1 Compares to OCR1A Register]
    T2 --> T3{OC1A Pin Output State}
    T3 -->|High| T4[Gate Voltage Vgs HIGH above Vth]
    T3 -->|Low| T5[Gate Voltage Vgs LOW below Vth]
    T4 --> T6[MOSFET Channel Conducts]
    T6 --> T7[Motor Terminals See Vsupply]
    T5 --> T8[MOSFET Channel Off]
    T8 --> T9[Motor Isolated Freewheel via Diode]
    T7 --> T10[Average Voltage Vavg equals D times Vsupply]
    T9 --> T10
    T10 --> T11[Rotor Settles to N rpm for Given D]
```

---

## 🛠️ Diagram C — Arduino Program Logic Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    INIT([BEGIN SETUP]) --> PINMODE[pinMode 9 as OUTPUT]
    PINMODE --> PINMODEA0[pinMode A0 as INPUT]
    PINMODEA0 --> SERIAL[Serial.begin 9600]
    SERIAL --> LOOPSTART([ENTER LOOP])
    LOOPSTART --> READPOT[analogRead A0 Returns 0 to 1023]
    READPOT --> MAPVAL[map Value to 0 to 255]
    MAPVAL --> PWMWRITE[analogWrite 9 MappedValue]
    PWMWRITE --> DELAY[delay 10 milliseconds]
    DELAY --> SERIALOUT[Serial.print Current Duty Cycle]
    SERIALOUT --> LOOPSTART
```

---

## 📐 Diagram D — Detailed Functional Circuit (Block View)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph POWERDOMAIN[POWER DOMAIN 12V]
        PS[12V DC SUPPLY]
    end
    subgraph CONTROLDOMAIN[CONTROL DOMAIN 5V]
        ARD[ARDUINO PWM PIN 9]
        POT[POTENTIOMETER 10K]
        LCD[16X2 LCD]
    end
    subgraph SWITCHDOMAIN[SWITCHING DOMAIN]
        MOS[N-CHANNEL MOSFET IRLZ44N]
        DIODE[FLYBACK DIODE 1N4007]
    end
    subgraph LOADDOMAIN[LOAD DOMAIN]
        MOT[DC SHUNT MOTOR 12V]
    end
    PS -->|Vplus| MOT
    ARD -->|Gate Drive 5V PWM| MOS
    POT -->|Reference 0-5V| ARD
    MOS -->|Switched Armature| MOT
    DIODE -->|Reverse Polarity Across Motor| MOT
    MOT -->|RPM Pulse| ARD
    ARD -->|I2C Data SDA SCL| LCD
    ARD -.->|Common Reference| GNDNODE[COMMON GROUND BUS]
    PS -.->|Common Reference| GNDNODE
```

---

## 📋 Lab Apparatus & Component Specifications (For Record)

| S.No | Component | Specification | Quantity | Purpose |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|---|
| 1 | Arduino UNO | ATmega328P, 16 MHz | 1 | PWM signal generator |
| 2 | DC Shunt Motor | 12 V / 24 V, ≤ 2 A | 1 | Load under test |
| 3 | N-Channel MOSFET | IRLZ44N (logic-level) | 1 | Electronic switch |
| 4 | Flyback Diode | 1N4007 (1 A, 1000 V) | 1 | Inductive spike protection |
| 5 | Potentiometer | 10 kΩ linear | 1 | Speed reference input |
| 6 | DC Power Supply | 12 V / 5 A regulated | 1 | Motor power |
| 7 | Breadboard & Jumpers | 840 tie-points | 1 | Prototyping |
| 8 | Multimeter | Digital, True RMS | 1 | Voltage / current measurement |
| 9 | Tachometer | Non-contact optical | 1 | RPM measurement |
| 10 | Oscilloscope (optional) | 2-channel, ≥ 20 MHz | 1 | PWM waveform visualization |

> [!WARNING]
> **Never connect the motor directly to the Arduino 5 V pin** — Arduino can source only ~40 mA per pin. The MOSFET is essential for current amplification.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 🅰️ Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define duty cycle. How is it generated using Arduino's `analogWrite()` function?**
`[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 4]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **duty cycle $D$** is the fraction of one PWM period for which the signal remains in the HIGH state. It is mathematically expressed as $D = T_{on} / T$ where $T_{on}$ is the ON-time and $T$ is the total period. The Arduino's `analogWrite(pin, value)` function accepts an integer `value` between **0 and 255**, and the internal Timer/Counter hardware maps it to $D$ such that:

$$
D = \frac{\text{value}}{255}
$$

For example, `analogWrite(9, 128)` produces a 50.2 % duty cycle square wave on pin 9 at approximately 490 Hz. The function does not actually produce a true analog output — it produces a fixed-frequency PWM signal whose average DC value is proportional to `value`.

---

### **Q2. Why is a flyback diode connected across the DC motor in a MOSFET-driven PWM circuit? What happens if it is omitted?**
`[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 4]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The armature winding of a DC motor possesses significant **inductance $L_a$**. When the MOSFET is switched OFF abruptly, the inductor attempts to maintain its current according to $V_L = L \frac{di}{dt}$. The resulting $\frac{di}{dt}$ is very large, producing a **high reverse-voltage spike** of the form $V = -L \frac{di}{dt}$ across the MOSFET. This spike can reach hundreds of volts, **exceeding the MOSFET's $V_{DS(max)}$ rating** and causing permanent destruction of the device.

The **flyback diode (1N4007)** is connected in reverse bias across the motor terminals (cathode to +ve, anode to −ve). During MOSFET ON state, it is reverse-biased and inactive. When the MOSFET switches OFF, the diode becomes forward-biased and provides a **closed freewheeling path** for the decaying inductive current, clamping the voltage spike to one diode forward drop (~0.7 V) and protecting the MOSFET.

---

## 🅱️ Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **📌 Question A — Choice 1**

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of **DC motor speed control using Arduino-generated PWM driving a power MOSFET**. Discuss the role of the **gate driver, flyback diode, and potentiometer** in the circuit. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **220 V, 10 A, 1500 rpm DC shunt motor** has armature resistance $R_a = 2 \, \Omega$ and field current $I_f = 1 \text{ A}$ (constant flux). When driven by a PWM signal of **70 % duty cycle** from a 220 V supply, calculate the **no-load speed** of the motor. Assume armature reaction is negligible. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to A(a):**

**Working Principle (3 marks):**

The Arduino UNO reads the position of a 10 kΩ potentiometer through its **analog input pin A0**. The internal **10-bit ADC** converts the 0–5 V analog signal into a digital value ranging from **0 to 1023**. This value is then mapped to the PWM range of **0 to 255** using the Arduino `map()` function.

The mapped value is written to **digital pin 9** using `analogWrite()`. Pin 9 is connected to the **gate** of an N-channel enhancement-mode MOSFET (IRLZ44N). The ATmega328P's **Timer1** generates a 490 Hz square wave on this pin. When the gate voltage $V_{GS} = 5 \text{ V}$ (which is well above the threshold $V_{th} \approx 1 \text{ V}$ for IRLZ44N), the MOSFET channel conducts, connecting the motor's negative terminal to ground. When $V_{GS} = 0 \text{ V}$, the MOSFET is OFF, and the motor is disconnected.

**Components and their roles (4 marks):**

- **Potentiometer (10 kΩ)** — Acts as a **manual speed reference**. Rotating the knob varies the resistance and hence the voltage at A0, which the Arduino interprets as desired speed.

- **MOSFET (IRLZ44N)** — Functions as a **fast electronic switch** capable of handling the motor's full armature current (up to 30 A pulsed, 17 A continuous).
